Rigor (78) drives the score: matched-FLOPs and matched-parameter isoFLOP design isolates state capacity as the causal variable, ablations directly falsify the competing hypothesis (learned M_0 rather than state size), evaluation is on third-party benchmarks (RULER, 15-task suite), code is released, and the scaling-law fit is validated at one held-out 8B point. Non-Obviousness (78) is high because the insight is that sparsity, not raw state size, is the scaling knob, and the specific fusion of the gated delta rule with product-key addressing plus the two-pointer sparse kernel and in-place-undo backward pass is not what a competent practitioner would reach for by default. Novelty (75) is NEW METHOD plus a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, tempered by concurrent work (Zhao and Jones, 2026) pursuing the same sparsification idea at smaller scale. Completeness (80) and Resilience (75) benefit from unusually honest limitation acknowledgment (10x lower MFU than the mature Gated DeltaNet kernel, memory footprint rivaling parameter count, a flagged RULER benchmark artifact that does not favor the method). Rigor is held below 85 by the total absence of seeds, error bars, or significance tests on the headline RULER margin (31.2 vs 20.0 at 1.4B) and by preprint status. Practical Impact (70) reflects released FAIR code offset by an explicitly immature kernel. Weighted composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 = 76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Linear RNNs (Mamba2, Gated DeltaNet, xLSTM) offer constant per-token compute and memory by compressing history into a fixed-size state, unlike transformer KV caches that grow linearly with sequence length. But that same fixed-size compression is why linear RNNs lag transformers on long-context recall and in-context learning: prior work (Arora et al., 2025) shows recall is fundamentally bounded by hidden-state size. Naively enlarging the state in Gated DeltaNet or Mamba2 is not free, since their dense outer-product updates make per-token FLOPs scale linearly with state size, so anyone deploying long-context agents, codebase-scale reasoning, or long-video world models on linear-RNN backbones faces a hard state-size-vs-compute tradeoff that this paper targets directly. Key Idea: The Gated DeltaNet update rule (decay-gated delta rule writing into a dense d_qk x d_v matrix) can be sparsified: instead of updating the entire dense state at every step, maintain a much larger N-slot memory table and, at each timestep, use Product-Key Memory-style factored addressing to select only W slots to write and R slots to read. Because top-k selection over the outer sum of two sqrt(N)-dimensional score vectors doesn't require materializing the full N-way score matrix, the read/write cost stays O(sqrt(N)) regardless of how large N (the memory) is — decoupling the size of the state from the per-token compute cost that governs FLOPs. Methodology: The pipeline per SDM layer: (1) project the input token into pre-PKM write keys k' and read queries q' (each split into two sqrt(N)-dimensional halves), compute their outer sums to score all N candidate slots without materializing the full score matrix, and take top-W write indices and top-R read indices; (2) apply a gated delta write only to the W selected slots, using a per-head learned forget gate alpha_t = exp(-A * softplus(...)) and input gate beta_t = sigmoid(...), matching the Gated DeltaNet recurrence but restricted to the addressed slots; (3) read via a weighted sum over the R selected slots; (4) RMS-norm, element-wise gate, and mix outputs across heads via W_o. Training uses chunk-wise parallelism: an intra-chunk phase computes sparse interaction matrices via a two-pointer merge over sorted top-W indices (exploiting that most token pairs share no slots) and a triangular solve for the WY representation, while a sequential chunk-wise recurrent phase handles the true memory-to-memory dependency; backward pass avoids storing per-chunk memory snapshots by undoing sparse in-place updates (an idea borrowed from Sparse Access Memory, Rae et al. 2016), reducing checkpoint memory from 226GB to 8GB per 8B layer at 16k sequence length. Three design decisions stand out: (1) matching parameters and FLOPs exactly between SDM and Gated DeltaNet (same W_q, W_k, W_v projection sizes) so any gain is attributable to state capacity alone — well-justified and central to the paper's causal claim; (2) using a single memory head (H=1) to maximize per-head state size under fixed FLOPs, exploiting a d^3 growth rate for memory size versus a d^2 model parameter count — justified by a cited finding (Arora et al., 2025) that single-head maximizes state at fixed budget, but this makes H a hand-tuned hyperparameter rather than something learned; (3) learning the initial memory state M_0 as a parametric-memory mechanism, motivated as "free" at inference (same FLOPs as null-init) — plausible but the ablation (Section 6) shows this benefit is secondary to state size, so its inclusion by default is a design choice more than a proven necessity. Key Results: IsoFLOP scaling law (Fig. 3, levels 1–8, up to 1.48B params/168.4B tokens, extrapolated to level 13 at 8.14B params/1.141T tokens): SDM's loss-vs-FLOPs power law has alpha=0.0628 (R^2=0.999) versus Gated DeltaNet's alpha=0.0652 (R^2=0.997) and Full Attention's alpha=0.0605 (R^2=1.000); the actual 8B SDM run falls within the 95% bootstrap CI of its predicted loss and slightly beats the 8B Full Attention model. Short-context tasks (Table 2, 13 reasoning/knowledge benchmarks): at 1.4B, SDM improves over Gated DeltaNet on 13/15 tasks with average accuracy 38.51% vs 37.98% (Gated DeltaNet) and lowest validation text NLL among all models (2.654); at 8B, average accuracy 56.84% vs 55.70% (Gated DeltaNet) and 56.65% (Full Attention). Long-context recall (RULER, 13 tasks, lengths 4k-131k): SDM reaches 31.2% average accuracy at 1.4B and 50.2% at 8B versus Gated DeltaNet's 20.0%/34.2%, though Full Attention still leads overall at 8B (61.2%) thanks to its unbounded KV cache, with SDM matching or beating Full Attention on only 4 of 6 (1.4B) or 3 of 6 (8B) individual RULER sub-tasks — multikey_2 remains a persistent weak point for all fixed-state models. Code-data perplexity by token position (Fig. 4, up to 1M tokens): SDM's perplexity drops toward ~2.0 at long context while Mamba2 and Gated DeltaNet plateau around 2.2-2.3. Ablations (Table 3) isolate state size as the dominant driver: SDM without learned M_0 still substantially beats Gated DeltaNet, and adding learned M_0 to Gated DeltaNet gives negligible improvement (0.849 to 0.850 NLL). Memory-size ablation shows monotonic NLL degradation from 0.914 (432MB) to 0.947 (27MB) as memory shrinks, but even the 27MB variant beats Gated DeltaNet on RULER (20.7% vs 16.0%). Baselines (Gated DeltaNet, Mamba2, Full Attention) are current, appropriately matched architectures, and the isoFLOP/isoparameter control is methodologically strong. This is a preprint (arXiv, July 2026) with no indication of peer review. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (sparsifying the Gated DeltaNet delta-rule update via Product-Key Memory-style factored addressing) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (state size, not learned initialization, drives linear-RNN in-context gains; sparsity permits three-orders-of-magnitude state growth at matched FLOPs). The genuinely novel piece is the specific fusion of the gated delta rule with sparse PKM-style read/write addressing and the accompanying chunk-wise training algorithm (two-pointer sparse interaction, in-place-undo backward pass); the constituent pieces (delta rule, PKM addressing, WY chunk-wise parallelism) are each pre-existing. The authors themselves note a closely related concurrent work (Zhao & Jones, 2026, "Fast-Weight Product Key Memory") that pursues a similar sparsification idea but at smaller scale (<=100M non-embedding params) without isoFLOP controls, so SDM's differentiator is scale and rigor of the FLOPs-matched comparison rather than the base idea of sparse memory itself. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and directly test the two causal claims the paper makes (state size vs. learned initial state; memory size vs. performance), which is a genuine strength. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere, including for the headline isoFLOP comparisons and RULER numbers, so it is unclear how much of the SDM-over-Gated DeltaNet margin (e.g., 31.2 vs 20.0 average RULER accuracy at 1.4B) would survive seed variance. Code is released at github.com/facebookresearch/sparse-delta-memory, a positive for reproducibility. Evaluation uses standard third-party benchmarks (RULER, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, ARC, PIQA, OpenBookQA, RACE, CommonsenseQA, BoolQ, TriviaQA, HumanEval+, NaturalQuestions, MMLU, GSM8K), not an author-constructed benchmark, which mitigates Goodhart's Law concerns. The scaling-law extrapolation from levels 1-8 to a level-13 (8B) prediction is a reasonable methodology but is inherently a single validated extrapolation point, not multiple independent confirmations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper's primary claims rest on pretraining runs and benchmark evaluation, not proofs). - Practical: Training was run on NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs (standard commercial hardware), and the largest model (8B params, 1.14T tokens) is within range of common large-scale training setups, but the paper states the current SDM kernel is immature (roughly 10x lower MFU than the optimized Gated DeltaNet FLA kernel), so today's practical wall-clock cost is materially higher than FLOPs parity suggests. - Efficient: By construction SDM matches Gated DeltaNet's FLOPs and parameter count exactly, but its memory footprint can rival or exceed the model's parameter count (e.g., the 8B SDM state occupies as much memory as 203,400 tokens of the 8B Full Attention model's KV cache) and its state resides in HBM rather than fast GPU SRAM, causing a 10x lower memory bandwidth utilization than Gated DeltaNet's compact state. - Effective: The evidence (isoFLOP scaling curves, RULER, and 15-task reasoning/knowledge suites) directly measures the paper's stated problem (long-context recall and in-context learning under fixed compute), not just a proxy, and shows consistent gains over Gated DeltaNet across scales. - Scalable: The paper validates its scaling-law prediction at one point beyond the fitted range (8B, extrapolated 5x beyond the largest directly-fit model in FLOPs terms), which is a genuine test of scalability, but does not test 10x-100x beyond that single 8B validation point, so behavior at frontier model scales (tens to hundreds of billions of parameters) remains unverified, only claimed by extrapolation of the fitted power law. The paper delivers a software artifact: training/inference code released on GitHub, though the authors explicitly flag the kernel as not yet performance-optimized. Strengths: - Rigorous isoFLOP, isoparameter experimental design that isolates the causal driver (state size sparsity) from confounds (parameter count, compute budget), which is uncommon rigor for architecture papers in this space. - A concrete algorithmic contribution beyond the high-level idea: the two-pointer sparse inner-product kernel and the in-place-undo backward pass are specific, described-in-enough-detail-to-reproduce techniques for making sparse chunk-wise training tractable. - Ablations that directly falsify a plausible alternative hypothesis (that gains come from the learned initial state rather than larger state), strengthening the causal claim about sparsity being the mechanism that matters. - Additional hybrid experiment (Appendix E, three-way SWA+FullAttn+SDM) showing complementary scaling behavior between SDM and Full Attention, hinting at practical deployment paths beyond the core 2-way comparison. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The authors state directly that more kernel research is needed: the current SDM implementation reaches roughly 10x lower Model FLOPs Utilization than the mature Gated DeltaNet FLA kernel because the sparse memory must live in HBM rather than fast SRAM. - They acknowledge the memory footprint itself (not FLOPs) is SDM's core limitation, noting it can be as large as the model's own parameters, making it unsuitable for resource-constrained deployment despite constant FLOPs. - They note an anomalous result on RULER's variable-tracking task where Gated DeltaNet counter-intuitively improves with longer sequences, attributing it to a benchmark design artifact (early clustering of distractor variables) rather than to their own method's behavior, showing willingness to flag benchmark quirks rather than only favorable results. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No error bars or seed variation anywhere, an omission of standard rigor for both the headline scaling-law fits and the isoFLOP benchmark tables, and no significance testing on the RULER/reasoning-task deltas that in several cases (e.g., ARC easy: -0.63
Recommended 2607.07386 PDF
#7 — Highly Innovative (75)

Teaching LLMs to Recommend and Defer in Underrepresented Epilepsy Care (1 ▲ )

Shreyas Rajesh, Kartik Sharma, Tonmoy Monsoor, Mehmet Yigit Turali, Richard Idro, Juliana Kayaga, Robert Sebunya, Tracy Tushabe Namata, Jessica Nichole Pasqua, Vwani Roychowdhury, Rajarshi Mazumder · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MANANA, a non-parametric prompt-learning framework that adapts an LLM to local anti-seizure medication (ASM) prescribing patterns from a small patient-level training set, plus Bayesian prompt averaging (BPA), a Bayesian model averaging procedure over the learned prompt trajectory that produces calibrated prescription probabilities and a deferral signal. - Why it's important: Specialist epilepsy care is scarce in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), and public medical AI benchmarks are dominated by high-income structured-EHR settings (e.g., MIMIC-IV), so an LLM decision-support tool needs to adapt to local prescribing practice and know when to defer to a scarce specialist rather than apply Western-prior medical knowledge blindly. - How it does it: A three-agent Predictor-Inspector-Architect loop accumulates case-grounded candidate learnings into an append-only evidence buffer and commits only recurring cross-patient corrections into an auditable memory (single rule list or multi specialist-agent population); BPA then treats the sequence of learned memory states as an ensemble of estimators, weighting each by held-out validation marginal likelihood (Beta-Binomial posterior) to get prescription probabilities for selective prediction. - The good: On two independently collected Ugandan cohorts, MANANA-Multi improves top-3 exact-match (EM@3) by 4-8 percentage points over prompt-optimization baselines (DSPy/GEPA, TextGrad, ExpeL) on monotherapy, and BPA achieves 99% top-1 precision on the most confident quartile of held-out Cohort B cases and 95% at 50% coverage, evidence that the confidence score is not just calibrated but clinically actionable for deferral. - The missing: The evaluation target is agreement with the treating physician's regimen, not ground-truth clinical optimality (e.g., seizure control), so the method could be learning to reproduce local resource constraints or physician idiosyncrasies rather than better clinical decisions; the paper acknowledges this and reports it plans an outcome-conditioned extension, but the current results do not establish that higher EM@3 correlates with better patient outcomes. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building LLM-based clinical decision support for resource-constrained settings, prompt-learning-as-alternative-to-fine-tuning, or Bayesian uncertainty estimation over prompt states; less relevant to readers focused on general-purpose medical QA benchmarks or high-resource EHR medication recommendation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (with a systems component; code released at roychowdhuryresearch/Manana). Rigor (78) is the largest contributor: 5-seed mean±std reporting, two independently collected Ugandan cohorts rather than splits of one dataset, an explicit distribution-shift analysis, a previous-regimen copy baseline as a Goodhart safeguard, and baselines with real teeth (DSPy/GEPA, TextGrad, ExpeL, XGBoost, EpiPick). Novelty (75) reflects a new problem setting (LMIC longitudinal ASM regimen prediction with deferral), a new dataset, and the evidence-gated separation of per-case diagnosis (Inspector) from cross-case memory commitment (Architect); BPA itself is a competent but incremental repurposing of Bayesian model averaging to prompt-state trajectories. Non-Obviousness (75) is high because the N=2 cross-visit gating mechanism targets a specific documented failure mode of reflection agents rather than generically 'adding memory', and using held-out validation marginal likelihood instead of LLM self-reported confidence is a non-obvious and empirically validated choice. Completeness (78) benefits from honest limitation acknowledgment: the authors state plainly that EM@3 measures agreement with the treating physician, not clinical optimality, and that BPA thresholds were chosen retrospectively. Resilience (70) is capped by exactly that endpoint problem, since the system may be learning local formulary and resource constraints rather than better medicine, and no blinded specialist adjudication of disagreement cases was run; Practical Impact (75) is strong given the released pipeline, 150-visit learning budget, and a 99%-precision-at-25%-coverage deferral policy that is directly actionable in a clinic workflow. Weighted composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*75 = 75.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frontline clinicians in LMICs manage longitudinal epilepsy medication regimens without adequate specialist support: over 80% of the ~50 million people with epilepsy live in LMICs, and diagnosis/treatment often falls to generalist providers with limited EEG or imaging access, medication stock-outs, and irregular follow-up. Existing medication-recommendation research (GAMENet, SafeDrug, and similar) is built and evaluated on structured, high-resource EHR data (e.g., MIMIC-IV), which does not transfer to narrative clinician notes, deployment-specific drug availability, cost, or follow-up norms found in an underrepresented clinic. A decision-support system for this setting needs two properties absent from prior systems: recommendations calibrated to local practice, and an explicit signal for when to defer to a human specialist. Key Idea: Separate error diagnosis from memory commitment. Rather than rewriting a single mutable prompt via textual gradients (as in TextGrad) or accumulating generic case-level lessons (as in ExpeL), MANANA's Inspector proposes case-grounded candidate learnings that sit in a persistent, append-only evidence buffer with per-case provenance, and the Architect commits a learning into the shared memory only when it is supported by at least N=2 distinct clinic visits. This evidence-gating constraint is the mechanism that prevents brittle overfitting to a single unusual patient, and it is also what makes the resulting memory trajectory usable as a well-behaved ensemble for Bayesian averaging (BPA), since each successive memory state reflects a different, principled stage of evidence accumulation rather than an arbitrary rewrite. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. (1) Adaptation: a Predictor LLM proposes 3 candidate ASM regimens per visit from patient history and the current memory state m_{t-1}; an Inspector LLM compares candidates against the physician-prescribed regimen (ground truth) and writes a structured report plus a candidate learning; these accumulate in an append-only buffer C_t; once per batch, an Architect LLM reads the buffer, current reports, and m_{t-1} to update memory via one of two variant-specific rules: MANANA-Single appends at most one new rule per round when supported by ≥2 learnings (a list of correction rules, |m_t| - |m_{t-1}| ≤ 1), or MANANA-Multi maintains a bounded population (target budget A_max=5) of specialist prompt-agents that can be spawned, edited, or pruned based on recurring cross-visit support. (2) Inference/BPA: the full trajectory of learned memory states m_1,...,m_T is treated as an ensemble; each state's predictive likelihood combines an empirical-Bayes candidate-position prior π=(0.85, 0.11, 0.04) estimated from a training subset with a Beta(1,1)-prior integrated validation likelihood over top-1/top-3 hits; states are weighted by temperature-smoothed (τ=5) normalized posterior support (top K=5 of T=15 rounds retained) to produce p_BPA(y|x), from which top-1/top-3 predictions and a per-case confidence score are derived. Three design decisions stand out: (i) the N=2 evidence-gating threshold for rule commitment is a reasonable but unvalidated hyperparameter (no ablation of N shown in the main text); (ii) the shared candidate-position prior π assumes a fixed Predictor confidence ordering across all memory states, which the paper acknowledges and relaxes in an appendix; (iii) using validation marginal likelihood (Beta-Binomial) rather than raw LLM self-reported confidence for BPA weighting is well-motivated given known LLM confidence-calibration failures, and is empirically shown (Table 2) to widen the confidence gap between correct and incorrect top-1 predictions versus majority vote. Key Results: On Cohort A (332 patients, 1,040 visits) and held-out Cohort B (367 patients, 1,509 visits, independently collected), evaluated as EM@3 (exact match of the physician-prescribed active-drug set among the top-3 returned regimens), MANANA-Multi reaches 89.7/95.3/97.0% (V1/V2/V3) on Cohort A monotherapy and 87.3/95.1/93.2% on Cohort B monotherapy, versus a Single-agent direct-prompting baseline of 78.4/84.5/90.3% (A) and 76.7/82.6/83.8% (B), and versus prompt-optimization baselines DSPy+GEPA (87.1-95.0% A, 81.8-94.8% B), ExpeL (67.2-82.7% A), and TextGrad (87.4-88.1% A). Classical ML (Naive Bayes, XGBoost) and the epilepsy-specific rule engine EpiPick trail substantially (49-75%). Polytherapy is markedly harder for all methods (MANANA-Multi 30.8-72.6% on Cohort A, 40.2-69.8% on Cohort B), reflecting the combinatorial difficulty of exact-matching a multi-drug set. For uncertainty: Beta-Binomial BPA versus majority vote on the MANANA-Multi trajectory improves top-1 accuracy (66%→70% Cohort A, 73%→75% Cohort B) at matched top-3 accuracy, and widens the confidence gap between correct/incorrect predictions (0.145→0.194 A, 0.173→0.214 B). Selective prediction (Table 3) shows precision of 96-99% on the most-confident 25% of cases and 90-95% on the most-confident half, versus 70-75% baseline precision across all cases. All results are from a single preprint with seeded (5-seed) mean±std reporting for stochastic methods, evaluated on gpt-oss-120b as the base LLM. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the Predictor-Inspector-Architect evidence-gated prompt-learning loop) + NEW METHOD (Bayesian prompt averaging over a learned prompt trajectory, an application of Bayesian model averaging/stacking machinery to prompt-state ensembles rather than parametric models) + NEW DATASET (two independently collected Ugandan pediatric epilepsy cohorts with longitudinal narrative notes) applied to a NEW PROBLEM SETTING (LMIC longitudinal ASM regimen prediction with deferral). The genuinely novel piece is the separation of error diagnosis (Inspector, per-case) from memory commitment (Architect, evidence-gated, cross-case), which differs mechanistically from TextGrad's direct gradient-style prompt rewriting and ExpeL's generic experience accumulation; BPA's use of a discrete prompt-state trajectory as a BMA ensemble (rather than deep ensembles or MC dropout) is a reasonable but incremental repurposing of established Bayesian averaging and stacking theory (MacKay 1992, Hoeting et al. 1999, Yao et al. 2018) to a new representation (prompt states instead of parametric models). Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present in appendices (component ablation, cross-model transfer from 120B to 20B, additional open-source backbones, MIMIC-IV reproducibility check, BPA weighting-model variation) but were not read in full here; the main text signals substantial ablation coverage rather than a single-configuration result. Results are seeded (5 seeds) with mean±std reported for stochastic methods, which is good practice, though it's unclear whether std reflects LLM sampling variance, data-split variance, or both. Code is released (GitHub: roychowdhuryresearch/Manana). A methodological concern: evaluation ground truth is the physician's prescribed regimen, not an independently validated ASM standard, so the primary metric (EM@3) measures agreement with a possibly resource-constrained or idiosyncratic local practice, not clinical correctness; the authors partially address this with an outcome-supported subset check (visits with documented seizure reduction/freedom) mentioned in Appendix D, and by noting an ongoing extension to predict seizure freedom directly. A previous-regimen copy baseline is also reported to check whether high EM@3 merely reflects unchanged prescriptions across visits, a reasonable Goodhart's Law safeguard. No external, independent physician panel was used to validate whether MANANA's recommendations are clinically sound beyond agreement with the observed prescriber. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (with a SYSTEMS component, since a GitHub repo is released). - Practical: Runs via LLM API/CLI calls (gpt-oss-120b on 4 NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs plus AWS Bedrock for additional inference) with no weight updates, avoiding retraining/fine-tuning infrastructure; the 20B backbone cross-transfer experiment (Appendix F) suggests some flexibility toward smaller/cheaper models, though the main results use a 120B model that is not trivially deployable on commodity clinic hardware. - Efficient: Learning uses only 150 visits over 15 update rounds per seed for training/validation (70-patient pool, 50 train/20 validation), which is a small labeled-data requirement relative to fine-tuning; however, cost scales with three LLM roles (Predictor, Inspector, Architect) per training round plus a K=5-state ensemble at inference, meaning inference cost is roughly K× a single-prompt call, and no latency/token-cost numbers are reported. - Effective: EM@3 gains over baselines are shown, and BPA's precision-coverage tradeoff (99% precision at 25% coverage, 95% at 50%) directly targets the stated deployment goal (route only confident cases for direct use, defer the rest); but "effective" here means agreement with the physician, not validated clinical improvement in seizure outcomes, so the paper measures a proxy for clinical effectiveness rather than effectiveness itself. - Scalable: Tested on two cohorts of several hundred patients each; no experiment tests scaling to more diverse clinics, more drug classes beyond 10, or larger patient populations. The transfer test across Cohort A→B (different documentation format, clinicians, prescribing patterns per Figure 3's distribution-shift analysis) is a meaningful generalization check at the tested scale, but 10×-100× scale-up (e.g., dozens of clinics, thousands of patients, broader drug action spaces) is untested and unclaimed. The work delivers a released code artifact (training/inference pipeline, prompts) rather than a trained model or fixed weights, consistent with its non-parametric, prompt-only design. Strengths: - The evidence-gated separation of per-case error diagnosis (Inspector) from cross-case memory commitment (Architect) is a specific, well-motivated design choice that directly targets the brittle-rule failure mode documented in prior reflection-style agents (Sclar et al. 2024, Huang et al. 2024), rather than a generic "add memory" contribution. - Two independently collected cohorts (not train/test splits of one dataset) with an explicit distribution-shift analysis (Figure 3, symmetric KL divergence across clinical and medication-use features) is a genuine transfer-robustness test uncommon in clinical LLM papers, which typically evaluate on a single institution's data. - BPA's use of held-out validation marginal likelihood (rather than LLM self-reported confidence, known to be poorly calibrated) to weight an ensemble of discrete prompt states is a concrete, quantitatively validated (Table 2, Table 3, Figure 2 reliability diagrams) improvement over majority voting for selective prediction, directly enabling a practical deferral policy. - Extensive baseline coverage (classical ML, an existing epilepsy-specific expert system EpiPick, and three modern prompt-optimization frameworks DSPy/GEPA, TextGrad, ExpeL) gives the comparison real teeth rather than only beating a strawman zero-shot baseline. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that (1) BPA gives a retrospective confidence score, not a deployment-ready deferral rule, since thresholds were chosen on held-out data rather than pre-specified, and would need review and workflow integration before clinical use; (2) the evaluation endpoint is agreement with the physician-selected regimen, not proof of clinical optimality, since physician choices also reflect formulary limits, drug availability, and follow-up constraints, meaning the model may be learning local resource constraints rather than better medicine. They flag both limitations without minimizing them and identify a concrete next step (predicting seizure freedom/reduction directly). Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The paper does not report absolute compute/token cost or wall-clock latency for the three-agent training loop or the K-state BPA inference ensemble, information a clinic operator would need to judge deployability. - No independent specialist adjudication of MANANA's recommendations against clinical guidelines was performed beyond agreement with the original prescriber and a partial outcome-conditioned subset check; a reviewer would want a prospective or blinded specialist rating of a sample of MANANA's top-1 recommendations, including the disagreement cases, to rule out the system learning locally consistent but suboptimal practice. - The 10-drug clinic action space is fixed and Uganda-specific; it's unstated whether the framework's evidence-gating threshold (N=2) or budget cap (A_max=5 specialists) were tuned on the same data used for final evaluation, which would be a methodological leakage risk not explicitly ruled out in the main text. - The comparison to CONSILIUM (an expert-designed hand-built multi-agent system from neurologist review) is deferred entirely to an appendix; given that CONSILIUM represents the "best a domain expert could hand-design,
Recommended 2606.31036 PDF
#8 — Highly Innovative (75)

GigaWorld-1: A Roadmap to Build World Models for Robot Policy Evaluation (31 ▲ )

GigaWorld Team, Angyuan Ma, Boyuan Wang, Bohan Li, Chaojun Ni, Guo Li, Guan Huang, Guosheng Zhao, Hao Li, Hengtao Li, Jingyu Liu, Jiwen Lu, Qiuping Deng, Tingdong Yu, Xuancheng Xu, Xinyu Zhou, Xiuwei Xu, Xinze Chen, Xiaofeng Wang, Xiaoyu Tian, Yang Wang, Yifan Chang, Yukun Zhou, Yun Ye, Zhenyu Wu, Zhanqian Wu, Zheng Zhu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WMBench (a benchmark of 324,000 annotated world-model rollouts paired with real-robot execution) and GigaWorld-1 (a world model built from the resulting design study) that together identify and validate which architectural and data choices make a video world model a reliable robot-policy evaluator. - Why it's important: Real-robot policy evaluation costs about 100 human-hours per 2,500 rollouts and cannot yield consistent reset states, so a trustworthy learned evaluator would remove the dominant bottleneck in iterating on robot foundation models. - How it does it: The paper isolates evaluator reliability from generic video quality by correlating 15+ automatic metrics against a 4-level human-annotated outcome score (WMES) across 7 backbones and 4 control schemes, then bakes the winning choices (channel-concatenated action control, hierarchical multi-scale memory with a first-frame anchor, Relative RoPE, and PhysData-heavy training mix) into GigaWorld-1. - The good: GigaWorld-1's closed-loop success-rate fit line (slope 1.134, near the y=x diagonal) is visibly closer to real-world outcomes than three CVPR-challenge baselines (slopes 0.689-0.770), directly demonstrating better preservation of policy-level ground truth, not just visual quality. - The missing: All headline numbers (WMES agreement, success-rate calibration, the 14.9% evaluator-alignment gain) come from the authors' own WMBench/GigaBrain-Challenge ecosystem with no external, independently constructed benchmark for cross-validation. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or selecting a world model as a robot-policy evaluator; the correlation study (Figs. 4-5) is a reusable methodological contribution even for readers who never adopt GigaWorld-1 itself.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (built world-model evaluator system, deployed closed-loop against real robot outcomes). Practical Impact (85) is the dominant term at 30% weight: code, pretrained checkpoints, curated datasets, and toolkits are stated as open-sourced, and the design recipe (channel-concat control, hierarchical memory, Relative RoPE, PhysData mix) is directly actionable. Non-Obviousness (78) is high because the finding that Background/Photometric Consistency anti-correlate with evaluator quality (rho -0.45, -0.42) inverts the field's default assumption that better video means better evaluation. Novelty (75) reflects a genuine new problem formulation (world model as policy evaluator) plus WMBench, though the architecture itself is an assembly of known components. Rigor (65) is pulled down by a real Goodhart's Law exposure: every headline number (14.9% evaluator-alignment gain, slope 1.134 calibration, 87.80% VLM agreement) comes from the authors' own WMBench/GigaBrain-Challenge ecosystem with no external benchmark, and Tables 2-4 and 9 report single-run deltas with no seeds or error bars. Completeness (75) and Resilience (62) are helped by explicit trade-off disclosure (AgiBot data hurts JEPA/Trajectory) and by the authors' own admission that GigaWorld-1 still underestimates success on contact-sensitive subtasks, but the closed-loop calibration claim rests on comparison against three challenge-submission baselines rather than an independent evaluator. Weighted composite: 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*85 = 75.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 62 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating robot foundation models (VLAs, world-action models) requires slow, costly, hardware-bound real-robot rollouts, unlike LLMs which have cheap standardized benchmarks; OpenVLA reportedly needs 100 human-hours for 2,500 rollout evaluations, and physical robots cannot guarantee identical reset states across trials. Classical simulators reduce cost but introduce a sim-to-real gap, especially for contact-rich or deformable manipulation. Prior work has shown world models can serve as policy evaluators but has not established which design choices make them reliable at this specific job, as opposed to merely generating plausible video. Key Idea: Evaluator reliability is governed by a different property than video quality: action-faithful consistency under long-horizon closed-loop rollout, not short-term visual realism. Metrics that reward static or visually stable output (Background Consistency, Photometric Consistency) actively mislead evaluator ranking because a trivial static-video generator scores well on them while ignoring the conditioning action entirely; the paper operationalizes this via Pearson correlation between automatic metrics and human-annotated closed-loop outcome scores (WMES) rather than trusting any single visual-quality metric. Methodology: Four-stage pipeline: (1) collect real-robot teleoperation and policy-rollout trajectories across 8 task families (2,989 trajectories, strict episode-disjoint train/test split); (2) train/adapt world models on the training split; (3) execute closed-loop autoregressive rollout in the world model, feeding back predicted observations to the policy; (4) score outcomes with WMES (a 4-level human/VLM-annotated ordinal scale: 0=incorrect outcome+low fidelity to 3=correct outcome+high fidelity, triple-annotated with senior spot checks) plus ~15 automatic diagnostics grouped into frame/representation fidelity, geometry/semantics/interaction, and motion/long-horizon rollout families. GigaWorld-1's architecture (built on Wan 2.1/2.2 backbones at 1.3B and 5B scale) reformulates a bidirectional video diffusion model as an autoregressive world generator (flow-matching objective, Eq. 39, then autoregressive denoising objective, Eq. 40) with three critical design decisions: (a) unified control injection using EE-pose maps for the static head camera and ray maps for wrist cameras, concatenated channel-wise into the noisy latent from the start of denoising (well-justified by Table 3, where this beats cross-attention and ControlNet-style spatial control on Trajectory Accuracy: 0.3528 vs 0.1620/0.2566); (b) hierarchical history injection combining a persistent first-frame anchor with short/mid/long-range memory buffers plus Relative RoPE to prevent temporal position drift beyond the training horizon (well-justified by Table 4's 40-second PSNR/FID/FVD comparison); (c) a training-data mixture of internet/physics video (1,298h), open-source robot data (5,377h), egocentric human data (2,411h), and Giga-collected demonstrations (3,894h), with the PhysData addition chosen for its favorable overall trade-off (Table 2: GigaData+PhysData raises average score from 0.5654 to 0.6144) over the narrower AgiBot robot-specific addition, which improves embodiment fidelity but degrades JEPA Similarity (-0.2426) and Trajectory Accuracy (-0.1084) due to embodiment mismatch, a trade-off the paper documents rather than glossing over. Distillation (ODE warm-start, optional; DMD2, mandatory) compresses the autoregressive teacher into a few-step student for practical rollout speed. Key Results: At the group level, Visual Fidelity metrics correlate most strongly with WMES (rho=0.78), Geometry (rho=0.71), Semantics (rho=0.59), Dynamics (rho=0.44); Interaction Quality (rho=-0.11) and Appearance Stability metrics (Background Consistency rho=-0.45, Photometric Consistency rho=-0.42) are negative predictors. At individual-metric level, Subject Consistency (rho=0.88) and Perspectivity (rho=0.86) are the strongest single predictors. A LoRA-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct VLM evaluator achieves 87.80% exact agreement and 99.16% adjacent agreement with human WMES ratings on 5,000+ videos (MAE 0.1304, quadratic weighted kappa 0.7349, Spearman 0.7574), validating VLM-based scaling of outcome annotation. On the final architecture comparison (Table 9, six-metric average across Aesthetic/Image/JEPA/Semantic/Subject/Trajectory), GigaWorld-1-Plus (5B) scores 0.6834 and GigaWorld-1-Nano (1.3B) scores 0.6717, versus the strongest baselines Cosmos-Predict2.5 (0.6123) and Wan 2.2 5B (0.5948) - an 11.6% and 14.9% improvement respectively. Long-horizon rollout quality (Table 4, 0-40s in 8s intervals) shows GigaWorld-1+Memory dominating PSNR/FID/FVD at every interval versus SVD, Cosmos2.5, LTX-Video, Wan 2.1/2.2, all of which degrade over the horizon (SVD FID rises from 142.84 to 419.21). On closed-loop success-rate alignment (Figure 16), GigaWorld-1's linear fit (slope 1.134, intercept -0.091) tracks the y=x diagonal more closely than three CVPR-challenge baseline models (slopes 0.689-0.770), indicating less systematic over/under-estimation of policy success. All comparisons are self-reported preprint results using the authors' own WMBench/GigaBrain-Challenge infrastructure; no third-party benchmark reproduces these numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION ("world model as policy evaluator" as a first-class research target, distinct from world models as data engines/policies/planners/critics), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (WMBench, with paired teleoperation and policy-rollout data and a 4-level outcome scale), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (appearance-stability metrics anti-correlate with evaluator quality; action-faithful long-horizon consistency, not photorealism, predicts evaluator reliability), and NEW SYSTEM (GigaWorld-1, an engineering synthesis of known components: LoRA-adapted diffusion transformer, autoregressive rollout via Forcing-style conversion, hierarchical memory, ray-map/EE-pose control, DMD2 distillation). The individual architectural components (RoPE variants, ControlNet-style conditioning, LoRA, DMD2) are each pre-existing; the novel contribution is the empirically-grounded combination and the correlational methodology that motivates each choice. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are extensive and metric-level (Tables 2, 3, 4, 9; Figures 4, 5, 11, 12), directly tying each design choice to the evaluator-relevant metric subset identified in Section 5, which is a methodologically disciplined approach (avoiding cherry-picking metrics that favor the proposed model). However, no error bars, confidence intervals on the ablation deltas, or multi-seed reproduction are reported for Tables 2-4 and 9 (only the correlation estimates in Sec 5.1 use bootstrap CIs). Evaluation is entirely on the authors' own WMBench and CVPR 2026 GigaBrain Challenge submissions; there is no cross-check against an independently constructed benchmark, which is a Goodhart's Law risk given the paper explicitly designed WMBench to match its own evaluator target. Code, pretrained model checkpoints, curated datasets, and toolkits are stated to be fully open-sourced (project page: https://open-gigaai.github.io/giga-world-1/; dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/open-gigaai/CVPR-2026-WorldModel-Track-Dataset), which is a strong reproducibility positive once released. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built world-model evaluator system, evaluated via closed-loop deployment against real robot outcomes), with an EMPIRICAL component (the correlational study). - Practical: Training uses 32 NVIDIA H20 GPUs per stage; inference is benchmarked on a single H20 96G GPU, so it is reproducible on high-end but commercially available hardware, not consumer hardware. - Efficient: Reports up to 35.93x inference speedup combining SageAttention, six-step DMD2 distillation, and Ulysses sequence parallelism versus an unstated baseline configuration; absolute latency/throughput numbers for the final deployed model are not given in the sections reviewed. - Effective: Evidence shows the model improves evaluator-alignment metrics (Table 9) and closed-loop success-rate calibration (Figure 16) relative to baselines, addressing the stated problem (policy-relevant outcome fidelity) rather than only a proxy (visual quality) - a genuine strength of the study design. - Scalable: Tested at 1.3B and 5B parameter scale and up to 40 seconds of autoregressive rollout; the paper does not test 10x-100x scale-up in either parameter count or rollout horizon, and explicitly notes mobile manipulation, dexterous in-hand manipulation, and safety-critical autonomy remain untested task classes. GigaWorld-1 delivers a released software artifact: code, pretrained checkpoints, curated datasets, and auxiliary toolkits (per the abstract), not merely a paper-form result. Strengths: - The systematic decoupling of "video quality" from "evaluator quality" via metric-group correlation against human-annotated outcome scores (Figure 4/5) is a genuine methodological contribution that generalizes beyond this specific model. - The identification of degenerate metrics (Background/Photometric Consistency negatively correlating with WMES) is a non-obvious, actionable finding that would otherwise mislead future evaluator-design work in this space. - The long-horizon rollout analysis (Table 4, Figure 12) convincingly demonstrates that short-horizon video quality is a poor proxy for evaluator reliability, an insight with direct design implications (memory matters more than raw visual fidelity). - Explicit trade-off documentation (Finding 8: AgiBot data improves embodiment fidelity but hurts generalization metrics) rather than only reporting favorable ablation deltas is a credibility positive. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state WMBench covers 8 task families but does not exhaust mobile manipulation, dexterous in-hand manipulation, or safety-critical autonomy; the study focuses primarily on video-centric world models, leaving other structured state-space or hybrid 3D approaches for future comparison; VLM-assisted success labeling still benefits from human verification in uncertain cases; and the success-rate bias analysis (Figure 17) shows GigaWorld-1 still underestimates success on contact-sensitive failure subtasks (e.g., -0.13, -0.11, -0.10, -0.10 deviations on several task4 subtasks), which the authors flag as an important direction for future work rather than a solved problem. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The entire empirical case rests on the authors' own WMBench and GigaBrain Challenge infrastructure; no external benchmark or independent research group's data is used to cross-validate either the metric-correlation findings or GigaWorld-1's headline 14.9% improvement, leaving open whether the correlation structure (e.g., Subject Consistency as top predictor) is an artifact of WMBench's specific task composition. The paper does not report statistical significance testing or variance across ablation runs (Tables 2-4, 9), so single-run deltas of a few percentage points (e.g., +0.0070 Image Quality from PhysData) cannot be distinguished from noise. Cosmos-3, mentioned as an omni-world-model competitor, is acknowledged as unavailable for comparison, so the strongest possible baseline may be absent from Table 9. The VLM evaluator's 87.80% exact agreement, while framed as "near-perfect," still means roughly 1 in 8 videos is scored at a different WMES level than a human annotator, a nontrivial error rate for a system intended to replace human judgment at scale. Bottom Line: Practitioners building or selecting a world model for robot-policy evaluation gain a genuinely useful, non-obvious metric-selection framework and a validated design recipe (channel-concat control, hierarchical memory, Relative RoPE, PhysData-heavy training), though the near-total reliance on the authors' own benchmark for every headline number means the specific magnitude of GigaWorld-1's advantage should be treated as a preprint claim pending independent replication.
Recommended 2607.02642 PDF
#9 — Highly Innovative (75)

TiRex-2: Generalizing TiRex to Multivariate Data and Streaming

P Podest, M Pichler, E Bürger, L Zólyomi… · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TiRex-2, a recurrent xLSTM-based time series foundation model that extends the univariate TiRex to multivariate forecasting with past and future-known covariates while supporting constant-cost streaming inference. - Why it's important: Existing multivariate forecasting foundation models (Chronos-2, MOIRAI, GTT) use Transformer backbones whose inference cost grows with context length and require full-history recomputation on every new observation, which is incompatible with continuous streaming deployments like industrial monitoring or flood forecasting. - How it does it: A bidirectional xLSTM time mixer combined with an asymmetric grouped-attention variate mixer lets future-known covariates be read bidirectionally while a provable masking scheme keeps target-variable predictions strictly causal, and a synthetic coupling pipeline manufactures diverse multivariate training samples from large univariate corpora. - The good: TiRex-2 achieves the best mean MASE on fev-bench among 9 zero-shot TSFMs while using about 2.2x fewer active parameters than Chronos-2 and about 4.3x fewer than TimesFM-2.5 (Figure 7), and stays flat on MASE when streamed to 32M steps, 4000x beyond its 8k post-training context (Figure 8). - The missing: The paper never reports wall-clock or FLOP measurements for the claimed O(VL) vs O(VL^2) streaming advantage; all efficiency claims rest on asymptotic parameter-count arguments and synthetic MASE curves rather than measured latency/throughput against the Transformer baselines it compares against. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating time series foundation models with covariates or streaming requirements; the asymmetric-mask causality proof and the synthetic multivariate-coupling pipeline are the two reusable ideas, while readers focused purely on offline batch accuracy can get the headline numbers from Figure 4 alone.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the strongest driver: ablations with mean ± std over 6 seeds, 95% confidence intervals on pairwise skill scores, ±1 SEM on the dysts results, and explicit removal of overlapping pretraining/eval data — this is well above the single-run point-estimate norm in the TSFM literature, and the authors add a 'Chronos-2 (no cross-learning)' control rather than hiding the confound. Novelty (75) reflects a genuine architectural resolution of a real tension (bidirectional future-covariate use versus strictly causal target prediction) backed by a formal proof (Proposition 1) with a necessity counterexample, though the ingredients (xLSTM, grouped block-diagonal attention, pinball quantile head) are recombined from prior work. Non-Obviousness (75) is high — the asymmetric grouped-attention mask is not what a competent practitioner would reach for by default, and the constant-cost streaming property tested to 32M steps (4000x the 8k post-training context) is a non-trivial demonstrated behavior. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) are pulled below the Rigor score by the gap between the 'state-of-the-art' framing and the 51% pairwise win rate against Chronos-2 (95% CI 40-60%), which the abstract does not surface, plus the absence of any measured wall-clock or FLOP data behind the central O(VL) vs O(VL^2) efficiency claim. Practical Impact (70) reflects a competitive model at 2.2x fewer active parameters than Chronos-2, but no code/weight release URL appears and the efficiency case rests on asymptotics. Weighted composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*70 = 75.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world forecasting systems (cloud operations, macroeconomics, hydrology, healthcare, industrial monitoring) observe multiple interacting variates arriving continuously, with a subset of future covariates (calendars, scheduled interventions) known in advance. Existing univariate time series foundation models (TiRex, Reverso, FlowState) scale well but ignore cross-variate dependencies via a channel-independence assumption. Existing multivariate foundation models (Chronos-2, MOIRAI, GTT) capture cross-variate dependencies but are built on Transformer attention, so per-step inference cost scales quadratically or at least linearly with the full context as new observations arrive, misaligned with streaming deployments that need constant-cost updates. Key Idea: An asymmetric grouped-attention mask in the variate mixer lets covariate tokens attend to target tokens (queries) while forbidding target tokens from attending to covariate tokens in a way that would leak future information; combined with a forward-only xLSTM state on target and past-covariate streams (and a separate bidirectional weight-tied xLSTM pass reserved for future-known covariates only), this yields a formally provable target-causality guarantee (Proposition 1) while still allowing the model to exploit future-known covariates bidirectionally. This is the mechanism that lets the model be simultaneously non-causal over covariates and causal over targets within a single forward pass, which the paper positions as a first among time series foundation models. Methodology: The pipeline: (1) input layer scales each variate with a variance-stabilizing arcsinh transform (with a learned/detected binary-variate bypass to avoid degenerate scaling of sparse binary covariates), patches into non-overlapping length-P segments, and embeds via a 2-layer residual MLP into token tensor H ∈ R^{V×L×D}; (2) N=12 alternating blocks, each composed of a time mixer (forward-only xLSTM for targets/past-covariates, plus a weight-tied reverse xLSTM pass fused linearly for future-known covariates) followed by a variate mixer (block-diagonal grouped multi-head self-attention with the asymmetric mask of Eq. 1, skipped entirely for univariate inputs to preserve efficiency); (3) an output MLP projects to K pinball-loss quantile forecasts per output patch, trained with the loss summed over all observed time steps (not just the horizon) for denser gradient signal. Critical design decisions: (a) using grouped block-diagonal attention rather than flattening all variates into one sequence (avoids quadratic blow-up and batch padding to the largest V) — reasonably justified by memory/compute arguments though not directly ablated against the flattening alternative; (b) the asymmetric mask itself, justified by a formal proof (Proposition 1, Appendix B) and a stated necessity counterexample; (c) synthetic multivariate coupling using six mechanisms (identity, functional, linear mixing, cointegration, linear/nonlinear structural causal models) sampled at training time to construct cross-variate dependency diversity from a pool of univariate series — justified qualitatively (curated multivariate corpora are "too narrow") but the actual coupling-mechanism diversity's contribution is not separately ablated (only "no future-known covariates" and few architectural ablations are reported). Key Results: On fev-bench and GIFT-Eval zero-shot benchmarks, TiRex-2 achieves the lowest MASE among compared models (Figure 4), beating Chronos-2, TimesFM-2.5, Toto-1.0, MOIRAI-2.0, TiRex, and FlowState-r1.1 depending on the benchmark; pairwise skill scores on fev-bench range from -3.4 vs. Chronos-2 (essentially tied) up to +62.5 vs. a naive baseline (Figure 5), with win rates over Chronos-2 at only 51% (near-parity, wide 95% CI 40-60%) despite the MASE aggregate ranking first. On synthetic streaming evaluation, MASE stays flat from 2k to 32M streamed steps (Figure 6/8), a 4000x extrapolation beyond the 8k post-training context, with no measured drift. On dysts chaotic-system long-horizon forecasting (135 trajectories, horizons up to 1056 steps), TiRex-2 beats Chronos-2 at short-to-moderate horizons with the largest gap at fine temporal granularity (Figure 9), though the gap narrows and both models plateau or degrade beyond horizon ~500. Ablations (Table 1, fev-bench, 6 seeds) show removing group attention costs +0.220 MASE (largest single ablation), and removing future-known covariates costs +0.033 overall / +0.114 on the future-covariate subset, confirming the covariate mechanism matters; other ablations (binary-aware scaler, forward-only, sLSTM-only, 8k→2k context) have small deltas (≤0.022). Parameter count: 38.4M active in univariate mode, +44.1M for multivariate (task-weighted expectation ≈53.84M on fev-bench). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the bidirectional-covariate/causal-target xLSTM architecture with asymmetric grouped attention) plus a formal NEW THEORETICAL RESULT (Proposition 1, target-causality proof) and an ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION / NEW DATA-GENERATION METHOD (the synthetic multivariate coupling pipeline). The genuinely novel piece is the combination of provable target-causality with bidirectional future-covariate exploitation in a single recurrent forward pass; the individual ingredients (xLSTM backbone, grouped block-diagonal attention, quantile pinball loss, reversible instance normalization) are drawn from prior work (Beck et al. 2024; Ansari et al. 2025; Kim et al. 2021) and recombined for this setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are reported with mean ± std over up to 6 seeds (Table 1), which is a genuine strength relative to typical single-run TSFM papers. Pairwise win-rate and skill-score comparisons include 95% confidence intervals (Figure 5), and dysts results report ±1 SEM over N=135 tasks (Figure 9). The paper states overlapping pretraining/eval datasets were removed per benchmark to rule out train-test leakage (Section E, not shown in first 20 pages but referenced). No code, weight, or dataset release URL appears in the reviewed pages; reproducibility of the exact synthetic coupling pipeline and training corpus composition depends on Appendix D/E/F which were not fully reviewed here. A methodological caveat the authors themselves flag: Chronos-2's "cross learning" (borrowing information across series in the same eval batch) confounds the fev-bench comparison, and they add a "Chronos-2 (no CL)" variant to partially control for this — a good methodological practice, though it also implicitly concedes the main Chronos-2 comparison may not be apples-to-apples without that control. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a trained, evaluated foundation model with released benchmark results). - Practical: Trained on 2 NVIDIA H100 GPUs for 700,000 steps; at 38.4-82.5M active parameters, inference is plausible on a single commercial GPU, though no explicit inference-hardware benchmarking is reported in the reviewed sections. - Efficient: The paper argues O(VL) per-block time-mixer cost versus O(VL^2) for attention-based time mixers, and claims constant per-patch streaming cost versus growing O(L) for KV-cached attention, but these are asymptotic architectural arguments; no measured wall-clock latency or throughput comparison against Chronos-2/MOIRAI/GTT is presented in the pages reviewed. - Effective: MASE/CRPS gains over baselines are real but often narrow (e.g., 51% pairwise win rate vs. Chronos-2 despite a lower aggregate MASE), suggesting the practical margin over the strongest baseline is modest rather than decisive. - Scalable: Streaming stability is empirically tested to 32M steps (4000x the training context), which is a genuine stress test rather than a bare claim; long-horizon forecasting is tested to 1056 steps on dysts, where TiRex-2's edge narrows at coarse granularity, indicating the advantage is not uniform at longer horizons. Software artifact: not stated in reviewed pages whether code/weights are released (TiRex and Chronos predecessors have public repos referenced, but no explicit TiRex-2 release URL appears in Sections 1-4). Strengths: - A formally proved causality guarantee (Proposition 1, with a stated necessity counterexample in Appendix B.3) rather than an empirically-hoped-for property, which is unusual rigor for a systems-style TSFM paper. - Genuine architectural resolution of a real tension (bidirectional covariate use vs. causal target prediction) that prior designs (TimeXer, Timer-XL, CITRAS) only partially address per the authors' own comparison (Section B.4, not fully reviewed but referenced). - Ablation study with multi-seed variance reporting isolates the contribution of group attention and future-known covariates specifically, rather than only reporting an aggregate leaderboard number. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that updating future-known covariates online requires recomputing their bidirectional representation, so pure constant-cost streaming does not extend to changing future-covariate values; they note this is "rarely a binding constraint" since future covariates (calendars, scheduled events) are typically static within a forecast window, and offer a workaround (shifting changed covariates backward into the causal region). This is a direct, non-minimized acknowledgment of a real architectural constraint. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No measured latency/throughput numbers accompany the asymptotic efficiency claims (O(VL) vs O(VL^2)); the entire efficiency case rests on architecture-level Big-O reasoning and MASE-stability curves, not on actual runtime comparisons against the Transformer baselines. - The fev-bench win rate vs. Chronos-2 is 51% (statistically indistinguishable from a coin flip given the 40-60% CI) even though the paper's headline framing emphasizes "state-of-the-art" and leads with the aggregate MASE plot; a reader relying only on the abstract would not learn how close the two models actually are pairwise. - The synthetic-coupling pipeline's six coupling mechanisms are motivated qualitatively but not individually ablated; it is unclear which mechanism(s) actually drive the "future-known covariates" ablation gain of +0.033/+0.114 MASE, so it's unknown whether the elaborate multi-mechanism design (vs. a simpler single mechanism) is load-bearing. - GIFT-Eval and fev-bench are both zero-shot benchmarks assembled by overlapping author communities (Ansari, Aksu, Woo et al. co-author several of the baseline models and both benchmarks); an independently curated, disjoint benchmark suite would strengthen the state-of-the-art claim. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying covariate-aware, streaming multivariate forecasting gains a concrete architectural template (asymmetric masked xLSTM) with a provable causality guarantee and competitive zero-shot accuracy at markedly lower parameter count than Chronos-2 or TimesFM-2.5, but should treat the "state-of-the-art" framing cautiously given the near-tied 51% pairwise win rate against Chronos-2 and the absence of measured latency data backing the core efficiency claim.
Search Results 2607.01204 PDF
#10 — Highly Innovative (75)

Refploit: Facilitating Exploit Construction via Code- Agent Trajectory Repair

Z Chen, Z Xue, J Zhou, X Hu, X Xia, X Yang · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Refploit, an LLM-based framework that repairs failed code-agent trajectories for reproducing Java library vulnerabilities from disclosed exploit references, using differential execution against vulnerable/patched versions to detect ineffective reproductions and constraint-guided recovery to fix them. - Why it's important: Disclosed exploit references are frequently incomplete or unrunnable, requiring 2.15 manual interventions per case on average, and current code agents often produce exploits that look successful but bypass the actual vulnerable logic entirely (e.g., reimplementing vulnerable functionality rather than invoking it), making automated impact assessment of the 10 million packages on Maven unreliable. - How it does it: Refploit segments a failed agent trajectory into completed/missing/misleading sub-tasks along three dimensions (Environment, Harness, Reproduction), derives preservation and repair constraints from an LLM judgment over differential execution evidence, then resumes the agent from a valid replay point under those constraints rather than restarting from scratch. - The good: Under DeepSeek-V4-Flash, Refploit reproduces 138 of 172 exploits (80.2%), a 64.3% relative improvement over the base mini-swe-agent (84 to 138) and outperforms Codex with GPT-5.4 (69.2%) and POCGEN (SOTA exploit generation, evaluated on predefined CWE types only). - The missing: The differential validation oracle itself is LLM-judged rather than independently verified against a formal specification, so misclassified progress (documented in the paper's own failure analysis, e.g., CVE-2021-39144) can silently corrupt which trajectory segments get preserved. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in automated vulnerability reproduction or agent-trajectory repair; the differential-execution-as-oracle idea and the trajectory-segment-to-subtask mapping are reusable outside this specific domain, but the work is Java/Maven-specific and evaluated on datasets covering only 61.9% of historical Maven vulnerability CWEs.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (built framework evaluated by execution behavior on 172 real CVE exploit references). Rigor (78) is the largest contributor: a three-way ablation isolates each component's marginal contribution, ground truth was established by two blinded annotators with Cohen's kappa 0.828, and baselines are contemporary and strong (Codex/GPT-5.4 at 69.2%, POCGEN as CWE-specific SOTA); it is held back from higher by single-run numbers with no variance analysis on a stochastic agent and code released only 'upon acceptance'. Novelty (75) reflects a genuine reframing — repair the failed trajectory rather than regenerate it — though differential execution against vulnerable/patched versions is itself a well-established oracle. Non-Obviousness (72) is driven by the motivating insight that agents produce exploits that build and run while never touching the vulnerable code path, and by mapping LLM-judged subtask completion back onto specific trajectory segments; the ablation showing raw diff-feedback reaches only 31.4% versus 55.8% confirms the targeted-constraint mechanism is where the gain lives. Completeness (78) and Resilience (72) are supported by the cross-backbone (Qwen3.5-27B: 27.3% to 55.8%) and cross-agent (OpenHands: 51 to 89) transfer results plus direct acknowledgment of the 61.9% CWE coverage gap and the pre-installed-JDK environment weakening the Environment dimension, but the LLM-judged oracle is a real load-bearing weakness the authors' own failure analysis (CVE-2021-39144) exposes. Practical Impact (75) reflects a usable artifact and dataset at ~$1.03 per case, discounted because neither is public yet. Weighted composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*75 = 75.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Security analysts assessing the downstream impact of Java library vulnerabilities need runnable proof-of-concept exploits, but public disclosures (NVD links, advisories, GitHub issues) are frequently incomplete: textual reproduction steps, partial test snippets, or advisory-level descriptions with no complete project. The authors' own manual study of 84 exploit references across two datasets found 159 required manual interventions to produce 74 valid runnable exploits (2.15 interventions per case, spanning harness construction, environment setup, and payload modification). Code agents (e.g., mini-swe-agent) are a natural automation candidate, but the paper shows they often generate outputs that look successful (project builds, code runs, no exceptions) while never touching the vulnerable code path, e.g., for CVE-2018-1002200 the agent implements its own ZIP extraction logic instead of invoking the vulnerable library API. Key Idea: A failed agent trajectory is not entirely useless. Even when an agent's final exploit fails to reproduce a vulnerability, the trajectory may already contain reusable completed sub-tasks (correct JDK selection, correct Maven skeleton, correct attack-surface identification) mixed with segments that represent missing work or actively misleading directions (e.g., simulating vulnerable behavior with hand-written code instead of invoking the real API). By explicitly classifying trajectory segments along three reproduction dimensions (environment, harness, reproduction) and mapping LLM-judged subtask completion status back to the specific trajectory steps that produced it, the system can selectively preserve, repair, or forbid-repeating specific trajectory segments instead of discarding the whole attempt and restarting, or naively feeding raw error/differential text back to the agent (shown in the ablation to be far weaker: 31.4% vs. 55.8%). Methodology: The pipeline has three stages operating in a loop bounded by a fixed token/step budget (5M input, 1M output tokens, 200 steps). (1) Differential-Guided Reproduction State Analysis: given an initial trajectory T_init = <S1...Sn> generated via a ReAct loop by a base code agent (mini-swe-agent, later OpenHands), the generated exploit e_init is executed on both the vulnerable version (R_vul) and the patched version (R_pat) via an execution process E; a valid reproduction requires observed behavior to diverge between the two (triggering on vulnerable, blocked on patched). (2) Progress-Guided Trajectory Analysis: when the differential result is negative, an LLM judge expands the vulnerability description into concrete subtasks under three fixed dimensions (Environment, Harness, Reproduction), judges each subtask's completion status (satisfied/incomplete/misleading) using search items κ that trace back to the trajectory steps that introduced the relevant capability, then maps that judgment onto trajectory segments (segmented by two anchor actions: reading the reference, running exploit commands). (3) Constraint-Oriented Trajectory Recovery: completed subtasks yield preservation constraints (retain the identified JDK, Maven skeleton, attack-vector choice); incomplete subtasks yield repair constraints (add the missing library, adapt the payload); misleading segments yield avoidance constraints (forbid re-simulating vulnerable behavior with local code). A replay entry point is selected by scanning the trajectory forward while segments remain valid; a recovery sub-agent is launched per subtask under the corresponding constraints, and the loop returns to differential re-validation until reproduction succeeds or the budget is exhausted. Critical design decisions: (a) reusing only the vulnerable/patched differential signal as ground truth rather than a hand-authored oracle per CWE (generalizes across 53 CWE types, unlike POCGEN's five predefined patterns, but inherits LLM-judgment risk); (b) fixed three-dimension decomposition (Environment/Harness/Reproduction) derived from an open card-sorting study of the manual reproduction interventions, giving a principled but not formally validated taxonomy; (c) selective trajectory replay (preserve valid prefix, target-specific repair) rather than full trajectory regeneration, which the ablation shows materially reduces steps needed (109.3 vs. 147.6 for No-Progress-Assessment) while improving effectiveness. Key Results: Evaluated on 172 exploit references for 143 CVEs across three open-source Java vulnerability datasets (CWE-Bench-Java, VISION, and a third from the same group), covering 53 CWE types. With DeepSeek-V4-Flash, Refploit reproduces 138/172 exploits (80.2%), versus mini-swe-agent baseline 84/172 (48.8%), Codex (GPT-5.4, xhigh context) 119/172 (69.2%), AutoGPT Classic 48/172 (27.9%). Against POCGEN (the CWE-specific SOTA baseline, evaluated only on its five predefined CWE types: path traversal, command injection, code injection, ReDoS, prototype pollution — 41 total cases), Refploit outperforms it in every applicable category (e.g., path traversal 74.3% vs. 31.4%, ReDoS 100% vs. 66.7%); POCGEN is inapplicable to prototype pollution in Java since it's a JS-specific class. Under a second backbone (Qwen3.5-27B), Refploit improves the base agent from 47/172 (27.3%) to 96/172 (55.8%), a 104.3% relative gain, showing the improvement is not solely attributable to a stronger backbone. Ablation (Qwen3.5-27B): No-Differential-Analysis drops to 69 (40.1%) despite fewer average steps (103.1) because it can't distinguish real from simulated success; No-Progress-Assessment reaches 85 (49.4%) but at the highest step cost (147.6); No-Constraint-Guidance reaches 72 (41.9%). Cross-agent generality: migrating the framework to OpenHands raises its base rate from 51 to 89 exploits (74.5% relative increase). Ground truth was established by two researchers independently annotating 1,114 confirmed reproductions in a blinded protocol, achieving Cohen's kappa of 0.828. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (trajectory-repair framework combining differential execution oracle with structured, dimension-based subtask decomposition and constraint-guided selective replay) applied to a NEW PROBLEM framing (repairing, rather than regenerating from scratch, code-agent exploit-reproduction trajectories). The differential-execution-as-oracle idea itself is a straightforward, well-established technique (compare behavior on vulnerable vs. patched versions); what's genuinely new is using LLM-judged subtask completion mapped back onto trajectory segments to drive selective preservation/repair/avoidance, replacing prior approaches that either regenerate wholesale or feed raw feedback back untargeted (validated by the ablation showing Diff-Feedback-Only reaches only 31.4%). Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes a genuine ablation isolating each of the three core components (differential analysis, progress assessment, constraint guidance), each shown to contribute independently. Ground truth used a two-annotator blinded protocol with inter-rater agreement reported (kappa 0.828) rather than automated or single-annotator labeling — a credible practice. No error bars or multiple-seed reporting are given for the main effectiveness numbers (Table II/III), and the paper does not report variance across repeated runs of the same agent, which matters since LLM agent trajectories are stochastic even at temperature 0.2. Baselines are contemporary and reasonably strong (Codex with GPT-5.4 under an xhigh-context setting, POCGEN as the SOTA exploit-generation method), which is a positive; POCGEN is explicitly noted to have been adapted to Java by the authors themselves, an "internal validity" threat the authors acknowledge directly. Code and dataset are stated to be released "upon acceptance" (not yet public at submission), so the 172-exploit dataset and Refploit implementation cannot currently be independently verified. The evaluation environment pre-installs four LTS JDK versions and standard tools (curl), a controlled setup the authors themselves flag as reducing the difficulty of the Environment dimension and therefore not fully testing that capability. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built framework evaluated by execution behavior on real CVEs). Practical: yes, it runs standard containerized Ubuntu 22.04 with off-the-shelf JDKs and open-weight or commercial LLMs (DeepSeek-V4-Flash, Qwen3.5-27B, GPT-5.4), no specialized hardware beyond what the base LLM API/inference requires. Efficient: cost is bounded per case (5M input / 1M output token budget, ~$1.03 per case using DeepSeek-V4-Flash per the paper's own estimate excluding cache hits, up to 200 agent steps) but recovery adds iterative loops beyond the base agent's single pass, and the paper doesn't report wall-clock time or total cost overhead relative to the base agent baseline. Effective: yes for the tested distribution, the differential-execution ground truth directly measures the target property (does the exploit trigger on vulnerable and not on patched), not a proxy metric. Scalable: untested at 10x-100x scale; the dataset covers 143 CVEs and 53 CWE types, but the authors explicitly note their datasets cover only 61.9% of CWEs seen in historical Maven vulnerabilities and that authorization/access-control CWEs (CWE-862, CWE-284, CWE-639) and platform-level CWEs (CWE-352, CWE-200) are absent from evaluation, so generalization to those categories is an open claim, not a measured one. The work delivers a software artifact (framework + dataset), to be released upon acceptance, not yet available at review time. Strengths: - The differential execution requirement (vulnerable-version triggers, patched-version blocks) is a meaningfully stronger validation oracle than checking process exit codes or absence of exceptions, and it directly catches the paper's own motivating failure mode (agents that reimplement vulnerable logic rather than invoking it). - The three-way ablation (differential analysis / progress assessment / constraint guidance) isolates each component's marginal contribution rather than treating the system as a monolith, and the No-Differential-Analysis variant's lower step count with lower accuracy is a genuinely informative negative result (fewer steps because false positives get accepted early, not because the method is more efficient). - Cross-agent transfer to OpenHands (51 to 89 exploits) is a real test of whether the trajectory-analysis logic is agent-specific glue code versus a generalizable capability, and the reported gain is substantial. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The exclusion of image-only exploit references (e.g., CVE-2021-40660) is explicitly noted as limiting generalizability to image-heavy disclosures; they partially test with a multimodal-capable model (Qwen3.5-27B) but do not systematically evaluate on image-based cases. - The 61.9% CWE coverage gap is acknowledged directly, with the authors naming which CWE categories are absent (authorization/access-control and platform-level vulnerabilities) rather than glossing over it. - LLM-judgment errors in progress assessment are named as a specific internal-validity threat: incorrect judgments can cause the system to preserve misleading trajectory components. - The pre-installed, standardized JDK/tool environment is acknowledged as reducing the evaluation's coverage of the Environment-Preparation dimension specifically. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No variance/repeatability analysis: LLM agents are stochastic, and the paper reports single-run effectiveness numbers per configuration without repeated trials or confidence intervals, so the size of the reported gaps (e.g., 80.2% vs. 69.2% against Codex) carries unstated uncertainty. - No wall-clock time or total dollar-cost comparison against baselines is reported; the per-case token budget is described but not the actual realized cost/time overhead of the iterative recovery loop versus a single-shot baseline, which matters for practical adoption by security teams under time pressure. - The paper does not report how many recovery iterations (rounds) are typically needed before success or exhaustion, information relevant to understanding the practical latency of the approach. - The differential oracle assumes patches directly eliminate vulnerable behavior; the authors already exclude two CVEs where this assumption failed (CVE-2021-43859, CVE-2022-22885) but do not quantify how often this assumption might be borderline-true rather than clearly false across the remaining 172 cases, leaving open whether some "successes" are partially confounded by patches that also change unrelated behavior. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on automated vulnerability-reproduction pipelines gains a validated, generalizable technique (differential-oracle-driven, constraint-guided trajectory repair) that meaningfully beats both a CWE-specific SOTA generator and an advanced commercial code agent, though the reported numbers rest on single-run, LLM-judged evaluation without variance analysis, and the framework itself is not yet available to verify independently.
Search Results 2607.01760 PDF
#11 — Innovative (74)

Video-Oasis: Rethinking Evaluation of Video Understanding (43 ▲ )

Geuntaek Lim, Sungjune Park, Jaeyun Lee, Inwoong Lee, Taeoh Kim, Dongyoon Wee, Minho Shim, Yukyung Choi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
