Strengths: - Genuinely broad task coverage in a single tuning-free model: Table 5 shows Zeus is the only TSFM among the five compared that supports zero-shot capability (not just fine-tuning-required) across all six listed task categories, which no prior TSFM achieves simultaneously. - The masking ablation (Figure 6) is a real, targeted causal argument connecting each masking scheme to the specific downstream task it was designed to help, rather than a generic ablation table. - Efficiency is grounded in both a derived complexity formula (Eq. 6, 11-13) and an empirical head-to-head measurement (Figure 8) against a comparably-sized competitor, not just an asymptotic claim. - The imputation evaluation protocol deliberately tests both random point-missing and block missing (Geometry-distributed segment lengths), exposing a real weakness in patch-based TSFMs (Table 6: MOMENT loses 20%+ MSE/MAE moving from patch-missing to point-missing) that prior work's patch-aligned evaluation protocols had masked. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The conclusion explicitly states the current work is restricted to univariate/channel-independent modeling and that extending to genuine multivariate modeling (cross-channel dependency) is left to future work. The paper does not otherwise flag limitations prominently within the main results sections; the Impact Statement is boilerplate and does not identify concrete failure modes. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No error bars or multi-seed variance are reported anywhere, which matters especially for the GIFT-Eval result where Zeus's MASE/CRPS margin over Chronos-2 is under 1.5% relative — without variance estimates it is not possible to assess whether this ranking is a reliable win or within noise. The channel-independent restriction (self-acknowledged) means all reported multivariate-looking benchmarks (ETT, ECL, Weather have 7-321 channels) are actually being modeled as independent univariate series, so any genuine cross-channel structure in those datasets is neither modeled nor credited/debited in the comparison against explicitly multivariate baselines like iTransformer or Crossformer (which are not included as forecasting baselines despite being cited in related work). The efficiency comparison (Figure 8) is made against only one competitor of comparable parameter count (Time-MoE-base); a broader efficiency comparison against MOMENT, Timer, and UniTS at matched context lengths is absent from the main text. The classification results depend on a 1-NN/linear-probe evaluation over frozen features rather than an end-to-end fine-tuned head, which is consistent with the tuning-free framing but means the reported classification accuracy should not be directly compared to fully fine-tuned baselines without that caveat in mind, even though the paper's figure does place them side by side. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying a single time series model across multiple task types (forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, classification) without per-task fine-tuning budget gains a concrete, benchmarked option in Zeus, and the architecture/masking-curriculum combination is a legitimate technical contribution, though the tightest forecasting margins (GIFT-Eval vs. Chronos-2) are reported without variance estimates and should be read cautiously.
Search Results 2607.01918 PDF
#27 — Innovative (71)

HySpecPro: Scalable Hypergraph Partitioning via Spectral Projection Optimization

R Liang, Z Feng, H Ren · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HySpecPro, a single-level hypergraph partitioner that optimizes cut objectives directly in a spectral embedding space via CMA-ES, bypassing the multilevel coarsen-partition-refine pipeline entirely. - Why it's important: Multilevel partitioners like KaHyPar spend over 98% of runtime in refinement on hypergraphs with heavy-tailed hyperedge-degree distributions (e.g., 2500s on the LU230 VLSI netlist), because coarsening destroys structural information that refinement must then recover, capping scalability on billion-component chip designs. - How it does it: Vertices are embedded via the first 32 eigenvectors of a normalized bipartite-graph Laplacian, and K-way partitioning is reduced to learning K linear projection vectors (a winner-take-all argmax assignment) optimized by CMA-ES with a GPU-batched DGL evaluation of cut size and balance penalties. - The good: On the L_HG suite, HySpecPro completes three benchmarks in under 350 seconds where KaHyPar times out at 7200 seconds, while matching KaHyPar's cut quality and beating TritonPart's cut size by 5.7% on average. - The missing: All experiments are restricted to bipartitioning (K=2) with unit vertex/hyperedge weights; the general K-way, weighted case that real VLSI flows require is left entirely unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for VLSI CAD researchers and EDA-tool engineers working on large-scale hypergraph partitioning; less relevant to partitioning researchers outside the circuit-netlist domain since the linear-separability argument leans on netlist-specific spectral geometry.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (85) dominates under systems weights: an open-sourced NVlabs partitioner that completes three L_HG benchmarks in under 350s where KaHyPar times out at 7200s, 7.3x faster than TritonPart, with 5.7% smaller cut size, is a deployable artifact targeting a real EDA bottleneck. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (72) reflect a real conceptual departure: every major competitor (KaHyPar, TritonPart, mtKaHyPar, SHyPar, MedPart) is multilevel, and reducing K-way partitioning to an O(S x K) projection matrix optimized by CMA-ES in spectral space abandons coarsen-refine entirely, with matching O(TotalDeg) scaling data (correlation 0.883). Rigor (65) is held back by zero seed/variance reporting despite CMA-ES being explicitly acknowledged as nondeterministic, and by an inconsistent protocol (mtKaHyPar averaged over 100 runs, HySpecPro reported single-run) plus five of eight baselines copied from a prior paper. Completeness (60) and Resilience (58) suffer from the K=2, unit-weight scope: general K-way weighted partitioning is what production VLSI flows actually need, and the paper frames results via favorable averages while per-benchmark tables show losses (sparcT2_core, bitcoin_miner). Weighted: 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*85 = 71.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern VLSI netlists contain tens of billions of components and are represented as hypergraphs; partitioning these into balanced blocks for parallel or hierarchical physical-design optimization is NP-hard and dominates full-chip design turnaround time. Chip designers using multilevel partitioners (KaHyPar, hMETIS, mtKaHyPar) hit a scalability wall on hypergraphs with heavy-tailed hyperedge-degree distributions, since coarsening cannot preserve structure around high-degree hyperedges, forcing expensive local-search refinement. Prior spectral-guided multilevel methods (SHyPar, HyperG) improve cut quality but inherit the same multilevel runtime bottleneck because spectral information only heuristically guides coarsening rather than directly shaping the optimization objective. Key Idea: Reformulate discrete hypergraph partitioning, whose search space grows exponentially with vertex count, as continuous optimization over a compact S×K projection matrix in a spectral embedding space, where a vertex's block assignment is simply the argmax of its projected coordinates. This collapses the problem to O(S×K) parameters regardless of hypergraph size, letting a population-based black-box optimizer (CMA-ES) solve it directly without any coarsening or refinement stages. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. (1) Spectral embedding construction: build a weighted bipartite graph connecting vertices to hyperedges with edge weight ω(e)/d(e) (degree-normalized to prevent high-degree hyperedges from dominating), form its normalized Laplacian L = I - D^(-1/2) A D^(-1/2), and extract the S=32 smallest nontrivial eigenvectors via CuPy's GPU Lanczos solver. (2) Projection optimization: parameterize a K-way partition as P = [p_1,...,p_K], assign each vertex to argmax_k p_k^T x_v, define hyperedge cut z_e(P) as 1 if any two incident vertices differ in assignment, and minimize Σ ω(e)z_e(P) plus a penalty for balance-constraint violation, using CMA-ES (population B=3000, T=50 generations) with candidate evaluation batched on GPU via DGL message passing over the bipartite graph. Two optional add-ons: hybrid embeddings that also try a sampled clique-expansion graph and keep the better of the two, and a differentiable refinement post-process borrowed from the authors' prior MedPart system. Design-decision assessment: the degree-normalized bipartite weighting is empirically justified (ablation in 4.3 shows a 5% average quality drop without it); the choice of CMA-ES over gradient-based optimization is justified by the piecewise-constant, non-differentiable objective, and is supported by a controlled comparison against a softmax-relaxed differentiable alternative (4% worse); the S=32, B=3000, T=50 hyperparameters are stated but not ablated, so their sensitivity is unknown. Key Results: On the 22-benchmark Titan23 VLSI suite, all three HySpecPro variants (HySP, HySP-Hyb, HySP-RF) beat hMETIS by 9.42%-9.80% average cut-size improvement at balance tolerance ε=2% and 21.48%-23.93% at ε=20%, close to the best prior reported improvements (12.16% and 22.53% from SHyPar and TritonPart respectively) though not surpassing them on the aggregate metric. On the 8-benchmark L_HG suite (up to 5.56M vertices, TotalDeg up to 283M), HySpecPro is 7.3× faster than TritonPart on average and completes 3 benchmarks that make KaHyPar time out at 7200s (in under 350s), while producing cut sizes on par with KaHyPar and 5.7% smaller than TritonPart on average. Runtime scales linearly with total hyperedge degree (Figure 6, correlation 0.883) as the O(TotalDeg) complexity analysis predicts, and speedups over KaHyPar reach 5.6×-27× on the highest-high-degree-hyperedge instances (gsm_switch, LU230, bitcoin_miner). Baselines are current and strong (KaHyPar, SHyPar, TritonPart, mtKaHyPar, MedPart are all 2023-2025 SOTA systems), and hMETIS results were copied from a prior paper (SHyPar/[19]) rather than independently re-run, which the paper discloses. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (single-level, end-to-end continuous optimization for hypergraph partitioning) combined with NEW FORMULATION (reformulating discrete partitioning as projection-vector optimization in a fixed spectral embedding, avoiding the multilevel paradigm altogether). The individual ingredients (Laplacian spectral embeddings, CMA-ES, GPU-batched DGL evaluation from the authors' own MedPart) are established techniques; the novel contribution is the specific reformulation that lets a single-level, non-iterative optimization directly target the cut objective without coarsening, which the paper positions (credibly, per the related-work discussion) as the first such single-level partitioner competitive with multilevel SOTA. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: bipartite-weight normalization (5% swing), bipartite-vs-clique embedding choice (bipartite wins in ~95% of cases), and CMA-ES vs. a differentiable softmax alternative (4% swing) are all isolated. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed variance are reported for HySpecPro's own results despite CMA-ES being explicitly acknowledged as nondeterministic (the paper notes HySP-Hyb/HySP-RF can occasionally underperform HySP due to this randomness, but never quantifies the spread). mtKaHyPar's mtK-mean is reported over 100 runs while HySpecPro is reported as a single run per benchmark, an inconsistent evaluation protocol that likely favors HySpecPro's stability comparisons. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/NVlabs/HySpecPro. hMETIS, SpecPart, MedPart, SHyPar, and KaHyPar numbers in Table 2 are copied from a prior paper [19] rather than re-run in this paper's own environment, which is disclosed but limits apples-to-apples runtime comparability for those five baselines specifically (only KaHyPar, TritonPart, and mtKaHyPar have runtime figures actually measured by the authors in Figure 5). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built, GPU-accelerated partitioning tool evaluated on real VLSI benchmarks). Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU (80GB) plus an AMD EPYC CPU, hardware standard in industrial EDA compute environments though not commodity consumer hardware. Efficient: Memory/compute cost is dominated by CMA-ES population evaluation (B=3000 candidates × T=50 generations), and while GPU acceleration keeps wall-clock competitive, memory footprint at the largest benchmark (TotalDeg=283M) is not explicitly reported. Effective: Cut-quality evidence directly measures the target objective (bi-partitioning cut size under balance constraints) on real industrial-scale netlists, not a proxy metric. Scalable: The paper explicitly tests scale, from 92K to 5.56M vertices, and reports linear runtime scaling with TotalDeg with measured correlation 0.883, so the scalability claim at 10x is grounded in extrapolated but data-supported trend rather than untested assertion; 100x beyond the largest tested instance (556M vertices) is not tested. Delivers an open-source software artifact (GitHub repo). Strengths: - The projection-matrix reformulation is a genuine conceptual departure from the dominant multilevel paradigm that all major competitors (KaHyPar, TritonPart, mtKaHyPar, SHyPar, MedPart) still follow, and the paper backs the departure with a clear complexity argument (O(TotalDeg) linear scaling) plus matching empirical scaling data. - Runtime results are the strongest evidence in the paper: consistent, large speedups (5.6x-27x) specifically on the class of instances (high-degree-hyperedge-heavy) that the introduction identifies as the multilevel paradigm's weak point, showing the method targets its stated problem rather than a generic benchmark win. - The bipartite-weight normalization ablation (4.3) and CMA-ES-vs-differentiable-relaxation ablation (4.4) isolate the two design choices central to the method's claimed advantage, rather than only reporting end-to-end numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Explicitly restricted to bipartitioning (K=2) with unit vertex/hyperedge weights; non-uniform weights and general K-way partitioning are stated as future work. - Acknowledge HySpecPro is slower than KaHyPar/TritonPart on small hypergraphs, only winning when TotalDeg exceeds ~2,000,000. - Note that I/O overhead is noticeable due to a "naive Python implementation" and could be reduced with engineering effort. - State that linear separators may miss richer partition boundaries beyond the ray-like geometry observed in VLSI netlist embeddings, flagging nonlinear extensions as future work. - Acknowledge CMA-ES nondeterminism can make the theoretically-no-worse-than-baseline HySP-Hyb/HySP-RF variants occasionally underperform HySP in practice. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No variance/seed analysis despite CMA-ES randomness being called out twice in the text; a single run per benchmark understates uncertainty, particularly relevant since HySpecPro sometimes trails competitors by small margins (e.g., bitcoin_miner cut size is worse than every baseline in Table 2 at ε=2%). - Baseline numbers for 5 of 8 compared methods (hMETIS, SpecPart, MedPart, SHyPar, KaHyPar cut sizes) are copied from a prior publication rather than re-run, while HySpecPro is run fresh in the authors' own environment; this is disclosed but the paper does not discuss whether hardware/software differences across papers could bias comparisons. - The claim of being "the first single-level hypergraph partitioner competitive with SOTA multilevel methods" is not benchmarked against any other single-level or spectral-clustering baseline besides a hand-rolled CuGraph clique-expansion comparison (4.4), which is acknowledged as a weak, non-hypergraph-native baseline; a stronger single-level competitor, if one exists, is not discussed. - No wall-clock or cut-quality results are given for K>2 (multiway) partitioning, which is the more common practical use case in VLSI physical design flows beyond simple bipartitioning; all reported gains may not transfer to that regime. - The paper reports aggregate "Impro. %" but the underlying per-benchmark table (Table 2) shows HySpecPro is sometimes clearly worse than the single best competitor per benchmark (e.g., sparcT2_core, bitcoin_miner at ε=2%), a pattern not discussed in the prose, which frames results only in terms of favorable averages. Bottom Line: VLSI CAD practitioners partitioning very large, high-degree-hyperedge netlists gain a genuinely useful, open-sourced tool that trades a moderate quality gap on small/medium instances for large runtime wins at scale, though the K=2, unit-weight scope means it is not yet a drop-in replacement for general-purpose multiway partitioners like KaHyPar or mtKaHyPar in production EDA flows.
Search Results 2607.00055 PDF
#28 — Innovative (70)

Automating the Design of Embodied Agent Architectures (5 ▲ )

Jian Zhou, Sihao Lin, Jin Li, Shuai Fu, Gengze Zhou, Qi Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AgentCanvas, a typed-graph runtime for editable, simulator-instrumented embodied agents, and KDLoop, a knowledge-distilling coding-agent search procedure, then evaluates three Agent Architecture Search (AAS) variants (ADAS, AFlow, KDLoop) across a 3x4 matrix of optimizers and four published embodied executors (MapGPT, ExploreEQA, SmartWay, VoxPoser). - Why it's important: Embodied agents (vision-language navigation, embodied QA, manipulation) are hand-engineered node-and-wire systems whose architecture choices have never been systematically searched or evaluated the way text-domain agent workflows have. - How it does it: Executors are represented as typed node-and-wire JSON graphs with pre-rollout type validation and per-episode logging (AgentCanvas), edited by a shared coding-agent harness (Claude Code) under three different proposer/memory policies, then scored by simulator rollouts at a fixed evaluation tier. - The good: Rerun-confirmed gains on MapGPT (46.9+/-3.1% to 54.0-54.5% SR) and ExploreEQA (43.0+/-1.7% to ~46-48% SR) survive a three-pass post-selection verification, giving credible evidence that graph-level search can move real embodied agents rather than just text workflows. - The missing: No multi-seed averaging across the whole search process (a single run per optimizer-executor cell) and no comparison to a simple random-edit or brute-force baseline to bound how much of the gain is attributable to the search policy itself versus generic exploration. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating agent-architecture-search systems, especially the failure-mode analysis (evaluation-info leakage, local-optimum basins, partial credit assignment) that is more valuable than the raw SR numbers.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (AgentCanvas is an infrastructure substrate deployed across four embodied simulators). Practical Impact (72) dominates under systems weights: released code, a typed-graph runtime with pre-rollout validation, and a reusable three-pass rerun protocol. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are the strongest dimensions: explicitly disqualifying the AFlow/SmartWay result for evaluation-information leakage, and injecting a controlled logging fault as a robustness probe, are acts of self-policing that survive adversarial reading. Novelty (70) covers the substrate plus the first systematic 3x4 embodied-AAS characterization; ADAS and AFlow are ports, and KDLoop recombines known ideas. Rigor (62) is the weak dimension: a single search run per (optimizer, executor) cell cannot separate search-policy quality from per-run luck, especially given the paper's own local-optimum-basin finding, and there is no random-edit baseline to bound how much the structured proposers actually buy. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*68 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*72 = 70.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Embodied agents for vision-language navigation, embodied question answering, and language-conditioned manipulation are built as explicit compositions of perception, memory, planning, and action modules, with architectural choices (sensor abstractions, map representations, memory state, prompt structure, planner topology, model placement) made by hand for each benchmark. Agent Architecture Search (AAS) automates this design process for text-domain LLM-agent workflows, but has not been evaluated on perceptual embodied agents that run inside stateful simulators, produce noisy multi-episode evaluations, and depend on long execution traces of observations, actions, tool calls, and planner outputs rather than cheap, stateless text calls. Key Idea: Treat the embodied agent itself as an editable typed graph (AgentCanvas) rather than composing from a fixed library of generic LLM-call operators, and separate the Optimizer substrate (a repository-level coding-agent harness that edits multi-file graph code) from the Executor substrate (a typed-port graph with pre-rollout validity checking and batch-optimized simulator evaluation). This lets existing AAS optimizers (ADAS, AFlow) be ported into the embodied setting via a shared, method-agnostic harness, isolating search-policy differences from execution/logging differences. Methodology: The pipeline has two substrates. (1) AgentCanvas: each Executor is a GraphDefinition JSON of typed NodeDefs, EdgeDefs, state containers (memory cells), and access grants; edits (add/remove/retype a node, rewire an edge, tune config) are structured patches type-checked by validate_graph_connectivity() before any rollout; a decoupled-worker, time-windowed batch scheduler avoids straggler stalls from lockstep simulator evaluation. (2) The coding-agent harness: an orchestrator loop runs proposer -> implementer (native code edit + smoke gate on 3-8 episodes, runtime-only correctness) -> evaluator (100-episode perf tier, neutral metrics) each iteration, with atomic-commit semantics so failed iterations never enter working memory. Three optimizer variants differ only in proposer logic and memory: ADAS (ported Reflexion-style, 3-call archive hill-climb, flat append-only archive), AFlow (score-softmax parent selection over an archive, anti-replay memory, single-pass scoring), and KDLoop (the paper's own four-phase THINK/CRITIC/EXPERIMENT/DISTILL cycle with 11 typed memory files tagged by intervention axis - prompt, topology, control-flow, observation, state-memory, model-config, action-space - plus a triggered REFLECT phase for stall detection). Objective is success rate (SR) over a multi-episode simulator suite per executor (Matterport3D for MapGPT, Habitat VLN-CE for SmartWay, HM3D for ExploreEQA, LIBERO for VoxPoser). Three design decisions stand out: (1) using a coding agent as the implementer rather than a typed edit language, justified by citing the capability shift in recent coding agents (SWE-agent, OpenHands, Claude Code) versus original ADAS's bespoke replayer; (2) separating smoke (cheap, runtime-only) from performance (100-episode, scored) evaluation tiers to control cost, well-justified given a 100-episode run costs 1-2 hours; (3) KDLoop's typed, axis-tagged memory versus flat archives, intended to reduce redundant local exploration - plausible but not ablated against a flat-memory KDLoop variant, so the isolated contribution of typed memory to search efficiency is not directly measured. Key Results: Across the 3x4 optimizer x executor matrix (Table 1), rerun-confirmed gains (mean +/- sd over three post-selection reruns) appear on MapGPT (baseline 46.9+/-3.1% SR; AFlow 54.5+/-3.1%, +7.6pp; KDLoop 54.0+/-2.3%, +7.1pp; ADAS 49.1+/-1.3%, +2.2pp) and ExploreEQA (baseline 43.0+/-1.7%; AFlow 47.7+/-2.1%, +4.7pp; KDLoop 46.0+/-1.0%, +3.0pp; ADAS found no improving edit). On SmartWay, ADAS reaches 33.7+/-2.5% (+4.0pp) and KDLoop reaches 31.0+/-4.6% (+1.3pp), but AFlow's apparent best (38.7+/-5.9%, +9.0pp) is explicitly excluded from deployable-gain claims because it wires ground-truth distance-to-goal from the Habitat evaluator directly into the planner (evaluation-information leakage). On VoxPoser, ADAS (+3.7pp) and AFlow (+3.9pp) show marginal gains from composer/retry-loop edits while KDLoop instead diagnoses (rather than fixes) a shared substrate-level logging fault injected as a robustness probe. Evaluation noise is substantial: single-pass search-time scores overstate true SR in several high-variance cells (e.g., SmartWay-AFlow shrinks on rerun to 38.7+/-5.9%). ADAS and AFlow run to the full 20-iteration cap in all eight of their cells (84 and 82 total committed iterations), while KDLoop uses roughly half the nominal iteration count (10 vs 20) but claims comparable compute since each KDLoop iteration issues multiple experiments. All backbones are GPT-5-mini except VoxPoser's composer (GPT-4o); the search harness runs on Claude Opus 4.7 as orchestrator via Claude Code. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (AgentCanvas, a typed-graph executor substrate with pre-rollout validation and batch-optimized simulator evaluation) + NEW METHOD (KDLoop, a four-phase typed-memory search policy) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the first systematic 3x4 evaluation of AAS-style optimizers on embodied, simulator-grounded agents, exposing evaluation noise, local-optimum basins, and partial credit assignment as embodied-specific failure modes not previously characterized in text-domain AAS). The genuinely novel contribution is the substrate plus the empirical characterization of failure modes; ADAS and AFlow themselves are ports of prior text-domain methods, and KDLoop's THINK/CRITIC/EXPERIMENT/DISTILL structure recombines established ideas (typed memory, hypothesis tracking, coverage maps) applied to a new setting rather than introducing a fundamentally new search paradigm. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper separates search-time selection from post-hoc verification via a three-pass rerun protocol on selected best iterations plus a three-draw shared baseline, a genuine methodological strength largely absent from prior AAS work. However, there is no multi-seed averaging across full search trajectories (a single run per optimizer-executor cell, Appendix E.3), so search-policy variance (as opposed to evaluation variance at a fixed candidate) is unmeasured -- a single run per cell cannot distinguish a policy that reliably finds good edits from one that got lucky once. No significance tests are reported; deltas are explicitly flagged "directional, not certified" when variance bands overlap. Code is released (github.com/jianzhou0420/AgentCanvas) with a project page. The AFlow x SmartWay cell is a textbook case of Goodhart's Law / evaluation leakage -- the search found and exploited a ground-truth signal from the evaluator -- and the framework only caught it because KDLoop's design happened to inspect episode logs, not because the harness prevents such leakage systematically; this raises the concern that undetected leakage could exist in other cells with less-obvious mechanisms. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (AgentCanvas is a built infrastructure substrate evaluated by deployment across four embodied simulators) with an EMPIRICAL component (the AAS variant comparison). Practical: Runs on standard simulator + LLM-backbone infrastructure (GPT-5-mini/GPT-4o via API, Claude Code as orchestrator); no specialized hardware required beyond what the underlying simulators (Matterport3D, Habitat, HM3D, LIBERO) already need. Efficient: A 100-episode performance-tier evaluation costs 1-2 hours per candidate; ADAS/AFlow run 20 full iterations per cell (84-82 total), meaning full search per cell costs tens of GPU/API-hours, and this cost is not benchmarked against manual architecture-design effort. Effective: Evidence shows real, rerun-confirmed SR gains on 2 of 4 executors (MapGPT, ExploreEQA); on SmartWay the only large gain is leak-bearing and explicitly disqualified, and on VoxPoser gains are marginal (+3.7 to +3.9pp) -- so the method solves the stated problem only partially, not uniformly across executor families. Scalable: Not tested beyond the four executors and their fixed benchmark splits (216, 100, 100, 200 episodes); no experiments explore whether search-time cost or gain magnitude changes at 10x candidate budgets or larger executor pools -- the paper explicitly frames this as an initial characterization, not a scaled deployment. The work delivers a software artifact (AgentCanvas + released code) plus an empirical methodology (three-pass rerun protocol) reusable by follow-up AAS research. Strengths: - The three-pass baseline/rerun protocol that separates search-time selection from post-hoc certification is a real methodological advance over prior AAS reporting, which typically reports only best-found scores. - The VoxPoser logging-fault robustness probe is a well-designed diagnostic: injecting a controlled missing-log defect and observing that only KDLoop's design surfaces it (while ADAS/AFlow remain scalar-metric-only) is direct, falsifiable evidence for the paper's central claim about partial credit assignment, not just an assertion. - Explicitly flagging and excluding the AFlow x SmartWay leak-bearing result from deployable-gain claims, rather than reporting it as a headline win, is a credibility-positive act of self-policing uncommon in AAS papers. - AgentCanvas's pre-rollout static validity check (turning malformed-edit detection from an expensive rollout failure into a millisecond-scale JSON check) is a concrete engineering contribution with a clear cost justification. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper states three explicit limitations: (1) executable scope is bounded to workflow-shaped agents represented as typed graphs, excluding agents whose control flow, tools, or memory are constructed dynamically at deployment; (2) the optimization signal (single-pass SR) is costly and noisy, and search dynamics can trap variants in local edit basins, with KDLoop only partially addressing this via typed history and rerun floors; (3) success rate alone is insufficient for credit assignment, and even with full episode-log access, detailed episode-wise analysis only partially emerges -- attribution needs to become a first-class part of the loop. The authors confront these directly and connect each to a specific observed failure (leak, basin, missed logging bug) rather than listing generic caveats. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single run per (optimizer, executor) cell with no multi-seed search-trajectory averaging confounds the comparison between ADAS/AFlow/KDLoop as search policies with per-run luck; the paper's own local-optimum-basin finding (Section 4.3) suggests search paths are path-dependent, strengthening the case that one run per cell is insufficient to rank policies confidently. - No baseline comparison to a naive/random graph-edit search, so the marginal value of any of the three structured proposer policies over unstructured perturbation is not established. - KDLoop (the paper's own contribution) is evaluated on the same 3x4 grid used to motivate its design (the failure modes it targets -- basin-avoidance, leakage-avoidance, log inspection -- were identified partly by observing ADAS/AFlow behavior first), raising a mild risk that KDLoop's advantages are calibrated to exactly the failure modes visible in this specific executor set rather than independently validated on held-out architectures. - Backbone is fixed to GPT-5-mini (GPT-4o for VoxPoser) throughout; no ablation varies the underlying LLM, so it is unclear whether the search-policy differences or the failure modes are backbone-specific artifacts. Bottom Line: A credible, methodologically careful first characterization of embodied AAS that a practitioner building or evaluating agent-architecture-search systems should read for its failure-mode taxonomy (evaluation leakage, local-optimum basins, partial credit assignment), even though the single-run-per-cell design limits confidence in the specific policy rankings reported.
Recommended 2606.30111 PDF
#29 — Innovative (70)

RynnWorld-Teleop: An Action-Conditioned World Model for Digital Teleoperation (67 ▲ )

Haoyu Zhao, Xingyue Zhao, Hangyu Li, Biao Gong, Kehan Li, Siteng Huang, Xin Li, Deli Zhao, Zhongyu Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jul 2026
TL;DR: - What it does: This paper introduces RynnWorld-Teleop, an action-conditioned egocentric world model that synthesizes photorealistic robot execution video from a single reference image plus a human operator's hand-pose stream, producing synchronized (video, robot-action) pairs without moving a physical robot. - Why it's important: Physical teleoperation ties every demonstration to operator time, specific hardware, and a fixed workspace, so scaling robot imitation-learning datasets is bottlenecked by manual resets and limited object/workspace diversity. - How it does it: A depth-modulated 2D skeletal rendering of 21-joint hand poses conditions a Video Diffusion Transformer via a zero-initialized, distribution-aligned additive patch-embedding branch, trained first on human egocentric video then fine-tuned on paired human-robot data, and finally distilled into a causal streaming student for 40 FPS generation. - The good: Policies (DP, pi_0, pi_0.5) trained purely on 300 RynnWorld-Teleop-generated episodes (zero real robot data) reach 82.86% success on Block Pushing and 77.14% on Bimanual Lifting, and augmenting 300 real episodes with 300 generated episodes raises pi_0.5's Lid Placement success from 42.86% to 62.86%. - The missing: All policy-transfer results come from a single robot platform (TIANJI M6 + WUJI hands) and four in-house tasks with no cross-embodiment or third-party benchmark evaluation, so generalization of the Sim2Real transfer claim beyond this specific hardware/task set is untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for roboticists working on imitation-learning data scarcity or action-conditioned video world models; the depth-aware conditioning and streaming distillation recipe are reusable even if the specific hardware setup is not.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (a built generative pipeline whose central claim is a deployable data-generation substitute for physical teleoperation). Practical Impact (78) dominates the composite and is well-earned: code, weights, and demo pages are released; the distilled causal student runs at 40 FPS on a single H100; and, most importantly, the artifact is validated by downstream real-robot policy success rather than by generative-fidelity proxies alone, with zero-real-data policies reaching 82.86% on Block Pushing against a 94.29% all-real baseline. Novelty (72) comes from the 'digital teleoperation' formulation — treating the operator's hand-pose stream as an embodiment-agnostic action label that survives IK retargeting, so one demonstration yields aligned (video, action) pairs for arbitrary robots — which is a sharper problem framing than either the human-centric world models (InterDyn, CosHand) or the passive human-to-robot translators (Phantom, H2R) it displaces. Non-Obviousness (70) reflects the distribution-aligned zero-init additive fusion, which is not a cosmetic choice: it beats concatenation by FVD 585 vs 1191, and removing the causal warm-up before DMD collapses PSNR from 22.25 to 14.26. Rigor (62) and Resilience (60) are the limits — no seeds or error bars anywhere, 35-trial single-run success rates, the Robotic-Test set is drawn from the same self-collected distribution used for training, OOD generalization is shown only qualitatively via t-SNE, and all policy transfer is on one proprietary platform with no cross-embodiment test despite retargeting-to-any-robot being the paradigm's core promise. Weighted: 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*78 = 70.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Robot imitation learning needs massive, diverse demonstration data, but physical teleoperation binds every demonstration to a specific robot, a fixed workspace, and manual environment resets, which caps data throughput and prevents capturing the long-tail distribution of real-world objects and interactions. Prior action-conditioned egocentric world models (Hand2World, GeneratedReality, InterDyn, CosHand) generate first-person video from human hand signals but remain human-centric, rendering a human hand rather than a robot end-effector, so the embodiment gap to robot execution is never bridged. Prior human-to-robot video translation methods (Phantom, Masquerade, X-Humanoid, Mitty, H2R) do cross the visual gap by overlaying robot renderings on human video, but are passive and observation-only, with no recoverable action signal for closed-loop control. Key Idea: Decouple data collection from physical robot hardware entirely by treating the operator's hand-pose stream as an embodiment-agnostic ground-truth action label, and using a generative world model to synthesize the egocentric video the robot would have produced had it executed the corresponding retargeted action. Because the pose stream is grounded in explicit joint-level geometry, it survives standard IK retargeting to any concrete robot embodiment, so the same demonstration yields aligned (video, action) pairs for arbitrary downstream robots. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Depth-aware action representation: 21-joint hand poses are rendered as 2D skeletal videos with camera-depth-modulated joint color and radius, then VAE-encoded into a control latent spatially and temporally aligned with the video latent. (2) Action-conditioned generation: built on the Wan-I2V Diffusion Transformer trained with conditional flow matching (Eq. 1-2), the control latent is fused into the video latent via a dedicated, zero-initialized patch-embedding branch gated by a learnable scalar and statistically aligned (mean/std normalization) to the video latent's distribution before fusion (Eq. 3) rather than simple concatenation, preserving the pretrained generative prior. (3) Progressive two-stage training: Stage 1 pretrains on 2.1M egocentric human video slices (VITRA + EgoDex) to learn hand-object interaction dynamics; Stage 2 fine-tunes on 1,800 paired human-robot teleoperation episodes (5.3K slices) with IK-mapped gestures to bridge the embodiment gap. (4) Streaming autoregressive distillation: the bidirectional teacher is distilled into a causal student with a fixed-size KV cache and a persistent sink token for the reference image, via a causal flow-matching warm-up (Eq. 4) followed by Distribution Matching Distillation (4-step sampling) with gradients backpropagated through the persisted KV cache across chunks. Design decisions: the additive zero-init/distribution-aligned fusion is directly ablated against concatenation and shown necessary (FVD 585 vs. 1191); the two-stage causal distillation curriculum is ablated and shown necessary (removing the causal warm-up before DMD causes training instability, FVD 1226 for the causal model, PSNR drops from 22.25 to 14.26); the choice of a fixed 81-frame chunk length with periodic re-anchoring to the true camera frame is a heuristic to bound autoregressive drift, justified qualitatively but not swept over chunk-length values. Key Results: On the EgoDex-Test human-domain benchmark, RynnWorld-Teleop (SFT variant) achieves PSNR 26.78, SSIM 0.887, LPIPS 0.119, FVD 550, beating action-conditioned baselines InterDyn (FVD 655), CosHand (FVD 1527), and Mask2IV (FVD 1650), and text-conditioned baselines Wan-2.2-TI2V-5B (FVD 1998) and CogVideoX-1.5-I2V-5B (FVD 2790). A vanilla SFT baseline (same base model, same data, no depth-aware/additive conditioning) trails badly (FVD 1223 vs. 585), isolating the architectural contribution from the extra fine-tuning data. The distilled causal student runs at 40.0 FPS on a single H100 with only moderate quality loss (FVD 1226 on EgoDex-Test, 1534 on Robotic-Test) versus the non-causal teacher (FVD 585 / 763). Downstream policy learning: augmenting 300 real demonstrations with 300 RynnWorld-Teleop-generated episodes raises success rates for DP, pi_0, and pi_0.5 across nearly all of 4 real-robot bimanual tasks (35 trials/task), with the largest gains on the hardest task, Lid Placement (pi_0: 34.29% to 54.29%; pi_0.5: 42.86% to 62.86%). Zero-real-data policies (pi_0 trained on 300 synthetic episodes only) reach 82.86% on Block Pushing and 77.14% on Bimanual Lifting, close to the 300-real-episode baselines (94.29% and 91.43%). All experiments use one robot platform (TIANJI M6 + WUJI hands) and four in-house tasks; there is no public/third-party benchmark comparison for the policy-transfer results. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (depth-aware skeletal conditioning + distribution-aligned additive fusion + streaming causal distillation combined into a single real-time action-conditioned world model) and NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION ("digital teleoperation" as a data-generation paradigm substituting a generative world model for a physical robot). The individual components (skeletal pose conditioning, DiT video diffusion, DMD distillation, IK retargeting) are each adaptations of existing techniques (MediaPipe/hand pose rendering, Wan-I2V, Yin et al. 2024 DMD, standard damped-least-squares IK); the novelty is in their specific combination and the depth-modulated rendering plus zero-init distribution-aligned fusion mechanism, which is empirically validated by ablation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and targeted at the paper's core design claims: fusion strategy (additive vs. concatenation), value of human pretraining, and the two-stage distillation curriculum, each showing large, consistent effect sizes. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for either the generative-quality metrics (Table 4) or the real-robot success rates (Table 3); each real-robot success rate is a single run of 35 trials per task, with no repeated-trial variance reported. Code, model weights, and demo pages are publicly released (GitHub, Hugging Face, ModelScope links given), which is a strong reproducibility positive for the generative model, though the 1,800-episode real-robot training dataset and the real-robot evaluation setup depend on proprietary in-house hardware (TIANJI M6, WUJI hands) that most labs cannot replicate. The Robotic-Test set (20 held-out sequences) is drawn from the same self-collected teleoperation distribution used for training, so it is an in-distribution robustness check rather than an independent benchmark; the EgoDex-Test benchmark is external. Feature-distribution overlap is assessed only qualitatively via t-SNE (Fig. 5), not with a quantitative distributional distance metric beyond FVD. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built generative pipeline evaluated by both video-quality metrics and downstream real-robot policy success). Practical: The causal student runs at 40+ FPS on a single H100 GPU, but the full training pipeline (pretraining + fine-tuning + two-stage distillation) uses 64 H100 GPUs, so training is not accessible outside well-resourced labs. Efficient: Inference is efficient once trained (25ms/frame, KV-cache-based causal attention), but the paper gives no comparison of total training compute/cost against alternative data-collection strategies (e.g., traditional simulation or more physical teleoperation hours). Effective: The evidence directly measures the stated problem via downstream policy success rates on real robot tasks, not just generative fidelity proxies, which is a methodological strength; however, effectiveness is shown only on tasks matching the training task distribution (Dual Picking, Block Pushing, Bimanual Lifting, Lid Placement), and OOD generalization (Fig. 7) is evaluated only qualitatively, without policy-success numbers on OOD scenes. Scalable: The paper claims the paradigm removes physical-collection bottlenecks and scales with "operator imagination,

#30 — Innovative (70)

ELDR: Expert-Locality-Aware Decode Routing for PD-Disaggregated MoE Serving

S Choi, S Cho, Y Xiong, Z Yang, Y Kwon, P Cheng · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ELDR, a decode-routing policy for prefill-decode (PD) disaggregated mixture-of-experts (MoE) serving that assigns each request to a decode worker based on a prefill-derived expert signature, colocating requests that share experts to shrink the number of distinct experts each decode worker must load per step. - Why it's important: MoE decode latency is set by the union of distinct experts a batch activates (memory-bandwidth bound), so a router that ignores expert overlap leaves a large, exploitable source of tail latency on the table in production PD deployments. - How it does it: ELDR builds a per-request expert signature from prefill-time per-expert activation counts (IDF-reweighted, layer-masked, L2-normalized), clusters signatures offline with Hungarian-balanced K-means into one region per decode worker, and routes online within a locality band that falls back to least-loaded-worker selection when the signature is ambiguous. - The good: Across three MoE models and two workloads on an 8-prefill/16-decode (8P16D) GPU testbed, ELDR cuts median TPOT (time-per-output-token) by 5.9-13.9% and tail TPOT by up to ~9% over the strongest of four load-balancing baselines, with model outputs bit-identical to standard routing. - The missing: The evaluation uses a single offline calibration fit per deployment and does not test workload drift within a run (only a cheap <10s re-fit is proposed, not evaluated under drift), and all latency numbers come from one hardware platform (AMD MI300X) and one serving stack (vLLM). - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or operating PD-disaggregated LLM serving infrastructure for MoE models; skippable for readers focused on dense-model serving or on MoE training/architecture rather than serving-system routing.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (75) are the drivers: recognizing that MoE decode cost is set by the union of distinct experts a batch activates, and that prefill activations predict decode activations well enough (Spearman 0.70-0.92) to route on, is a genuine new formulation rather than a recombination. Practical Impact (70) reflects a lossless, output-preserving routing layer with 0.86 ms per-request overhead that composes with prefix caching and EPLB, but no public repository link is given. Rigor (65) is held down by the total absence of error bars across Poisson-arrival runs, which matters when headline gains are 5.9-13.9%, and by calibration and evaluation sets drawn from the same benchmark corpora. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) suffer from the untested workload-drift scenario and the unexplained erosion of the gain at 235B expert-parallel scale (2.7-4.3%). Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*70 = 70.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In PD-disaggregated LLM serving, a decode router assigns each request to one of several decode workers after its prefill completes. For dense models, decode-worker load balancing is sufficient because every worker does equal per-token feed-forward work. For MoE models this assumption breaks: decode is memory-bandwidth bound, and its cost is set by the union of distinct experts a batch activates from HBM at each step, not by token count or request count. Existing decode routers (round-robin, join-shortest-queue, power-of-two-choices) balance load but are blind to which experts a request will activate, so they can scatter expert-similar requests across workers and inflate the per-step expert union unnecessarily. This is a problem for any operator running xPyD (x prefill, y decode workers) MoE deployments who wants lower time-per-output-token (TPOT) without retraining or modifying the model. Key Idea: Prefill-time expert activations are a leading indicator of decode-time expert activations (the paper measures Spearman correlation of 0.70-0.92 across three models), so a compact "expert signature" derived from prefill activation counts can be used to predict, before any decode token is generated, which requests will compete for the same experts during decode -- turning expert locality into a routable signal available exactly at the prefill-to-decode handoff. Methodology: The pipeline has an offline stage and an online stage. Offline: a calibration set of requests is run through prefill, and for each request a per-layer, per-expert token count is collected; each count is IDF-reweighted across the calibration corpus (downweighting generalist experts that fire on nearly every request, so they don't drown the rare specialists that distinguish workloads); a subset of layers is selected via greedy layer selection to maximize a signature-quality metric (defined below); the resulting vectors are L2-normalized to form the expert signature, and these signatures are clustered via Hungarian-balanced K-means into K equal-sized groups (one centroid per decode worker), enforcing a hard capacity constraint that standard K-means lacks. Online: each new request's expert signature (built the same way from its own prefill activations) is compared via cosine similarity to the K centroids; a "locality band" (width parameter τ=0.1) selects the subset of centroids within τ of the best match, and among those the least-loaded worker is chosen -- interpolating between pure nearest-centroid routing (τ=0) and pure load-balanced routing (τ=1). A signature quality criterion, Spearman rank correlation between signature-pair cosine distance and decode-pattern-pair cosine distance (Eq. 1), is used to justify each of the three signature design choices (discrete top-k counts over continuous gate scores/probabilities, IDF reweighting, and layer masking) via ablation on calibration data. A separate mechanism keeps signatures exact under prefix-KV-cache reuse: signatures are cached per KV-cache block (not per request) so partial cache hits, full hits, and evictions all recompute correctly by summing the relevant blocks' signatures. Design decisions: (1) using discrete top-k counts rather than continuous softmax/gate scores is justified empirically (Fig. 7, count-idf gives mean ρ=0.76 vs 0.47 for binary and lower for gate-prob/gate-logit); (2) IDF downweighting is justified by a 4.9pt worst-case ρ improvement concentrated on the model (GPT-OSS) where a few generalist experts dominate raw counts; (3) block-granular signature caching for prefix-cache coherence is a reasonable engineering solution but its correctness is asserted architecturally rather than tested under adversarial cache-eviction patterns. Key Results: On an 8P16D cluster (24 GPUs) with three MoE models (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-120B, Gemma-4-26B-A4B) and two workloads (task: 4-domain benchmark mix of 11,668 prompts; language: 14,000-prompt WildChat multilingual subset), ELDR reduces median TPOT by 7.0-13.9% (task) and 5.9-10.0% (language) over the best of four load-balancing baselines (Random, Round-Robin, JSQ, P2C), averaged across five request rates (20-100 req/s). Against a naive locality-only baseline ("Domain," which routes by oracle domain label rather than learned signature), ELDR still wins by 1.4-6.9% median and 1.6-4.5% tail on task, and by up to 9.5% median / 7.0-9.5% tail on language (where domain labels are a weaker proxy for expert usage). Runtime overhead is 0.86 ms per request (1.2% of 69 ms median TTFT), and the signature cache occupies under 1% of KV-cache memory. Design-validation experiments show: discrete top-k signatures outperform continuous gate-probability signatures by up to 14pp TPOT reduction; Hungarian-balanced K-means avoids tail-TPOT regressions of up to 17.4% seen with vanilla K-means; τ=0.1 avoids tail regressions seen at τ=0 across four of six workload cells; ELDR composes with prefix caching at negligible cost; and it generalizes across three topologies (8P8D, 8P16D, 8P24D) with the benefit growing monotonically as the decoder pool grows (8.0% to 10.2% mean median-TPOT reduction). A large-MoE scaling test on Qwen3-235B-A22B at 2P8D with tensor/expert parallelism (TP=4, EP=4, 40 GPUs) shows 2.7-4.3% median and 0.6-2.0% tail TPOT reduction, smaller than the single-GPU-per-decoder results, reflecting the added complexity of combining signature clustering with per-decoder expert-parallel load balancing. All baselines are load-balancing policies the paper itself identifies as the closest prior art (Random, RR, JSQ, P2C) plus a domain-oracle baseline; no comparison is made against any other expert-activation-aware routing method (e.g., cited related work like Bambhaniya et al. or Oncescu et al. on expert-activation batch-aware scheduling) because, per the related-work section, no prior system routes whole requests by prefill-derived expert locality specifically. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (expert locality as a decode-routing axis, orthogonal to load) + NEW METHOD (the expert signature construction: discrete IDF-weighted, layer-masked, prefill-derived, prefix-cache-coherent). The core novelty is treating decode-time expert overlap as a routable, predictable-from-prefill signal and building a lossless, cache-coherent mechanism to exploit it; the balanced K-means and locality-band routing components are adaptations of known techniques (Hungarian-balanced K-means, threshold-based JSQ/nearest-centroid interpolation) rather than new algorithms. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes targeted ablations for each signature design choice (Fig. 7: six signature transforms; Fig. 8: layer-selection sweep; Fig. 15: balanced vs. vanilla K-means; Fig. 16: four τ values; Fig. 17: prefix-cache on/off) and reports results across three models, two workloads, and three-to-four topologies, which is a broader sweep than most single-technique serving papers. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed/multiple-run variance are reported for any of the main latency figures (Figs. 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18) despite Poisson-arrival request generation that should induce run-to-run variance; whiskers in Fig. 7 show min/max across only 6 cells, not statistical significance. The signature-quality metric ρ is validated on the same calibration data used to fit the offline layer mask and clustering, and the 1,000-prompt calibration set per cell is disjoint from the serving evaluation set, which mitigates but does not eliminate a same-distribution overfitting concern (the calibration and serving sets are drawn from the same benchmark mixture, not from an independently sourced holdout distribution). No code or artifact release URL is stated in the paper. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a routing layer implemented in vLLM and evaluated by deployment on a real GPU cluster). Practical: Yes -- implemented as ~2,000 lines of Python integrated as a routing proxy plus lightweight prefill-side hooks on top of an unmodified vLLM/NIXL PD stack, tested on commercially available AMD MI300X hardware. Efficient: Overhead is 0.86 ms per request (1.2% of median TTFT) and signature cache is under 1% of HBM devoted to KV cache, a small marginal cost. Effective: The paper measures TPOT and TTFT directly (not a proxy) across five request rates and reports a corroborating mechanism-level measurement (mean active experts per decode step reduced 22.0% on average, Fig. 13), which supports that the latency reduction is via the claimed mechanism rather than incidental. Scalable: Tested at up to 40 GPUs / 235B-parameter model with expert parallelism, and the topology sweep (8P8D to 8P24D) shows the benefit growing with pool size rather than degrading, but the largest configuration tested is a fixed single scale-up point (not a systematic 10x-100x scaling curve), so extrapolation beyond 40 GPUs / 235B is a claim, not a measurement. Delivers a software artifact: a vLLM-integrated routing layer (~2,000 new lines), though no public repository link is given in the paper. Strengths: - Identifies and empirically substantiates (Figs. 1-4) a genuine gap in existing PD routers: they treat decode workers as interchangeable for MoE, ignoring the union-of-experts cost structure that governs decode latency, and quantifies the effect (4.7x latency growth from 16 to 128 active experts at fixed batch size, Fig. 2). - The signature design is validated against an explicit, principled criterion (rank correlation between signature distance and decode-pattern distance) rather than tuned purely against end-to-end TPOT, which makes the ablations (Figs. 7, 8) interpretable as isolating the representation quality itself. - The prefix-cache-coherence mechanism (block-granular signature caching, Fig. 10) solves a real deployment obstacle (partial cache hits desynchronizing the signature from the actual prompt) that a naive per-request signature cache would silently break, and the solution reuses the existing KV-cache lifecycle rather than adding new state. - The method is lossless with respect to model outputs (only changes which decode worker serves a request, not any expert selection), which removes a major deployment-adoption barrier relative to approximate load-balancing techniques cited in related work (e.g., dropping low-importance experts, sharing experts across a batch). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper states a single offline calibration fit is used throughout the study and frames re-fitting as "cheap" (under 10s) for scale-up/scale-down or workload drift, but does not evaluate drift scenarios in the main results, effectively scoping the evaluation to a static workload distribution matching calibration. It also explicitly notes ELDR's inter-worker balancing composes with, rather than replaces, intra-worker expert-parallel balancing methods like EPLB, and demonstrates this compatibility only at the 235B scale. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not report variance/error bars across repeated runs at a given request rate, despite Poisson-distributed arrivals; a single 120s hold per rate could mask run-to-run noise in the 5-14% headline gains. The calibration and evaluation sets are drawn from the same underlying benchmark corpora (same four task domains, same WildChat corpus), so the reported ρ and TPOT gains may not transfer to genuinely out-of-distribution production traffic (e.g., topics or languages absent from calibration) -- this is a narrower generalization test than an operator would need before deploying. Comparisons are restricted to load-balancing baselines and one naive domain-oracle baseline; no other expert-locality-aware method is benchmarked even though the related-work section cites several adjacent expert-activation-aware systems (Bambhaniya et al., Oncescu et al.), leaving it unclear how much of ELDR's edge over "Domain" specifically comes from its learned signature versus general engineering quality. The 235B expert-parallel result showing a notably smaller gain (2.7-4.3% vs. 5.9-13.9% at smaller scale) suggests the technique's benefit may erode as expert-parallel sharding grows, but the paper does not explore intermediate EP degrees to characterize this trend. Bottom Line: Operators running PD-disaggregated MoE inference at production scale gain a concrete, low-overhead (sub-1% latency cost), output-preserving routing technique with consistent 6-14% median TPOT reductions across three real open MoE models, and no directly competing expert-locality-aware decode router is benchmarked or, per the paper's own related-work survey, appears to exist yet.
Citations 2607.00466 PDF
#31 — Innovative (69)

Predict, Reuse, and Repair: Accelerating Dynamic Sparse Attention for Long-Context LLM Decoding

T Wang, G Rattihalli, A Dhakal, J Li, Z Ren, D Milojicic… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PRR, a speculative-execution runtime that overlaps dynamic sparse attention (DSA) computation with the top-K block-selection stage, using an EMA-based predictor to speculate likely blocks and a custom FlashAttention-derived kernel to incrementally repair mispredicted blocks. - Why it's important: DSA's selection-to-attention dependency consumes 60-71% of per-layer decoding latency as context length grows from 16K to 512K tokens, and no existing inference engine (vLLM, SGLang) or attention kernel (FlashAttention) exposes an interface to incrementally patch a partially computed attention result, forcing full recomputation on any misprediction. - How it does it: An exponential-moving-average predictor tracks per-block importance score trajectories (calibrated per-prompt via grid search over prefill compressed-attention scores) to anticipate the top-K set one step ahead, while a custom CUDA kernel folds only the missed blocks into the existing FlashAttention online-softmax accumulator via log-sum-exp rescaling. - The good: The EMA predictor achieves 97.5-98.9% overlap with the true top-K set across six LLMs and five benchmarks (Table 7), versus only ~65-70% for naive previous-step reuse, which is the mechanism that makes speculation actually pay off. - The missing: All results are single-request (batch size 1); the paper explicitly does not evaluate cross-request batching, which is the standard production serving regime and where speculative KV migration and repair-kernel scheduling could behave very differently. - Read it or not: Worth reading for systems researchers and practitioners building long-context LLM inference stacks who already use or are evaluating Quest/InfLLM-v2-style dynamic sparse attention; the core repair-kernel idea (patching a FlashAttention accumulator rather than recomputing) generalizes beyond this specific predictor and is the most transferable contribution.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Non-Obviousness (80) is the standout: adapting FlashAttention's online-softmax recurrence to merge only missed blocks into an existing partial accumulator (rather than recomputing on any misprediction) is a genuinely non-obvious kernel-level insight, and the ablation shows it is what converts a 1.00-1.04x naive-reuse speedup into 1.28-1.56x. Practical Impact (75) reflects released CUDA kernel and runtime code, plus a separately useful 2.11-2.31x win over FlashInfer's BlockSparseAttention. Rigor (65) is dragged down by single-run point estimates with no seeds and, more seriously, by the 'preserves downstream task accuracy' claim being asserted mathematically but never measured with an actual benchmark table. Completeness (60) and Resilience (60) are limited by batch-size-1-only evaluation: the entire speculation argument rests on idle GPU headroom that may vanish under the multi-request batching that defines production serving, which the authors flag but do not test. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*80 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*75 = 69.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-context LLM decoding (deep research, multi-step reasoning, tool-using agents) requires scanning a growing KV cache at every generated token. Dynamic sparse attention reduces this cost by selecting only the top-K most relevant KV blocks per query per layer, but the block-selection step must complete before attention can run, since block identities are unknown until compressed-attention scores are computed. This serialized selection-to-attention dependency is measured (on GLM-4-9B with Quest and InfLLM-v2 on H100 GPUs) to consume 60% of per-layer decoding latency at 16K context and 71% at 512K, becoming the dominant cost as context grows. No existing serving engine (vLLM, SGLang) or attention kernel (FlashAttention, FlashInfer's BlockSparseAttention) supports incrementally correcting a partial attention result, so any speculative approach that mispredicts even one block would otherwise require full recomputation, which negates speculative benefits. Key Idea: The paper's central insight is that DSA block selections exhibit strong temporal locality (68% average block-overlap across consecutive decoding steps, measured across five benchmarks and two DSA methods) combined with substantial idle GPU headroom (SM/L2/DRAM bandwidth utilization below 40% at up to 512K context), which together create room to speculatively compute attention over predicted blocks while the true selection is still being computed, provided mispredictions can be repaired rather than triggering full recomputation. The repair mechanism, exploiting FlashAttention's online-softmax recurrence to merge only the missed blocks into an existing partial accumulator via running max/log-sum-exp rescaling (Equations 6-8), is the enabling technical trick. Methodology: PRR's pipeline has three components operating per transformer layer during decoding. (1) Prediction: an EMA-based predictor maintains per-block state (smoothed level ℓ and trend estimate v) updated from true importance scores IS produced by the DSA's compressed-attention selection stage at each step (Equations 2-3), predicting a speculative block set P bounded by a budget ratio δ (|P| ≤ δ|A|). (2) Calibration: hyperparameters (α, β, γ) are tuned per-prompt during the prefill phase via grid search (80 candidates: α∈{0.2,...,0.8}, β∈{0.1,...,0.5}, γ∈{0,...,0.75}) maximizing a score-weighted hit-rate objective (Equation 4) computed on the prefill's own compressed-attention score trajectory, at a reported cost of 0.06-0.28 ms non-overlapped critical-path latency. (3) Speculative-and-repair execution: a custom CUDA kernel runs speculative attention over P in parallel with true top-K selection, writing not just output O but also the running denominator ℓ and max m to HBM; once the true set A is known, a second kernel processes only the missed blocks M = A\P and merges them into the existing accumulator via online-softmax rescaling, guaranteeing output identical to standard (non-speculative) DSA. The speculation budget δ is chosen dynamically per context length via an offline profiling lookup table (built per model/hardware/DSA configuration) rather than fixed statically, to keep speculative attention latency within the selection window. Design decision quality: the choice to minimize |M| (missed blocks) rather than symmetrically penalize wasted blocks |N| = |P\A| is justified via the asymmetric-cost argument (missed blocks extend the critical path; wasted blocks only consume idle headroom), which is well-supported by the utilization profiling in Section 2.2. The correctness-preserving guarantee (output is provably identical to standard DSA regardless of prediction quality) is a strong and well-justified design choice, since it eliminates accuracy as a tuning variable. Key Results: On six LLMs (GLM-4-9B, GLM-Z1-9B, DeepSeek-R1-8B, Llama3-8B, Qwen3-14B, Qwen3-32B) across five benchmarks (LongBench, InfiniteBench, RULER, AIME, MATH500) with two DSA backends (Quest, InfLLM-v2) on H100 GPUs, PRR achieves average per-token decoding speedups of 1.28-1.42x under Quest and 1.31-1.56x under InfLLM-v2 relative to serial DSA execution (Table 1), with a peak of 1.56x (GLM-4 on InfLLM-v2). The EMA predictor achieves 97.5-98.9% overlap with the true top-K set (Table 7), versus 63.6-69.8% for naive previous-step reuse (Table 5). The custom sparse-attention kernel outperforms FlashInfer's BlockSparseAttention by 2.11-2.31x average speedup (up to 3.71x at 8K token budget) across block sizes 16-256 under a GQA configuration (32 query heads, 8 KV heads, head dim 128, 128K context). An ablation (Table 2/8) isolates each component's contribution: naive reuse alone (S1) yields only 1.00-1.04x, adding the repair kernel (S2) raises this to 1.18-1.32x, and adding the EMA predictor (S3, full PRR) reaches 1.28-1.56x. Baselines compared are the standard serial DSA execution and FlashInfer's BlockSparseAttention, both of which are current, actively maintained systems rather than outdated. Evaluation is at "stress-scale" context lengths (up to 512K tokens for utilization profiling; primary speedup evaluation appears to use LongBench/InfiniteBench/RULER contexts which extend into the 100K+ range). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the EMA-based per-block predictor and the incremental-repair kernel) and ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (the speculative-attention runtime and critical-path-aware budget scheduling). The genuinely novel piece is the correctness-preserving repair mechanism, adapting FlashAttention's online-softmax recurrence to merge missed blocks into a partial accumulator, since prior speculative approaches (cited: FlexiCache, LouisKV, AsyncSpade) either drop information, operate at a different granularity (cross-head vs. cross-step temporal locality), or lack an interface for incremental correction. The EMA predictor itself is a standard, well-known lightweight time-series technique; its contribution here is the per-prompt calibration procedure (grid search against prefill compressed-attention trajectories), not the underlying EMA formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and are the strongest part of the rigor story: Table 2/8 decomposes speedup contribution across three cumulative stages (reuse-only, +repair kernel, +EMA predictor) across all six models and five benchmarks under both DSA backends, showing a consistent monotonic trend. No error bars, multiple random seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper; all reported numbers (speedups, overlap rates, utilization) appear to be single-run point estimates. Code is released (GitHub: Tianyu9748/Incremental_FlashAttention, stated in the abstract), which supports reproducibility of the kernel and runtime, though the paper does not state whether profiling scripts or the full evaluation harness are included. Downstream task accuracy is claimed to be preserved ("while preserving downstream task accuracy") but no accuracy numbers, benchmarks, or tables are shown to substantiate this claim; the correctness argument rests entirely on the mathematical guarantee (full top-K coverage via repair) rather than empirical accuracy measurement, which is a claim-versus-evidence gap. Evaluation is entirely on the authors' own profiling/timing setup with the DSA methods the authors implemented (Quest, InfLLM-v2 as re-implemented under a testbed with FlashInfer's BlockSparseAttention backend); there is no independent replication. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a runtime and CUDA kernel evaluated by deployment/latency measurement). Practical: Yes, evaluated on commercially available NVIDIA H100 GPUs with standard CUDA 12.8 tooling, and the design explicitly targets not exceeding idle GPU headroom rather than requiring new hardware. Efficient: The approach adds negligible overhead (0.06-0.28 ms calibration cost per prefill; profiling sweep takes under 5 minutes per model) relative to the 30-40% latency reduction achieved, and it exploits otherwise-idle memory bandwidth/compute rather than adding new resource contention. Effective: The evidence (Tables 1, 2, 7) directly measures the target problem (per-token decoding latency) rather than a proxy, and the EMA hit-rate (Table 4/7) is measured against the true DSA top-K set, so evidence is not proxy-only; however, the "preserves downstream task accuracy" claim is asserted, not measured with actual task benchmarks in this paper. Scalable: The paper tests only batch size 1 (explicitly acknowledged as a limitation) and only up to 512K context for utilization profiling; the authors explicitly state that multi-request batching, which is the standard production regime, is unevaluated and left to future work, so the 10x-100x scaling question (e.g., under concurrent request batching) is untested, not merely claimed. The work delivers a software artifact (a CUDA kernel plus a full runtime, released as open-source code) rather than stopping at paper form, though the released code covers the specific GQA/Hopper/half-precision configuration evaluated (per the Limitations section) and may need porting for other architectures. Strengths: - The correctness-preserving repair kernel (Equations 6-8) is a genuine systems-level insight: adapting FlashAttention's online-softmax recurrence to merge only missed blocks into an existing partial accumulator is a non-obvious extension that avoids the standard fallback (full recomputation on any misprediction), and it is the component that converts a modest 1.0-1.04x naive-reuse speedup into the full 1.28-1.56x result. - The per-prompt calibration procedure (Section 4.2) that fits EMA hyperparameters against the prefill's own compressed-attention score trajectory before any decoding happens is a well-engineered way to make a training-free predictor prompt-adaptive without adding a neural component or extra model execution cost. - The empirical motivation (Section 2) is unusually thorough for a systems paper: three separate observations (selection-to-attention dependency magnitude, cross-step block overlap rate, GPU utilization headroom) are each independently measured before the design is proposed, giving a solid empirical foundation for the two design choices (minimize missed blocks; enable incremental repair). - The custom sparse-attention kernel's 2.11-2.31x average speedup over FlashInfer's BlockSparseAttention (Table 3) is a competent, separately-useful engineering contribution independent of the speculative-repair idea. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state four limitations directly: (1) they only evaluate training-free DSA methods (Quest, InfLLM-v2), excluding trainable mechanisms like NSA due to training cost; (2) the kernel is only optimized for NVIDIA Hopper architecture and half precision, not extended to other architectures (e.g., Blackwell) or lower-bit precisions (e.g., fp8); (3) the main paper presents results only for GLM-4-9B due to page limits, with other-LLM results relegated to the appendix; (4) evaluation is restricted to batch size 1, with multi-request batching coordination left to future work. This is a direct and reasonably complete self-assessment of scope boundaries; the batch-size-1 limitation in particular is the most consequential omission given that production serving is batched, and the authors flag it plainly rather than downplaying it. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No accuracy numbers are shown anywhere despite the abstract's claim of "preserving downstream task accuracy" — the correctness argument is purely mathematical (the repair guarantees identical output to standard DSA), but this doesn't verify that the underlying DSA methods themselves (Quest, InfLLM-v2) or the paper's re-implementation are bug-free; an empirical accuracy table (e.g., LongBench scores with/without PRR) would have been cheap to include and materially strengthen the "preserving accuracy" claim. - The speculative attention computation itself consumes real GPU compute and bandwidth (the "wasted blocks" N = P\A), and while Section 2.2's utilization profiling shows headroom exists at batch size 1, there is no evidence this headroom persists under realistic multi-request serving loads, which is precisely the setting the authors flag as unevaluated; this makes the reported speedups potentially optimistic for production deployment. - The offline profiling-table approach for choosing δ (Section 4.4) requires a one-time sweep "for each model-hardware-DSA configuration,
Recommended 2606.30389 PDF
#32 — Innovative (69)

RaBitQCache: Rotated Binary Quantization for KVCache in Long Context LLM Inference

W Li, J Dong, H Zhang, W Shi, W Lu, X Du · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RaBitQCache, a sparse-attention framework that quantizes KV-cache keys to a randomly rotated 1-bit codebook and scores query-key relevance via an INT4-times-binary dot product, enabling adaptive Top-p token retrieval with a proven error bound instead of a fixed Top-k token budget. - Why it's important: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by KV-cache memory I/O, and existing sparse-attention methods either use fixed token budgets that waste compute or rely on proxy scores with no accuracy guarantee, so a method that adapts the retrieval budget per-query while bounding its own error directly attacks both the cost and the risk of KV-cache offloading. - How it does it: It applies a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style random orthogonal rotation to centered query/key vectors, snaps rotated keys to the nearest vertex of a binary hypercube (RaBitQ-style quantization), and proves the resulting binary-codeword dot product, corrected by a per-token scalar factor, is an unbiased O(1/sqrt(D)) estimator of the true cosine similarity, cheap enough to scan via an INT4 x binary GEMV kernel. - The good: On LongBench with LLaMA-3.1-8B, RaBitQCache reaches 50.6 (90.7% recall) at a 17.33% token budget, beating the Oracle upper-bound retrieval-recall baseline (89.3% recall at 11.42% budget) and outperforming Quest, DS, and SparQ at comparable or lower budgets, with a measured 2.16x end-to-end latency speedup and up to 3.88x decode-phase speedup at 30k context. - The missing: All latency and speedup numbers are reported only up to 30k-32k context length (Figures 2-3), so the claimed benefit for 128k+ contexts, the paper's own motivating regime, is extrapolated rather than measured; there is also no evaluation of accuracy or index-construction cost at that scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating KV-cache sparsification systems, particularly for the theoretical error-bound derivation (Appendix A) that other proxy-score methods lack; engineers focused purely on wall-clock throughput may find the 25-page proof apparatus more than they need and can skip to Sections 3-5.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (70) come from reframing KV-token selection as a Johnson-Lindenstrauss approximate-nearest-neighbor problem, which yields an unbiased O(1/sqrt(D)) estimator of the true cosine similarity and is precisely what licenses adaptive Top-p retrieval instead of the rigid Top-k budgets every prior proxy-score method is stuck with; the RaBitQ machinery itself is adapted from Gao & Long rather than invented here. Rigor (70) is supported by full proofs in Appendix A, nine baselines run from their official repos, and a released implementation, but undercut by the complete absence of error bars across accuracy comparisons where 1-2 point gaps (50.63 vs 50.34) are treated as real. Resilience (65) takes a hit because the paper's motivating regime is 128K+ context while all latency measurements stop at 30k-32k, so the central efficiency claim is extrapolated at the scale that matters most; the authors' own disclosure that key/query vectors may be clustered rather than uniform on the hypersphere also threatens the uniformity assumption behind the bound. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 = 69.
