empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-context LLM serving is bottlenecked by KV-cache memory bandwidth: self-attention cost grows quadratically with sequence length, and the KV cache itself consumes GPU memory linearly, limiting achievable context windows (2K-4K historically, now 128K+) on fixed hardware. Prior sparse-attention work exploits the empirical observation that only a small subset of cached tokens materially affects attention output, but existing dynamic selection methods have two flaws the paper targets directly: their proxy scores for token importance are inaccurate (Quest's page-level selection retrieves irrelevant tokens due to discrete importance distributions; DS's partial-dimension estimation deviates from the true distribution) and none of them carries a theoretical error bound, which forces all of them into rigid fixed-token-budget (Top-k) retrieval rather than adaptive, per-query budgets (Top-p) that would track the true, layer- and head-varying sparsity of attention. Key Idea: Reframe the search for important KV tokens as a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style approximate nearest-neighbor problem: apply a shared random orthogonal rotation to centered query and key vectors, then quantize the rotated keys to +-1/sqrt(D) hypercube vertices (1 bit per dimension). The rotation, combined with the classic result that random high-dimensional unit vectors are nearly orthogonal, makes the binary-codeword dot product an unbiased estimator of the true cosine similarity with a provable O(1/sqrt(D)) error bound, in contrast to prior proxy scores that only preserve relative token ordering. Methodology: The pipeline has two phases. Prefill/index construction (Algorithm 1): compute a batch centroid c_k, center and normalize each key vector, apply the fixed random rotation P (generated once at model init), take the sign of each rotated dimension to form a D-bit binary codeword C_b per token, and compute a scalar correction factor alpha per token (the inner product of the reconstructed codeword with the rotated key). Decode (Algorithm 2): for each new query, center/rotate it the same way, quantize it to an INT4 vector via uniform scalar quantization, compute the binary-index-times-INT4 dot product with a custom GEMV CUDA kernel, reconstruct the normalized similarity by dividing by alpha, then run an adaptive Top-p selection (Algorithm 3, a ternary-search value-partitioning scheme replacing O(L log L) sorting with linear-time bounds tightening) to pick the minimal token set covering probability mass p, and finally run attention over the selected sparse tokens plus a local sliding window. Three design decisions stand out: (1) centering on prefill-phase centroids rather than per-batch statistics, justified by the claim that decode-phase queries don't materially shift the centroid, empirically validated by only a 0.38-point score drop when re-centering is ablated (63.63->50.25 on LLaMA-3.1-8B) — a modest but real effect the paper is transparent about; (2) INT4 query quantization, justified via Theorem A.2 showing B_q=Omega(log log D) suffices to keep this error below the estimator's own O(1/sqrt(D)) floor; (3) asynchronous pipelining of index construction behind the O(L^2) dense prefill attention and lazy batched index updates during decode, which are engineering choices (not derived) but are directly measured (<10% prefill overhead, Figure 2a). Key Results: On LongBench (13 tasks, averaged) with LongChat-7B-v1.5-32k and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, RaBitQCache at p=0.95 (17.33% average budget) scores 36.21/50.6, versus Full Attention 35.78/50.58 and an Oracle upper bound of 35.84/50.31 at 11.4% budget — RaBitQCache slightly exceeds Full Attention on LLaMA-3.1-8B despite using less than a fifth of the cache. Against fixed-budget baselines at comparable cost, Quest-4096 (40.29% budget) reaches 36.28/86.6% recall on LongChat, marginally ahead of RaBitQCache's 89.85% recall but at more than double the token budget. Against five more baselines (MagicPIG, PyramidKV, SnapKV, PQCache, KIVI) on LLaMA-3.1-8B, RaBitQCache posts the highest average LongBench score (50.63 vs. 44.91-50.34). On RULER (LLaMA-3.1-70B, 8K-64K) RaBitQCache matches or exceeds Full Attention at 8K-32K while using only 1076-3581 tokens as context grows, and on GSM8K it matches DS/SparQ's 0.77 accuracy while achieving the highest attention recall (0.888) among sparse baselines. End-to-end latency: 2.16x speedup over full-precision vLLM+FlashAttention-2 baseline (Figure 3), with the Int4DotBinary kernel itself reaching up to 3.43x speedup over a naive CUDA implementation (Table 5). All experiments run on NVIDIA Hopper GPUs; baselines use their official repos with recommended configurations, which is a reasonably strong and current comparison set (Quest, DS, SparQ, MagicPIG, PyramidKV, SnapKV, PQCache, KIVI, all 2023-2025). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the rotated-binary-quantization proxy score and its unbiased-estimator derivation) plus NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the kernel-level GEMV, Top-p, and pipelining implementation). The core inner-product-decomposition and codebook-quantization machinery is adapted directly from RaBitQ (Gao & Long, 2024), originally developed for approximate nearest-neighbor search; the genuine novelty is applying and re-deriving this machinery for the query-key attention-score setting (with query-side INT4 quantization, adaptive Top-p sampling via a new ternary-search kernel, and prefill/decode system co-design), rather than a wholly new quantization theory. Rigor & Reproducibility: The core theoretical claims (Theorem A.1 unbiasedness and O(1/sqrt(D)) error, Theorem A.2 on quantization bit-width) are proven in full in Appendix A with explicit Hoeffding-inequality and tail-bound arguments, a genuine strength relative to prior proxy-score methods that offer no guarantees. Empirically, results are reported as single numbers per task/method with no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed reporting anywhere in the main text or appendix tables, which is a real gap for a paper making comparative accuracy claims against nine baselines. An ablation on centroid re-centering is reported (Table 12, appendix) and ablation on threshold p sensitivity (Figure 4) is included. Code is stated to be released at a GitHub URL (Sakuraaa0/RaBitQCache). Baselines are evaluated on their own recommended configurations from official repositories, avoiding a straw-man comparison. No mention of held-out test contamination or benchmark-specific tuning of p (a single p=0.95 or p=0.9 is applied per benchmark globally), which is reasonable practice but not stress-tested per-task. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built inference system evaluated via deployment-style latency and accuracy benchmarks). Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA Hopper GPUs via a vLLM+FlashInfer+LMCache integration (~5000 lines of code), using only commercially available hardware. Efficient: Index overhead is approximately 3.5% of full KV-cache memory for FP16/D=128 (Appendix C.1) and the binary-INT4 kernel achieves 2.27x-3.43x speedup over naive GEMV, translating to 2.16x measured end-to-end latency reduction. Effective: Evidence is attention-recall and downstream task-score based (not merely proxy-score correlation), showing near-full-attention quality at large budget reductions across LongBench, RULER, and GSM8K, which is a reasonably direct measure of the stated problem. Scalable: Tested contexts top out at 30k-32k for latency figures and 64K for RULER; the paper's motivating claim is 128K+ context support, but no experiment reaches that scale, so scalability beyond ~64K is asserted, not demonstrated. Strengths: - The unbiased-estimator proof (Theorem A.1) with an explicit O(1/sqrt(D)) error bound is a genuine theoretical contribution missing from comparable proxy-score sparse-attention methods (Quest, DS, SnapKV), and it is what enables principled adaptive Top-p retrieval rather than fixed Top-k. - The linear-time ternary-search Top-p kernel (Algorithm 3) replacing an O(L log L) sort is a concrete systems contribution with a measurable payoff, evidenced in the time-breakdown analysis (Figure 5) showing token-selection overhead is negligible relative to attention compute savings. - The asynchronous pipelining of index construction behind dense prefill attention, plus lazy batched decode updates, is a well-motivated hardware-aware design that the paper backs with a direct <10% prefill overhead measurement rather than a claim alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly flag, in the Impact Statement, that the method still carries a theoretical possibility of information loss despite the proven error bounds, and recommend caution for high-stakes applications (medical, legal document review) requiring absolute precision. They also flag in Section 5.4 that recent work (Xing et al., 2026) suggests key/query vectors are tightly clustered rather than uniformly distributed on the hypersphere in practice, which could invalidate the uniformity assumption underlying their O(1/sqrt(D)) bound, and they explicitly leave "a more rigorous theoretical treatment of these settings" as future work rather than glossing over it. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No error bars or multiple-seed results anywhere despite comparing against nine baselines on accuracy-sensitive benchmarks; differences of 1-2 points (e.g., RaBitQCache 50.63 vs. PQCache 50.34 in Table 3) are reported as if deterministic. - All latency/speedup experiments stop at 30k-32k tokens; the paper's stated motivation is 128K+ contexts, so the central efficiency claim is unverified at the scale that matters most. - Combining RaBitQCache's adaptive retrieval with KIVI-style low-bit compression of the retained tokens is explicitly named by the authors as "future work,
Search Results 2606.31519 PDF
#33 — Innovative (69)

The Mirage of Optimizing Training Policies: Monotonic Inference Policies as the Real Objective for LLM Reinforcement Learning (140 ▲ )

Jing Liang, Hongyao Tang, Yi Ma, Yancheng He, Weixun Wang, Xiaoyang Li, Ju Huang, Wenbo Su, Jinyi Liu, Yan Zheng, Jianye Hao, Bo Zheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Monotonic Inference Policy Update (MIPU), a two-step LLM RL framework that constructs a sampler-referenced candidate training update (Step 1) and then accepts or rolls back the resulting synchronized inference policy based on an estimated post-update inference gap (Step 2), rather than judging updates solely by training-side surrogate improvement. - Why it's important: LLM RL pipelines split rollout generation (inference engine, e.g. vLLM/SGLang) from gradient computation (training engine, e.g. FSDP/Megatron), and even with synchronized parameters these engines assign different probabilities to the same trajectories, so a training-side gradient step can improve the training policy while silently degrading the policy actually deployed for inference. - How it does it: The paper decomposes inference-policy improvement J(mu_{k+1}) - J(mu_k) into three additive terms (post-update inference gap, training-side update, pre-update inference gap), optimizes the latter two terms with a truncated importance-sampling GRPO surrogate (TIS-style, factoring the ratio into pre-update mismatch times current update and clipping the mismatch weight at w_max=2), then estimates the first term after synchronization via validation rollouts under the new inference policy and rejects candidates whose estimated gap falls below a (linearly annealed) tolerance -c_t. - The good: Under FP8-quantized rollout, a high-mismatch setting, MIPU is the only method among Baseline GRPO, MIS, and LR-decay that avoids collapse or sharp degradation over ~800 training steps on both Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-1.7B, while also posting the highest average pass@1 (66.71% vs. 65.66% for the best baseline on 4B; 53.97% vs. 52.23% on 1.7B). - The missing: Every experiment relies on a single artificially induced mismatch source (FP8-quantized rollout on H100s) on two moderate-scale models (1.7B and 4B parameters) with one training corpus per model; there is no test under naturally occurring BF16 training-inference mismatch (the more common production setting) or at larger model scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or debugging RLVR pipelines that split training and inference engines, since it reframes training-inference mismatch as an objective-misalignment problem (wrong policy is being improved) rather than purely a numerical-precision problem; skip if the interest is RL theory in the classical single-policy setting, since MIPU offers no formal monotonic-improvement guarantee, only an empirical risk-reduction heuristic.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (78) carry this: the three-term telescoping decomposition of inference-policy improvement reframes training-inference mismatch from a numerical-precision nuisance into an objective-misalignment problem, and the observation that J(pi_{k+1}) >= J(pi_k) does not imply J(mu_{k+1}) >= J(mu_k) is a clean formal point the entire TIS/MIS line of work missed. Rigor (60) is the drag: no seeds or error bars on the headline Table 1 numbers, which is a serious omission for a method whose central claim is about stabilizing a high-variance process, and the tolerance-schedule hyperparameters appear tuned on the same runs that produce the results. Completeness (68) and Resilience (65) are helped by the honest negative result (stricter acceptance thresholds are not monotonically safer) and the explicit admission that MIPU offers no formal guarantee, but hurt by testing only an artificially amplified FP8-rollout mismatch rather than the common BF16 production regime. Impact (60) reflects roughly doubled per-step rollout cost with no wall-clock accounting. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*60 = 69.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 68 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM RL post-training (GRPO, DAPO, and related RLVR recipes) separates rollout generation, handled by an inference engine such as vLLM or SGLang, from gradient computation, handled by a training engine such as FSDP or Megatron, to keep throughput acceptable at LLM scale. Even when the two engines hold identical parameters after synchronization, differences in precision, kernel implementation, and decoding backend cause them to assign different probabilities to the same generated trajectory. This training-inference mismatch has been documented by several concurrent works (TIS, MIS, learning-rate decay, FP16-rollout fixes) as a source of training instability and collapse. Those remedies all treat the mismatch as noise on the training side to be filtered, clipped, or damped. The paper's motivating claim is that this framing overlooks the deployment target: the policy that actually serves users is the inference policy mu, not the training policy pi, so an update that provably increases the training-side surrogate objective does not logically imply the synchronized inference policy improves; formally J(pi_{k+1}) - J(pi_k) >= 0 does not imply J(mu_{k+1}) - J(mu_k) >= 0. Key Idea: The performance-difference identity is applied twice in a telescoping decomposition: J(mu_{k+1}) - J(mu_k) = [J(mu_{k+1}) - J(pi_{k+1})] + [J(pi_{k+1}) - J(pi_k)] + [J(pi_k) - J(mu_k)], i.e. post-update inference gap + training-side update + pre-update inference gap. The middle term is what canonical LLM RL (GRPO/PPO) already optimizes; the paper's contribution is to treat the first term, the gap introduced by resynchronizing to the (mismatched) inference engine, as an explicit, measurable acceptance criterion rather than an unmodeled residual. This turns policy improvement into a two-stage propose-then-validate procedure, conceptually similar to a trust-region accept/reject test but defined on the inference policy's realized behavior instead of the training policy's surrogate objective. Methodology: Step 1 (sampler-referenced policy update) targets the combined last-two decomposition terms J(pi_{k+1}) - J(mu_k). The paper shows this reduces to a GRPO-style clipped surrogate weighted by a sampler-referenced correction, and analyzes three implementations in Appendix B: PPO-IS (clips the full trainer-to-sampler ratio pi_theta/mu_k directly, which conflates pre-existing mismatch with the current update and makes the trust region overly conservative), Vanilla-IS (factors the ratio into pre-update mismatch pi_k/mu_k times current update ratio pi_theta/pi_k, clips only the current-update term, but the unbounded mismatch weight can amplify gradient variance), and TIS (the adopted variant: truncates the mismatch weight at w_max=2 while clipping only the current update). Step 2 (inference-gap-aware acceptance) estimates the post-update gap T_post = J(mu_{k+1}) - J(pi_{k+1}) after synchronizing the candidate to the inference engine. Because this quantity requires the advantage under the not-yet-realized pi_{k+1}, the paper substitutes a reverse-identity proxy anchored at mu_{k+1} and estimated from validation rollouts sampled by mu_{k+1}, using a length-normalized importance ratio (Eq. 9) to stabilize the estimator. The candidate is accepted if the estimated gap exceeds a dynamic, linearly annealed tolerance -c_t (larger tolerance early in training, tightening to a fixed value after 100 steps); rejected candidates roll back both the trainer state and inference engine to the prior checkpoint. Three design decisions stand out: (1) truncating rather than fully clipping or leaving unbounded the pre-update mismatch weight is empirically justified in Appendix B via a direct ablation against PPO-IS and Vanilla-IS; (2) using a reverse performance-difference identity to make the acceptance proxy computable from realizable validation rollouts is a reasonable but unproven approximation, since the proxy estimates an expectation under mu_{k+1}, not the true target under pi_{k+1}, and the paper states this explicitly ("does not prove the update is unreliable"); (3) the annealed tolerance schedule is justified by an ablation (Appendix C) showing static strict thresholds (c=0 or c<0) cause persistent rollback and stalled learning, but the specific annealing schedule and its two hyperparameters (c_start, c_end) appear to be manually tuned per model rather than derived from a principled rule. Key Results: Table 1 reports pass@1 on five math benchmarks (MATH-500, AIME24 avg@16, OlympiadBench, Minerva, AMC23 avg@16) under FP8-quantized rollout. On Qwen3-4B, MIPU reaches 66.71% average versus 64.42% (Baseline GRPO), 63.42% (MIS), and 65.66% (LR-decay); on Qwen3-1.7B, MIPU reaches 53.97% versus 51.73%, 51.73%, and 52.23% respectively. Critically, the paper's "Stable" column shows only MIPU avoids collapse or sharp degradation across ~800 training steps; all three baselines exhibit some form of unstable training (Figure 2 shows Baseline and MIS crashing after a transient peak around step 150-300). Ablation (Table 2, Qwen3-4B) isolates Step 1 alone (65.36% avg, best on AIME24 at 43.56/41.11 and Olympiad at 68.45) versus Step 2 alone (62.81% avg, similar to baseline) versus the combination (66.71%), showing the two steps address distinct failure modes: Step 1 improves candidate quality, Step 2 prevents accumulation of unreliable synchronized updates. Appendix C's tolerance sensitivity study is a useful negative result: stricter acceptance (c=0 or c=-0.0001) is not monotonically safer and instead causes persistent rollback and a lower performance plateau after ~280 steps, undercutting a naive "just reject more" intuition. Scale is moderate: 1.7B and 4B parameter models, 8 H100s, training sets of 1491-5759 filtered examples, response length 8192 tokens; this is standard reasoning-RL scale, not stress scale. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (the three-term inference-policy-improvement decomposition and the MIPI principle) plus NEW METHOD (the two-step MIPU realization with a TIS-derived Step 1 surrogate and a reverse-identity acceptance proxy for Step 2). The genuinely novel contribution is the objective-level reframing, treating the inference policy rather than the training policy as the object of monotonic improvement, and the accept/rollback mechanism built on an estimable proxy for a task-defined quantity. The Step 1 surrogate itself is a straightforward extension of existing training-inference correction work (TIS, Yao et al. 2025); the paper is explicit about this lineage and correctly frames Step 1 as adapting prior art while Step 2 is the new piece. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and instructive: Table 2 isolates Step 1 vs. Step 2 vs. combined; Appendix B compares three Step-1 implementation variants (PPO-IS, Vanilla-IS, TIS) with training curves for reward, mismatch-K3, gradient norm, and clip ratio; Appendix C sweeps the acceptance tolerance c across four settings. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for the main Table 1 results, a real gap given the training instability the paper itself documents (a single seed cannot distinguish a genuine improvement from a lucky run, especially for a method whose central claim is about stabilizing a high-variance process). No code, data, or model-weight release URL is given in the visible text beyond a project page link (https://anitaleungxx.github.io/MIPU); the appendix lists license terms for all training/eval datasets and infrastructure (Apache-2.0 for Qwen3, ROLL, vLLM, Megatron), suggesting an artifact release is planned or intended, but no direct code repository is confirmed in-text. The evaluation benchmarks (MATH-500, AIME24, AMC23, OlympiadBench, Minerva) are standard, not self-constructed, which is a positive against Goodhart's Law concerns. The comparison baselines (MIS, LR-decay) are recent (2025-2026) and directly relevant to the mismatch problem, not weak strawmen. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and experiments the paper actually runs on real RLVR training). Practical: Runs on standard multi-GPU RL infrastructure (8xH100, Megatron training engine, vLLM inference engine) already used for the baselines; no added hardware requirement is introduced beyond a validation-rollout pass per training step. Efficient: Step 2 requires an additional validation-batch rollout and reward/advantage computation at every accepted-or-rejected step, roughly doubling rollout cost per iteration relative to a Step-1-only method; the paper does not report wall-clock or compute overhead numbers, only training-step counts, so the actual efficiency cost is not quantified. Effective: The evidence (Table 1, Figure 2, ablations) directly measures the target problem (pass@1 on held-out math benchmarks under high mismatch) rather than a proxy, and the stability claim is directly observable in the training curves, a genuine strength. Scalable: Not tested; the paper explicitly flags this in Limitations, restricting claims to 1.7B/4B models and stating that larger-model behavior is future work, so the practical impact for frontier-scale training runs is unverified. Strengths: - The three-term performance-difference decomposition (Eq. 5) is a clean, verifiable formal argument, not just a narrative reframing, and it correctly identifies that canonical LLM RL methods provide no guarantee on the inference-side quantity they never measure. - The ablation design (Table 2, Appendix B, Appendix C) isolates each design choice individually rather than only reporting the final combined method, which lets a reader attribute gains to specific mechanisms. - The negative result on stricter acceptance thresholds (Appendix C) is intellectually honest: it shows that naively rejecting more updates does not monotonically improve stability, a nuance the authors could have omitted. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that experiments are limited to moderate-scale models (1.7B/4B) due to compute constraints and that whether the same mismatch patterns hold at larger scale or in more diverse RL systems is future work. They also state plainly that Step 2's validation-based post-update gap proxy is one instantiation among a flexible design space, and that MIPU provides no formal monotonic-improvement guarantee, only a heuristic that reduces the risk of unrealized gains. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of the method's theoretical limits. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No seed variance or error bars on the headline Table 1 numbers, despite the paper's own framing that RL training is fragile and prone to instability; a single run cannot rule out that the reported gains are within run-to-run noise. - The mismatch condition tested (FP8-quantized rollout) is an artificially amplified stress case chosen specifically because it induces "high mismatch"; it is not established whether the more common production setting (BF16 training vs. BF16 inference, which still has nonzero but smaller mismatch per the cited Yao et al. and Liu et al. work) would show gains of similar magnitude, or whether MIPU's added rollout overhead would be worth it under milder mismatch. - The tolerance-schedule hyperparameters (c_start, c_end, the 100-step annealing window) are set per model without a stated tuning protocol or held-out selection procedure, raising the possibility that the reported configuration was selected via the same runs used for the headline results. - No comparison against combining MIPU's Step 1 with the paper's own MIS or LR-decay as a Step 2 alternative to its own acceptance proxy, so it is unclear how much of the total gain is attributable specifically to the reverse-identity proxy design versus simply having some form of rollback mechanism. Bottom Line: A practitioner running RLVR-style LLM post-training with split training/inference engines gains a concrete, low-overhead diagnostic (the post-update inference gap) and a workable rollback mechanism for a real instability source, and the reframing of "what should the RL objective actually optimize" is conceptually useful beyond the specific FP8 setting tested; the missing seed-variance reporting and single-mismatch-regime evaluation mean the magnitude of the claimed gains should be treated as suggestive rather than conclusive pending replication under standard-precision training.
Recommended 2606.29526 PDF
#34 — Innovative (69)

Evolutionary Feature Engineering for Structured Data

EO Taga, Y Zhuang, ME Ildiz, P Mol, A Das… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Evolutionary Feature Engineering (EFE), a framework that uses an LLM as an evolutionary variation operator to discover fit/transform preprocessing programs for structured data, instantiated as EFE-Time (invertible time-series normalizations) and EFE-Tab (parsimonious tabular feature programs). - Why it's important: Time-series foundation models (TSFMs) and tabular learners are frozen artifacts that can't be retrained per dataset, so a cheap, automatically-discovered preprocessing layer that adapts them to dataset-specific quirks (exponential trends, diurnal cycles, redundant columns) has direct deployment value without touching model weights. - How it does it: An LLM (Claude Opus-4.6) proposes complete Python program rewrites inside an OpenEvolve/MAP-Elites loop, receiving only aggregate metadata, summary statistics, and validation feedback (never raw series values or test data), and candidates are scored against an identity-transform baseline with an explicit complexity penalty. - The good: On the synthetic exponential-growth benchmark, the evolved transform cuts MASE by roughly an order of magnitude in the best per-series cases (e.g., Fig. 10a: MASE 10.68 to 1.63) by mapping data into a near-stationary space before forecasting, and the effect is not just curve-fitting to Chronos-2: the same frozen programs transfer unmodified to TimesFM-2.5, Moirai-2-Small, and Reverso-Nano with consistent 2-4% average MASE gains. - The missing: There is no ablation isolating which prompt-feedback components (dataset context vs. evaluation summary vs. inspiration examples) actually drive the gains, so it is unclear how much of EFE's benefit comes from the evolutionary loop versus from simply giving an LLM good summary statistics once. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners deploying TSFMs or tabular AutoML pipelines who want a lightweight, model-agnostic preprocessing layer, but the small-model (Qwen3.5) results in the appendix show the method is currently frontier-LLM-dependent and not yet a turnkey open-weight solution.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is the strongest dimension: current baselines (CAAFE, LLM-FE) under a matched 100-iteration budget, 3-seed mean±std, released code, and a small-model ablation that functions as a genuine negative control rather than a cherry-picked positive. Novelty (65) is capped because LLM-as-evolution-operator over code (AlphaEvolve/OpenEvolve, LLM-FE, CAAFE) is a crowded lineage; the invertible full-program formulation with fitted train-time state is a real but incremental delta, which also holds Non-Obviousness at 60. Completeness (75) and Resilience (65) are pulled down by the absence of any prompt-component ablation, so the mechanism behind the gains is undissected, and by the modest headline effect (3.0% mean MASE) against overlapping variance bands with no significance tests. Practical Impact (70) reflects a released repo and frozen programs that drop into existing pipelines at negligible inference cost, discounted by the frontier-LLM dependency and unreported search-phase dollar cost. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 = 69.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Feature engineering (normalization, detrending, ratios, interactions) can substantially improve downstream model performance, but useful transformations are dataset-specific and hard to specify in advance by fixed rules like RevIN or arcsinh scaling. Practitioners using off-the-shelf time-series foundation models or tabular classifiers/GBMs have no easy way to inject dataset-specific preprocessing without retraining the model or hand-engineering features per dataset. The gap being closed: prior LLM-based feature-engineering methods (CAAFE, LLM-FE) evolve single transformation functions using dataset context, but none evolve full invertible programs with fitted train-time state and an explicit inverse, which is required for post-hoc normalization ahead of a frozen forecaster. Key Idea: Represent preprocessing as an executable fit/transform(/inverse_transform) Python program, evolved by an LLM under a full-program AlphaEvolve-style rewrite paradigm rather than parameter tuning, with the downstream model held fixed so that search operates entirely on the representation passed into it. The core trick for EFE-Time specifically is closing the loop with an explicit invertibility constraint and validity gate (length/order/NaN preservation, exact numerical inverse to 1e-4), which lets the evolved program learn any invertible mapping (log, asinh, detrend, seasonal-phase centering, recent-window robust scaling) rather than being restricted to a single fixed family like RevIN. Methodology: The pipeline has three components: a Prompt Generator (dataset context, summary statistics, evaluation feedback, and inspiration examples from the MAP-Elites archive), an LLM generator that proposes a full candidate program, and an Evaluator that checks executability/leakage, inserts the candidate before the fixed downstream model (Chronos-2 for EFE-Time; TabPFN-v2 for EFE-Tab), and scores it against the identity baseline. The evolutionary loop is OpenEvolve with 3 islands, 0.70 exploitation / 0.10 exploration / 0.20 elite-selection ratios, code-diversity MAP-Elites coordinates (8 bins), and Claude Opus-4.6 at temperature 0.7, run for 100 iterations (300 for the small-model ablation). Objective: EFE-Time score is (1 − MASE(p)/MASE(p_id)) minus a runtime penalty, masked to zero for non-invertible programs; EFE-Tab score is mean ΔAUC over identity minus penalties for feature count k(p)/n and runtime. Three critical design decisions: (1) restricting fit() to see only the observed history (no future targets, no other series, no baseline forecasts) to prevent leakage — well-justified and enforced by an explicit validity gate; (2) using an identity-program offset of 0.5 in the combined score so invalid/failing programs are distinguishable from merely-bad-but-valid ones — a pragmatic but somewhat ad hoc scoring convention; (3) EFE-Tab's Pool A/Pool B resampling at every iteration to prevent overfitting a fixed split — reasonable, and the paper shows (Fig. 6c) an inverted-U in the pool-A fraction that supports the choice of 70/30. Key Results: On 10 GIFT-Eval datasets with Chronos-2, EFE-Time yields mean improvements of 3.0% MASE, 3.6% wQL, 3.7% MAE over the identity baseline (Table 1), rising to ~19% MASE on CovidDeaths and reaching statistical near-zero on regular datasets like kdd_cup_2018 and restaurant (Table 6). Cross-model transfer without re-evolution gives 2.2-2.4% average MASE improvement on TimesFM-2.5, Moirai-2-Small, and Reverso-Nano. Combining the evolved transform with fine-tuning Chronos-2 is additive: +16.67% MASE over zero-shot on the 3-dataset subset versus +9.80% (EFE alone) or +5.45% (fine-tuning alone) (Figure 4). On synthetic exponential-growth series (100 series, length 5000, 128-step horizon), EFE-Time cuts MASE from 4.10 to ~3.3-3.6 and wQL by an order of magnitude (0.0338 to ~0.028-0.033) across three TSFMs. For EFE-Tab, on 9 TabArena binary-classification datasets, EFE-Tab achieves the best mean rank (1.39) against no-FE, CAAFE, and LLM-FE for single decision trees (Table 2), with a similar advantage for LightGBM (mean rank 1.94) and near-parity with TabPFN (mean rank 2.22 vs CAAFE's 2.44). Baselines (CAAFE, LLM-FE) are current, relevant automated feature-engineering competitors run under the same 100-iteration query budget, not weak strawmen. Scale is standard (GIFT-Eval and TabArena benchmark-sized datasets, not stress-scale), and the small-model ablation (Qwen3.5 4B-35B) is a useful negative control showing gains do not scale monotonically with model size and are unstable (large WQL regressions on some seeds/datasets). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (evolving full invertible time-series transform programs with fitted state, distinct from LLM-FE's single-function evolution) and NEW FORMULATION (framing feature engineering broadly as evolutionary search over stateful fit/transform programs, unifying time-series and tabular under one paradigm). The genuinely novel piece is the invertible full-program evolution for time-series normalization (the paper claims to be first at this); the tabular side (EFE-Tab) is a more standard combination of existing evolutionary-code-search machinery (OpenEvolve/AlphaEvolve) applied with a parsimony penalty, differentiated mainly by its explicit complexity/feature-count regularization rather than by a new search mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: cross-model transfer (Fig. 3), fine-tuning-additivity (Fig. 4), decision-tree-depth sensitivity (Fig. 6a), training-size sensitivity (Fig. 6b), and a small-model scan (Appendix C.8) that serves as a genuine negative-result stress test rather than cherry-picked positives. Results are reported as mean ± standard deviation across 3 independent seeds/folds (Tables 1, 2, 6, 7), which is a meaningful but modest sample for variance estimation; no significance tests (e.g., paired t-test) are reported despite overlapping confidence intervals visible in several rows (e.g., SZ_TAXI_15T shows exactly 0.0% improvement with ±0.0 std, suggesting the transform reduces to identity there). Code is released at https://github.com/egetaga/EFE. A methodological concern: EFE-Time programs are selected via validation MASE improvement over p_id and then evaluated once on a frozen held-out test split per seed — this is a standard train/val/test split, not Goodhart's-Law-style same-benchmark tuning, but the paper does not report how often the frozen p* overfits to validation-specific noise (e.g., the CovidDeaths seed-2 run in Fig. 8 shows a sharp early drop that plateaus, suggesting possible early-iteration luck rather than systematic search improvement). Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (the paper runs actual forecasting and classification experiments with real downstream models). - Practical: Yes — evolved programs are plain Python fit/transform classes that insert directly into existing pipelines; evolution itself used only 4 L40S GPUs, 8 CPU cores, 128GB RAM (Appendix C.7), and the frozen program has negligible inference-time cost (transform_time_sec and forecast_time_sec are reported in the evaluation summary and are small, e.g., 0.13-0.16s for 223 series). - Efficient: The evolutionary search itself costs 100 LLM-driven iterations per dataset/seed (300 for small models), which is expensive at the search stage (repeated Claude Opus-4.6 calls) even though the resulting frozen program is cheap to deploy; the paper does not report absolute wall-clock or dollar cost of the evolution phase itself, only noting API cost constrained dataset coverage ("API costs prevented us from conducting experiments on the entirety of GIFT-Eval and TabArena"). - Effective: The evidence directly measures the stated problem (forecasting error reduction via MASE/wQL/MAE, and classification AUC), not a proxy, and the per-series qualitative analysis (Appendix E) is candid that gains concentrate on datasets with exploitable structure (periodicity, exponential trend) and vanish on datasets driven by exogenous, non-Markovian factors (restaurant, bitbrains_rnd/H) — this honesty about failure modes is a rigor positive. - Scalable: Not tested at 10x-100x scale; the largest tabular dataset used is BankMarketing (45,211 rows, Table 4) and the largest EFE-Time dataset is M4Yearly (22,974 series), both well within standard benchmark scale, and the paper explicitly states cost constraints prevented running the full GIFT-Eval/TabArena benchmarks, so scalability beyond the tested regime is unclaimed and unevaluated. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source code at the stated GitHub repo) plus the evolved programs themselves as a reusable output, not merely a paper-form technique. Strengths: - The invertibility validity gate for EFE-Time (exact numerical inverse check, hist_len-based position tracking for detrending/seasonal ops) is a concrete, well-engineered mechanism that prevents a common failure class (position-index bugs between fit-time history length and forecast-time array length), and the appendix's worked example (Table 8, covid_deaths lineage) shows this gate actually catching and recovering from two consecutive zero-score mutations. - The additive combination of EFE-Time with fine-tuning (Figure 4) is a genuinely useful empirical finding: it demonstrates the evolved normalization captures a complementary signal to what gradient-based adaptation captures, which is not obvious a priori. - The per-series qualitative analysis (Appendix E, Figures 10-14) is unusually candid for this genre of paper: it explicitly documents where and why the method fails (exogenous, non-Markovian drivers like COVID death surges or holiday-driven restaurant demand) rather than only showcasing aggregate wins. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state the evolutionary process uses fixed hyperparameters (exploration-exploitation ratio, number of islands) and leave studying their effect to future work. They acknowledge API cost prevented full-benchmark coverage on GIFT-Eval and TabArena, so results are on a curated subset chosen "for domain diversity" rather than the complete benchmarks. They explicitly show (Appendix C.8) that the method is unstable with smaller, non-frontier LLMs (Qwen3.5 family), with performance not scaling monotonically with model size and some configurations showing large WQL regressions despite improved medians — a direct, unhedged admission of a real limitation. They also flag in the Broader Impact section that EFE-Tab's use of semantic column-name cues could introduce spurious or discriminatory features and recommend human review before deployment. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No ablation isolates which pieces of the prompt (dataset context vs. evaluation-summary feedback vs. inspiration examples vs. MAP-Elites diversity) actually drive the improvement — the paper's own conclusion section names this as future work, but it means the mechanism behind EFE's gains is not yet dissected. The comparison in Table 2/7 evaluates CAAFE and LLM-FE under the same 100-iteration budget as EFE-Tab, but does not report the actual LLM API cost or wall-clock time of each method, so it's unclear whether EFE-Tab's advantage on decision trees comes from a better search algorithm or simply a different budget-efficiency tradeoff. All time-series experiments evolve against Chronos-2 only, and the "cross-model transfer" claim (Section 3.2.2) rests on 3 additional TSFMs tested on the same 10 GIFT-Eval datasets that were used to select p*, so it demonstrates architecture-transfer but not dataset-transfer of the learned normalization — an open question the paper does not address. The synthetic exponential-growth generator (Appendix C.6) is designed by the same authors evaluating the method, and no external, independently-sourced synthetic benchmark is used to corroborate the "TSFMs struggle with exponential growth" claim. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying TSFMs or TabPFN/LightGBM pipelines gains a concrete, empirically validated technique for squeezing 3-10% error reduction out of frozen models via learned normalization or compact feature programs, but should treat the evolutionary search cost and current frontier-LLM dependency as real deployment constraints rather than incidental details.
Citations 2607.01548 PDF
#35 — Innovative (69)

TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution

JA Wang, K Wang, P Nie · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TestEvo-Bench, an executable, live benchmark of 746 test-generation and 509 test-update tasks mined from 152 open-source Java Maven repositories, each anchored to a real commit pair with an execution environment for pass rate, coverage, and mutation-score scoring. - Why it's important: Existing test-generation and test-repair benchmarks score against a fixed code snapshot or rely on static diff heuristics, so they cannot verify whether an agent-produced test actually reflects a code change or would even run. - How it does it: A three-phase pipeline mines adjacent commit pairs, executes four cross-revision test/code triples (T-old to C-old, T-new to C-new, T-old to C-new, T-new to C-old) to label task type and filter noise, then packages only tasks where developer test edits are semantically tied to and executable against the code change. - The good: Every retained task is grounded in actual execution outcomes across old and new revisions rather than diff similarity, and the benchmark is timestamp-anchored so scoring can be restricted to post-training-cutoff subsets for contamination control. - The missing: The benchmark covers only Java/Maven projects and four harness-model configurations (Claude Code + Opus 4.7, Gemini CLI + Gemini 3.1 Pro, SWE-Agent + each), so generality across languages, build systems, and a broader agent population is unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating coding agents on test-maintenance tasks; the execution-grounded construction pipeline is the reusable contribution even if the specific Java/Maven scope narrows applicability elsewhere.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (benchmark plus execution/evaluation infrastructure). Practical Impact (80) carries the score: a released Hugging Face dataset under CC BY 4.0, a Maven/JUnit evaluation runner, and a live leaderboard constitute a real artifact practitioners can use today, and the execution-grounded labeling is a defensible improvement over the diff-heuristic prior benchmarks (CEPROT, TARGET, SYNTER). Rigor (60) and Non-Obviousness (55) pull the composite down: no ablations on the construction pipeline's filtering thresholds (the 12-hour gap, refactoring exclusion are borrowed unvalidated from Sun et al. 2023), no error bars or multiple seeds on the Success% numbers despite agent stochasticity, no comparison against non-agentic tools (EvoSuite, Randoop) or against developer-written test adequacy in the main text, and the live/contamination-aware design is adapted from LiveCodeBench rather than newly conceived. Novelty (65) reflects NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK with a genuinely better construction oracle, not a new problem class. Weighted composite: 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 = 69.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Developers evolve tests alongside code changes, either writing new tests to capture new behavior or updating existing tests to match a code edit; prior repository-mining work (Zaidman et al. 2011, Lubsen et al. 2009, Marsavina et al. 2014, Levin and Yehudai 2017) establishes this co-evolution as central to maintenance. Existing test-generation benchmarks (EvoSuite, Randoop, TestPilot, AgoneTest) synthesize tests for a static code snapshot, decoupled from any code change, and existing test-update/repair benchmarks (CEPROT, TARGET, SYNTER, TestUpdater, ReAccept) rely on static diff signals as ground-truth labels without confirming the resulting test is executable or semantically tied to the change. Neither line evaluates whether an automation agent actually understood how a code change should propagate into the test suite, and none support contamination-aware evaluation of the increasingly popular coding-agent harnesses (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, SWE-Agent). Key Idea: Use static commit-history diffs only to identify candidate test-code pairs, then validate every candidate by actually rebuilding and executing the project across four cross-revision test/code combinations (old test on old code, new test on new code, old test on new code, new test on old code). The outcome pattern across these four executions determines both whether a pair is retained (discriminating signal exists, i.e., the new/updated test behaves differently on old versus new code) and which track (generation vs. update) the task belongs to, replacing diff-heuristic labeling with execution-verified labeling. Methodology: The pipeline has three phases. (1) Mining: from 2,262 candidate GitHub Java/Maven repos filtered by stars (>500), license, buildability, and Java version, 248 repos pass repository-level checks; for each, the 300 most recent pre-cutoff commits are walked to form adjacent commit pairs that build cleanly and pass tests on both endpoints, with runtime instrumentation recovering per-test-method production-code dependencies, yielding 166 repos with dependency-mapped pairs and 59,950 candidate test-code change records. (2) Cleaning: candidate pairs are rebuilt and executed across the four cross-revision triples; aggressive filters discard temporal misalignment (>12h gap), ambiguous test-dependency changes, refactoring-only changes (detected via RefactoringMiner 2.0), and cases where execution replication is invalid, leaving 13,868 high-quality records from 91 repos. (3) Task construction: inserted tests are filtered to drop "redundant" cases (new test also passes on old code, i.e., not behaviorally discriminating); changed tests are filtered to drop pure "refinement" (typo/coverage edits rather than functional updates) using T-old to C-old pass/fail as the discriminator. Evaluation uses Maven-scoped recompilation of only the affected module, isolated per-method JUnit execution in fresh JVMs, JaCoCo coverage intersected with focal dependency lines, and Universal Mutator mutation scoring restricted to those lines — both reported only for the produced tests that pass (CovOnPass, MutOnPass), since the metrics are undefined for failing tests. Design choices: (a) using execution rather than diff similarity as the correctness oracle is well-justified given the paper's stated goal of avoiding false credit for tests that happen to pass without capturing behavior; (b) the 12-hour revision-pair gap and refactoring exclusion are borrowed from prior co-evolution-mining practice (Sun et al. 2023) rather than newly derived, a reasonable but not independently validated choice; (c) restricting to Java/Maven trades generality for engineering tractability, acknowledged directly by the authors. Key Results: On test generation (746 tasks, 1,961 methods), Claude Code + Opus 4.7 and Gemini CLI + Gemini 3.1 Pro tie at 77.5% Success%, SWE-Agent + Opus 4.7 at 66.1%, SWE-Agent + Gemini 3.1 Pro at 68.6%; Redundant% (test passes on new code but also on old code, so it doesn't demonstrate understanding of the change) stays high at 17.4-19.9% across all four agents, while ExecFail% (at most 0.4%) and CmplFail% (at most 2.3%) are small. On test update (509 tasks, 1,138 methods), Gemini CLI (74.6%), Claude Code (74.4%), and SWE-Agent + Gemini 3.1 Pro (73.9%) cluster tightly, while SWE-Agent + Opus 4.7 trails at 65.6%, driven by an 11.0% HrnsFail% (harness produced no test or a malformed edit) versus at most 3.5% for the other three. CovOnPass ranges 74.3-78.0% (generation) and 79.1-79.4% (update); MutOnPass ranges 55.0-57.1% (generation) and 44.6-46.0% (update). Under cost caps, the two Opus 4.7-driven agents are markedly more cost-sensitive: at a $1 per-task cap, Claude Code's Success% drops from 70.6% to 44.2% (generation) and 86.1% to 54.8% (update), and SWE-Agent + Opus 4.7 drops from 59.5% to 18.8% and 73.2% to 18.1% respectively; the Gemini 3.1 Pro-driven agents degrade less sharply. Performance also declines gradually on more recent time-segmented subsets for test generation (interpreted as reduced generalization to data outside training distribution), while test update stays comparatively stable across time. Baselines are the four agents against each other; there is no comparison against non-agentic test-generation tools (e.g., EvoSuite, Randoop) or against the original developer-written tests' own coverage/mutation scores in the main text (deferred to an appendix). Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (primary), NEW METHOD (the execution-grounded construction pipeline itself). The novelty is the construction pipeline that converts static commit co-change signals into execution-verified, labeled benchmark tasks with a live-ingestion, contamination-aware structure borrowed conceptually from LiveCodeBench (Jain et al. 2025) and applied for the first time (per the authors' claim) to test/code co-evolution; the agent evaluation itself is a standard application of off-the-shelf harnesses and models to the new benchmark, not a methodological contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported on the construction pipeline's filtering thresholds (e.g., the 12-hour gap, refactoring exclusion) beyond citing prior practice — it is not shown how much data or task quality changes if these thresholds are varied. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests accompany the Success% numbers; each number is a single macro-average across tasks, and agent stochasticity is not quantified. The benchmark and evaluation runner are stated to be open-sourced (Hugging Face dataset at huggingface.co/TestEvo-Bench/datasets under CC BY 4.0, leaderboard at testevo-bench.com), which supports reproducibility of downstream use, though the paper does not report the code repository URL for the harness itself in the excerpted text. A methodological strength is the explicit avoidance of Goodhart's Law risk: because tasks are validated by execution rather than by diff similarity to a fixed reference, an agent cannot game the benchmark by imitating developer edits textually (the paper explicitly notes an alternative test is acceptable as long as it passes and exercises the changed behavior). A remaining concern is that the four evaluated agents are evaluated using developer-authored task specs and prompts constructed by a single shared prompt builder, so agent comparisons may be sensitive to prompt-formatting choices not stress-tested across variations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (the paper delivers a benchmark plus execution/evaluation infrastructure, not a trained model or theoretical result). - Practical: Yes — the harness runs on standard Maven/JUnit tooling (JaCoCo, Universal Mutator) and requires only a JVM and network access to clone repos; no specialized hardware is needed. - Efficient: A full agent evaluation run across all tasks takes about 72 machine-hours, parallelizable across machines; the authors note this is materially more expensive than diff-similarity scoring, a stated tradeoff for reproducibility. - Effective: The execution-grounded pass/fail/coverage/mutation metrics directly measure whether a produced test compiles, passes on the intended revision, and exercises the changed code, which is a stronger effectiveness proxy than diff similarity, though Redundant% at 17-20% shows a meaningful fraction of "successful" generation-track tests still fail to demonstrate behavioral understanding under the paper's own criteria. - Scalable: The live-benchmark design (periodic re-mining, timestamp-anchored subsets) is built for growth, but the current snapshot (13,868 retained records from 91-166 repos, out of 59,950 candidates and 2,262 initial repos) shows aggressive attrition at each filtering stage; whether the pipeline scales cost-effectively to 10x-100x more repositories or non-Java ecosystems is stated as future work, not measured. The benchmark delivers a released dataset artifact (Hugging Face) and an evaluation runner/leaderboard, not a reusable proof technique. Strengths: - Execution-grounded labeling (four cross-revision triples) is a genuine methodological improvement over static-diff-labeled prior benchmarks (CEPROT, TARGET, SYNTER, TestUpdater), directly addressing a validity gap the authors demonstrate (Redundant% of 17-20% shows diff-based labeling alone would overcount successes). - Live, timestamp-anchored design enabling contamination-aware evaluation is a genuinely useful benchmark-engineering pattern, adapted from LiveCodeBench to a new task domain. - The Maven-scoped, per-method isolated JVM execution environment with coverage/mutation intersected against focal dependency lines is carefully engineered to avoid confounds from unrelated project changes or cross-method state leakage. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three limitations: (1) the benchmark is restricted to Java/Maven projects, with extension to Python/JavaScript left as future work despite claiming the pipeline is language-agnostic in principle; (2) only four harness-model configurations (spanning two harnesses times two models) are evaluated, a small subset of possible agent configurations, with community submissions and additional open-source models planned; (3) the execution-based evaluation setup is materially more expensive than diff-similarity scoring, requiring cluster-scale parallelization to bring 72 wall-clock hours down to a few hours. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not compare its curated tasks' developer-written test coverage/mutation scores against agent-produced tests within the main text (deferred to Appendix B, not visible in the excerpted pages), which would establish whether current agents match, exceed, or fall short of human-level test adequacy — a natural and important baseline for a benchmark framed around agent capability. The GitHub star threshold (>500) and license/build filters bias the repository sample toward well-maintained, popular projects, which may not represent the broader long-tail of real-world Java codebases where test co-evolution practices are less disciplined; this selection effect is not discussed. The 17.4-19.9% Redundant% rate, while flagged as a finding, is not decomposed further (e.g., is it concentrated in certain repo types or code-change categories?), leaving unclear whether it reflects a fixable prompt/harness issue or a fundamental agent limitation in inferring test oracles. Bottom Line: Researchers and practitioners building or evaluating test-generation/test-update agents gain a substantially more rigorous evaluation instrument than prior diff-heuristic benchmarks, and the execution-grounded construction pipeline itself is a reusable methodological contribution beyond the specific released dataset.
Search Results 2607.02469 PDF
#36 — Innovative (69)

G-RRM: Guiding Symbolic Solvers with Recurrent Reasoning Models

T Bertram, S Bhavnani, R Freinschlag, E Kobler… · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces G-RRM, a neuro-symbolic framework that uses a trained symbol-equivariant recurrent reasoning model (SE-RRM) to order variable/value branching choices in backtracking search and CDCL SAT solvers (Glucose 4.1, CaDiCaL 3.0.0), while leaving solver correctness and completeness untouched. - Why it's important: Recurrent reasoning models like HRM/TRM predict full Sudoku solutions in one forward pass with no correctness guarantee, so coupling them to a symbolic solver converts a high-accuracy but unverified neural predictor into a system with guaranteed-correct output. - How it does it: SE-RRM's per-cell symbol scores are converted into a value ordering per variable (Eq. 1), which seeds backtracking's digit order or a CDCL solver's initial literal phases before search runs unmodified. - The good: On 9x9 Sudoku, guided backtracking and Glucose 4.1 show statistically significant wall-clock speedups of 33.3x (median, p<0.001) and 1.70x (median, p<0.001) respectively, with the effect traced mechanistically to conflict-count reduction, not just aggregate runtime. - The missing: The study covers only Sudoku on up to 25x25 grids with 90-1000 test instances per size, and excludes SE-RRM inference time from all wall-clock comparisons, so the reported speedups do not reflect true end-to-end latency. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on neuro-symbolic combinatorial solving or looped-transformer reasoning models who want a concrete, mechanistically explained case study of when neural search guidance helps versus when solver overhead cancels the benefit; general ML practitioners can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (75) leads: paired Wilcoxon/t-tests across 3 seeds, conflict-count mechanism traced separately from wall-clock, and an honest CP-SAT hint-repair appendix that reports a 4x slowdown rather than hiding it. Non-Obviousness (75) is the standout dimension: identifying CaDiCaL3's phase persistence versus Glucose4's VSIDS reinitialization as the determinant of whether neural guidance helps or hurts is a mechanistic root-cause finding a practitioner can act on, exactly the kind of 'why does it work' analysis in the user's positive set. Novelty (60) is a coupling mechanism, not a new architecture. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) are high because the excluded SE-RRM inference time is disclosed and the negative CaDiCaL3 result is reported. Impact (45) is held down by no code release and Sudoku-only scope. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*45 = 69.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recurrent reasoning models (HRM, TRM, SE-RRM) solve constraint satisfaction problems like Sudoku with strong empirical accuracy (up to 91.1% fully-solved on 9x9) but provide no correctness guarantee: a single greedy forward pass can output a constraint-violating grid. Symbolic solvers (backtracking, CDCL SAT, CP-SAT) guarantee correctness but search blindly without exploiting statistical regularities the neural network has learned. The gap is how to combine the neural model's predictive strength with the solver's exactness without sacrificing either, an instance of Kautz's Type 3 neuro/symbolic pipeline architecture. Key Idea: Treat SE-RRM's output not as a final answer but as a value-ordering heuristic: for each cell, sort candidate digits by the model's predicted score and hand that ordering to the solver as its branching/phase-selection policy. The solver retains full completeness and correctness; the neural network only affects the direction of search, never the search space itself. This decouples predictive risk from soundness risk entirely. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Encode the Sudoku as either a search-tree problem for backtracking or a CNF formula (N^3 Boolean variables, ALO/AMO clauses per row/column/box, 11,988 clauses for 9x9 up to 752,500 for 25x25) for CDCL solvers. (2) Run SE-RRM (a looped Transformer with axial self-attention over both position and symbol axes, trained via deep supervision with stop-gradient truncation) to produce a score matrix Y-hat in R^(I x K); per-variable value orderings pi_i are obtained via argsort (Eq. 1). (3) Inject the ordering into the solver: for backtracking, digits are tried in SE-RRM's preferred order at each minimum-remaining-values-selected cell; for CDCL solvers, the top-scored digit per cell initializes the Boolean variable's phase before search begins. Critical design decisions: (a) using argsort rather than hard commitment to the argmax choice, preserving completeness when the network is wrong; (b) exploiting a known asymmetry between Glucose4 (VSIDS-driven phase reinitialization on restart, allowing self-correction) and CaDiCaL3 (persists external phases across restarts, so wrong hints stick); (c) excluding SE-RRM inference time from wall-clock comparisons, which isolates the solver-side effect but understates real deployment cost. Key Results: On 9x9 Sudoku (91.1% SE-RRM fully-solved rate, n=1,000 test puzzles), median conflict counts drop to 0 for guided backtracking, Glucose4, and CaDiCaL3 (Table 1), and guided backtracking achieves a 33.251x median wall-clock speedup (p<0.001) with Glucose4 at 1.699x (p<0.001); CaDiCaL3 shows no significant median speedup (1.021x, n.s.) and a significant mean slowdown (0.896x) attributable to fixed phase-initialization overhead of ~1.7ms. At 16x16 (22.0% FSR, n=100) and 25x25 (51.1% FSR, n=90), conflict-count reductions persist (e.g., -53.2% median for Glucose4 at 16x16) but wall-clock gains are mostly non-significant except Glucose4's 2.204x (perfect-hint, p<0.001) and 1.169x (perfect-hint, p<0.05) at 16x16/25x25 respectively. A supplementary CP-SAT study (Appendix C, Table 5) with four configurations (default, naive-fixed, soft-hint, repaired-hint) shows repaired hints drive mean conflicts to 0.0 at 9x9 but incur 4x wall-clock overhead versus default CP-SAT at 16x16 (0.0971s vs. 0.0237s) because a low 22% hint-feasibility rate forces expensive repair-then-fallback. No SAT/CSP baselines outside the paper's own solver variants are compared; all comparisons are self-relative (guided vs. unguided same solver). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the G-RRM search-guidance mechanism connecting SE-RRM scores to backtracking cell/digit ordering and CDCL phase initialization) plus NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the specific conditions, search-space size and solver overwrite capability, under which neural guidance yields significant speedup or not). The neural component (SE-RRM) and symbolic solvers (Glucose4, CaDiCaL3, CP-SAT, backtracking) are all pre-existing; the contribution is the coupling mechanism and the systematic characterization of when it helps, not a new learning architecture or solving algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Wall-clock comparisons use paired statistical tests (Wilcoxon signed-rank on medians, paired t-test on means) with significance thresholds reported per cell across 3-run seed collapsing, which is a stronger standard than typical single-run benchmark papers. Ablation-style structure is present via the perfect-hint vs. imperfect-hint split and the four-configuration CP-SAT comparison, which functions as an ablation of hint quality and repair strategy. No code, data, or model-weight release URL is stated in the visible text. All evaluation is on the authors' own generated or borrowed Sudoku datasets (9x9 from Wang et al. 2025 / Freinschlag et al. 2026; 16x16/25x25 self-generated via py-sudoku, 100 train / 90-100 test), with no comparison against external SAT competition benchmarks or independently curated hard instances, and sample sizes for 16x16/25x25 (n=100, n=90) are small, limiting statistical power for the non-significant results reported there. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes, the symbolic solvers run on standard CPU hardware; SE-RRM training/inference presumably requires a GPU but this is not detailed. Efficient: Not stated in comparable terms; inference time for SE-RRM is explicitly excluded from all reported comparisons (Limitations), so the true end-to-end compute/latency cost relative to unguided solving is unknown. Effective: Partially: guidance measurably reduces conflicts across all tested grid sizes and solvers, but only backtracking and Glucose4 convert this into statistically significant wall-clock wins; CaDiCaL3 shows no benefit or a slowdown, showing the method solves a proxy (conflicts) more reliably than it solves the actual deployment objective (wall-clock time). Scalable: Tested only up to 25x25 (clue density ~50%); the paper explicitly proposes a future "Solve-Learn-Extrapolate" loop to push to larger sizes but does not test beyond 25x25, and the diminishing wall-clock returns already visible at 16x16/25x25 raise doubt about further scaling. No software artifact release is mentioned; the work stops at paper form with a research-prototype SE-RRM implementation described as not exploiting available performance optimizations. Strengths: - Mechanistic decomposition of why speedups occur (conflict-count reduction) versus whether they show up in wall-clock time, isolating solver overhead as a confound rather than reporting a single aggregate number. - Identification of a genuine, non-obvious architectural distinction (CaDiCaL3's strict phase persistence vs. Glucose4's VSIDS-driven phase reinitialization) that determines whether neural guidance helps or actively hurts, a finding a practitioner can act on directly when choosing a solver to pair with neural hints. - The CP-SAT appendix's hint-repair analysis (C4) quantifies a genuine tradeoff: near-zero conflicts at the cost of large wall-clock overhead at 16x16 due to low hint feasibility (22%), which is a concrete, falsifiable characterization rather than a vague caveat. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - SE-RRM inference time is excluded from all wall-clock results; the authors confront this directly rather than hiding it, and explain the exclusion is to isolate the solver-side effect rather than to inflate results. - The study is confined to Sudoku and does not confirm findings on other combinatorial domains. - The SE-RRM implementation is described as a straightforward research prototype, not optimized for engineering performance, which the authors note could affect any future end-to-end comparison. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No baseline comparison against alternative variable-ordering heuristics that do not require training a neural model (e.g., dynamic degree/weighted heuristics, or classic non-neural warm-start methods), so it is unclear how much of the observed gain is attributable specifically to the RRM's learned signal versus any reasonable static or dynamic value-ordering prior. - Sample sizes at 16x16 (n=100) and 25x25 (n=90) are small relative to 9x9 (n=1,000), and most of the non-significant results are concentrated exactly at these smaller-sample sizes, so the paper's own claim that gains "diminish" at scale is partly a statistical-power artifact rather than a confirmed effect, a distinction the paper does not fully disentangle. - The excluded SE-RRM inference cost is a first-order deployability question the paper defers entirely; a practitioner cannot determine from this paper whether G-RRM is a net win once GPU inference time is added to the 9x9 backtracking speedup of 33x on solve times measured in single-digit milliseconds. - The paper's own "Solve-Learn-Extrapolate" framing in Future Work implicitly acknowledges the 16x16/25x25 fine-tuning used only 100 samples each, but does not discuss how sensitive the reported FSR (22.0% at 16x16) and downstream guidance quality are to this very small fine-tuning set.
Search Results 2607.02491 PDF
#37 — Innovative (68)

OmniOpt: Taxonomy, Geometry, and Benchmarking of Modern Optimizers (64 ▲ )

Siyuan Li, Jiabao Pan, Yumou Liu, Zhuoli Ouyang, Xin Jin, Xinglong Xu, Jingxuan Wei, Shengye Pang, Jintao Che, Xuanhe Zhou, Conghui He, Cheng Tan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OmniOpt, a unified survey-and-benchmark framework that maps over 100 LLM optimizers onto a five-stage meta-pipeline and a four-axis linear-minimization-oracle (LMO) decomposition, then benchmarks 24 representative optimizers across 4 model scales (60M-1B), 4 architectures, and context lengths from 256 to 32k tokens. - Why it's important: Optimizer choice for LLM pretraining is now a joint compute/memory/tuning-budget decision spread across 100+ methods described in incompatible notation, and prior benchmarks show rankings flip with scale, batch size, and protocol, so practitioners lack a principled way to compare or combine methods. - How it does it: Every optimizer update is decomposed into five pipeline stages (routing, gradient transformation, state evolution, reconstruction, finalization) and four LMO-driven axes (update domain, state estimator, geometry/precondition operator, finalization wrapper), which exposes where methods act and whether two mechanisms can be stacked or conflict for the same slot. - The good: A controlled mechanistic ablation of Muon (Fig. 20, Table 18) isolates Newton-Schulz orthogonalization as the indispensable core (removing the second moment collapses PPL from 17.78 to 70.74; adding NS alone recovers to 16.86), and shows LR-scaling/Nesterov gains stack on standard Transformers but not on Gated DeltaNet, giving mechanism-level (not just leaderboard) evidence. - The missing: All benchmark numbers stop at 1B parameters and ~30k training steps, so the paper's central claim (no universal optimizer, mechanism-dependent trade-offs) is unvalidated at the multi-billion-parameter, long-horizon regime where these optimizers are actually deployed in practice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone selecting or designing an LLM-training optimizer, especially the Muon ablation (Section 6.3) and the APOLLO short-context/long-context reversal (Section 6.2.4); skip the 100+-method taxonomy tables (Section 5) unless building a similar unifying framework, since they add compactness but not new empirical evidence.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The composite is held in the Innovative band by strong Non-Obviousness (70) and Completeness (75): the Muon mechanistic ablation isolates Newton-Schulz orthogonalization as the load-bearing component (PPL 70.74 without it, 16.86 with it alone) and the APOLLO short-to-long-context reversal (13.53 at 256 tokens to 35.40 at 32k) converts a confusing leaderboard flip into a falsifiable rank-bounded-compression claim. Rigor (65) is the limiter: no seeds, error bars, or significance tests across a 24-optimizer x 4-scale x 4-architecture grid, so close rankings (Muon 13.72 vs RMNP 13.87) are indistinguishable from noise, and the benchmark stops at 1B parameters and roughly 30k steps, well below the regime where optimizer choice is actually contested. Weighted composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 = 68.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practitioners choosing an optimizer for LLM pretraining face over 100 published methods (Adam-style, sign-based, matrix/orthogonalized, low-rank, sharpness-aware) whose papers are organized around a local mechanism name rather than where in the update pipeline that mechanism intervenes. This makes it hard to tell whether two optimizers touch the same operation (and therefore compete) or different operations (and can be composed). Compounding this, empirical claims in the optimizer literature are protocol-sensitive: conclusions about a method's advantage can change with model scale, batch size, tuning budget, and context length, and there is no shared mathematical vocabulary connecting sign-based, matrix-preconditioned, low-rank, and curvature-aware methods. Key Idea: Every optimizer update can be read through linear minimization oracles (LMOs) over norm balls: the choice of norm ball (Euclidean, max-norm, spectral-norm) determines the update direction (normalized gradient, sign, or matrix-polar direction), and this single geometric primitive unifies sign updates, spectral orthogonalization, and Kronecker/diagonal preconditioning under one notation. Composed with a five-stage operational pipeline (route parameters, transform the gradient, evolve state, reconstruct the update, finalize/write back), this gives a two-axis lens: where a method intervenes operationally, and why its resulting direction takes a specific mathematical form. Methodology: The framework has four layers. (1) A universal meta-pipeline (S0-S5: signal acquisition, parameter routing, gradient transformation, state evolution, update reconstruction, update finalization) into which every optimizer is mapped, with an "identity-mapping principle" that most methods deviate from AdamW defaults at only one or two stages (e.g., Muon is primarily S1/S2, GaLore spans S1-S4, Lion is S2/S3). (2) A four-axis LMO decomposition (update domain, state estimator, geometry/precondition operator, finalization wrapper) that gives each optimizer a coordinate tuple. (3) A dual-dimension taxonomy assigning 108 optimizers to five non-overlapping mechanism families (T1 element-wise adaptive moments, T2 matrix-structured, T3 discretization/sign, T4 state compression, T5 curvature-aware) crossed against six effect objectives (convergence, compute cost, memory, stability, hyperparameter robustness, generalization). (4) A two-stage benchmark: Stage 1 sweeps 24 optimizers on C4/LLaMA at 60M-1B params, sequence length 256, measuring PPL, per-step runtime, and optimizer-state memory with S5 regularization (weight decay, clipping) disabled to isolate S2/S3 mechanisms; Stage 2 transfers the strongest Stage-1 optimizers to FineWeb-Edu at 32k context, 340M/1B scale, across Transformer++ and three linear-attention architectures (GDN, DeltaNet, GLA), with S5 regularization enabled. Auxiliary analyses use gradient-norm coefficient of variation (GNormCV) for stability and a 3-point (0.2x/1x/5x) learning-rate perturbation for hyperparameter robustness. A design decision worth flagging: Stage 1 deliberately disables weight decay/clipping to isolate S2/S3 machinery, which is well-justified for mechanism attribution but means Stage-1 rankings do not represent production-recipe behavior (addressed by re-enabling regularization in Stage 2). Key Results: At 1B on C4 (Stage 1), APOLLO (T4, state-compressed) achieves the best PPL of 13.53 versus AdamW's 14.48, using only 0.79 GB optimizer-state memory versus AdamW's 4.99 GB, while Muon and MARS-Shampoo (T2) reach 13.72-13.73 PPL but cost 379-514 ms/step versus AdamW's 19 ms and Lion's 12 ms. On Stage-2 long-context transfer (FineWeb-Edu, 32k tokens), this reverses sharply: APOLLO becomes last or near-last in 8 of 8 architecture-scale scenarios under both PPL and downstream Commonsense-Reasoning accuracy, while SOAP (T2) holds the top PPL position in 7 of 8 scenarios. A matched sequence-length ablation (Table 15) isolates the mechanism: APOLLO degrades from 13.53 PPL at 256 tokens to 35.40 at 32k (+21.87, roughly 3x the AdamW baseline degradation of +7.39), attributed to its fixed low-rank random-projection subspace becoming lossy as gradient effective rank rises with context length. On CIFAR100 vision backbones, no optimizer dominates across architectures (AdaBelief best on ResNet50 at 80.53%, Muon best on DeiT-S at 77.38%, Adan best on CAFormer-S12 at 84.89%), and Muon's Transformer-specific advantage (+5.2 points over AdamW on DeiT-S, modest gain on ResNet50) supports the claim that matrix-structured directions suit correlated attention-layer gradients specifically. The Muon mechanistic ablation (C4-350M) shows removing the second moment without adding orthogonalization is catastrophic (PPL 17.78 to 70.74), and Newton-Schulz orthogonalization alone recovers to 16.86 (already beating AdamW), with LR scaling and Nesterov contributing smaller refinements to 16.60. These gains are architecture-conditional: on Gated DeltaNet the combined gain (24.12) does not improve over the best single gain, unlike on standard Transformers where gains stack (13.58 combined vs. 13.64 single-gain at 1B). Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (the LMO-driven four-axis decomposition unifying sign/orthogonal/preconditioned directions under one geometric language) plus NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the APOLLO short-to-long-context collapse and Muon's architecture-dependent gain stacking). The taxonomy itself is a structured literature organization rather than a new algorithm; the genuinely novel contributions are the unifying mathematical framing and the controlled multi-axis benchmark evidence, particularly the sequence-length and cross-architecture ablations that were not previously isolated as controlled single-variable experiments in the cited comparison works (Table 1/2). Rigor & Reproducibility: The benchmark is well-controlled along several axes: a strict controlled-variable protocol tunes only optimizer-related hyperparameters while holding architecture/data/schedule fixed, and the sequence-length and Muon ablations isolate single variables cleanly. Code, website, and Hugging Face links are provided (referenced in Figure 1, though exact URLs are not transcribed here). However, several rigor gaps stand out: no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any PPL or accuracy number in Tables 13-18, so it is not established whether e.g. a 0.01-0.05 PPL gap between top Tier-I optimizers (Muon 13.72 vs. RMNP 13.87 at 1B) is distinguishable from run-to-run noise. Training runs are capped at 10k-100k steps depending on scale (never stated beyond ~30k steps at 1B), which the authors themselves flag as leaving multi-billion-parameter, long-horizon behavior unvalidated.
