Two design decisions stand out: opaque per-run node/paper labels are not used here (that's the companion thematic-grouper design elsewhere), but the DAG's strict type-consistency constraint (predecessor output type must match successor input type) is well-justified as a mechanism to keep LLM-generated workflows executable; the choice to derive preference pairs from synthetic corruptions rather than mining real failure cases is more debatable, since it may not cover the failure distribution actually produced by imperfect 9B-scale generation. Key Results: On a held-out benchmark (five search directions: predecessor, successor, sibling, benchmark, survey; 6–15 gold papers per case, Semantic Scholar metadata), PaperPilot-9B (multi-turn, trained) achieves Hit@5=77.0, Hit@10=83.5, Hit@15=89.5, Recall@50=40.0, MRR=59.4, nDCG@10=32.5 at $0.0180/case, versus the untrained Qwen3.5-9B+Toolset baseline's Hit@5=58.0, MRR=47.5, nDCG@10=26.8, error rate 9.5%. GPT-5.4 with Web Search + PaperPilot-Toolset in multi-turn is the overall best system (Hit@5=84.0, MRR=71.8) but at $0.1508/case, roughly 8x PaperPilot-9B's cost; OpenAI DeepResearch, evaluated one-shot, costs $6.09/case for Hit@5=72.0. A workflow-level ablation (Table 3) shows PaperPilot-9B's whole-workflow TF-IDF cosine similarity to reference workflows improves from 0.047 (base Qwen3.5-9B) to 0.362, and a sensitivity analysis finds retrieval quality peaks around candidate-pool size K=8-10 and degrades at K=20 (Recall@50 drops from 0.411 to 0.374), indicating larger first-stage pools introduce distractors rather than helping. A human study (6 participants, 5 sessions each) reports PaperPilot success rate 74.7% versus GPT-5.4's 32.0% and DeepResearch's 8.0%. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (paper search as DAG-structured workflow induction with typed operators, rather than fixed pipeline or free-language agentic reasoning) plus NEW METHOD (two-phase SFT+DPO training specifically for workflow generation and editing) and a NEW BENCHMARK (held-out multi-turn literature-search dataset with five search-direction protocols and an LLM-simulated user with leakage control). The individual retrieval operators (keyword search, citation expansion, LLM reranking, NLI filtering) are standard techniques; the novelty is in the explicit, editable, type-checked graph representation and the training recipe that teaches a 9B model to construct and locally repair such graphs under user feedback, not in any single retrieval component. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present at both retrieval level (Table 2, varying tool setup and turn count) and workflow-generation/editing level (Table 3, Figure 6-7, comparing against Qwen3.5-9B, Kimi K2.6, GPT-5.4), and a candidate-pool sensitivity sweep (Figure 8, Table 6) is reported. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed variance are reported anywhere in the main results or ablations. Code and a project website are linked (unspecified license/access details in the excerpt), which supports reproducibility of the toolset and possibly the benchmark. The paper's own retrieval and workflow-generation benchmarks were both constructed with the same broader team's protocol (citation-graph-derived gold sets, LLM-assisted synthesis) and the multi-turn evaluation depends entirely on a single simulated user model (Qwen3.5-397B) shared across all systems being compared, which is a reasonable design for fair comparison but means the headline multi-turn numbers are not validated against real human interaction except in the small separate human study. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (built system evaluated via retrieval and workflow-generation metrics). Practical: Yes — the 9B model plus symbolic toolset runs at $0.0180/case, implying feasible deployment on a single modern GPU without proprietary API access. Efficient: Substantially cheaper than GPT-5.4+Web+Toolset (8x) and DeepResearch (~340x), while retrieval quality (Hit@5=77.0) trails the best GPT-5.4 configuration (84.0) by 7 points. Effective: Evidence shows it improves Hit@5/MRR/nDCG over the untrained 9B baseline and reduces execution errors to zero, but the retrieval task is evaluated only on CS literature with hidden gold-paper sets built via a citation-graph-plus-LLM protocol, which is a reasonable proxy but not an open-ended real-world query stream. Scalable: Not tested — the paper explicitly notes the benchmark focuses on CS literature and does not test whether the toolset or trained model generalizes to other domains, larger corpora, or longer interaction horizons than the 4-5 turn cap used in evaluation. Delivers a software artifact: a project website, code repository, and trained PaperPilot-9B model/toolset (per the paper's links), plus a released benchmark protocol. Strengths: - The DAG-with-typed-operators representation is a concrete, checkable answer to the "agent tool use is hard to inspect and steer" problem, distinct from ReAct/Toolformer-style flat interleaving or DSPy/AFlow's code-level workflow compilation, by targeting paper-search-specific operators and multi-turn dialogue-conditioned editing. - The corrupted-workflow DPO construction (1,733 hard pairs from filtered teacher trajectories) is a reasonably clever way to generate hard negatives without additional human annotation, targeting the specific structural/semantic failure modes (invalid references, dropped nodes, shifted filters) that matter for this task. - The cost-effectiveness framing (CE and Rel. Eff. metrics, Table 2) makes the practical tradeoff explicit rather than only reporting top-line retrieval numbers, showing PaperPilot-9B is competitive with systems using far larger backbone models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The predefined symbolic operator library may not cover all domain-specific search behaviors. - Workflow supervision data comes from teacher-model trajectories and may inherit the teacher's biases or blind spots. - The benchmark focuses primarily on CS literature with controlled hidden-gold evaluation, which may not reflect open-ended real-world literature search. - The multi-turn setting relies on an LLM-based user simulator; despite leakage-control mechanisms, simulated feedback cannot fully replace human interaction. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The gold-paper sets (6-15 papers per case) are constructed via citation-graph signals, human filtering, and LLM-assisted synthesis — the LLM-assisted portion is not detailed enough to rule out circularity with the LLM-based systems being evaluated. - No variance/seed information is given anywhere, so the magnitude of gains (e.g., Hit@5 58.0 to 77.0) cannot be checked against run-to-run noise. - The human study uses only 6 participants across three unrelated majors (Biology, Social Science, Computer Science) for a CS-literature-search task, which is a small and partly mismatched sample for validating generalizable claims about search quality. - The paper does not report what happens when the trained 9B model encounters search intents outside the five trained directions (predecessor/successor/sibling/benchmark/survey), leaving open how brittle the induced workflows are to novel intent categories. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating tool-augmented literature-search agents gains a concrete, well-ablated alternative to free-form agentic tool use, with strong evidence that explicit DAG workflows plus targeted SFT+DPO training let a 9B model match much of GPT-5.4's multi-turn retrieval quality at roughly 1/8 the cost, though the reliance on simulated-user evaluation for all main numbers means the human-study result (74.7% success) is the more meaningful signal and its small sample warrants a light discount.
Recommended 2607.00597 PDF
#49 — Innovative (67)

Safety Testing LLM Agents at Scale: From Risk Discovery to Evidence-Grounded Verification

Y Feng, R Lin, M Wen, Q He, Y Guo, Y Ding, Y Wu… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VERA, an automated pipeline that discovers agent-safety risk taxonomies from the literature, compiles them into 1,600 executable safety test cases with deterministic verifiers, and runs them adaptively against production LLM agents in isolated sandboxes. - Why it's important: Existing safety benchmarks are hand-curated and hard-coded, so they cannot keep pace with the combinatorial growth of risks, attack methods, and tool-execution environments as agents gain more autonomy. - How it does it: A three-stage pipeline (literature-driven taxonomy discovery, combinatorial goal/case composition with programmatic initial states and verifiers, and adaptive multi-turn execution judged by evidence-grounded verifiers that prioritize environment state over tool logs over agent responses) replaces manual scenario authoring and LLM-judge self-report. - The good: The evidence-hierarchy verifier design (state > tool-call > response, Eq. 7) is validated indirectly by a 20.1-point ESR jump from benign to single-channel attacks and asymmetric benign-vs-attack verification difficulty, showing the framework distinguishes real safety violations from compliance theater rather than trusting model self-report. - The missing: The LLM-based goal composer, taxonomy summary agent, and initializer/verifier generators are themselves unverified generation steps; the paper reports no audit of false-positive/false-negative rates in the auto-generated verify.py scripts beyond "regenerate on syntax error," so verifier correctness is asserted, not measured. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating computer-use/tool-using agents at a security-conscious organization; the released VERA-Bench (1,600 cases, 124 risk categories) and the evidence-grounded verification pattern are directly reusable, though the taxonomy-discovery and goal-generation machinery is less battle-tested.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) leads: code and VERA-Bench (1,600 executable cases, 124 risk categories, full execution artifacts) are publicly released, and the evidence-priority verifier pattern (state over tool-call over response) is directly reusable. Non-Obviousness (65) and Novelty (65) come from the three-taxonomy combinatorial composition plus the asymmetric evidence hierarchy applied uniformly across four structurally different agent frameworks. Rigor (60) is the main drag: the taxonomy, goal composer, initializer, and the verify.py scripts that are ground truth for every ESR/ASR number are all LLM-generated with no human audit of false-positive/false-negative rates, and no seeds or error bars are reported. Completeness (60) is limited by missing per-backend-LLM breakdowns despite testing five models, and by the 61.7% R-Judge guard result being framed as a win without noting it is low in absolute terms. Weighted composite: 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*80 = 67.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations deploying LLM agents that call external tools (email, code hosting, payment, messaging, search) face safety risks such as credential leakage, unauthorized system modification, and unsafe code execution. Existing evaluation approaches are either prompt/response-level refusal checks that ignore whether harm was actually executed, or trajectory-level benchmarks built on manually curated, hard-coded scenario/rule sets that cannot scale to the combinatorial growth of risk categories, attack methods, and tool environments, and that tightly couple risk definitions to specific environment implementations, making them costly to extend as agents and tool ecosystems evolve. Key Idea: Treat agent safety testing as a software-engineering testing problem: decompose it into three orthogonal, literature-grounded taxonomies (risk consequence, attack method, execution environment), take the combinatorial (Cartesian-product-style) cross of taxonomy leaves to generate concrete, executable "safety cases" (goal + programmatic initial state + deterministic verifier), and judge outcomes from observable execution evidence (environment state, tool-call logs, agent response, in that priority order) rather than from the model's own claims or an LLM judge's read of the transcript. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Continuous Risk Exploration: a Summary Agent iteratively crawls roughly 800 papers from arXiv/OpenReview, applying four operations (create/update/merge/delete) to grow three hierarchical taxonomies (risks R, attack methods M, environments Ω_E) until they converge, yielding 124 leaf risk categories, 77 leaf attack methods, and 30 leaf environment categories. (2) Executable Test-Case Construction: an LLM goal composer G maps sampled tuples (r, m, e) plus a dynamically-updated demonstration set D to concrete safety goals (Eq. 4), filtered for minimum-length and type validity; each accepted goal is compiled into a full case σ = ⟨g, s0, V_g⟩ where s0 is built via programmatic calls to tool-execution-environment interfaces and V_g is a generated deterministic Python verifier; cases needing internal-reasoning-trace access (unobservable) are filtered out, and each retained base scenario is expanded into benign, single-channel, and multi-channel variants. (3) Adaptive Execution and Evidence-Grounded Verification: a Control Agent observes per-turn output o_i = ⟨r_i, L_i, Δs_i⟩ from a configurable MCP-based tool gateway and adapts its next message/injection policy π_ctrl(σ, c_i) (Eq. 5) for up to 10 turns; the multi-channel threat model injects adversarial content into tool results via one of four transformation modes (identity/append/prefix/override); final judgment applies V_g = (V_g^state ▷ V_g^tool ▷ V_g^resp), an asymmetric rule where a Control Agent-reported failure is scored y=0 without invoking the verifier, while a reported success must be confirmed against environment/tool evidence before y=1. The three critical design choices are: (a) opaque combinatorial composition with post-hoc dedup/normalization to avoid a sparse or redundant Cartesian product — reasonably justified by the reported >40% ESR floor across all 80/110 aggregated cells; (b) the evidence-priority ordering (state over tool-call over response) — justified conceptually (prevents false positives from verbal-only compliance and false negatives from claimed-but-unexecuted refusal) but not validated against a ground-truth-labeled subset; (c) using isolated Docker Compose sandboxes (12 containers, 5 self-hosted backend services) for reproducible s0 — well-justified for determinism but limits environment realism to what the 5 mocked services (Mailpit, Gitea, Blnk, Databag, SearXNG) can represent. Key Results: Evaluated on four agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, Claude Code) across multiple backend LLMs (GPT-5.2, Gemini-3, Qwen-3.7, Kimi-K2.6, GLM-5.2), producing VERA-Bench: 1,600 executable base scenarios (from 39,078 candidate tuples after filtering) spanning 124 risk categories. Average attack success rate (ASR, i.e., 100-ESR) reaches 90.6% under single-channel and 93.9% under multi-channel attacks; overall ESR (agent "safety") ranges from 70.3% (OpenClaw) to 88.6% (Claude Code) across attack modes, with benign-task ESR only 70.5% on average, notably lower than attack ESR, attributed to benign verifiers requiring full multi-predicate task correctness versus attack verifiers requiring only a single observable violation. A downstream guard-model experiment fine-tunes Qwen3-Guard on VERA-derived data, improving R-Judge accuracy from 59.4% to 61.7% (exceeding LlamaGuard3's 53.7%, NemoGuard's 54.4%, BraveGuard's 57.8%) and improving in-distribution VERA classification from 0.670/0.468/0.637 (Acc/Rec/F1, base Qwen3-Guard) to 0.930/0.903/0.941 (fine-tuned). No comparison is made to non-VERA safety benchmarks' reported ASR numbers on the same four agent frameworks, so the paper's claim of "significantly exceeding" static-benchmark ASR levels is qualitative, not a controlled head-to-head. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the VERA framework and its unified execution contract across heterogeneous agents) and NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (VERA-Bench, 1,600 cases with three-tier variants and deterministic verifiers). The literature-driven taxonomy construction and combinatorial goal composition are a NEW METHOD for scenario generation, though the underlying building blocks (LLM-based taxonomy extraction, red-teaming with adaptive multi-turn control agents, code-level verifiers) are individually established in cited prior work (e.g., DoomArena [17], DecodingTrust-Agent [20], Advagent [21]); the genuine novelty is the systematic three-taxonomy combinatorial composition plus the evidence-priority verification hierarchy applied uniformly across a common agent-adapter interface. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation-style evidence exists in the form of the single-channel vs. multi-channel vs. benign comparison (Table IV) and per-agent breakdowns, which functions as a component-contribution analysis for the adaptive control mechanism, but there are no repeated-seed or error-bar/confidence-interval results for any ESR/ASR number, and no significance testing across agent-framework comparisons. Code and the VERA-Bench dataset are publicly released (github.com/Yunhao-Feng/Vera). A methodological concern: the taxonomy, goal composer, initializer, and verifier are all constructed by the same class of LLM tooling that is later being tested, and no separate audit quantifies verifier false-positive/false-negative rates against human-labeled ground truth; the paper asserts asymmetric judgment eliminates false positives/negatives (Section IV-C3) but presents no direct measurement, only the plausibility argument from the benign/attack ESR asymmetry. The guard-model evaluation is self-generated data evaluated in-distribution (VERA-Bench) plus one external benchmark (R-Judge), a reasonable but narrow generalization check. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (VERA is an evaluated testing infrastructure/tool). Practical: Yes, it runs on commodity Docker Compose infrastructure (12 containers per sandbox) without specialized hardware. Efficient: Runs are described as "moderate" (median 155k input tokens, 3k output tokens, 11 tool calls per execution) but the 95th-percentile run uses 789k input tokens and 38 tool calls, and no wall-clock or dollar-cost totals for the full 1,600-case x 3-variant x multi-model evaluation are reported. Effective: Evidence shows the framework surfaces real, reproducible safety violations (deterministic verifiers, replayable records) across four production agents, though effectiveness of the taxonomy-discovery and goal-generation stages themselves is asserted via downstream ESR patterns rather than directly measured against human-curated ground truth. Scalable: The paper demonstrates scale within its own construction (800 papers, 39,078 candidate tuples, 1,600 retained cases, 5 backend LLMs, 4 agent frameworks) but does not test or discuss what happens at 10x-100x more agent frameworks or tool environments; the exploration-budget-limited taxonomy convergence process is claimed but not stress-tested for saturation behavior at larger literature corpora. The work delivers a software artifact: the VERA framework, the MCP-based tool gateway, and the released VERA-Bench dataset with full execution artifacts (attack_plan, mcp_logs, trace.json, verify.py). Strengths: - The evidence-priority verification hierarchy (state ▷ tool-call ▷ response) is a concrete, implementable answer to the long-standing red-teaming problem of models claiming compliance or refusal without matching executed behavior, and the paper offers indirect but real evidence (benign vs. attack ESR asymmetry) that it functions as intended. - The three-taxonomy combinatorial design cleanly separates "what/how/where" for agent risks and is empirically shown not to degenerate into sparse or redundant coverage (all reported aggregate cells exceed 40% ESR), which is a genuine validation of the composition strategy rather than an assumption. - The common adapter interface enabling identical scenario execution across four structurally different agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, Claude Code) is a real engineering contribution that most cited prior red-teaming platforms lack, since those tightly couple risk definitions to a single environment implementation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The paper explicitly scopes out training-phase attacks (fine-tuning poisoning, backdoor injection) as untestable through the agent's runtime interface, and only retains training-related categories when they manifest as inference-time behaviors. - Section VI notes that off-the-shelf guard models "do not transfer especially well" to VERA-Bench, which the authors attribute honestly to the benchmark's richer attack diversity and environment grounding rather than framing it as evidence their benchmark is simply harder in an uninteresting way. - The Control Agent's adversarial budget is capped at 10 interaction turns; the authors do not claim results generalize beyond this budget. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No human-in-the-loop audit of the LLM-generated verify.py scripts is reported; since these are the ground truth for every ESR/ASR number in the paper, any systematic bias in the verifier-generation LLM (e.g., a model that is lenient toward its own family's outputs) would propagate silently through all downstream results. - The five self-hosted backend services (Mailpit, Gitea, Blnk, Databag, SearXNG) are simplified stand-ins for real-world email, code-hosting, payment, messaging, and search systems; no discussion addresses whether findings transfer to production services with richer authentication, rate-limiting, or UI complexity that could change attack feasibility. - The paper does not report per-backend-LLM breakdowns (only per-agent-framework), despite testing 5 different backend models, so it is unclear whether the reported vulnerabilities are agent-framework properties, backend-model properties, or an inseparable interaction of both. - The downstream guard-model result (R-Judge accuracy of 61.7%) is a modest absolute number even as the best-reported baseline; the paper frames this as "exceeding all off-the-shelf baselines" without acknowledging that 61.7% accuracy on a binary/few-class safety classification task is still low in absolute terms. Bottom Line: Practitioners building or auditing tool-using LLM agents gain a directly reusable, evidence-grounded testing methodology and a sizeable released benchmark (VERA-Bench), but should treat the reported ASR/ESR numbers as a first data point rather than an audited ground truth, since the verifier-generation pipeline that produces those numbers is itself unvalidated against independent human judgment.
Search Results 2607.01793 PDF
#50 — Innovative (67)

Scaling Mixture-of-Experts Video Pretraining for Embodied Intelligence (39 ▲ )

Shuailei Ma, Jiaqi Liao, Xinyang Wang, Jingjing Wang, Chaoran Feng, Zijing Hu, Chong Bao, Zichen Xi, Yuqi Gan, Weisen Wang, Yanhong Zeng, Qin Zhao, Zifan Shi, Wei Wu, Hao Ouyang, Qiuyu Wang, Shangzhan Zhang, Jiahao Shao, Yipengjing Sun, Liangxiao Hu, Lunke Pan, Nan Xue, Kecheng Zheng, Yinghao Xu, Xing Zhu, Yujun Shen, Ka Leong Cheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LingBot-Video, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Diffusion Transformer (DiT) video foundation model that unifies text-to-image, text-to-video, and image-to-video generation with an explicit embodied-AI training recipe (robot-augmented data, physical-plausibility rewards, and an action-conditioned world-model variant). - Why it's important: Video foundation models optimized purely for visual fidelity do not enforce physical consistency, so robotics applications that want to use video models as world simulators, policy evaluators, or data engines cannot trust their outputs for planning or training. - How it does it: Sparse MoE feed-forward layers (DeepSeekMoE-style fine-grained experts with shared experts, auxiliary-loss-free bias-corrected routing) decouple total parameter capacity from per-token compute, combined with a five-stage curriculum, a real-video-vs-generated-video preference post-training scheme (RealNFT), and six specialized reward models for GRPO-style RL fine-tuning. - The good: The active-parameter-matched comparison (MoE 13B-A1.4B vs. Dense 1.3B) and the cross-compute comparison (MoE 30B-A3B approaching Dense 14B) directly demonstrate capacity-compute decoupling on validation loss, and the 0.97x-to-3.18x inference speed ratios against dense models at up to 1M tokens substantiate the efficiency claim with real latency numbers rather than FLOP estimates alone. - The missing: All comparisons against dense baselines use training/validation loss curves rather than downstream generation-quality metrics (FID, VBench, human eval) at matched compute, so the MoE-vs-dense advantage is not verified on the same benchmarks used to compare against competitor models. - Read it or not: Worth reading for MoE video-diffusion practitioners and embodied-AI researchers evaluating open video world models; the architecture and infrastructure sections (sparse routing, sequence-wise balance loss, RL system design) are the most transferable content, while the benchmark claims should be read with the caveat that most competitors are recent open-source models the authors chose to compare against.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (82) dominates at 30% weight: open weights, GitHub repo, HuggingFace release, a Diffusers-compatible package and SGLang serving adapter, for a 120B-total-parameter MoE video foundation model. Novelty (65) is real but bounded — DeepSeek-style fine-grained MoE routing is imported from the LLM literature, and GRPO/DiffusionNFT are adaptations; the new part is the video-specific instantiation (sequence-wise balance loss over packed video sequences, six decomposed reward models). Rigor (62) gets credit for the genuinely controlled scaling comparisons (MoE 13B-A1.4B vs. Dense 1.3B at matched active params; MoE 30B-A3B vs. Dense 14B) but is reduced because those comparisons are loss-curve-only and never connected to the downstream benchmark leadership claimed elsewhere, RBench baselines are partly borrowed rather than re-run, and the primary embodied-domain evidence is an opaque self-designed internal benchmark. Completeness (60) and Resilience (58) reflect honest scoping of the unconverged 120B run but a purely qualitative A2V result presented as key evidence of embodied transfer. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*82 = 67.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Robotics and embodied-AI applications increasingly want to use video generation models as implicit world simulators for policy evaluation, synthetic data generation, and action-conditioned planning. However, mainstream video foundation models (diffusion or autoregressive) are trained on internet video corpora and optimized for aesthetics, text alignment, and creative content generation, not for physical correctness, contact dynamics, or long-horizon state consistency. This creates a domain mismatch: a model that produces visually compelling video may still hallucinate object permanence violations, incorrect action outcomes, or non-physical interpenetration, none of which are penalized by standard training objectives. The gap affects anyone trying to use video models for robot manipulation, navigation, or autonomous driving simulation. Key Idea: The core technical insight is capacity-compute decoupling via sparse Mixture-of-Experts applied to video diffusion transformers: by replacing the dense feed-forward block with fine-grained routed experts plus always-on shared experts, the model can scale total parameter capacity (up to 120B) to absorb heterogeneous physical priors (fluid dynamics, rigid-body contact, diverse embodiments) while holding active per-token FLOPs constant, which is otherwise prohibitive for million-token spatiotemporal sequences. This is paired with a secondary idea: closing the sim-to-real physical-plausibility gap not through better architecture alone but through curated embodiment-heavy data (70,000+ hours of robot manipulation/navigation/egocentric footage) and reward models that explicitly score physical plausibility and task completion rather than only aesthetics and text alignment. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Architecture: a task-unified single-stream DiT processes concatenated visual-latent and Qwen3-VL-4B condition tokens through blocks with QK-normalized attention, Multi-Modal 3D RoPE, and AdaLN-single modulation; the FFN in each block is replaced by a sparse MoE layer combining N_s shared experts and top-K_r of N_r routed experts (fine-grained, DeepSeek-style), with sigmoid-affinity routing, group-limited communication, and an auxiliary-loss-free online bias-correction load-balancing scheme plus a sequence-wise balance loss added to the flow-matching diffusion loss. A cascaded refiner upsamples 480p base output to 1080p via a thresholded rectified-flow trajectory starting from a degraded, VAE-encoded low-res condition rather than pure noise. (2) Data: a Data Profiling Engine tags every sample across five dimensions (structural, semantic, motion, camera, quality) feeding a World-Knowledge Topological Graph (semantic + action trees, 50,000 leaf concepts) used for distribution-aware, loss-informed up/down-weighting; dense structured JSON captions are generated for images/video/VLA/egocentric data, decoded at inference by a two-stage Caption Rewriter (Expand then Map) to bridge the prompt-brevity train/inference gap. A five-stage curriculum progressively introduces video, multi-task conditioning (T2V/TI2V), distribution harmonization, and high-res refinement. (3) Post-training: GRPO with a single stochastic denoising step per rollout (Flash-GRPO-style credit assignment), Coefficients-Preserving Sampling to avoid SDE noise-schedule mismatch, and six independently normalized reward models (vision quality, action-centric VQA-based text-video alignment, dynamic degree, motion coherence via playback-speed estimation, human-motion-consistency via a fine-tuned VLM, and a caption-conditioned physical-plausibility/task-completion scorer). A separate real-vs-generated preference stage (RealNFT, built on DiffusionNFT) uses real video clips as positive samples and generated clips as negative samples with a KL regularizer against the base model, avoiding reward-model-based reward hacking. Critical design decisions: (a) fine-grained routing (128 experts, top-8) chosen over coarse routing (64 experts, top-4) based on validation loss ablation at equal total parameters — well-justified with a direct comparison; (b) expert count fixed at E=128 despite E=256 showing further gains, trading a marginal loss improvement for lower communication/memory overhead — a reasonable but somewhat arbitrarily thresholded choice; (c) using r=0 (no reward model) for the rejected sample in RealNFT to avoid reward-model overhead, which simplifies the pipeline but forgoes graded preference signal for how bad the generated sample actually is. Key Results: On the authors' internal benchmark (T2V/TI2V x general-quality/embodied-domain, 5-point compared to Cosmos 3, Wan 2.2 A14B, HunyuanVideo 1.5, LongCat-Video, LTX-2.3), LingBot-Video ranks first on TI2V general quality and embodied-domain scores (e.g., TI2V domain avg. lead over Cosmos 3's egocentric/navigation categories) and ranks second on T2V general quality but still leads on T2V embodied-domain score. On RBench (650 prompts, 5 open+3 closed-source competitors), LingBot-Video achieves the top average score (0.620) among all evaluated models including three closed-source systems (Wan 2.6: 0.607, Seedance 1.5 pro: 0.584, Veo 3: 0.563), and leads specifically on Long-horizon (0.634) and Quadruped (0.758) sub-scores. On Physics-IQ Verified (66 controlled physical experiments, I2V mode), LingBot-Video scores 40.4, ahead of Cosmos 3 (39.5), HunyuanVideo 1.5 (33.4), Wan 2.2 A14B (32.2). A blind pairwise Good-Same-Bad user study across 400 prompts against six open-source and four commercial models shows LingBot-Video with higher Good than Bad rates against all open-source TI2V baselines but trailing commercial models (Kling-V3, Seedance 2.0) in both T2V and TI2V. Scaling ablations show MoE 13B-A1.4B beats Dense 1.3B at matched active parameters (training/val loss only, no downstream metric), and MoE 30B-A3B approaches Dense 14B performance; inference speed ratios (dense/MoE latency) reach 1.50x-3.18x at 1M-token sequences against Dense 6B/14B/30B, with near-parity (0.97x) against the active-parameter-matched Dense 3B. The action-conditioned variant (LingBot-Video-A2V), fine-tuned 8k steps on Fourier GR-1 trajectories, is shown qualitatively (not quantitatively, beyond narrative claims) to outperform DreamDojo on EgoDex/DreamDojo-HV held-out evaluations for physical-law adherence and action following. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (sparse MoE video-diffusion architecture with fine-grained expert scaling to 120B parameters, multi-reward GRPO post-training with single-step credit assignment applied to video, real-video-preference RealNFT adaptation) + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (open-source MoE video foundation model, SGLang-compatible serving stack, diffusion-native RL infrastructure) + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (data profiling engine, token-budgeted packing, composable 4D parallelism). The genuinely novel contribution is combining DeepSeek-style MoE routing techniques (already established in LLMs) with video diffusion transformers at this scale and pairing it with an embodiment-aware data/reward pipeline; the individual components (MoE, GRPO, rectified-flow refiners, DiffusionNFT) are each adaptations of existing techniques to a new setting rather than fundamentally new formulations. Rigor & Reproducibility: Scaling ablations (expert count, fine-grained vs. coarse routing, active-parameter and cross-compute comparisons) are shown via training/validation loss curves, which is appropriate for demonstrating the MoE mechanism but does not connect back to the downstream benchmark numbers used elsewhere in the paper — no ablation isolates which reward dimension, curriculum stage, or data source drives the final quality/embodied-domain gains. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported anywhere; benchmark scores are single numbers. The paper is a preprint (arXiv, industry lab, Ant Group/Robbyant) with no independent peer review. Model weights, code, and website are released (https://github.com/robbyant/lingbot-video, https://huggingface.co/robbyant/lingbot-video), which is a meaningful reproducibility positive for an industry paper of this scale. A methodological concern: the internal benchmark (Fig. 15) and RBench/Physics-IQ Verified are both used to declare state-of-the-art status, but RBench baselines are partly "sourced from RBench" rather than independently re-run by the authors (stated in Table 1 footnote), raising a consistency question about whether all models were evaluated under identical conditions. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built, open-sourced model and serving stack) with EMPIRICAL evaluation. - Practical: Yes for inference — the released Diffusers-compatible package and SGLang-native serving path with FP8 Triton kernels target commodity multi-GPU deployment; training at 120B total parameters requires large-scale distributed infrastructure (FSDP/HSDP, sequence parallelism, expert parallelism, DeepEP) not accessible to most labs. - Efficient: The MoE design keeps active parameters constant (1.4B-11B) while scaling total capacity to 120B, and inference speed ratios of 1.50x-3.18x versus similarly-scaled dense models at 1M-token sequences are reported, though absolute latency numbers (seconds per video) are not given. - Effective: Benchmark results (internal, RBench, Physics-IQ Verified, human GSB study) show the model solving the stated problem relative to other open-source models, though it trails select closed-source commercial models (Kling-V3, Seedance 2.0) in the human study, indicating the "solves the problem" claim holds only relative to the open-source frontier. - Scalable: The 120B scaling study (Fig. 6b) is explicitly early-stage and not trained to convergence; the paper states this directly, so the 10x-100x scaling claim beyond 120B is unverified and explicitly flagged as such by the authors. Delivers a software artifact: open-source model checkpoints, GitHub repo, and a HuggingFace release, plus a Diffusers-compatible package and SGLang serving adapter. Strengths: - The active-parameter-matched (MoE 13B-A1.4B vs. Dense 1.3B) and cross-compute (MoE 30B-A3B vs. Dense 14B) scaling comparisons are a genuine, controlled empirical demonstration of capacity-compute decoupling for video diffusion, not just an LLM-literature analogy. - The sequence-wise auxiliary balance loss (borrowed from DeepSeek-V3 but applied per packed video sequence rather than per global batch) directly addresses a video-specific pathology: long spatiotemporal sequences can hide routing imbalance that batch-level statistics would mask. - The six-way decomposed reward system for RL post-training (vision quality, action-centric VQA alignment, dynamic degree, motion coherence via playback-speed estimation, human-motion-consistency, physical plausibility) is a more fine-grained diagnostic decomposition than the single-scalar reward models typical in prior video RL post-training work, and the qualitative before/after comparisons (Figs. 12-13) show concrete artifact fixes (hand deformation, object duplication, non-physical penetration). - RealNFT's use of real video clips as the "optimal" sample in preference optimization is a practical way to inject ground-truth physical realism without depending entirely on a learned reward model, reducing one axis of reward-hacking risk. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state the 120B-parameter scaling runs are not trained to full convergence due to resource constraints, and explicitly frame those results as an "early-stage" and "initial validation" rather than a final claim. They note the coarse-vs-fine routing and expert-count ablations are conducted under a fixed pretraining mixture, implicitly scoping the generalizability of those specific numeric comparisons. The user study section acknowledges that LingBot-Video trails stronger commercial models in absolute terms, presenting this directly rather than obscuring it. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not report any downstream generation-quality metric for the dense-vs-MoE scaling comparisons, so the loss-curve advantage (Figs. 3-6) is never connected quantitatively to the benchmark leadership claimed elsewhere; it is plausible but unverified that the loss gap translates proportionally into the RBench/Physics-IQ gains. The RBench comparison table sources "some models" from the RBench paper itself rather than uniformly re-evaluating all baselines in-house, which risks protocol mismatches (different inference settings, prompt sets, or sampling parameters) that could inflate or deflate relative standings. The Action-to-Video (A2V) post-training results are presented only qualitatively (Fig. 18, two example rollouts) with no quantitative EgoDex/DreamDojo-HV metric reported, despite this being framed as a key test of embodied transfer. The internal benchmark categories (Human Interaction, Physical Simulation, Robotics, Egocentric, Navigation) are the authors' own construction with no stated inter-rater reliability or scoring methodology beyond "comprehensive evaluation," and since it is the primary evidence for the T2V/TI2V domain-score leadership claim, its opacity is a meaningful gap for an entirely self-designed benchmark used to declare state-of-the-art status. Bottom Line: Researchers building or evaluating MoE video-diffusion architectures or embodied video world models gain concrete, reproducible engineering patterns (fine-grained MoE routing recipes, sequence-wise balance loss, diffusion-native RL infrastructure) from this preprint, but the benchmark superiority claims rest partly on an unreleased internal benchmark and partially borrowed baseline numbers, so practitioners should treat the state-of-the-art positioning as directional rather than independently verified.
Recommended 2607.07675 PDF
#51 — Innovative (67)

InternVLA-A1.5: Unifying Understanding, Latent Foresight, and Action for Compositional Generalization (17 ▲ )

Haoxiang Ma, Junhao Cai, Xiaoxu Xu, Hao Li, Yuyin Yang, Yang Tian, Jiafei Cao, Hongrui Zhu, Zherui Qiu, Zhaxizhuoma, Yuqiang Yang, Jiaqi Peng, Xueyuan Wei, Yangkun Zhu, Jiahao Jiang, Xing Gao, Hanqing Wang, Feng Yuan, Kailin Li, Xueyue Zhu, Tai Wang, Yan Ding, Jiangmiao Pang, Jia Zeng, Jingjing Zhang, Bowen Zhou, Yao Mu, Chunhua Shen, Weinan Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces InternVLA-A1.5, a vision-language-action (VLA) model that predicts robot actions through a lightweight unified expert attached to a frozen pretrained VLM, using learnable "foresight tokens" that distill future-state knowledge from a frozen pretrained video generation model without ever synthesizing pixels. - Why it's important: Prior unified VLA models that jointly learn semantics and pixel-level future prediction erode the VLM's pretrained language understanding and pay the full compute cost of video generation, which limits both instruction-following accuracy and real-time deployability. - How it does it: A small set of learnable foresight query tokens attends to the shared multimodal context inside a Mixture-of-Transforms unified expert, and a video-prediction loss backpropagated through a frozen WAN2.2-5B video generator shapes these tokens into a compact latent code, which is then reused to condition flow-matching action generation; the video branch is discarded at inference. - The good: InternVLA-A1.5 achieves the best or second-best result on all six simulation benchmarks tested (e.g., 98.9% average on LIBERO, 80.8% on SimplerEnv versus 57.1% for pi_0.5, 93.2% on RoboTwin), and ablations (Table 8) show consistent degradation on four benchmarks when the video loss or foresight tokens are removed, isolating the mechanism's contribution. - The missing: The real-world instruction-following and long-horizon (MOF) results compare against only two baselines (pi_0.5, Motus) on four tasks the authors themselves designed, with no independent benchmark, no statistical variance reporting across the reported single-run success rates, and no ablation isolating the foresight mechanism's real-world contribution (only simulation ablations are run). - Read it or not: Worth reading for VLA/robot-manipulation researchers evaluating unified perception-foresight-action architectures; the foresight-token idea (world-model distillation without pixel generation) is a reusable design pattern even for readers not using this specific stack.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (72) is the standout: recasting future-state prediction as latent querying against a frozen video generator, so the policy inherits dynamics priors without ever synthesizing pixels and drops the generator at inference, is a genuinely non-obvious resolution of the semantics-vs-dynamics-vs-latency tension, and it is a reusable design pattern beyond this stack. Novelty (70) credits the mechanism while discounting the fact that every component (Qwen backbone, WAN2.2, flow matching, FAST tokenization) is off-the-shelf and the paper frames itself as an incremental fix to InternVLA-A1. Rigor (65) is held back by zero seeds or error bars across six sim benchmarks and four real tasks, with the 75.9% vs 77.8% Sort Tubes gap plainly inside noise. Completeness (65) and Resilience (62) are limited by the unablated alpha:beta=1:10 weighting (precisely the knob that is supposed to fix the objective interference the paper claims to solve) and by the largest real-world margin (MOF, 76.4% vs 29.3%) coming from a self-designed task. Impact (72) reflects released code and weights plus 0.1s inference on a single 5090. Weighted composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*72 = 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: General-purpose robot manipulation policies need both the semantic priors of pretrained vision-language models (for instruction grounding and generalization) and the physical dynamics knowledge available in pretrained video generation models (for anticipating how the world evolves under an action). Existing "unified" architectures that try to combine both by training generation and action objectives jointly on top of a VLM backbone tend to (1) erode the backbone's pretrained semantic and instruction-following ability once heavy generation/action objectives dominate training, (2) suffer negative interference between heterogeneous objectives (flow-matching action regression, pixel reconstruction, language modeling) trained jointly from scratch, and (3) waste the dynamics priors already encoded in large pretrained video generators by training a visual-prediction module from scratch. This is a real deployment constraint for anyone building closed-loop, real-time manipulation policies, since discarding the video generator at inference is necessary for low-latency control but keeping its knowledge is necessary for physical plausibility. Key Idea: Recast future-state prediction as a latent-querying problem rather than a pixel-generation problem: a small number of learnable foresight tokens attend to the current multimodal context and are trained (via a loss backpropagated through a frozen video generator) to become a conditioning code that a pretrained video model can use to imagine the correct future. Because the video generator's weights stay frozen and only the foresight tokens and the upstream context encoder are updated, the policy inherits world-model dynamics priors as a byproduct of matching the generator's expectations, without learning to synthesize video itself, and the generator is entirely removable at inference. Methodology: The architecture is a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT): a pretrained VLM backbone (Qwen-3.5 2B, hybrid attention with 3 Gated DeltaNet linear-attention layers interleaved with 1 full-attention layer) handles multimodal perception, and a lightweight "unified expert" (460M params, same hybrid architecture at reduced hidden dimension) handles action generation; the two interact only through the shared full-attention layer. Training has two pretraining stages plus post-training. Stage 1 (300K steps) casts both VQA data and robot demonstrations into one chat-template format (Figure 3) and trains a joint next-token cross-entropy objective (Eq. 1-2) over subtask description and FAST-tokenized discrete action chunks, preserving the VLM's native representation. Stage 2 (600K steps) adds the unified expert: M=50 learnable foresight tokens attend to the visual-language context (Eq. 3), their output embeddings condition a frozen WAN2.2-5B video generator via cross-attention (replacing its native T5 text encoder) under a flow-matching video-prediction loss (Eq. 4) over N=4 future frames; in parallel, the unified expert predicts continuous action chunks via a separate flow-matching objective (Eq. 5-7) conditioned on the same context and foresight tokens. The combined objective is L_stage2 = L_stage1 + alphaL_video + betaL_action with alpha=1, beta=10 (Eq. 8). A hybrid attention mask (Figure 5) enforces causal ordering between VLM and expert token groups, bidirectional attention within the action-expert group, and explicitly masks the expert away from ground-truth FAST action tokens during training to prevent leakage. Three design decisions stand out: (1) casting all modalities into one chat-template with a shared vocabulary/loss — well-justified via the loss-curve comparison in Figure 10 showing faster convergence; (2) freezing the video generator so no additional inference cost is incurred — directly validated by the reported ~0.1s inference step; (3) alpha:beta=1:10 weighting between video and action losses — asserted as "balance" but not ablated over alternative weightings, so its optimality is not established. Key Results: Across six simulation benchmarks the model attains the best or highly competitive scores: SimplerEnv (WidowX) 80.8% average (+23.7 points over pi_0.5's 57.1%, second-best 79.2% from Xiaomi-Robotics-0), LIBERO 98.9% average (best, narrowly ahead of Xiaomi-Robotics-0's 98.7%), LIBERO-Plus (zero-shot) 84.8% total (best, ahead of pi_0.5's 84.4%), RoboTwin 2.0 93.2% average (best, ahead of Being-H0.7's 89.9% and LingBot-VA's 92.2%), DOMINO zero-shot 27.7% SR (best among baselines, improving to 29.3% after fine-tuning), EBench Test split 35.2% SR / 49.5 score (best, ahead of LingBot-VA's 30.9%/47.6). Baselines include recent, credible systems (pi_0, pi_0.5, GR00T-N1.7/N1.5, OpenVLA-OFT, LingBot-VA, Fast-WAM, Being-H0.7, Xiaomi-Robotics-0), most from 2025-2026, so the comparison set is current rather than outdated. Ablations (Table 8, on LIBERO/LIBERO-Plus/RoboTwin/DOMINO) show removing the video loss drops LIBERO-Plus from 84.8% to 78.0% and DOMINO from 27.7% to 25.3%; removing foresight tokens drops LIBERO-Plus to 77.9% and DOMINO to 23.8%, confirming both components contribute, with larger effect under zero-shot distribution shift and dynamic-object scenarios. Real-world experiments (single 5090 GPU, four tasks: Sort Tubes, Insert Tubes, Move Tubes, MOF) show InternVLA-A1.5 beats Motus on all four and beats pi_0.5 on three of four (Insert Tubes +20.8 points, Move Tubes +7.8 points, MOF 76.4% vs pi_0.5's 29.3% and Motus's 0%), while trailing pi_0.5 slightly on Sort Tubes (75.9% vs 77.8%), attributed to Sort Tubes being close to plain pick-and-place where pi_0.5 is already strong. Held-out instruction-binding decomposition (Figure 9) shows the model's advantage holds on out-of-distribution bindings, not just memorized ones, supporting the compositional-generalization claim. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the foresight-token latent-querying mechanism for distilling a frozen video generator into a policy without pixel-level generation) plus NEW SYSTEM (the full MoT architecture combining a native VLM backbone, unified expert, and foresight-conditioned flow matching). This is a genuine contribution in how future-prediction supervision is structured (latent query against a frozen generator, discarded at inference) rather than in the individual components — the VLM backbone (Qwen-3.5), the video generator (WAN2.2-5B), flow-matching action heads, and FAST tokenization are all existing techniques; the paper's own related-work discussion frames it as an incremental refinement of its prior InternVLA-A1 model, addressing three specific failure modes (semantic drift, objective interference, from-scratch visual prediction) identified in that and other prior unified models. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations isolate both the video loss and the foresight tokens across four benchmarks (Table 8), which is a meaningful methodological strength. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere — all simulation and real-world numbers are single-run success rates, and real-world tasks (15-20 trials per binding) have enough trial-count variance that a several-point gap (e.g., the 75.9% vs 77.8% Sort Tubes result) could plausibly fall within noise. Code, model weights, and homepage links are referenced (GitHub, Model links in the abstract) though exact evaluation scripts for the real-world tasks are not verifiable from the paper alone. Baselines are current and include the authors' own prior model (InternVLA-A1) and several other 2025-2026 systems, avoiding the weak-baseline problem. One methodological concern: several benchmarks used for comparison (RoboTwin, DOMINO, EBench) are recent (2025-2026) and some are co-developed or closely associated with overlapping author networks in this fast-moving VLA subfield, though this is not disqualifying since numbers are also compared against external published baselines on each. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system built and evaluated through simulation and real robot experiments). Practical: Yes — real-world experiments run on a single NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU, a commercially available consumer/prosumer card. Efficient: The unified expert is only 460M parameters versus a much larger VLM+generator combination, and because the video branch is discarded at inference, one inference step takes about 0.1s with no additional per-step generation cost relative to non-world-model VLA baselines; exact FLOPs/memory versus specific competing methods (e.g., LingBot-VA, Fast-WAM) are not reported. Effective: Evidence directly supports the stated problem across six simulation benchmarks and four real-world tasks with held-out instruction bindings specifically designed to test grounding rather than memorization, which is a solid effectiveness test beyond a single proxy metric. Scalable: Not tested — the paper reports results only at the trained/evaluated scale (1.2M episodes, 3M multimodal samples); no experiments probe what happens at 10x-100x model size, data scale, or task-suite size, so scalability claims beyond the tested regime are unsupported. The work delivers a software artifact: code and model weights are referenced as released (GitHub, model link in Figure 1 caption area) alongside the paper. Strengths: - The foresight-token mechanism is a clean, well-motivated solution to a real tension in unified VLA models (semantic preservation vs. dynamics-prior injection vs. inference cost), and the ablation study (Table 8) provides direct causal evidence for each component's contribution. - Real-world evaluation explicitly separates seen from held-out instruction bindings (Figure 9) to test genuine compositional generalization rather than relying on aggregate success rate alone, which is more rigorous than most VLA papers' real-world evaluation design. - Consistent state-of-the-art or near-state-of-the-art results across six simulation benchmarks spanning different simulators, embodiments (single-arm, bimanual, mobile), and generalization regimes (zero-shot camera/lighting/layout shift, dynamic object interaction) demonstrates the approach is not overfit to one evaluation setting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Foresight supervision spans only the short horizon of one action chunk, so the policy absorbs local dynamics priors but does not perform long-horizon imagination or explicit planning with the world model. - The video generator stays frozen and generic, so the inherited priors are bounded by how well WAN2.2's pretraining covers embodied/robotic scenes specifically. The authors confront both limitations directly and frame them as future work rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No statistical uncertainty (seeds, confidence intervals) is reported for any result, simulation or real-world, despite several head-to-head margins being within a few points — this makes it hard to judge whether reported gains (e.g., the 75.9% vs. 77.8% Sort Tubes shortfall, or point-level RoboTwin gains over LingBot-VA) are robust or noise. - The alpha=1, beta=10 loss-weighting choice (Eq. 8) is stated but not ablated; given that objective interference between heterogeneous losses is explicitly called out as the problem this work solves, the specific weighting that resolves that interference deserves its own sensitivity analysis, which is absent. - Real-world comparisons are limited to two baselines (pi_0.5, Motus) on four author-designed tasks; there is no independent, third-party real-world benchmark, and the MOF long-horizon task (where the gap is largest, 76.4% vs. 29.3%/0%) is entirely self-designed, which is the single benchmark most susceptible to implicit tuning toward the proposed method's strengths.
Recommended 2607.04988 PDF
#52 — Innovative (67)

RynnWorld-4D: 4D Embodied World Models for Robotic Manipulation (72 ▲ )

Haoyu Zhao, Xingyue Zhao, Siteng Huang, Xin Li, Deli Zhao, Zhongyu Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RynnWorld-4D, a tri-branch diffusion model that co-generates future RGB, depth, and optical-flow video from a single RGB-D image and language instruction, plus RynnWorld-4D-Policy, an inverse-dynamics head that reads the model's internal 4D features to output robot actions in one forward pass. - Why it's important: 2D video world models lose 6-DoF spatial structure and produce temporally inconsistent object scales, which limits their usefulness for precise, dexterous, bimanual robot control. - How it does it: A frozen text cross-attention, per-modality branches with a Joint Cross-Modal Attention module (3D RoPE, tanh-gated zero-init output projection) inserted every three transformer blocks keep RGB, depth, and flow mutually consistent, and depth+flow back-project into metric 3D scene flow. - The good: On a 50-sequence world-modeling benchmark, RynnWorld-4D nearly doubles depth accuracy over the strongest prior 4D baseline (δ1 = 0.610 vs. 0.327 for 4DNeX) and is the only method reporting synchronized optical flow (AEPE 0.170), with matching ablations showing each design choice (Joint Cross-Modal Attention, RoPE, modality-specific FFN, modality adaptation stage) causally drives these gains. - The missing: All real-world manipulation results come from 35 trials per task on a single robot platform (TIANJI M6 + WUJI HAND) built and evaluated by the same lab that curated the training data, with no third-party replication, no cross-embodiment or cross-hardware test, and no confidence intervals on the 35-trial success rates. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building world models for robot policy learning, particularly the Joint Cross-Modal Attention design and the RGB-DF projective-4D-representation idea; the real-world numbers should be read as a single-lab proof of concept rather than an externally validated benchmark.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is solid: co-generating optical flow alongside RGB and depth inside one diffusion process is a real advance over TesserAct's static surface normals, and the RGB-DF projective representation is an economical way to add 3D grounding without abandoning a pretrained 2D video backbone. Non-Obviousness (68) is credited to the tanh-gated zero-init fix for Joint Cross-Modal Attention, which is specifically motivated by ControlNet's documented saddle-point deadlock rather than asserted. Completeness (72) reflects nine ablations, each isolating one design decision with large consistent effects (AEPE 0.170 to 0.729 without 4D pretraining), plus honest scope statements on the 9 Hz ceiling and single-view limitation. Rigor (62) and Resilience (60) are pulled down by a Goodhart concern — the 50-sequence world-model benchmark is a held-out split of the same sources used for training, not an independent benchmark — and by real-world results resting on 35 trials per task on the lab's own TIANJI M6 + WUJI HAND with no confidence intervals, while differences as small as 94.29% vs 91.43% are treated as meaningful. Impact (72) is the strongest dimension: released weights on GitHub/HuggingFace/ModelScope, a 254.4M-frame annotated dataset, and 9 Hz control on a single RTX 5090. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*72 = 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 72 Resilience 60 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Robotic manipulation policies that plan from generative video world models are limited by the 2D projective nature of pixel space: predicted object scales fluctuate and shapes morph unphysically across frames, and no explicit depth or motion signal is available to ground 6-DoF end-effector actions. Prior 3D/4D scene modeling (NeRF, 3D Gaussian Splatting) is either optimization-heavy and scene-specific, or feed-forward but object-centric and multi-view-dependent, and none of these readily inherits the strong generative priors of large pretrained 2D video diffusion models. The paper targets researchers building embodied world models and downstream manipulation policies who need a representation that is both physically grounded and compatible with pretrained video-diffusion backbones. Key Idea: Represent 4D scene evolution as three synchronized 2D-aligned video streams, RGB, depth, and optical flow (RGB-DF), rather than as explicit 3D volumes, Gaussians, or point clouds. Depth lifts pixels to 3D position and depth+flow together back-project into per-point metric 3D scene flow, so 3D geometry and motion are recoverable from a purely 2D-format generative process, letting the model directly extend a pretrained 2D video diffusion backbone (Wan 2.2) instead of building new 3D-native machinery. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Data curation: Rynn4DDataset 1.0 combines 254.4M frames from human egocentric video (Epic-Kitchens, EgoVid) and robot manipulation datasets (RoboMIND, RDT-1B, Galaxea, RoboCoin, AgiBot), auto-annotated with Qwen3-VL captions, DPFlow optical flow, and Depth Anything 3 monocular depth. (2) RynnWorld-4D: a tri-branch transformer (one branch per modality, each initialized from the same pretrained RGB backbone) with a Joint Cross-Modal Attention (JA) module inserted every 3 of 30 layers; each branch produces one query and a shared K/V pair, cutting cross-attention parameter cost from 18d² to 12d² per block, with 3D RoPE restricting attention to same-timestep tokens and a tanh-gated zero-initialized output projection (chosen over ControlNet's double zero-init to avoid a saddle-point deadlock). Training uses a 3-stage curriculum: Stage 1 trains branches independently (flow matching, λ_flow=0.5); Stage 2 freezes the backbone and trains only JA modules with branch dropout (p=0.2); Stage 3 unfreezes everything for full joint SFT (p=0.1). All stages share one Gaussian noise sample across modalities to keep denoising trajectories aligned. (3) RynnWorld-4D-Policy: a frozen RynnWorld-4D acts as a 4D vision encoder; a Flow Former (spatial cross-attention + temporal self-attention with learnable queries) compresses concatenated tri-branch hidden states into a fixed representation, which conditions a 4-step Euler-ODE flow-matching policy head producing 10-action chunks. Critical design decisions: sharing text K/V across branches to save parameters (reasonably justified, ablated indirectly via shared-FFN result), the zero-init+learnable-gate JA design (explicitly justified against ControlNet's known deadlock failure mode), and extracting policy features from a single diffusion timestep (t=500, block 15) rather than full denoising, which is the mechanism enabling 9 Hz control but is not itself ablated against alternative extraction timesteps/layers. Key Results: World modeling (50 held-out sequences from Rynn4DDataset's robot sources): RynnWorld-4D achieves δ1=0.610 depth accuracy and 0.170 AEPE optical flow error, versus 4DNeX (δ1=0.327), TesserAct (δ1=0.279, AEPE N/A), and Free4D (δ1=0.179); RGB fidelity (SSIM 0.754, PSNR 17.85) trails the larger Wan-2.1-I2V-14B on raw IQ (0.635 vs. 0.684) but the paper argues this is expected since RynnWorld-4D optimizes reconstruction fidelity, not creative generation. Real-world manipulation (six bimanual tasks, 35 trials each, TIANJI M6 + WUJI HAND): RynnWorld-4D-Policy averages higher success than Diffusion Policy, π0, and π0.5, with the largest gap on Hand-over (28.57% vs. 17.14% for DP, 2.86% for π0, 0.00% for π0.5) and ties or near-ties on Dual Picking (94.29%) and Block Pushing (97.14% vs. π0.5's 100.00%). Ablations show w/o 4D pretraining collapses AEPE from 0.170 to 0.729, w/o RynnWorld-4D (ResNet-18 encoder instead) drops Dual Picking success from 94.29% to 71.43%, and w/o RoPE in JA raises AEPE from 0.170 to 0.210. Baselines (DP, π0, π0.5) are current, published 2024-2025 methods, an appropriate and reasonably strong comparison set for the manipulation task, though all real-world numbers come from the same lab's hardware and data collection. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (tri-branch RGB-DF diffusion architecture with Joint Cross-Modal Attention), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (Rynn4DDataset 1.0), NEW FORMULATION (projective 4D representation with metric scene-flow back-projection). The genuinely novel piece is the co-generation of optical flow alongside RGB and depth within one diffusion process (prior closest work, TesserAct/RGB-DN, uses static surface normals instead of flow); the tri-branch-with-shared-cross-attention architecture pattern and the flow-matching policy head are largely standard components applied in a new combination. Rigor & Reproducibility: Extensive ablations are present (independent branches, w/o modality adaptation, w/o 4D pretraining, w/o RoPE in JA, shared FFN, and policy-side w/o RynnWorld-4D, RGB-only, RGB+Depth, RGB+Flow) and each isolates a specific design claim with matching directional results. However, no error bars, seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere, and world-modeling metrics (IQ, MS, SC, δ1, AEPE) are evaluated on a 50-sequence held-out set drawn from the same source datasets used for training (Rynn4DDataset), raising a Goodhart's-Law concern: the benchmark is close to in-distribution rather than an independent stress test. Real-world manipulation results (Table 5) come from a single lab's own hardware, own robot, and own data collection with no external dataset or third-party replication, and no code, weights, or dataset release date is confirmed in-text beyond listed GitHub/HuggingFace/ModelScope links (URLs given: alibaba-damo-academy.github.io/RynnWorld-4D, github.com/alibaba-damo-academy/RynnWorld-4D, huggingface.co/Alibaba-DAMO-Academy/RynnWorld-4D). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (with a SYSTEMS component for the real-time control claim). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU with FP8 quantization and FlashAttention 3, i.e., a high-end consumer/workstation GPU, not a data-center cluster. - Efficient: Full RynnWorld-4D forward pass costs 990 ms (89.5% of total 1,106 ms cycle), versus 85 ms for depth estimation and 8 ms for the action head; the tri-branch transformer itself remains the dominant cost, and no comparison to compute cost of purely 2D policies (DP, π0) is given. - Effective: Real-world success rates (65.71%-97.14% across six tasks) show the method solves the actual manipulation tasks, not just a visual-fidelity proxy, though "success within 120 seconds" over only 35 trials leaves wide confidence intervals unreported. - Scalable: Not tested. The paper trains on 254.4M frames and evaluates control at 9 Hz on 6 tasks; no experiment addresses 10x-100x scale in either dataset size, task diversity, or multi-robot/multi-view settings, and the authors explicitly flag multi-view/multi-robot generalization as future work. Software artifact: the paper states GitHub, HuggingFace, and ModelScope links for RynnWorld-4D, implying a released model/weights, though this analysis cannot verify current availability or license terms from the PDF alone. Strengths: - The RGB-DF projective representation is a genuinely economical way to add 3D grounding to a 2D video diffusion backbone without redesigning the generative architecture around explicit 3D structures (Gaussians, meshes, point clouds). - The zero-init-plus-learnable-gate fix for the Joint Cross-Modal Attention module is a specific, well-motivated architectural choice that explicitly addresses a documented failure mode (ControlNet's saddle-point deadlock) rather than being asserted without justification. - The ablation suite is unusually complete for a systems paper: nine separate ablations (five world-model-side, four policy-side) each isolate one architectural or training decision and show consistent, large effect sizes (e.g., AEPE 0.170 to 0.729 without 4D pretraining). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state two limitations directly: (1) the diffusion denoising process still introduces computational overhead, capping effective control frequency at ~9 Hz on an RTX 5090, insufficient for ultra-high-frequency control; (2) the model is optimized for egocentric single-view perspectives, and extending 4D consistency to multi-view or multi-robot collaborative setups is left as future work. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - World-model evaluation (Table 4) is conducted on a held-out split of the same source datasets used for pretraining (Rynn4DDataset sources), which is a weaker generalization test than an independent benchmark; the paper does not report results on a fully external 4D benchmark. - Real-world manipulation trials (Table 5) use only 35 trials per task on a single custom hardware platform (TIANJI M6 + WUJI HAND) built by the same group; no cross-embodiment, cross-lab, or cross-hardware validation is presented, and success rates lack confidence intervals despite differences as small as a few percentage points being highlighted as meaningful (e.g., 94.29% vs. 91.43%). - The policy extracts features at a single fixed diffusion timestep (t=500) and layer (block 15); this specific choice is stated but not ablated against alternatives, so it is unclear how sensitive downstream success rates are to this hyperparameter. - The 9 Hz "real-time" claim is qualified by 10-step action chunking executed open-loop between planning cycles; the paper's own robustness argument (objects bumped mid-chunk are corrected only at the next 9 Hz update) is plausible but not stress-tested with adversarial perturbations or quantified recovery-time data. Bottom Line: A practitioner building generative world models for robot control gains a concrete, well-ablated architecture (RGB-DF tri-branch diffusion with Joint Cross-Modal Attention) and a large new 4D-annotated dataset, but the real-world success numbers should be treated as a single-lab proof of concept pending independent replication on different hardware.
Recommended 2607.06559 PDF
#53 — Innovative (67)

Fast Rashomon Sets of Sparse Rule Sets

C Molero-Río, B Li, T Wang, C Rudin · link.springer.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FastSRS, a simulated-annealing algorithm that uses three theoretical bounds to prune the search space and efficiently generate large Rashomon sets of multi-value sparse rule sets (SRS). - Why it's important: Interactive interpretable-model workflows need many near-optimal models for humans to explore and negotiate constraints on, but generating even one high-quality sparse rule set is a hard combinatorial problem, and generating hundreds is normally computationally prohibitive. - How it does it: Three theorems bound which rules can ever appear in an optimal rule set (by negative support, positive support, and total rule count), shrinking the candidate rule space before and during a guided simulated-annealing search over multi-value rule sets. - The good: Against ERS, the only other Rashomon-set method for rule sets, FastSRS produces larger, more accurate, and sparser Rashomon sets in seconds to minutes versus ERS's 2 days to 2 weeks on three larger datasets (recidivism, invehicle, adult). - The missing: The paper never reports how close its bound-restricted search actually gets to the true unrestricted Rashomon set (no exhaustive-search ground truth on any real dataset), so completeness of the reported Rashomon sets is asserted by pointer to an appendix, not demonstrated in the main text. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or using Rashomon-set / interpretable-ML tooling, since the pruning theorems are a generalizable technique; less essential for practitioners who only need a single sparse rule set, where FastSRS is a modest, not dramatic, improvement over BRCG/RIPPER.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (algorithmic contribution with theorems, but the claims are carried by experiments). Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) rest on three closed-form, provable rule-elimination bounds derived from the regularized SRS objective, which is a sharper contribution than a generic pruning heuristic; the simulated-annealing machinery around them is standard. Rigor (70) has 5-fold CV, 6 single-model baselines, released code, and an ablation isolating the static bounds, but no variance is reported across SA random seeds despite the method being inherently stochastic, and no guided-vs-uniform proposal ablation separates the bounds' contribution from the proposal design. Completeness (60) is the main drag: the paper never verifies against an exhaustive Rashomon set that the bound-restricted search does not silently drop legitimate near-optimal models, which is the load-bearing claim of a Rashomon-set method, and that gap also holds Resilience at 60. Impact (70) credits the orders-of-magnitude speedup over ERS (days-to-weeks down to seconds-to-minutes) and a public repo, discounted for a niche user base. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*70 = 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practitioners building interpretable rule-set (DNF, "OR of ANDs") classifiers for high-stakes domains (healthcare, criminal justice, credit) face an interaction bottleneck: the first sparse model an algorithm outputs is rarely acceptable to a domain expert, and fixing it by reformulating the objective and rerunning optimization can take weeks per iteration. The Rashomon set paradigm addresses this by generating many near-optimal models up front for a human to browse interactively, but naively enumerating near-optimal rule sets is far more expensive than finding one optimal model, and existing rule-set learners (mathematical programming, sampling, greedy heuristics) already struggle to find a single high-quality sparse model on datasets of even moderate size. Multi-value rule sets (allowing disjunctions inside a single condition, e.g., color = red OR blue) improve the achievable accuracy-sparsity trade-off over single-value rules, but cannot be built by pre-mining rules with off-the-shelf frequent-pattern miners the way single-value rule sets can, so the search must operate at the level of individual conditions, which blows up the combinatorial space further. Key Idea: Three closed-form bounds derived directly from the regularized objective (loss + per-condition/rule/value sparsity penalties) certify that any rule with negative support size above N+/N − (C1+C2+C3), or positive support size below len(r)·C1 + C2 + val(r)·C3, or any rule set exceeding L[t]_BestSoFar / (C1+C2+C3) rules, cannot appear in an optimal solution. These bounds let the algorithm discard the overwhelming majority of the frequent-pattern-mined candidate rule pool before and during search, rather than having the optimizer discover infeasibility empirically. Methodology: Training has two stages. Stage 1 mines candidate rules via FP-growth over binarized (decile-split numeric, one-hot categorical, plus negations) features, using a minimum positive support threshold set to the max of a user threshold and the Theorem-2 bound, then discards remaining rules violating the Theorem-1 negative-support bound. Stage 2 runs simulated annealing (SA) over rule sets, minimizing L(R) = misclassification rate/N + C1·n_condition(R) + C2·n_rule(R) + C3·n_value(R), with a temperature schedule T[t] = T0/(1+t). The proposal mechanism is guided rather than uniformly random: at each step a misclassified example is sampled (biased toward examples near the decision boundary, based on historical mis/well-classification counts after the first 30 iterations), and the move set (add/remove rule, condition, or value) is chosen conditional on whether that example is a false negative or false positive, with Theorem 3's dynamic rule-count bound gating whether AddRule is even offered. A warm-start variant runs SA on a data subsample first for large datasets. The Rashomon set is simply every sampled rule set across the run whose objective is within a factor (1+ε) of the best found (equation 3), so the ε-Rashomon set is a byproduct of the single annealing trajectory rather than a separately targeted enumeration. The three design decisions (static bounds from mining-time theorems, guided rather than uniform proposals, and defining Rashomon membership post hoc from the sampled trajectory) are reasonably justified: the ablation (Fig. 1) empirically confirms the static bounds cut runtime substantially without hurting accuracy, and the guided proposal choice is standard SA practice for combinatorial objectives, but the paper does not compare guided vs. uniform proposal sampling as an ablation, so its specific contribution to search efficiency (versus the bounds) is not isolated. Key Results: On 7 public datasets (heart N=297, diabetes N=768, compas N=6907, fico N=10459, recidivism N=11645, invehicle N=12079, adult N=32561), the static bounds (Theorems 1-2) reduce runtime by 0.84%-36.72% across datasets with accuracy differences of only -1.16% to +2.37% (Fig. 1a), and on adult with varying C2 the candidate rule pool shrinks from roughly 14,000 to near-zero viable rules at higher C2 (Fig. 1b). Against 6 baselines (BRCG, BRS, MRS, LIBRE, LIBRE3, RIPPER) for single-model quality, FastSRS matches or exceeds test accuracy at comparable sparsity (≤12 conditions) on all 7 datasets, with RIPPER competitive on 4/7 datasets but using less sparse rule sets, and BRS/LIBRE/LIBRE3 generally worse on both accuracy and sparsity or speed. Against ERS (Ciaperoni et al., 2024), the only other Rashomon-set-for-rule-sets method, FastSRS produces Rashomon sets with higher test accuracy and fewer conditions on recidivism, invehicle, and adult (Fig. 5a) while running orders of magnitude faster (Fig. 5b: ERS took between 2 days and 2 weeks to hit a time limit on these datasets vs. FastSRS's fast completion); Rashomon-set sizes average above 15 models across all tested ε and C settings when allowed to run the full 500 iterations (Fig. 5c). No baselines are described as outdated per se, though BRCG/BRS/RIPPER are all 2017-2020-era methods; ERS (2024) is the contemporaneous comparator for the paper's core Rashomon-set claim. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the bound-guided SA algorithm for multi-value SRS) and NEW FORMULATION (framing Rashomon-set generation for rule sets as a search-space-pruning problem via provable rule-elimination theorems rather than exhaustive or branch-and-bound enumeration). The three theorems are genuinely novel contributions (closed-form, provable bounds on optimal-rule support and rule-set size derived from the regularized objective); the SA search machinery itself (guided proposals, warm start, temperature schedule) is a standard combination of established techniques applied to a new setting (multi-value rule Rashomon sets).
