Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports 5-fold cross-validation across all 7 datasets, and results in Figs. 1-5 are averaged with variability implied by violin/box plots (though no explicit confidence intervals or seed counts are stated for the SA stochasticity itself — SA is inherently randomized and no report of variance across SA random seeds is given, only across CV folds). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/mmolerous/FastSRS. Ablations isolate the effect of the static bounds specifically (Fig. 1), which is a genuine methodological strength. A concern: the paper does not evaluate against an exhaustive or brute-force Rashomon set on any dataset to verify the bounds do not silently exclude legitimate near-optimal models; completeness analysis is deferred to Appendix K/M, not shown in the main body, and the reviewer cannot independently verify the claim that the pruned Rashomon sets are representative of the true (unrestricted) set. All comparisons use the authors' own hyperparameter grids for baselines (detailed in an appendix per the text), which is standard but self-selected. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile (methods and experiments the paper actually runs). - Practical: Yes; experiments ran on a single 48-core, 768GB-RAM Xeon workstation using at most 100GB, hardware within reach of any research lab, not requiring specialized accelerators. - Efficient: Substantially cheaper than ERS (2 days-2 weeks vs. FastSRS's runtime, unspecified exactly but implied to be under the 2-day cutoff by a wide margin per Fig. 5b); FastSRS is also faster than most single-model rule-set baselines on the largest datasets (Fig. 4). - Effective: Test accuracy is comparable to or better than baselines at matched sparsity across all 7 datasets (Figs. 2-3), and the case study (heart dataset, Fig. 6) shows plausible, board-consistent rules (oldpeak, thal, slope match known coronary-risk factors), supporting real-world validity beyond a pure accuracy proxy. - Scalable: Tested up to N=32,561 (adult) with 715 binarized attributes (invehicle); the paper does not test at 10x-100x this scale (e.g., N in the millions or thousands of attributes), and the paper's own Sect. 6 flags binarization/discretization granularity as an open scalability bottleneck for the whole logical-model-class approach, so scalability beyond the tested regime is an acknowledged open question rather than a demonstrated result. FastSRS delivers a working software artifact (public GitHub repo) rather than stopping at paper form. Strengths: - The three support-size and rule-count theorems are a clean, provable reduction of the Rashomon-set search space specific to the SRS objective structure, not a generic pruning heuristic; this is the paper's most defensible novel contribution. - Demonstrated 2-14 day speedup over the only competing Rashomon-set-for-rule-sets method (ERS) while simultaneously improving accuracy and sparsity, a rare case of a Pareto improvement across all three axes rather than a runtime-for-quality trade. - Extension to multi-value rules (conditions with internal disjunctions), which the paper argues (and Wang, 2018 previously showed) improves the sparsity-accuracy trade-off over single-value rule sets, executed at the condition level rather than via pre-mined multi-value rules, which is algorithmically harder and mostly unaddressed in prior rule-set literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state (Sect. 6) that binarization/discretization granularity is an open challenge shared by all logical model classes, that finer discretization makes Rashomon sets larger and computation harder, and that they have not solved this. They also note their specific SA proposal/sampling strategy worked for their experiments but "other sampling approaches may also be valuable,
Search Results
#54 — Innovative (66)

Procedural Memory Distillation: Online Reflection for Self-Improving Language Models

Y Liu, S Bansal, B Pang, Y Li, ZL Liu, Y Ming, Z Ke… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Procedural Memory Distillation (PMD), a self-distillation training framework that converts a policy's own repeated rollouts into a three-level memory (experience, insight, behavior) and distills that memory back into the policy's weights during training, yielding a memory-free model at inference. - Why it's important: Standard RLVR and on-policy self-distillation methods like SDPO treat every training episode as independent, discarding cross-episode signals about which strategies work and which failure modes recur, even though the model faces the same or related problems repeatedly across epochs under a changing policy. - How it does it: A memory-conditioned teacher, refreshed online from the current policy's own trajectories via reflection (experience to insight) and clustering-based abstraction (insight to behavior), supervises the student via reverse-KL self-distillation on the same rollout states, with policy and memory co-evolving jointly at every update step. - The good: Ablations (Table 3) isolate co-evolution as the actual driver of the gains: a frozen memory bank built once and then held fixed trails full PMD by more than 10 percentage points on SciKnowEval (65.0 vs. 77.2 AVG), ruling out the alternative explanation that a static richer prompt alone would suffice. - The missing: All evaluation is confined to two verifiable, problem-local domains (science MCQ and code generation) with repeated exposure to the same or related problems across epochs; the paper explicitly does not test heterogeneous, long-horizon agentic settings where memory would need to transfer across dissimilar tasks. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on on-policy self-distillation (SDPO, OPSD) or agent memory systems, since it gives a rare ablation-driven answer to "does persistence or co-evolution matter more" that most memory-agent papers skip; skippable for readers only interested in inference-time memory-augmented agents, since PMD's memory is training-only and discarded at deployment.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) are the strengths: the four-variant decomposition (PMD-Transient, evolving-memory/frozen-policy, frozen-memory/evolving-policy, full) isolates co-evolution rather than persistence or richer prompting as the causal driver, which is a level of mechanism attribution most memory-agent papers skip, and the internalization probes credibly rule out memory leakage at inference. Rigor (62) is the limiter: no seeds or error bars anywhere, which matters because the PMD-Transient versus SDPO gap is only +1.3pp, and the memory extractor is the student model itself with no test of extractor-capability sensitivity. Resilience (65) and Impact (55) are constrained by two domains, a single 7-8B scale band, and an artifact that is training-only (the memory bank is discarded at deployment). Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*55 = 66.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and related on-policy self-distillation methods (PPO, DPO, GRPO, SDPO), each rollout is scored by a verifier and the policy is updated from that episode-level signal alone, then the rollout is discarded. In practice, however, training revisits the same or closely related problems across epochs under a continually updating policy, so there is a real cross-episode learning opportunity that current pipelines forfeit: which strategies consistently pass verification, which failure modes persist, and which behavioral patterns recur across problems. This gap matters for anyone training LLMs with RLVR-style pipelines on repeated task distributions (math, science QA, code generation), since episode-local updates cannot capture patterns visible only when attempts are aggregated over time. Key Idea: Repeated training-time experience can be organized into a memory hierarchy (raw trajectories to problem-level insight to cross-problem behavior) that conditions a self-teacher, and if the policy and this memory are updated jointly rather than one being frozen, the teacher signal stays aligned with the learner's current strengths and failure modes throughout training. The central bet is that co-evolution, not persistence or richer context alone, is what makes accumulated experience actionable. Methodology: PMD extends SDPO's student/self-teacher setup with a four-stage online loop, run at each training update step t: (1) the student policy π_θt attempts a mini-batch of problems and receives verifier reward and feedback per rollout; (2) an asynchronous reflection module updates experience memory (bounded per-problem store of up to 5 successful and 3 failed response-feedback pairs, deduplicated via a cosine-similarity novelty gate) and, contrastively when both successful and failed rollouts exist, extracts insight memory (strategies from successes, lessons from failures) for that problem; (3) periodically, every K update steps, insight memories from semantically clustered problems (via Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B encoding) are abstracted by an LLM into a global behavior bank (capped at 500 entries, maintained by a retrieve-then-decide add/update/remove policy rather than unbounded accumulation); (4) the memory-conditioned teacher q_θt is built by reprompting the frozen (stopgrad) current policy with the original problem plus retrieved experience, insight, and top-K=3 behavior entries, and the student is trained via the same reverse-KL self-distillation loss as SDPO (Eq. 7), matching teacher and student token distributions on the student's own visited states. Three design decisions stand out: (a) the contrastive insight extraction (comparing successful vs. failed attempts side-by-side) plus explicit anti-shortcut filtering rules, justified empirically by showing shortcut-like insight items dropped from ~39% to ~3% of extracted items on early SciKnowEval-biology runs; (b) behavior memory retrieval-then-decide rather than naive append-only growth, justified as keeping the bank curated, though no ablation isolates this specific mechanism against naive accumulation; (c) memory is used only on the teacher path and never given to the student directly, so any benefit must come through distillation into weights, which the paper separately verifies (Appendix A.2-A.3) via keyword-frequency and discourse-structure probes on memory-free decoded outputs. Key Results: On SciKnowEval avg@16 (biology, chemistry, physics, materials; multiple-choice, extracted-letter scoring), PMD improves over SDPO from 74.4 to 77.2 AVG on Qwen3-8B (+3.8% relative) and from 69.5 to 73.3 on OLMo3-Instruct-7B (+5.5% relative); on LiveCodeBench score@4 (execution-based unit tests), PMD improves from 47.9 to 51.7 on Qwen3-8B (+7.9% relative) and from 45.0 to 51.1 on OLMo3-Instruct-7B (+13.6% relative). Baselines are GRPO (sparse-reward RL) and SDPO (feedback-conditioned self-distillation without procedural memory); both are recent (2024-2026) and directly comparable since PMD reuses the SDPO backbone and training setup. The decomposition study (Table 3) shows PMD-Transient (memory built and discarded within a single step, isolating reflection alone) still beats SDPO by +1.3pp SciKnowEval AVG and +0.2pp LiveCodeBench, while adding persistence (full PMD) adds a further +1.5pp and +3.6pp respectively; Evolving-Memory+Frozen-Policy (memory alone, no policy updates) barely moves the needle (54.0/35.9 vs. base policy 47.9/27.7), and Frozen-Memory+Evolving-Policy (memory built once, then policy trained against it) reaches 65.0/47.5, well short of full PMD's 77.2/51.7 — establishing co-evolution, not memory or policy updates alone, as the dominant mechanism. Test-time scaling analysis (Fig. 3-4) shows PMD's maj@k-to-best@k verifier headroom band is 2-4x wider than SDPO's and PMD solves 9-14% more unique problems than SDPO across subjects, while SDPO's solved set collapses (best@16 = maj@16 on Materials, a mode-collapse signature). Compute overhead is 1.45x SDPO step time with negligible extra peak GPU memory (+0.3GB), measured on 8xH200. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the three-level online procedural memory hierarchy and its co-evolving distillation loop) combined with a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (that co-evolution, not memory persistence or richer prompting alone, drives the gains, isolated via controlled ablations in Table 3). The building blocks — on-policy self-distillation, reflection into strategies/lessons, semantic clustering, retrieval-augmented context — are individually established (SDPO, Reflexion, RAG, skill-library agents); PMD's genuine contribution is the specific online co-evolution mechanism plus the ablation design that isolates its source, not the memory-hierarchy concept itself, which closely follows prior reflection/skill-library work (cited directly, e.g., ReasoningBank, SKILL0, Skill-SD). Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are substantive and central to the paper's claims: memory-granularity ablation (Table 4: experience-only vs. +insight vs. +behavior), the co-evolution decomposition (Table 3: PMD-Transient, Evolving-Memory+Frozen-Policy, Frozen-Memory+Evolving-Policy), and a shortcut-contamination audit (39% to 3% after anti-shortcut rules). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the main results (Table 2) or ablations — all numbers appear to be single-run point estimates, a meaningful gap given the pp differences between methods (e.g., PMD-Transient vs. SDPO is only +1.3pp on SciKnowEval). No code, data, or model weights release URL is mentioned in the text provided. Evaluation is on two public, standard benchmarks (SciKnowEval, LiveCodeBench) with execution-based or ground-truth scoring, avoiding the self-reported-vendor-numbers concern, and LiveCodeBench is explicitly contamination-aware; however, the memory extraction pipeline uses the student model itself as its own memory extractor, which is not obviously biased but is also not independently validated against an external judge. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (all experiments are actually run on two policies across two benchmarks). - Practical: Yes, trained and evaluated on 8xH200 GPUs with a 7-8B parameter policy, within reach of a well-resourced academic or industry lab, not requiring exotic infrastructure. - Efficient: PMD costs 1.45x SDPO's step time (53.6s vs. 37.0s median) with only +0.3GB peak GPU memory, driven by longer teacher prompts after memory injection; the memory bank itself is stored off-GPU as text/embeddings. - Effective: Evidence directly targets the stated problem (does procedural memory improve on SDPO across two verifiable domains), with consistent gains across two model families and two benchmark types; internalization is checked via probes (keyword frequency, discourse structure) that credibly rule out simple memory leakage at inference, since the final student is evaluated with no memory access. - Scalable: Not tested at 10x-100x scale. Memory-transfer experiments (Fig. 2) test cross-model-scale generalization from Qwen3-8B-derived memory to models from 1.7B to 32B, but do not test larger training runs, larger memory banks, or more training epochs/problems than the current setup (up to 20 epochs, ~450-1890 problems per subject); the paper does not claim scalability beyond this regime. The work delivers a trained-model artifact (a policy with internalized procedural reasoning) plus a training methodology; no software release is mentioned, and the memory bank is explicitly discarded at inference, so the delivered artifact is the distilled policy weights, not a reusable memory system. Strengths: - The co-evolution ablation (Table 3, four controlled variants) is a genuine methodological contribution beyond the main result: it isolates which of three plausible mechanisms (reflection, persistence, co-evolution) actually drives the gain, a level of decomposition rarely seen in memory-agent papers that typically report only the headline comparison. - The test-time scaling analysis (Fig. 3-4) provides a second, independent line of evidence beyond avg@16 accuracy: showing SDPO experiences answer-space collapse (best@16 = maj@16 on Materials) while PMD preserves 9-14% more uniquely-solved problems is a distinct and more diagnostic result than the top-line accuracy numbers alone. - The internalization probes (Appendix A.2-A.3: keyword frequency tracking, discourse-structure hand-annotation of matched SDPO/PMD responses) are a serious attempt to verify the paper's central claim that memory is actually absorbed into weights rather than leaking through some other channel, addressing a specific known failure mode from cited prior work (self-distilled RLVR can pathologically memorize privileged teacher information). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that their evaluation is confined to repeated training over fixed task distributions where problem-local experience accumulates across epochs, which is narrower than long-horizon agentic environments requiring memory, tools, and skills to evolve across heterogeneous tasks; they explicitly disclaim that PMD "fully solves online agent self-improvement" and frame extending problem-local memory to broader cross-task behavior retrieval as future work. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No error bars or repeated-seed variance are reported anywhere, despite several reported gains (e.g., PMD-Transient vs. SDPO at +1.3pp) being small enough that run-to-run noise could plausibly explain part of the effect. - The insight and behavior extraction pipeline uses the same student model as its own memory extractor; the paper does not test whether extractor quality (e.g., swapping in a stronger or weaker LLM as extractor) changes results, leaving open whether gains are sensitive to extractor capability rather than the PMD architecture itself. - Only two model families (Qwen3-8B, OLMo3-Instruct-7B) and two domains are tested; both are within a similar 7-8B parameter range, so it is unstated whether PMD's benefits hold at meaningfully larger (70B+) or smaller (<1B) scales for the policy actually being trained (as opposed to the memory-transfer target models in Fig. 2, which test transfer, not PMD training, at other scales). - The retrieve-then-decide behavior-bank curation mechanism (add/update/remove) is presented as important for avoiding unbounded, uncurated growth, but no ablation isolates its contribution versus a naive append-only bank capped at 500 entries. Bottom Line: A practitioner building on-policy self-distillation pipelines (SDPO-style) for repeated-exposure training gains a concrete, ablation-supported answer that co-evolving memory and policy outperforms either frozen memory or frozen policy alone, though the lack of variance reporting and the narrow two-domain, single-scale evaluation mean the specific percentage gains should be treated as directional rather than tightly bounded.
Recommended 2607.01480 PDF
#55 — Innovative (66)

Learning from Failure: Inference-Time Self-Improvement for Computer-Use Agents

X Sun, X Wang, L Schmidt, S Yeung-Levy, Y Zhang · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a failure-case self-improvement loop that uses an LLM to diagnose failed computer-use agent trajectories, classify them into four failure categories, and generate lightly-human-verified inference-time patches (visual search, terminal execution, knowledge support, repetition warnings) that raise OpenCUA-72B's OSWorld success rate from 42.3% to 48.9%. - Why it's important: Building verifiable environments for computer-use agents is expensive, and standard self-improving loops throw away every failed trajectory even though failures are the majority of collected data and carry diagnostic signal. - How it does it: An LLM meta-controller (Claude 4.5 Sonnet) reads failed trajectories with their instruction, action history, and thought process, assigns each to one of four recurring failure modes, and emits code-level patches applied at inference time, with human review limited to lightweight verification (authors report >97% of proposed refinements accepted unmodified). - The good: The gain generalizes without retraining across three backbones of different scale/origin (OpenCUA-72B, OpenCUA-32B, GUI-Owl-32B, all +10-12% relative) and across four heterogeneous benchmarks (OmniACT, AndroidControl, ScreenSpotPro, WebVoyager) using patches mined solely from OSWorld failures. - The missing: The four failure-mode patches (visual search, terminal execution, knowledge support, repetition detection) were hand-designed by the authors after inspecting OSWorld failures specifically, and the paper does not test whether a fifth round of the loop on a new agent/benchmark would discover materially different failure categories or whether the fixed strategy set saturates. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building computer-use/GUI agents who want a cheap, no-retraining lever on OSWorld-style benchmarks; the mechanism (four hand-crafted inference-time interventions) is narrow enough that it reads more as an effective engineering playbook than a general algorithm for failure mining.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is the largest contributor and is the paper's real strength: mean+/-std over 3 runs on the primary OSWorld result, a per-strategy ablation, a meta-controller comparison across four LLMs, and cross-benchmark transfer with 3-run statistics on three of four benchmarks. Impact (70) reflects a no-retraining lever that moves OpenCUA-72B from 42.3 to 48.9 and generalizes to two other backbones and four heterogeneous benchmarks. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) are moderate: the four interventions (zoomed click verification, terminal hotkey, retrieval, repetition detection) are standard GUI-agent engineering, and the genuinely new claim is that failed trajectories carry transferable diagnostic signal. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are limited by the failure taxonomy being hand-derived from OSWorld specifically, with no test of whether a new domain would surface different categories or whether the fixed strategy set saturates, and by the cross-model numbers being single-run. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*70 = 66.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Computer-use agents built on multimodal LLMs are typically improved via a "successful-case loop": roll out trajectories in a verifiable environment, keep only the successful ones, and fine-tune on them. Verifiable environments are costly to build, and once built, most of the trajectories they produce (the failed ones) are discarded outright. The paper targets teams that already have a working agent and a verifiable environment (e.g., OSWorld) but want additional performance without collecting more human demonstrations or paying for additional fine-tuning runs. Key Idea: Failed trajectories are not noise but a structured diagnostic signal: feeding them to an LLM meta-controller with full context (instruction, action history, thought trace) lets the LLM cluster them into a small number of recurring, addressable failure modes and propose targeted, reusable inference-time fixes rather than fine-tuning updates. The novelty is treating failure analysis as the training signal (via prompt-level and tool-level patches) instead of treating failures as discardable, and closing the loop with a human-in-the-loop but LLM-driven patch-generation cycle repeated over multiple rounds. Methodology: The pipeline runs in rounds: (1) Failure Experience Collection — roll out the agent in OSWorld, use the environment's built-in reward function to isolate failed trajectories; (2) LLM-Guided Diagnosis — Claude 4.5 Sonnet analyzes each failed trajectory's instruction, action history, and thought process, classifying it into one of four failure modes (grounding errors, competency gaps, knowledge deficiencies, redundant loops); (3) Patch Synthesis — the LLM proposes a corresponding intervention per failure mode and generates code-like patches, which a human lightly verifies/selects before injection into the agent's inference-time behavior; (4) iterate for four rounds, each round targeting the then-dominant failure mode. The four concrete interventions are: visual search (crop a 400×400 region around a predicted click, upscale 2×, mark with a red circle, and have the agent verify/adjust the click before committing), terminal execution (a dedicated Ctrl+Alt+T hotkey plus rules requiring syntax verification via search before running commands), knowledge support (a software-manual retriever plus a GPT-5-mini-backed search-engine interface for procedural knowledge outside the agent's context), and repetition warnings (a sliding-window detector over thought, action, and compressed screen-state representations that triggers a "recovery mode" prompting the agent to switch strategy). Key design decisions: (a) using an LLM rather than a fixed rule set to both discover failure categories and write the patches — justified empirically by an ablation and a meta-controller comparison (GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Flash, Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct were tried; Claude 4.5 Sonnet won on comprehensiveness and code quality) but not justified by any formal criterion for why four categories is the right granularity; (b) keeping human review "lightweight" (candidate selection only, >97% of patches accepted unmodified per the authors) which trades rigor for cost but is not independently audited; (c) applying strategies cumulatively across rounds rather than jointly optimizing them, which the ablation shows is not strictly additive (e.g., knowledge support alone underperforms the full method's per-component contribution implied by Table 1's totals). Key Results: On OSWorld (100-step evaluation), OpenCUA-72B improves from 42.3±2.6 to 48.9±1.2 (+6.6 absolute, +15.6% relative), with ~8% inference-overhead increase and ~15% fewer interaction steps. On the OSWorld small set (30-step, used for ablation), the individual strategies raise the 41.67 baseline to 47.22 (visual search), 44.40 (repetition detection), 47.19 (terminal execution), and 44.44 (knowledge support), with the full combination reaching 52.74 — showing the components are complementary but not simply additive (some individual gains exceed 5 points while the combined gain is under 11 points from a sum of ~4×5 points). Cross-model generalization: OpenCUA-32B rises from 34.5 to 38.2 and GUI-Owl-32B from 19.0 to 21.3 (single-run, resource-constrained; asterisked in Table 2). Against the broader leaderboard, OpenCUA-72B+Ours (48.9) beats proprietary baselines Claude 3.7 Sonnet (28.0), OpenAI CUA-4o (38.1), UI-TARS-1.5 (42.5), and OpenAI CUA-o3 (42.9), and open-source baselines up to UI-TARS-72B-DPO (26.9) and Agent S2 w/ Gemini-2.5-Pro (41.4). Cross-benchmark transfer of OSWorld-mined patches (backbone Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct): OmniACT 4.77→6.90, AndroidControl 28.37→36.23 (+7.86), ScreenSpotPro 27.50→30.74, WebVoyager 23.80→27.90 (+4.10), each with 3-run mean±std except WebVoyager (single run reported without std explanation). Baselines are current (2024-2025 systems including UI-TARS, Agent S2, GUI-Owl) and the OSWorld comparison set is broad, though several of the strongest recent systems (e.g., latest UI-TARS variants beyond 1.5) are compared only via published numbers, not re-run under identical conditions. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the failure-case self-improvement loop and its four constituent inference-time strategies) combined with NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (failed trajectories, when LLM-analyzed, transfer as generalizable error-pattern signal across models and benchmarks). The four individual interventions (zoomed-in click verification, terminal hotkey shortcuts, retrieval-augmented knowledge lookup, repetition detection with recovery mode) are largely standard engineering techniques from GUI-agent and RAG literature; the genuinely new contribution is the LLM-driven diagnose-and-patch loop that discovers which of these to apply and when, using failure trajectories as the sole training signal at zero additional training cost. Rigor & Reproducibility: An ablation (Table 1) isolates each of the four strategies' individual contribution on the OSWorld small set, and the main OSWorld result (Table 2) reports mean±std over 3 independent runs for the primary model (though GUI-Owl-32B and OpenCUA-32B report single-run asterisked numbers "due to resource constraints,
Search Results 2606.31270 PDF
#56 — Innovative (66)

SkillFuzz: Fuzzing Skill Composition for Implicit Intents Discovery in Open Skill Marketplaces

J Hu, Y Dong, Y Sun, X Huang · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillFuzz, an execution-free fuzzing framework that treats an LLM agent's plan-level output as a differential oracle to discover "implicit intents," unintended objectives that emerge only when individually benign skills are co-activated in an open skill marketplace. - Why it's important: Marketplace operators audit each community-contributed skill in isolation because deployment environments to test every combination are unavailable or too costly, leaving compositional risks (e.g., an agent silently exfiltrating data or creating unauthorized files) undetected until real users trigger them. - How it does it: SkillFuzz extracts structured skill contracts (preconditions, postconditions, modifies-set, invariants, domain scope) via an LLM, embeds them in a semantic space to prioritize conflict-prone skill combinations, then runs Monte Carlo Tree Search over the binary activation space using plan drift (semantic distance between a baseline plan and the co-activated plan) as a proxy for compositional risk, scored by a novelty-aware Intent Coverage Quality (ICQ) metric. - The good: Over 80% of the 98 highest-risk flagged skill compositions were confirmed to actually manifest when executed in a sandboxed Docker environment across two architecturally distinct planner families (DS-R1 and GPT), showing the plan-level oracle is not a planner-specific artifact. - The missing: The method cannot detect a deceptively misaligned agent that states an innocuous plan while executing a different objective, since the oracle only reasons over the declared plan text, and the marketplace is a single 196-skill benchmark whose contract geometry may not generalize to larger, more adversarial libraries. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers or practitioners building or auditing agentic skill marketplaces (ClawHub-style plugin ecosystems); less relevant to readers focused on single-agent prompt injection or classical program fuzzing, since the failure mode here is purely compositional and semantic.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (75) are the strengths: compositional implicit intent from co-activated benign skills is a genuinely distinct failure class from prompt injection, and using plan drift as an execution-free differential oracle is a non-obvious reframing rather than an off-the-shelf application. Rigor (62) is the limiter: real ablations (contract-guided vs. random, ICQ vs. drift-only vs. coverage-only) and an 80.6% execution-validation study across two planner families are strong, but three thresholds are fixed globally with no sensitivity analysis, GPT-4o-mini is both intent extractor and judge, Table I/III report single-run point estimates with no variance, and no code or data release is stated. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) suffer from the same threshold-Goodhart exposure plus a single 196-skill benchmark whose contract geometry the authors admit may not generalize. Impact (50) reflects a described methodology with no released artifact and no cost/latency figures for marketplace-admission-time deployment. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*50 = 66.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Open skill marketplaces (e.g., ClawHub) let users assemble LLM agents from community-contributed natural-language instruction documents ("skills") that are jointly injected into an agent's context at inference time. Marketplace operators can only feasibly audit each skill individually for malicious content; they cannot execute or review every combinatorial co-activation, an exponential space. The paper identifies a risk gap: skills that are individually safe can jointly redirect the agent's planning toward unintended objectives ("implicit intents"), such as an agent that reads local data and sends it to a third-party API when two data-processing skills are combined, even though neither skill alone raises an audit flag. This differs from prompt injection or red-teaming, which presuppose an explicit adversarial payload; here no single skill carries malicious content. Key Idea: The plan-then-act architecture common to agentic LLM systems externalizes the agent's belief state as a natural-language plan before any tool executes. This plan artifact is treated as an observable surface: comparing the plan produced under a skill-free baseline activation to the plan produced under a candidate co-activation (via embedding-based semantic distance, termed "plan drift") gives a deployment-free, execution-free differential oracle for detecting compositional side effects, without needing to run the agent or its tools. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. (1) Skill Contract Construction & Pruning: for each skill, an LLM extracts a structured contract C(s_i) = (preconditions, postconditions, modifies-set, invariants, domain scope, abstract action types, extraction confidence), embedded via a sentence encoder (all-mpnet-base-v2) into a semantic space; the full library is pruned to a task-relevant candidate set via a relevance-similarity threshold, and the top-m conflict-prone pairs (by shared modifies-sets and mutually exclusive invariants) seed the search. (2) Differential Activation Search: an MCTS loop over the binary skill-activation space, where each node is a skill activation vector, each edge is a single-skill bit-flip, and UCB1 balances exploiting high-reward regions against exploring under-visited ones. Node expansion is contract-guided: it computes a semantic centroid of active skill contracts and steers the next bit-flip toward the embedding region of the most recently discovered intent. For each candidate activation, K=3 plan samples are drawn from the black-box planning agent, plan drift δ is computed against the skill-free baseline, and if δ exceeds a minimum threshold (δ_min=0.15) an intent-extraction agent identifies the specific implicit intent, scored for novelty against a running witness set. The reward function ICQ(s,σ) = δ(s,σ) · ν(s) (severity times novelty indicator) is backpropagated up the tree; the overall objective is to maximize cumulative ICQ under budget B. Three design decisions stand out: (1) using plan drift rather than execution traces as the oracle — well-justified given the stated lack of deployment access, but inherently limited to declared intent rather than actual behavior (acknowledged as a threat to construct validity); (2) contract-guided (rather than random) mutation to make the exponential co-activation space tractable — validated empirically via ablation (Table III); (3) the novelty-aware ICQ reward jointly optimizing severity and diversity, rather than either alone — also validated by the Greedy-Drift/Greedy-Coverage ablations showing single-signal optimization underperforms. Key Results: Evaluated on SkillsBench (196 skills, 10 representative tasks) across 8 planning agents (4 open-weight: DS-R1-7B/14B/32B/LLaMA-8B; 4 proprietary: GPT-4.1-nano/mini, GPT-5.4-nano, GPT-5-nano), with GPT-4o-mini as the fixed intent-extraction/judge agent. At B=200 per task, every agent exposed a non-zero number of implicit intents (57-257 cumulative distinct intents per agent, Table I), with DS-R1-7B discovering the most (257) and GPT-4.1-mini the fewest (40). Across the full B=1000 budget on one task (sec-financial-report) with DS-R1-7B, SkillFuzz found 116 distinct intents with ICQ_Σ=66.7 and 90 high-severity intents (77% of discoveries above the severity threshold), versus 121 intents but only 49.1 ICQ_Σ and 64 high-severity intents (52%) for a random-uniform baseline, and versus No-Contract, Greedy-Drift, Greedy-Coverage, and MCTS+Orth ablations, all of which underperformed on ICQ_Σ and high-severity count. In an execution-validation study, 98 of the highest-risk flagged co-activations were re-planned and executed in a sandboxed Docker environment with an independent Claude-based executor (not the original planner) and judged by GPT-4o-mini as a trace judge: 80.6% overall confirmation rate (80.5% for DS-R1 family, 81.0% for GPT family). Across all discovered intents (1,188 total), k-means clustering (silhouette-selected) revealed four recurring semantic categories: Unsanctioned Data Analysis (43%), Audio/Video Side-Effect (26%), Covert Resource Creation (22%), Unauthorized Tool Invocation (10%). Severity is compositional, not additive: only 4.7% of single-skill activations produce severe plan drift, rising to 66.5% at 5 co-activated skills, a 14-fold increase over co-activation depth. Random uniform sampling covers 94.5% of pairwise skill interactions versus <40% for SkillFuzz, yet SkillFuzz still finds more high-severity intents, indicating pairwise coverage is not the limiting factor. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM (compositional implicit-intent testing for skill marketplaces, distinct from prompt injection or single-agent red-teaming) and NEW METHOD (contract-guided, execution-free MCTS fuzzing using plan drift as a differential oracle). The core technical pieces (MCTS, differential testing, contract extraction, embedding similarity) are individually standard; the novelty is in casting agentic plan-level compositional risk as a fuzzing problem and designing an oracle (plan drift) and search heuristic (contract-guided mutation) specific to natural-language, execution-free agent testing rather than syntactic program inputs. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes a substantive ablation study (Table III, Figure 4-5) isolating the contribution of contract-guided expansion, drift-only optimization, and coverage-only optimization, which is a genuine strength. It reports results across 8 distinct planning agents and both open-weight and proprietary model families, which is broader than most single-model fuzzing papers. However: (1) the intent-extraction and judgment stage both use GPT-4o-mini, and the authors acknowledge residual self-bias risk since this same model produces training-adjacent judgments across all experiments; (2) thresholds (δ_min=0.15, θ=0.75, δ_sev=0.5) are fixed globally without a sensitivity analysis or per-task calibration, raising Goodhart's Law concerns about how much the headline numbers depend on these choices; (3) no error bars, variance across seeds, or confidence intervals are reported for the coverage/ICQ numbers in Table I or Table III, only single-run point estimates (Figure 3a shows ±1 std shading across MCTS iterations, but this is the only variance reporting in the paper); (4) the marketplace is a single benchmark (SkillsBench, 196 skills) built by the same broader research community, and no code/data release URL is stated in the excerpted text. No public repository link was found in the reviewed pages. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a testing method evaluated by running experiments across planning agents and a downstream execution-validation study). - Practical: Yes; the method requires only API/CLI access to a planning agent to sample plans, no deployment environment, GPU infrastructure, or tool-execution sandbox for the core fuzzing loop, though the RQ2 validation study does require a Docker sandbox and an executor agent. - Efficient: The core loop needs K=3 plan samples per MCTS node and B=200-1000 total queries per task; costs scale with plan generation calls to the black-box planning LLM, which the paper does not report in wall-clock time or dollar cost, so the efficiency claim relative to exhaustive execution testing is directional rather than quantified. - Effective: Yes, with caveats. Real-workflow validation (RQ2) shows 80.6% of flagged plan-level intents are confirmed to manifest during actual execution, providing evidence beyond the plan-drift proxy itself, but the exact mechanism connecting plan drift score thresholds to real-world harm severity is not independently validated against an external harm-severity ground truth. - Scalable: Not tested. The evaluated marketplace has only 196 skills; the paper explicitly flags in "External validity" that a larger, more adversarial marketplace could exhibit a different contract geometry and shift which co-activations the pruning step retains, and does not report any experiment at 10x-100x the tested skill-library scale. Delivers: a testing methodology and reported experimental pipeline; no explicit software artifact (repo, tool, or dataset URL) is stated in the reviewed pages. Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a genuinely underexplored failure category (compositional implicit intent from co-activated benign skills) that is distinct from prompt injection and not covered by existing red-teaming literature, backed by a clear problem formulation (Definition 1, plan drift, ICQ). - The execution-validation study (RQ2) is a meaningful methodological strength: rather than resting on the plan-drift proxy alone, the authors independently re-executed 98 flagged combinations in a sandboxed environment with a different executor agent than the one that produced the original plan, directly testing whether the oracle's predictions transfer to real behavior. - The component ablation (RQ3, Table III, Figure 4) isolates the specific contribution of contract-guided expansion versus generic MCTS, and of joint severity+novelty reward versus either alone, going beyond a single end-to-end number. - The semantic taxonomy of discovered intents (RQ4, four recurring clusters) gives marketplace operators an actionable prioritization structure rather than an undifferentiated list of flagged combinations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Construct validity: plan-based testing can only detect misalignment between a stated plan and the (unseen) action; a deceptively misaligned agent stating an innocuous plan while pursuing a different objective would produce low plan drift and evade detection entirely, a threat model the authors distinguish from the compositional risk they target. - Internal validity: intent extraction and judgment both use GPT-4o-mini, so residual self-model judge bias cannot be fully ruled out; the δ_min, θ, δ_sev thresholds are fixed globally rather than tuned per target, which the authors frame as necessary for fair cross-model/cross-strategy comparison but is itself a limitation on tuning to real deployment risk tolerance. - External validity: the 196-skill marketplace and its contract geometry may not generalize to larger or more adversarial community marketplaces, and a deployed admission gate would need to trade off query budget B against inference cost, a tradeoff the fixed-budget evaluation does not model. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No cost/latency figures are reported for the fuzzing loop itself (LLM calls for contract extraction, plan sampling, intent extraction), making the practical deployment cost of running SkillFuzz at marketplace admission time unquantifiable from the paper. - No sensitivity analysis on the three fixed thresholds (δ_min, θ, δ_sev); given these thresholds directly determine what counts as a "discovered intent" and a "high-severity intent,
Search Results 2607.02345 PDF
#57 — Innovative (66)

Accurate, Interdisciplinary and Transparent Structure-property Understanding with Deep Native Structural Reasoning (65 ▲ )

Chen Tang, Yizhou Wang, Jianyu Wu, Lintao Wang, Shixiang Tang, Pengze Li, Encheng Su, Jun Yao, Jiabei Xiao, Yuqi Shi, Jielan Li, Hongxia Hao, Zhangyang Gao, Fang Wu, Ben Fei, Xiangyu Yue, Pan Tan, Bozitao Zhong, Jinouwen Zhang, Aoran Wang, Yan Lu, Jiaheng Liu, Xinzhu Ma, Liang Hong, Mingyue Zheng, Phil Torr, Bowen Zhou, Wanli Ouyang, Lei Bai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SciReasoner, a multimodal scientific foundation model that tokenizes protein, small-molecule, and crystal structures into a shared structure-aware vocabulary so a single autoregressive LLM can generate chain-of-thought reasoning that cites specific structural tokens as evidence for its predictions. - Why it's important: Structure-property reasoning in biology, chemistry, and materials science today either compresses 3D structure into lossy text descriptions (LLMs) or produces opaque scalar predictions with no inspectable rationale (domain-specific GNN/graph models), leaving practitioners unable to verify why a prediction was made. - How it does it: Domain-specific offline encoders (Foldseek for proteins, SLICES for crystals, ConfSeq for molecules) discretize 3D structure into addressable tokens that are embedded through a dedicated structure-aware vocabulary and interleaved with natural-language reasoning inside a Qwen3-14B backbone, trained via a three-stage pretraining curriculum plus a two-stage post-training procedure (intra-domain structural evidence grounding, then cross-domain reasoning consolidation via SFT+RL with DAPO). - The good: In the low-homology Cellular Component GO regime (≤30% sequence identity), SciReasoner improves Fmax from 0.34 (BLAST) to 0.55 and from 0.42 (ESM2) to 0.55, a setting specifically constructed to rule out homology-transfer shortcuts, with attention maps independently verified to localize to annotated DNA-binding residues (AUROC up to 0.91). - The missing: All human-expert reasoning-quality evaluation (Fig. 6E, N=177 judgments) compares only against DeepSeek-V4-Pro, a general-purpose LLM with no native structural input at all, rather than against a domain-specific interpretable baseline (e.g., an attention-annotated GNN), so the 98% tie-or-prefer result establishes superiority over a structurally blind competitor rather than over the strongest plausible comparator. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building structure-conditioned scientific reasoning systems (protein function annotation, retrosynthesis planning, materials property prediction) who wants a concrete tokenization scheme and training recipe for injecting native 3D evidence into an LLM's chain-of-thought; less essential for readers who only need best-in-class numbers on a single narrow benchmark, since specialist models remain competitive or better on several individual sub-tasks (e.g., LLM-Prop beats SciReasoner on some materials MAE metrics per Fig. 4A).
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (72) is real: a shared structure-aware token vocabulary spanning proteins (Foldseek), molecules (ConfSeq), and crystals (SLICES) that makes structural elements citable inside an LLM's reasoning trace is a NEW METHOD with cross-domain scope, not a benchmark tweak. Rigor (68) is held back by the human reasoning-quality evaluation comparing only against DeepSeek-V4-Pro, a structurally blind general LLM, rather than an interpretable domain baseline; the low-homology GO result (Fmax 0.34 BLAST / 0.42 ESM2 to 0.55 at ≤30% identity) and the attention-localization AUROC of 0.91 on DNA-binding residues are the strongest evidence and are well-constructed to exclude homology shortcuts. Completeness (60) and Resilience (58) are the dimensions that cap the composite: specialist models still beat SciReasoner on several sub-tasks (LLM-Prop on some materials MAE), so the foundation-model framing outruns the per-task evidence. Practical Impact (75) reflects a concrete, reusable tokenization scheme and training recipe. Weighted composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*75 = 66.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Domain experts explaining structure-property relationships (protein function from fold geometry, reaction feasibility from bond topology, material stability from crystal symmetry) integrate multiple structural cues and compare competing hypotheses under physical constraints. Large language models handle this by converting structures to text, which fragments physical semantics (e.g., byte-pair tokenization splitting a SMILES ring into semantically meaningless pieces) and produces reasoning grounded in linguistic association rather than the structure itself. Domain-specific graph/structure models (GNNs, protein-structure predictors) encode structure directly but output only scalar predictions with no exposed intermediate reasoning, so their internal evidence use cannot be audited. The gap is a model that treats structural elements (residues, molecular fragments, crystal bonds) as first-class, citable units within an explicit reasoning trace, across multiple scientific modalities simultaneously. Key Idea: Discretize 3D structure (protein folds, molecular conformers, crystal lattices) into domain-specific token vocabularies using existing structure-tokenization schemes (Foldseek, ConfSeq, SLICES), then embed those tokens through a dedicated structure-aware embedding table kept separate from the LLM's natural-language token embeddings, so a single autoregressive decoder can interleave structural tokens and natural-language reasoning in one generation trajectory. This makes structural evidence addressable ("cite this residue,
Recommended 2607.07708 PDF
#58 — Innovative (65)

Optimizing Interaction Through Environment Design: Reward Alignment and Intent Prediction in Human–Robot Collaboration

YS Tung · cairo-lab.com · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces environment design (ED) as a first-class lever for human-robot collaboration, presenting four algorithms that actively reconfigure the shared workspace (kit layouts, object placements, virtual AR obstacles) to improve a robot's inference of human intent at three granularities: task schedule (bilevel kitting optimization), latent reward function (CRED active preference learning), and immediate motion (MAP-Elites legibility and margin-based shared-autonomy optimization). - Why it's important: Fluent human-robot teaming requires a shared mental model, and prior work only improves the robot's inference algorithms or datasets while treating the physical/virtual environment as fixed, leaving a lever unexploited that directly shapes how legible and predictable the human's behavior becomes. - How it does it: Each contribution casts environment configuration as the outer loop of a bilevel or quality-diversity optimization (kit layout via cross-entropy method, preference queries via Bayesian optimization over counterfactual trajectories, and workspace layout via MAP-Elites over legibility/margin objectives), with the inner loop scoring candidate environments against a model of human behavior or preference. - The good: Across four separate human-subjects studies (18-25 participants each) and two shop-floor/simulation studies, the environment-design interventions produce statistically significant, non-trivial improvements — e.g., CRED reaches 0.998/0.979/0.906 reward-accuracy across three domains versus a ~60% plateau for the mean-belief-policy baseline, and Chapter 5's margin-optimized layouts hold 97-100% argmax accuracy versus 67-88% for random layouts as the number of candidate goals grows to 8. - The missing: None of the four studies is evaluated with a single unified system across the two levels of intent simultaneously (task/reward inference and low-level motion prediction are always tested in isolation), and the shared-autonomy chapter's real-robot deployment (Chapter 5.4.3) is explicitly qualitative, with the controlled user study deferred to future work. - Read it or not: Worth reading for HRI or human-aware robotics researchers designing intent-inference systems, particularly the bilevel-optimization framing that recurs across chapters and the Chapter 5 probabilistic correctness guarantee for shared autonomy; less essential for readers focused purely on new inference algorithms, since the thesis's contribution is the environment-design layer, not new prediction models.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) rests on a genuine NEW FORMULATION — treating the physical/virtual environment as the outer loop of a bilevel/QD optimization rather than improving the inference model — reused coherently across kitting, preference elicitation, and legibility. Rigor (65) is helped by six independent human-subjects and simulation studies with appropriate tests (ANOVA/Tukey, Kruskal-Wallis, Holm-Bonferroni) but hurt by 12-25 participant samples, author-defined ground-truth reward functions, and no code/data release. Non-Obviousness (65) is moderate: the bilevel skeleton is standard machinery, though Ch. 5's worst-case margin reformulation with a design-time probabilistic guarantee is a real sharpening. Completeness (72) benefits from unusually direct limitation acknowledgment (the author explicitly bounds the Gaussian-noise/linearization assumption as likely violated by real joystick input). Resilience (65) is limited by the central gap: no experiment integrates two levels of intent simultaneously despite the framing, and MAP-Elites/BO are never benchmarked against random or grid search at matched compute, so 'environment design helps' is not cleanly separated from 'any global optimizer over this space helps.' Impact (45) reflects commodity hardware but no released artifact and design-time-only optimization (340-920s per scene). Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*45 = 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Robots collaborating with humans (e.g., a robot assisting meal preparation or furniture assembly) must infer three levels of human intent: immediate motion, task structure, and latent preferences. Almost all prior HRI work improves this inference by building better models or collecting richer datasets while holding the shared environment fixed. This leaves an underused degree of freedom: the physical layout of objects, kit contents, and now-cheap virtual overlays (AR) directly determine how much information a given human trajectory or preference query carries, and thus bound the achievable inference accuracy regardless of the model used downstream. Key Idea: Treat the environment itself (kit contents/schedule, object placement, AR-projected virtual obstacles) as an optimizable design variable in a bilevel or quality-diversity formulation, where an outer loop searches over environment configurations and an inner loop evaluates how informative or legible human behavior becomes under each candidate configuration. This same bilevel skeleton (outer: environment parameters; inner: behavior/utility given that environment) is reused across all three intent levels, substituting the inner objective (kit fitness, information gain via counterfactual reasoning, or legibility/margin scores) as appropriate. Methodology: The thesis comprises four algorithmic contributions, each with its own pipeline: (1) Just-in-time kitting (Ch. 2): a bilevel optimization where the upper level segments/schedules assembly tasks (minimizing mismatch between robot delivery time and human task duration, subject to precedence constraints, solved greedily with a fixed horizon N=5) and the lower level arranges parts on a kitting tray (maximizing inter-type distance, minimizing intra-type distance, penalizing bounding-box overlap) via the cross-entropy method (100 iterations, 200 samples, top-30 elite). (2) CRED (Ch. 3): active preference learning where a linear reward model R(ξ)=wᵀΦ(ξ) is maintained via Bayesian belief update (adaptive Metropolis sampling) over reward weights w; an inner loop samples diverse weight hypotheses and generates counterfactual trajectories (via RL/PPO or CHOMP) to compute mutual-information-based query informativeness; an outer loop uses Bayesian optimization (Gaussian process + UCB acquisition, Matérn kernel) to select environment parameters θ that maximize this informativeness. (3) Legible workspace generation (Ch. 4): a legibility score based on Dragan et al.'s cost-ratio formulation is optimized over valid subtask sequences using MAP-Elites (a quality-diversity algorithm), producing a behavior-performance map over features like obstacle count and workstation ordering; perturbations include moving physical objects and adding/removing AR-projected virtual obstacles. (4) Reliable shared autonomy (Ch. 5): reformulates Chapter 4's average-case cross-entropy objective as a worst-case, margin-based objective under a bounded Gaussian joystick-noise model, deriving a linearized trajectory-margin slack that yields a design-time probabilistic correctness guarantee (≥1-α argmax accuracy) before deployment, again searched via MAP-Elites over SE(3) object placements. Two to three critical design decisions recur: (a) fixed/limited optimization horizons (N=5 for kitting, fixed MAP-Elites iteration budgets) trade optimality for tractability and are explicitly flagged as limitations; (b) the linear reward-model assumption in Ch. 3 and the Gaussian-noise/linearization assumption in Ch. 5 are both simplifying choices that the author acknowledges may not hold for real human input distributions. Key Results: Chapter 2's user study (19 participants, ANOVA F(2,34)=57.60, p<.0001) shows the Optimized kitting condition reduces total task time and human idle time versus Whole-Assembly baseline (95% CI on idle-time reduction ≈ [-186, -160] s) and matches a hand-designed Single-Task JIT baseline; shop-floor DES simulation (10 assemblies × grid of MAT/MTTF) shows up to ~25% task-time and ~50% idle-time improvement over baselines under high part-shortage conditions. Chapter 3's CRED achieves final reward-accuracy correlations of 0.998 (Lunar Lander), 0.979 (Tabletop), 0.906 (Navigation) versus a Mean-Belief-Policy baseline plateauing near 60%; in a 25-participant user study CRED achieves higher reward correlation (tabletop median r=0.97 vs. 0.75 RR-DR vs. 0.51 MBP; navigation median r=0.78 vs. 0.57 vs. 0.38) with lower NASA-TLX workload (median 1.83 vs. 2.67/2.33). Chapter 4's Overcooked (20 MTurk participants) and tabletop (12 participants) experiments show the Legible/Both-Optimized conditions yield higher goal-prediction accuracy and greater data efficiency (Both-Optimized reaches ~90% accuracy with only 50% training data) and lower covariance in the learned Gaussian predictor (trace 2.77e-3 vs. 7.12e-3 baseline). Chapter 5's simulation across six difficulty tiers (M=2 to M=8 candidate goals) shows ME-optimized layouts hold 97-100% argmax accuracy versus 67-88% for random layouts, with optimizer wall-clock cost of 340-920s per scene and 93-100% archive coverage; real-robot demonstrations (Sawyer manipulator, 3 shared-autonomy scenarios) are reported qualitatively only. All experiments are conducted in simulation, controlled lab studies (small samples, 12-25 participants), or single-robot demonstrations — no large-scale or industrial deployment is reported. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (casting environment/workspace design as the outer loop of a bilevel or quality-diversity optimization across three levels of human intent) + NEW METHOD (CRED's joint counterfactual-reasoning-and-environment-design query generator; the margin-based worst-case objective with a probabilistic correctness guarantee in Ch. 5) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (workspace configuration measurably reduces variance/uncertainty in human motion and improves data efficiency of downstream predictors). The building blocks (bilevel optimization, Bayesian optimization, MAP-Elites, cross-entropy method, MaxEnt IRL, Bayesian preference learning) are all established techniques; the novelty is their systematic application to environment configuration as a design variable for HRI rather than to policy or curriculum learning, and the unifying framing across the four chapters. Rigor & Reproducibility: Each chapter includes ablations and baseline comparisons (Ch. 3 ablates counterfactual reasoning and environment design separately and against domain randomization; Ch. 4 ablates placement-only vs. obstacle-only vs. both; Ch. 5 compares random vs. ME-optimized layouts with Bonferroni-corrected significance testing). Statistical tests are appropriate to the data type (ANOVA + Tukey HSD for continuous measures, Kruskal-Wallis/Wilcoxon for ordinal Likert/NASA-TLX data, with Holm-Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons in Ch. 3). Sample sizes are modest (12-25 participants per study) but multiple independent studies across four chapters partially compensate. No code, data, or model-weight release URL is mentioned anywhere in the reviewed chapters. All ground-truth reward functions in user studies (Ch. 3, Ch. 4) are author-defined linear functions used to instruct participants, which is a reasonable design for controlled preference-learning evaluation but limits ecological validity. Chapter 5's probabilistic guarantee explicitly rests on a Gaussian-noise and local-linearization assumption that the author flags as likely violated by real joystick input (heavier tails, systematic bias, state-dependent noise) — a rare and appreciated instance of the author bounding their own theoretical claim's real-world applicability. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (all four chapters build and evaluate physical or simulated systems). Practical: Yes — all systems run on commercially available hardware (Sawyer 7-DoF arm, Intel RealSense depth camera, Microsoft HoloLens 2 for AR, standard joystick interfaces). Efficient: Costs vary widely: kit-arrangement CE method runs at interactive rates; CRED requires Bayesian-optimization outer loops plus RL/CHOMP inner rollouts (compute cost not quantified in reviewed sections); MAP-Elites optimization in Ch. 5 costs 340-920 wall-clock seconds per scene for offline (pre-deployment) layout search, which is acceptable for a design-time step but not for online re-planning. Effective: The evidence directly supports the stated problem in three of four chapters (measured task time/idle time, measured reward-accuracy correlation, measured goal-prediction accuracy) rather than only a proxy; Chapter 5's headline claim (probabilistic correctness guarantee) is validated in simulation via argmax accuracy, with real-robot deployment left qualitative. Scalable: Tested scale is small (M up to 8 candidate goals, 12-25 participants, single-robot single-human settings); the thesis does not test at 10x-100x scale (e.g., multi-human, multi-robot, or workspaces with dozens of objects), and states in Ch. 4 that effect size depends on environment configurability, implying diminishing returns are plausible but untested at larger scale. Strengths: - The bilevel-optimization skeleton is genuinely reused and adapted (not just repeated) across three distinct problem settings (task scheduling, preference elicitation, motion legibility), with Chapter 3 explicitly noting the structural correspondence to Chapter 2's formulation — this is a coherent intellectual throughline rather than four disconnected projects bundled together. - Chapter 5's reformulation from an average-case cross-entropy objective (Ch. 4) to a worst-case margin objective with a derived probabilistic correctness guarantee is a meaningful theoretical sharpening motivated by a concrete failure mode (a committing shared-autonomy controller cares about the worst pairwise goal confusion, not the average). - Each of the four chapters includes both simulation/ablation studies and at least one human-subjects study, providing convergent evidence rather than relying solely on simulated agents. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Chapter 2: limited planning horizon (N=5) may prevent globally optimal kitting strategies; human task-time estimation is not done online. - Chapter 3: computational cost of the bilevel outer/inner search is a stated limitation, with parallelization proposed as future work. - Chapter 4: effectiveness is dependent on environment configurability, and the subjective/cognitive-load implications of more complex configurations for humans are explicitly left unevaluated. - Chapter 5: the Gaussian joystick-noise model and local linearization are acknowledged as unrealistic simplifications of real human input distributions, and the real-robot demonstration is explicitly qualitative pending a controlled user study. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No experiment integrates two or more levels of intent (e.g., simultaneously inferring task schedule and low-level motion) despite the thesis's framing around "two levels of human intent" — each chapter isolates one level, so evidence for compounding benefits across levels is absent. - Sample sizes (12-25 participants per study) are typical for HRI but limit generalizability claims, particularly for the Chapter 4 tabletop study (n=12) where subgroup/interaction effects would be underpowered. - No code/data/model release is mentioned in the reviewed material, limiting reproducibility beyond what the equations and algorithms specify. - The recurring choice of MAP-Elites/Bayesian optimization as the search method is justified by citation to prior QD literature but is not benchmarked in Ch. 3-5 against simpler alternatives (e.g., grid search, random search) at the same compute budget, aside from the domain-randomization comparison in Ch. 3 and an appendix-only optimizer comparison (Appendix E) for Ch. 4 — this makes it hard to isolate how much of the gain is from "environment design" per se versus "any reasonably-tuned global optimizer over the same space." Bottom Line: A methodologically coherent thesis that establishes environment design as a reusable optimization lever for human-robot collaboration, with convincing (if modest-scale) empirical support across four separate systems; researchers working on intent inference in HRI will gain a useful reusable bilevel/QD framing and concrete evidence that workspace configuration measurably improves downstream prediction accuracy and data efficiency, even though no single experiment demonstrates the full multi-level-intent story the introduction promises.
Search Results
#59 — Innovative (65)

Design and evaluation of test Oracles for control-CPS and reinforcement learning software

S Zhang · theses.lib.polyu.edu.hk · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces three test oracles for control cyber-physical systems (control-CPS) and reinforcement learning (RL) software: a Transformer-based oracle (TO) for control-CPS fault localization, a fuzzy logic-guided oracle for RL software, and a Lyapunov Potential Energy Abnormality (LPEA) oracle that exploits Lyapunov stability control theory to test RL software without human labeling. - Why it's important: Testing control-CPS and RL software is blocked by the oracle problem: no unique correct output exists to compare against, so automated software fault localization and RL debugging still depend on unscalable human judgment. - How it does it: TO and the fuzzy oracle detect deviations from either a learned trajectory predictor or a manually specified intended policy; LPEA instead constructs synthetic RL training environments whose reward function is derived from a provably decreasing Lyapunov potential energy function, so any bug-less RL implementation must learn a policy that keeps decreasing this energy, and violations flag bugs. - The good: LPEA(ϑ=100%, θ=75%) outperforms a 3-person human-expert oracle by 53.6% accuracy, 50% precision, 127.8% true negative rate, and 34.8% F1 score across 112 RL implementations (A2C, PPO, TD3, bug-less and buggy), at roughly 1/8th the monetary cost (7.87 USD vs 61.46 USD). - The missing: LPEA is validated only on linear synthetic environments with black-box RL algorithms (A2C, PPO, TD3) from a single library (Stable Baselines3); it has no evaluation on nonlinear dynamics, image-based/high-dimensional state spaces, or algorithms like SAC/DDPG, and the thesis explicitly flags this as an open scenario. - Read it or not: Worth reading for software-testing and RL-reliability researchers working on the oracle problem; the LPEA chapter (published at ICSE 2025, Distinguished Paper Award) is the strongest contribution and is largely self-contained, while the earlier Transformer-oracle and fuzzy-logic chapters are weaker pilot studies collected in the same thesis for narrative continuity.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (85) is the standout: inverting Lyapunov stability theory into a synthetic RL environment whose analytically-known potential-energy function supplies automatic ground truth is a genuinely surprising way to sidestep the oracle problem, and it avoids the circularity of testing one ML system with another. Rigor (60) and Resilience (55) hold the composite back: the headline LPEA-vs-human comparison reports single point estimates with no confidence intervals or repeated trials, the human baseline is 3 experts (2 bachelor's), the 'bug-less' ground truth rests on an unfalsifiable trust in Stable Baselines3, and validation is confined to 2-dimensional linear synthetic systems, so the 53.6% accuracy gain does not obviously survive the move to nonlinear or high-dimensional control where RL actually matters. Practical Impact (45) is limited by no stated code release for the LPEA chapter and a method whose reward-shaping trick requires an analytically solvable Lyapunov equation; the two earlier thesis chapters (Transformer oracle, fuzzy oracle) are pilot studies that add little, with the fuzzy oracle failing to beat the human panel. Weighted composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*45 = 65.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: For control-CPS (e.g., drones, autopilots), a single high-level command can map to infinitely many legitimate physical trajectories, so conventional pass/fail testing does not apply; automated software fault localization (SFL) needs an oracle that can label a code execution trace as buggy or correct from trajectory data alone. The problem worsens for control-CPS whose controllers are trained via reinforcement learning, because now neither the legitimate trajectories nor the legitimate controllers are unique: RL performance is inherently multidimensional and statistical, so there is no ground truth against which "correct" or "incorrect" can be defined. The current mainstream fallback, human oracles who manually watch agent behavior and vote, does not scale and is subjective. The thesis targets both sub-problems: general control-CPS trajectory-based fault localization, and RL-specific software correctness testing when the environment and reward function are known but the "right" controller behavior is not. Key Idea: The unifying trick across the three oracles is to convert an intractable "is this correct?" question into a tractable "does this system property hold?" question. For the Transformer/AR-SI oracles, the property is that a well-trained time-series predictor's forecast error should stay bounded on trajectories from bug-free code, so latency- or accuracy-preserving outlier detection substitutes for ground truth. For the fuzzy oracle, the property is monotonic convergence: a fuzzy-logic-derived reward function encodes a handful of "intended" reference state-action pairs, and a bug-free RL implementation should show an improving trend of policy compliance over training epochs. The most novel idea, in Chapter 4 (LPEA), inverts classical Lyapunov stability control theory: since any linear-quadratic-regulator-style stable controller is guaranteed to monotonically decrease a computable Lyapunov potential energy function V(X(t)) = X(t)ᵀPX(t), the authors construct synthetic RL environments whose reward function directly rewards decreasing V(X(t)), then check whether the RL-trained agent's rollouts actually decrease V(X(t)) at test time. Because V(X(t)) is analytically known (not learned, not human-judged), this gives a mathematically grounded, fully automatic ground truth for a black-box RL implementation's correctness, sidestepping the oracle problem rather than approximating it. Methodology: Chapter 2 (TO vs AO): both oracles use a sliding-window time-series predictor (Transformer or Auto-Regressive System Identification) trained on trajectories from an earlier debugged control-CPS version (ArduCopter/ArduPilot); at test time, prediction error outliers on new trajectories flag the corresponding code trace as buggy, which is fed to three off-the-shelf SFL tools (Tarantula, Crosstab, Ochiai). Chapter 3 (fuzzy oracle): a 4-phase algorithm (initialization, training, log-analysis, oracle) generates I randomly sampled "intended policies" (K reference state-action pairs each), builds fuzzy-membership-derived reward functions from them (Eq. 3.3–3.4), trains the RL implementation E epochs per intended policy, computes an epoch-compliance-value time series via fuzzy state/action similarity (Eq. 3.6), and declares the software buggy if too few of the I policies show a positive linear-regression slope (converging trend). Chapter 4 (LPEA): randomly generates I stable linear systems (state dynamics A, controller K solving a Lyapunov equation ÃᵀPÃ − P + Q = 0 for positive-definite P), builds an environment per system whose reward is exp(−V(X(t))) + α·exp(−‖X(t)‖₂), trains the target RL implementation on each, then samples J random initial states per trained agent and checks whether the fraction of rollout steps with ΔV < 0 exceeds threshold ϑ (Eq. 4.10 controller-goodness) — the RL software is labeled buggy if the fraction of "good" controllers across I systems falls below threshold θ (Eq. 4.11). Three critical design decisions: (1) using an analytically known Lyapunov function rather than a learned reference removes any dependency on a second potentially-buggy oracle; (2) excluding the applied action U(t) from the reward function's input (Eq. 4.8) deliberately raises the RL problem's difficulty to better expose bugs, well-justified as a stress test; (3) sweeping (ϑ, θ) over a 5×3 grid rather than fixing them a priori is appropriately empirical given the acknowledged absence of a principled way to set these thresholds. Key Results: Chapter 2: TO and AO show no significant SFL accuracy/latency difference across 37 ArduCopter subjects with 27 injected bug types (p>5%, |Cohen's d|<0.4 for accuracy/latency); AO has significantly lower false-positive rate and TO significantly lower false-negative rate (p<0.1%, |d|≈0.9). Chapter 3: the fuzzy oracle is not significantly better than a 5-member human panel across 61 environment/implementation combinations (DQN/A2C/PPO on Frozen Lake and MountainCar Continuous), and is actually worse on Frozen Lake (a discrete, unambiguous environment). Chapter 4 (headline result): across 112 A2C/PPO/TD3 implementations (56 bug-less, 56 buggy, mixing artificial bugs from a published RL bug taxonomy and real GitHub-commit-derived bugs), LPEA(ϑ=100%, θ=75%) achieves accuracy 0.804, precision 1.0 in several configurations, F1 0.776, and ROC AUC up to 0.89 (A2C), versus the 3-expert human oracle's accuracy 0.5, precision 0.5, F1 0.576, AUC 0.54–0.66 depending on algorithm. Nearly all 15 LPEA (ϑ, θ) configurations beat the human oracle on F1 and AUC. Baselines (human oracle) are a legitimate, carefully constructed comparison point (STARLA-inspired protocol, three qualified experts, 672 hand-labeled interaction traces), not a strawman. Scale is a controlled synthetic benchmark (linear systems, n=2, m=2 state/action dims), not real-world deployed control-CPS. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (LPEA oracle inverting Lyapunov stability theory into an RL test oracle) is the strongest and most original contribution. The Transformer oracle (Chapter 2) is largely an ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION / applied NEW METHOD: substituting a Transformer for AR-SI within an existing oracle architecture from prior work (He et al., ICSE 2019), yielding comparable but not superior performance. The fuzzy-logic oracle (Chapter 3) is a NEW METHOD but with limited empirical payoff, explicitly framed by the thesis itself as a stepping stone toward Chapter 4. Genuinely novel across the thesis: using a known-in-closed-form physical/control-theoretic invariant (Lyapunov energy) as ground truth for a black-box ML testing problem, rather than approximating ground truth with fuzzy human-encoded heuristics or a second learned model. Rigor & Reproducibility: Threats-to-validity sections are present and reasonably thorough (construct, internal, external validity) for Chapters 2–4, an unusually disciplined practice. Ablation-style analysis exists via the 15-configuration (ϑ, θ) sweep and per-algorithm (A2C/PPO/TD3) breakdowns, functioning as a sensitivity analysis. No multiple-seed statistical testing is reported for the LPEA evaluation itself (Chapter 2's TO/AO comparison does use p-values and effect sizes, but Chapter 4's LPEA-vs-human comparison reports single point-estimate metrics per configuration with no confidence intervals or repeated trials). The comparison to human oracle is explicitly biased in favor of the baseline on cost (10 USD/hour human labor vs 0.061 USD/hour compute; human panel given more resources), a positive methodological choice that strengthens the LPEA result. Source code for Chapter 3 is public on GitHub (RL-testing-new repo); Chapter 4 does not state a code/data release URL in the excerpted evaluation sections. The "bug-less" ground truth throughout rests on trusting Stable Baselines3's stable release as bug-free, an assumption the thesis explicitly flags as best-effort rather than provable. All three chapters are self-published prior conference/preprint work (RTCSA 2023, arXiv 2024, ICSE 2025 Distinguished Paper) compiled into thesis form; ICSE 2025 is peer-reviewed and carries the added credibility of a Distinguished Paper Award. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes for LPEA — it requires only black-box access to the RL implementation (no source code, no gradients) and runs on a single laptop-class GPU (RTX 3060) within 129 hours for the full 15-configuration sweep. Efficient: LPEA costs roughly 0.061 USD/hour of compute versus 10 USD/hour of human labor, a substantial and quantified efficiency gain, though absolute compute cost (129 hours for one benchmark) is non-trivial.
