Effective: The evidence shows LPEA solves the stated problem (distinguishing bug-less from buggy RL implementations) on the tested benchmark, but effectiveness beyond linear synthetic control systems is unverified — the reward-shaping trick requires an analytically solvable Lyapunov equation, which does not generalize to nonlinear/high-dimensional real-world control tasks without further work. Scalable: Untested at 10x–100x scale; the thesis's own Future Work section (Section 5.3) names generalizing to nonlinear/broader control theories and closing the sim-to-real gap as open problems, i.e., the authors acknowledge rather than claim scalability. Strengths: - The LPEA oracle's core insight, using a system with a provable, closed-form monotonic invariant as an automatically-labeled RL test environment, is a genuine methodological contribution that avoids the circularity of using one imperfect ML system to test another. - Careful, quantified fairness control in the LPEA-vs-human comparison (explicitly biasing cost and resource allocation toward the human baseline) strengthens confidence in the reported gains. - Consistent, explicit threats-to-validity discussion across all three chapters, including honest acknowledgment when a proposed method (fuzzy oracle) underperforms expectations, is good scientific practice rather than result-shopping. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The thesis directly states LPEA's core limitation is that it verifies whether RL software can learn a hidden policy at all, not its performance in specific complex application scenarios, and that it faces challenges in high-dimensional nonlinear or dynamic open-world environments (Section 5.1.3). The fuzzy oracle is explicitly conceded to be "not significantly better than the human oracle" except on a few metrics (Chapter 3 conclusion). The Transformer oracle's poor adaptability to rapidly changing control-CPS systems is stated directly, alongside its dependency on a stable, well-debugged prior software version. The authors also flag that optimal (ϑ, θ) configuration for LPEA remains an open problem solved only by brute-force grid search. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - LPEA's synthetic benchmark uses low-dimensional linear systems (n=2 state, m=2 action dimensions) exclusively; no evidence is given that Lyapunov equations remain tractably solvable, or that the reward-shaping trick still exposes bugs, at higher-dimensional or nonlinear systems, which is precisely where RL is most attractive relative to classical control. - The "bug-less" ground truth for both Stable Baselines3 (LPEA) and ArduCopter (TO/AO) rests on an unfalsifiable assumption (widely used, therefore trustworthy); the thesis acknowledges this but does not attempt any independent verification, e.g., via formal methods or a second orthogonal oracle. - The human-oracle baseline in Chapter 4 uses only 3 experts (2 bachelor's, 1 PhD) evaluating 672 traces in 1.5–2.5 hours each; this is a thin sample for claiming general superiority over "human judgment,
Search Results
#60 — Innovative (64)

Do Medical Vision Language Models Actually See? A Counterfactual Grounding Framework and Hard-Negative Contrastive Training for Visually-Reliant Medical …

A Zafar, LK Murali, S Bharadwaj, A Vashist, J Wu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a counterfactual evaluation framework that decomposes medical VLM accuracy into visual and textual-shortcut components, and proposes CORAL, a LoRA fine-tune of Qwen2.5-VL-7B trained with a Contrastive Grounding Objective (CGO) that penalizes answer invariance under CLIP-mined hard-negative image swaps. - Why it's important: Medical VLMs can pass standard VQA benchmarks by exploiting text-only shortcuts (question phrasing, demographic correlations) rather than reading the image, a failure mode FDA guidance on AI/ML software as a medical device flags as a deployment risk. - How it does it: Substitute the input image with five controlled surrogates (blank, patch-shuffled, absent, CLIP-retrieved hard negative, corrupted), measure eight grounding metrics from the resulting accuracy deltas, and train CORAL with a GRPO reward term that fires when the model's extracted answer does not change between the real image and a CLIP-similar, label-different hard negative. - The good: CORAL yields a statistically resolved -8.0 pp reduction in Visual Hallucination Rate (95% CI [-11.75, -4.25] pp, P<0.001), a 57% relative drop in confidently-wrong-with-real-image-but-right-with-shuffled-image cases, while neither of two RL baselines (MedVLThinker RL(text), RL(image)) achieves a significant gain on any metric. - The missing: The paper's own IS_pred metric shows CORAL is worse-grounded than the base model in one specific sense (0.698 vs. 0.518 fraction of items where the extracted answer is unchanged under image shuffle), and the headline accuracy/VHR results rest on only n=100 items per benchmark (n=400 total) with an admitted train/eval overlap on PMC-VQA, the benchmark showing the largest accuracy gain. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or auditing medical VLMs who needs a concrete, reproducible protocol for measuring visual shortcut exploitation; the CORAL training recipe itself is a secondary contribution with mixed, partially self-undermined results.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (60) is the binding constraint: paired bootstrap CIs (B=10,000) and McNemar tests are proper practice, but the authors themselves flag a PMC-VQA train/eval overlap on the benchmark showing the largest accuracy gain (+15 pp), n=100 per benchmark leaves two of five headline grounding metrics statistically unresolved, and no multi-seed CORAL training variance is reported, so the +6.7 pp gain rests on one optimization trajectory. Novelty (65) is moderate because counterfactual shortcut detection is a direct transplant of general-domain VQA work (Agrawal 2016, VQA-CP, Liang 2020) into the medical setting; the CLIP-hard-negative-as-both-audit-and-reward mechanism and the medical grounding gap quantification are the genuinely new pieces. Non-Obviousness (70) is lifted by the RL(image) negative result, that an RL objective trained on image-question-answer triples achieved its highest accuracy on no-image prompts, which is a real caution rather than a predictable finding. Completeness (75) is boosted by the paper reporting IS_pred against its own interest, but Resilience (60) suffers because that same metric undercuts the central 'more visually reliant' claim and no ablation isolates CLIP retrieval from contrastive training in general. Weighted composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 = 64.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Medical VLMs report strong VQA accuracy (PathVQA, PMC-VQA, SLAKE, VQA-RAD) but standard accuracy metrics cannot distinguish a model that reads the image from one that exploits co-occurrence patterns between question phrasing, demographic text, and answer labels baked into institution-specific clinical datasets. This matters for clinical deployment: a model selecting correct answers for the wrong reason may fail silently outside its training distribution, and existing saliency-based grounding methods (Grad-CAM, attention visualization) measure attribution, not behavioral reliance, while general-domain counterfactual VQA work (Agrawal et al. 2016; Bitton-Guetta et al. 2023) has no medical-specific counterpart. Key Idea: Operationalize "visual grounding" purely behaviorally: a model is visually reliant only if its prediction changes when the image is replaced by an uninformative or misleading surrogate. The paper's single technical trick for training is to make hard-negative retrieval (CLIP-similarity-matched, label-different images) the basis of both the evaluation condition and the training reward signal, so the same mechanism that audits shortcut exploitation is used to penalize it during fine-tuning. Methodology: Four components. (1) CORAL: a LoRA adapter (r=16, alpha=32) on frozen Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, trained with GRPO under a Contrastive Grounding Objective r_CGO = r_acc + lambda 1[Ans(real) != Ans(hard-negative)], lambda=0.5 chosen via held-out sweep, KL coefficient beta=0.01, group size G=8, 2,000 steps on 4x80GB H100s. (2) Hard-negative mining: FAISS exact inner-product search over CLIP ViT-L/14 embeddings, constrained to label(x_j) != y_i, yielding 5,778 pairs at mean cosine similarity 0.967. (3) Six counterfactual image conditions (real, blank, patch-shuffled, image-absent, hard-negative, corrupted) applied identically across models. (4) Eight grounding metrics (VRS, BD, IS_pred/IS_raw, VBR, VHR, NVCR, HVRR, HN-GAP) with paired-bootstrap 95% CIs (B=10,000) and McNemar tests. Design decisions: using extracted-answer matching (Ans()) rather than raw output strings for IS is well-justified and explicitly validated by showing the raw-string variant produces an artifactual, format-biased ranking; using CLIP-retrieved (rather than synthetic) hard negatives ties the contrastive signal to naturally-confusable images, a reasonable but unvalidated assumption that CLIP similarity tracks clinically-relevant visual confusability; the lambda=0.5 weight is tuned on a validation sweep but the paper does not report the sweep results or sensitivity to this choice. Key Results: On paired evaluation across PathVQA, PMC-VQA, SLAKE, VQA-RAD (n=100 each, n=400 total), CORAL improves macro accuracy by +6.7 pp over Qwen2.5-VL-7B base (95% CI [+0.75, +12.5] pp, P(delta>0)=0.988) and reduces VHR by -8.0 pp (CI [-11.75,-4.25] pp, P<0.001). Neither RL(text) (-0.3 pp, n.s.) nor RL(image) (+2.3 pp, CI crosses zero) achieves significant accuracy gains. VRS improves directionally (0.128->0.165) but the CI crosses zero at this n (P(delta>0)=0.86). Per-benchmark: PMC-VQA (CORAL's training domain) shows the largest accuracy effect (+15 pp, CI excludes zero) but flat VHR, while PathVQA shows an accuracy regression (-5 pp, n.s.) alongside the largest CI-excluding-zero VHR reduction (-12 pp). Cross-domain diagnostics (n=500, 10 benchmarks) show image substitution costs <=6.5 pp on medical benchmarks versus 48-61 pp on general-domain benchmarks (ChartQA, VQAv2, GQA, ScienceQA), and the RL(image) checkpoint achieves its highest accuracy on no-image prompts (0.548, highest of any model tested) and shuffled prompts (0.488) versus real images, indicating RL(image) sharpened its language prior rather than its grounding. IS_pred is worse for CORAL (0.698) than the base model (0.518), meaning CORAL reaches the same multiple-choice answer under image shuffle 70% of the time, a result the authors report against their own interest. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (CGO training objective) + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (counterfactual evaluation framework with eight metrics) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (medical VLMs show far lower image-substitution cost than general-domain VLMs; accuracy-only RL sharpens rather than corrects language priors). The counterfactual-substitution methodology itself is a direct extension of established general-domain VQA shortcut-detection work (Agrawal et al. 2016; VQA-CP; Liang et al. 2020's contrastive counterfactual objective); the genuinely new elements are the medical-specific metric suite, the medical-domain quantification of the grounding gap, and swapping synthetic perturbation for CLIP-retrieved hard-negative retrieval as the contrastive training signal. Rigor & Reproducibility: Paired bootstrap CIs (B=10,000) and McNemar tests are reported for all headline comparisons, and the paper explicitly reports a metric (IS_pred) that cuts against its own contribution rather than suppressing it, a positive credibility signal. However: (1) the authors state outright that PMC-VQA, the benchmark with the largest accuracy gain (+15 pp), overlaps between CORAL's training pool and its evaluation set, an explicit train/eval contamination risk they acknowledge but do not quantify or correct for. (2) n=100 per benchmark for the primary paired comparison is small; VRS and BD, two of five headline grounding metrics, remain statistically unresolved at this scale. (3) No multiple-seed or multiple-training-run variance is reported for CORAL itself, only bootstrap CIs over the fixed evaluation set, so training-run-to-training-run variance in the CGO fine-tune is unmeasured. (4) The paper states code, data, and model weights will be released but does not give a URL/repo in the excerpt provided. (5) Comparison is limited to one backbone family (Qwen2.5-VL) and one RL baseline family (MedVLThinker); generalization to LLaVA-Med, BiomedGPT, HuatuoGPT-Vision is explicitly deferred. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes, training runs on 4x80GB H100s with a LoRA adapter (r=16) on a 7B backbone, and inference is standard bfloat16 greedy decoding, both commercially accessible configurations. Efficient: Not quantified relative to alternatives; no wall-clock, memory, or per-query latency comparison against RL(text)/RL(image) baselines or against full fine-tuning is given. Effective: Partially. Accuracy and VHR gains are statistically resolved, but the same evaluation reveals CORAL is worse than the base on IS_pred (an image-sensitivity proxy), so the paper does not show the model actually became more visually reliant in a global sense, only that it became less prone to one specific asymmetric failure mode. Scalable: Not tested. The evaluation is capped at n=100/benchmark for the primary claims and n=500/benchmark for cross-domain diagnostics; the authors explicitly defer larger-n confirmation of VRS/BD to future camera-ready work rather than presenting it here. Strengths: - The metric suite decomposes a single accuracy number into a family of falsifiable behavioral quantities (VRS, BD, VHR, IS_pred/raw, VBR, HN-GAP), each with a stated failure interpretation, which is more diagnostic than the single blindfolding metric in prior general-domain work. - The paper reports a result unfavorable to its own model (CORAL's higher IS_pred) rather than omitting or downplaying it, and explicitly separates the raw-string IS variant (which would have favored CORAL) from the extracted-answer variant it argues is correct. - The RL(image) text-prior signature (highest no-image accuracy of any model, including the base) is a genuinely informative negative result: it demonstrates that an RL objective trained on image-question-answer triples can still degrade grounding, which is a useful caution against assuming multimodal RL training data implies grounded learning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Explicitly acknowledged: PMC-VQA train/eval overlap, small n=100 per-benchmark sample size limiting VRS/BD to directional (not resolved) status, IS_pred's inability to distinguish true answer invariance from distinct reasoning paths converging on the same choice, and narrow model scope (Qwen2.5-VL/MedVLThinker family only, no LLaVA-Med/BiomedGPT/HuatuoGPT-Vision). - The authors confront these directly in a dedicated Limitations section rather than burying them, a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No lambda sensitivity analysis is shown despite lambda=0.5 being a load-bearing hyperparameter selected via an unreported validation sweep. - No comparison of CGO against a simpler baseline that penalizes answer changes via synthetic perturbation (blank/shuffle) rather than CLIP-retrieved hard negatives, so the paper does not isolate whether CLIP-based retrieval specifically (versus contrastive training in general, already explored by Liang et al. 2020) drives the gains. - The PathVQA result (accuracy regression, VHR improvement) is interpreted as "the objective suppressing some correct image-invariant predictions along with incorrect ones", a plausible but unverified mechanism; no analysis isolates which specific items regressed or whether they share identifiable characteristics. - No training-run variance (multiple seeds of CORAL fine-tuning) is reported, so it is unclear whether the +6.7 pp accuracy gain would replicate across independent training runs versus reflecting one favorable optimization trajectory. Bottom Line: A practitioner auditing or building medical VLMs gains a reusable, well-instrumented counterfactual evaluation protocol and a sobering empirical finding that RL fine-tuning on image-grounded data does not guarantee grounding, but should treat the CORAL accuracy gains cautiously given the acknowledged PMC-VQA train/eval overlap, the small n=100 per-benchmark sample, and CORAL's own worse-than-baseline IS_pred score.
Search Results 2607.03647 PDF
#61 — Innovative (64)

WildCity: A Real-World City-Scale Testbed for Rendering, Simulation, and Spatial Intelligence (4 ▲ )

Xiangyu Han, Mengyu Yang, Jiaqi Li, Bowen Chang, Ziyu Chen, Hexu Zhao, Rahul Kumar Agrawal, Anthony Rodriguez, Fiona Hua, Marco Pavone, Chen Feng, Yiming Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WildCity, an 18-trajectory, 1,507 km real-world multimodal dataset from autonomous-fleet logs across six US cities, plus an urban-tailored 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction baseline and a closed-loop simulator built on top of it. - Why it's important: Foundation models for embodied spatial reasoning are validated at room or city-block scale, and no existing real-world dataset combines surround-view sensing, continuous multi-kilometer traversals, and multi-city coverage needed to test whether reconstruction and reasoning methods scale to an entire city. - How it does it: The baseline decomposes camera pose into a shared ego pose plus fixed rig extrinsics, adds ground-plane regularization and a separate sky MLP to stabilize unbounded street geometry, and uses a diffusion-based render-repair-augment loop (Difix3D+) to patch extrapolated viewpoints, all trained with multi-GPU Gaussian sharding. - The good: On the 2.5 km Atlanta sequence the method cuts depth L1 error to 6.62 m versus 8.35-15.4 m for CityGaussianV2, H-3DGS, and VGGT-Long+CityGS while also winning on PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS, showing that geometry-aware constraints, not just spatial partitioning, are needed for accurate city-scale reconstruction. - The missing: All quantitative reconstruction results come from two or six single-log sequences per city, and no experiment reports whether the pipeline actually scales to the full 1,507 km / 3.01M-keyframe corpus rather than 5 km chunks; the embodied-reasoning claim rests on one qualitative demo (Alpamayo-1) with no quantitative task metric. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating large-scale street-view 3DGS reconstruction or city-scale embodied-AI benchmarks; the dataset and the scalability/extrapolation/uncertainty framing are the main value, not the reconstruction method itself, which is an incremental combination of known techniques.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Practical Impact (72) and Completeness (70) carry the score: the 1,507 km / 3.01M-keyframe multi-city surround-view corpus is a genuine scale jump on an axis where prior real-world datasets were uniformly weak (83.7 km avg log vs. 0.06-10 km), and the authors confront limitations directly (mask boundary errors despite 91.58% mIoU, residual pose drift, Difix3D+ repair called 'a costly post-hoc solution' that hallucinates). Rigor (68) is solid for this compute-heavy literature (component ablation isolates pose/ground/sky, and the 2D-vs-3D misalignment result where removing pose optimization barely moves PSNR but explodes D-L1 from 13.26 m to 148.54 m is a reproducible methodological warning), but there are no seeds, no error bars, and main-paper evaluation is confined to two sequences that double as the dataset's own promotional evidence. Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (50) are the two dimensions holding the composite below 75: the reconstruction baseline is an acknowledged ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION assembling rig-aware pose factorization, sky MLPs, ground regularizers, and diffusion view-repair, each established in prior work. Resilience (65) is damaged by the gap between the title's city-scale reconstruction claim and the evidence, which tops out at 2.5 km per sequence with no full-log or multi-log merge attempted, and by the closed-loop simulation headline resting on one qualitative Alpamayo-1 rollout with no success or collision rate. Weighted composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*72 = 64.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Researchers building spatially-grounded AI, from photorealistic city digital twins to VLM-based navigation agents, lack real-world data that is simultaneously large-scale (tens of square kilometers), continuous (long unbroken trajectories rather than isolated clips), and multimodal (surround-view RGB, LiDAR, GPS, IMU). Existing options force a tradeoff: synthetic city datasets (MatrixCity, SS3DM) give scale and clean labels but suffer sim-to-real gap; real driving datasets (nuScenes, Argoverse 2, Waymo Open, PandaSet, WayveScenes101) offer real sensor noise but only short isolated clips (avg 0.06-0.2 km); route-level datasets (KITTI-360, Oxford RobotCar) give longer traversals but limited geographic diversity or surround-view sensing; reconstruction-oriented sets (Block-NeRF, Oxford Spires, H-3DGS) push scale (up to 9 km) but remain confined to single blocks or single cities. No dataset jointly provides real-world data, surround-view sensing, continuous coverage, and multi-city street-level imagery at city scale. Key Idea: The dataset contribution has no single algorithmic trick; its value is a data-engineering pipeline (SLAM+GPS pose refinement, motion-compensated LiDAR deskewing, SAM3-based semantic masking gated by 3D-tracklet velocity to separate genuinely moving from parked/stationary objects, and standardized 5 km/multi-length chunking) applied at unprecedented real-world scale. The reconstruction baseline's core insight is that spatial partitioning/hierarchy (the strategy used by CityGaussianV2, H-3DGS) is necessary but not sufficient for long, narrow street-view trajectories with sparse view overlap; additional structural priors, specifically rig-consistent pose optimization and explicit ground/sky decomposition, are what actually fix the geometric ambiguity that partitioning alone leaves unresolved. Methodology: Data pipeline: (1) ego poses from onboard SLAM refined with GPS and loop closure; (2) per-sensor measurements aligned to front-camera timestamps via nearest-neighbor lookup (Eq. 12) rather than resampling to a fixed rate, keeping timestamp mismatch under 50 ms; (3) sweep-level LiDAR motion compensation warping each point to the keyframe reference time (Eq. 14); (4) SAM3 text-prompted masks for ground/sky/movable-object regions, refined by 3D tracking cuboids that reclassify an instance as truly dynamic only if velocity exceeds 1 m/s (Eq. 15-16), separating parked cars from moving traffic; (5) uniform 0.5 m keyframe subsampling and partitioning into 5 km chunks, with sub-trajectories released at six lengths (50 m to 5 km) for controlled-scale evaluation. Reconstruction baseline: standard 3DGS alpha-blending (Eq. 1) as the rendering base, jointly optimized with (a) rig pose decomposition, factoring each camera pose into a shared per-keyframe ego pose and fixed rig extrinsics (Eq. 2-3), constrained toward SLAM/calibration priors via a pose-distance regularizer; (b) a separate view-dependent sky MLP (3-layer, width 64, degree-6 positional encoding, 16-d per-image appearance embedding) composited as an infinite background (Eq. 4), decoupling sky appearance from scene geometry; (c) ground regularization via three losses on Gaussians classified as ground: a distortion loss penalizing vertical-coordinate spread within camera-depth slices, an alignment loss encouraging vertically-thin ground splats, and an opacity loss pushing ground Gaussians opaque (Eq. 5-7), active only during the first 20k training steps; (d) an iterative render-repair-augment loop using Difix3D+ to hallucinate plausible content at extrapolated poses and feed the repaired image back into the training set (Eq. 8-10); (e) GrendelGS/gsplat-style multi-GPU Gaussian sharding for memory-bounded training at up to 30M primitives. Training: 600k steps, depth supervision via disparity loss (weight 0.1), densification from step 10k-60k adding 0.5% of current Gaussian count per 100-step interval, spherical-harmonics degree 1, two H200 GPUs. Design-decision assessment: the rig-pose factorization is well-justified as it directly encodes the physical constraint that all six cameras share one rigid mount, unlike prior independent-camera pose optimization; the ground regularization is reasonable but its curriculum-only (first 20k steps) activation is an engineering heuristic without ablation on the cutoff choice; the Difix3D+ repair loop is acknowledged by the authors themselves as "a costly post-hoc solution" that can hallucinate content when the underlying reconstruction is weak, i.e., it treats a symptom rather than the stated root cause of weak extrapolation priors. Key Results: Dataset scale: 3.01M keyframes, 18 logs across 6 cities, 1,507.1 km total (avg 83.7 km/log, avg 2.5 hours/log), ~40.18 km^2 coverage per city; semantic masks validated against 100 manually annotated images reach 91.58% overall mIoU (88.48 dynamic, 92.01 sky, 94.24 ground). Reconstruction: on Ann Arbor-0.5k (0.25 km), the method scores PSNR 29.99 dB, SSIM 0.917, LPIPS 0.240, D-L1 15.16 m, roughly matching or trailing H-3DGS (27.59 dB) and CityGS (24.41 dB) on raw PSNR but with much lower depth error than both (18.45 m and 17.05 m respectively) and far below vanilla 3DGS (32.7 m). On Atlanta-5k (2.5 km), the method wins outright: 23.14 dB PSNR, 0.799 SSIM, 0.477 LPIPS, 6.62 m D-L1, versus the next-best CityGS at 21.27 dB / 8.35 m and H-3DGS at 21.40 dB / 14.37 m. A feed-forward pose/point-cloud initializer (VGGT-Long) fails to substitute for SLAM-based pose optimization on long sequences, collapsing PSNR to 13.40 dB when substituted into the CityGS pipeline versus 23.14 dB for the paper's own pose-optimized approach. Cross-city evaluation (Table 7, 2.5 km each) shows PSNR ranging 23.6-26.6 dB across the six cities, all above 25 dB except Atlanta (23.59 dB) and Arlington (24.95 dB), attributed to denser intersections and more dynamic objects. Component ablation (Table 5) shows removing pose optimization causes the largest single degradation on Atlanta (D-L1 jumps from 13.26 m to 148.54 m, an order-of-magnitude collapse) despite only modest 2D-metric change, explicitly demonstrating that 2D image similarity can misalign with 3D geometric quality. Scale-degradation comparison against synthetic Carla data (Fig. 8) shows real WildCity degrades 4.34 dB PSNR from 0.1k to 2k keyframes versus 0.57 dB for Carla, indicating real-world degradation is not explained by capacity alone. Baselines are current (CityGaussianV2 2024, H-3DGS 2024, VGGT-Long 2025) and represent the relevant state of the art for large-scale 3DGS reconstruction; the comparison set is reasonably strong, though the paper does not compare against 2025 feed-forward reconstruction methods beyond VGGT-Long (e.g., DUSt3R extensions) at full scale. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (primary contribution: the only dataset combining real, multi-city, surround-view, continuous city-scale route coverage per Table 1) and ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (the reconstruction baseline is a targeted combination of existing techniques, rig-aware pose optimization, sky MLPs, ground regularization losses, and diffusion-based view repair, each individually established in prior work, assembled and tuned for the specific failure modes of long narrow street-view trajectories). No new formulation, method, or empirical finding beyond the applied setting; the genuine novelty is the data corpus and the systematic scalability/extrapolation/uncertainty analysis it enables, not a new algorithmic idea. Rigor & Reproducibility: A component ablation (Table 5, ground/sky/pose-opt on/off) isolates each module's contribution on two cities and reveals the 2D-vs-3D metric misalignment noted above; this is a genuine strength. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere, standard for this compute-heavy reconstruction literature but still a gap. Evaluation is confined to two representative sequences in the main paper (Ann Arbor-0.5k, Atlanta-5k) with a six-city extension only in the appendix; the two main-paper sequences double as the dataset's own promotional evidence, though the semantic-mask validation against independent manual annotations (Table 2) and the cross-baseline comparison somewhat mitigate self-serving-benchmark concerns. Code/data release: a project page URL is given (github.io) but no explicit statement in the read sections confirms code or dataset public release terms, license, or download instructions; this should be checked on the project page before assuming full reproducibility. Baselines are trained under their own native training budgets and hardware rather than a fixed iteration cap, which the authors justify (different baselines have structurally different optimization budgets) but which also means the specific PSNR/D-L1 numbers depend partly on how well each baseline's official pipeline was tuned to this new data format. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (reconstruction pipeline validated on real driving logs) with a SYSTEMS component (the released dataset infrastructure and closed-loop simulator). - Practical: Training runs on two H200 GPUs at the reported scale (30M Gaussians, 600k steps); minimum VRAM scales from 1x24G at 0.5 km to 2x80G at 5 km (Table 3), so full city-scale reconstruction requires datacenter-class multi-GPU hardware, not a single consumer GPU. - Efficient: Not quantified in wall-clock training time; VRAM cost scales roughly linearly with route length and Gaussian count is uncapped except by the 30M budget, and the paper explicitly flags that Gaussian growth is a bottleneck at city scale. - Effective: The method reduces depth error substantially over baselines on the two tested sequences and the ablation confirms pose optimization is the dominant driver of geometric accuracy, but effectiveness is only measured via PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS/D-L1, not via any downstream simulation or task-completion metric beyond a single qualitative VLA navigation demo. - Scalable: Figure 8 explicitly shows quality degrading as trajectory length grows from 0.1k to 2k keyframes even with matched Gaussian budgets, and the paper states this in its own discussion box: current methods "do not scale gracefully to continuous city-scale scenes." The paper tests up to 2.5 km per sequence, well short of the full ~83.7 km average log length or the 1,507 km corpus; no experiment demonstrates the pipeline actually completing at full-log or full-city scale. Software artifact: the dataset itself (WildCity) is the deliverable artifact along with the semantic-mask generation pipeline and the reconstruction baseline code (implied by project page); a trained model or public leaderboard is not described in the read sections. Strengths: - The dataset is a genuine scale jump on the specific axis (real + multi-city + surround-view + continuous route length) where prior work was uniformly weak, per the direct quantitative comparison in Table 1 (83.7 km avg route vs. 0.06-10 km for prior real-world datasets). - The 2D-vs-3D metric misalignment finding (Table 5: removing pose optimization barely changes PSNR/SSIM but explodes D-L1 from 13.26 m to 148.54 m) is a specific, reproducible, and useful methodological warning for the field, not a generic observation. - The systematic real-vs-synthetic degradation comparison (Fig. 8) isolates that real-world reconstruction failure is not purely a capacity/scale artifact by holding Gaussian budget fixed and showing real data degrades 7-10x faster than Carla on PSNR/LPIPS. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state semantic masks are automatically generated and may contain boundary errors and rare-category mistakes despite the 91.58% mIoU validation; that GPS-anchored poses avoid unbounded horizontal drift but leave residual sub-centimeter horizontal and centimeter-per-kilometer vertical drift; and that the paper's own extrapolation repair via Difix3D+ is "a costly post-hoc solution" that "can hallucinate content when the base reconstruction is weak." This is a direct, specific confrontation of limitations rather than boilerplate hedging. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No quantitative embodied-reasoning or navigation benchmark is run in the reconstructed digital twin; the closed-loop simulation claim (Section 4.3, Fig. 6) is illustrated with a single qualitative Alpamayo-1 rollout and no success rate, collision rate, or task metric, despite this being framed as a headline capability in the abstract ("Beyond reconstruction... enables closed-loop simulation"). - Reconstruction is validated at 0.25-2.5 km per sequence; the paper's own discussion concedes scalability degrades with length, but no experiment attempts the full ~83.7 km average log or a multi-log city merge, leaving the actual city-scale (tens of km^2) reconstruction claim in the title partially unvalidated by the reconstruction experiments themselves (only the dataset coverage, not the reconstruction, is validated at full city scale). - The six cities are all US cities collected by a single autonomous-fleet operator (May Mobility); geographic and sensor diversity (e.g., non-US traffic patterns, different camera rigs, weather extremes) is unaddressed, which may limit generalization claims about "diverse urban styles." - VGGT-X was tested as an alternative feed-forward initializer and excluded from the main table for producing "extremely sparse and unstable point clouds" on multi-thousand-view sequences; this negative result is disclosed only briefly in the appendix rather than analyzed, and it is unclear whether other recent feed-forward 3D methods (DUSt3R-family) were attempted at comparable scale. Bottom Line: The WildCity dataset itself is a genuine, well-validated contribution that fills a specific and previously-unaddressed real-world data gap (multi-city, surround-view, continuous city-scale street routes); the accompanying reconstruction baseline is a competent but incremental engineering combination whose main scientific payoff is the demonstrated 2D/3D metric misalignment finding, and practitioners doing large-scale urban 3DGS or embodied-AI benchmarking should read this primarily for the dataset and the scalability/extrapolation/uncertainty framing, not for a new reconstruction algorithm.
Recommended 2607.06838 PDF
#62 — Innovative (64)

AutoMem

Shengguang Wu, Hao Zhu, Yuhui Zhang, Xiaohan Wang, Serena Yeung-Levy · arxiv.org · 🥇Top AI Papers of the Week
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AUTOMEM, a framework that automates two aspects of LLM agent memory management, the file-schema/prompt scaffold and the model's memory-decision proficiency, using meta-LLMs that review full episode traces. - Why it's important: Long-horizon agent tasks (10^4-10^5 steps) make memory mistakes invisible for thousands of steps, so manual tuning of memory scaffolds and no prior method trains the model's own skill at using memory as a distinct capability. - How it does it: A meta-LLM (Claude Opus 4.6/4.7) reads complete episode logs like a code reviewer, first rewriting the agent's file-system memory scaffold (Outer-loop 1), then curating the agent's own good memory decisions as LoRA training data for a separate frozen-task/trainable-memory-specialist split (Outer-loop 2). - The good: On Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, memory optimization alone (no task-policy weight changes) roughly doubles to quadruples progression rate on Crafter (25.0%->51.4%), MiniHack (7.5%->30.0%), and NetHack (0.42%->1.85%), reaching parity with Claude Opus 4.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro Thinking on these games. - The missing: All experiments use episodic memory reset at the start of each episode; the paper does not test persistent cross-episode memory, non-game/real-world tasks, or whether a single scaffold/specialist generalizes across environments (three separate scaffolds and specialists were trained, one per game). - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building long-horizon LLM agents or working on memory architectures, since it isolates memory as an independently optimizable axis with a concrete, replicable meta-LLM-driven optimization loop; less relevant if your interest is task planning or reasoning rather than memory.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Practical Impact (75) is the strongest dimension: released code (github.com/autoLearnMem/AutoMem), a 32B open-weight model reaching parity with Claude Opus 4.5 on Crafter/MiniHack, and a scaffold-revision loop that transfers to other agent stacks. Rigor (58) is the limiter: the scaffold-optimization gating criterion accepts revisions based on the same fixed 10-seed set used for the reported results, which is a Goodhart problem the paper does not flag, and no significance tests accompany percentage-point gains with overlapping error bars (NetHack 1.57%+-0.35 to 1.85%+-0.44). Novelty (62) reflects that file-system memory, LoRA fine-tuning, and meta-LLM architecture search are all established; the combination and the memory-specialist/task-model weight split are the new parts. Completeness (70) is helped by direct acknowledgment of episodic-only memory, game-only evaluation, and per-environment scaffolds. Weighted composite: 0.20*62 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 = 64.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating on long-horizon tasks (game episodes spanning 10^3-10^5 steps) exceed the context window, so external memory (files, retrieval stores, scratchpads) is required. Existing approaches treat memory as a fixed architectural module rather than a skill the model exercises, and manually tuning the memory scaffold (file schema, prompts, action vocabulary) or the model's proficiency at using it is intractable because a single memory mistake (e.g., a missed map coordinate at step 50) may not surface as a failure until step 800, making trajectory-level human review of complete episodes impractical at this scale. Key Idea: Promote file-system operations (read, write, search, append, create) to first-class actions in the same action space as task actions, so every memory decision is a traceable, observable step in the trajectory; this traceability lets a strong "meta-LLM" (not the acting agent) review complete episode traces the way a code reviewer reads an execution log, and use that review to drive two separate optimization axes: the memory scaffold (structure) and the acting model's memory decisions (proficiency), rather than optimizing memory as a byproduct of task-reward-based RL or a fixed retrieval architecture. Methodology: The inner-loop agent is a single LLM (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, served via vLLM) that at each step runs a LOG routine (decide whether/how to record the environment's response) and a PLAN routine (search/read memory files, then commit a world action), operating on a directory of memory files (e.g., dungeon_map.txt, inventory.txt, strategy.txt). Outer-loop 1 (scaffold optimizer) has a meta-LLM (Claude Opus 4.6, effort=max) read full episode traces plus agent code and memory files, then rewrite the code/prompts/file schema; a revision is accepted only if it strictly improves mean progression on the same fixed evaluation seeds (gated retry: 1 retry in-session on failure, else restart from a clean session), iterating to convergence (2-5 iterations per environment). Outer-loop 2 (training engine) has a meta-LLM (Claude Opus 4.7, effort=max) orchestrate three joint decisions: (a) collect ~100-400 episode traces from the optimized scaffold, (b) curate training examples from the agent's own memory-operation text (filtering out examples with no memory-op signal, trimming gameplay-action text so only memory-operation reasoning remains), and (c) select a matching LoRA configuration (rank, alpha, batch size, epochs, differs per environment), then fine-tune a dedicated "memory specialist" (LoRA-adapted copy) that handles LOG and the memory-consultation part of PLAN, while a separate frozen "task model" (same base weights, unmodified) commits world actions — a short handoff passes conversation state from specialist to gameplay model after the last memory operation. Three design decisions stand out: (1) gating scaffold revisions strictly on eval-seed improvement prevents accepted-but-harmful edits, a reasonable safeguard though it risks overfitting to the fixed 10-seed evaluation set; (2) splitting memory-specialist and task-model weights isolates the training signal to memory behavior and explicitly preserves task competence, well-justified by the reported +9-18% relative gain stacking cleanly on top of scaffold gains; (3) using opaque per-run labels is absent here (that's the companion grouper/ranker system in a different paper) — not applicable. Key Results: On BALROG (Crafter, MiniHack, NetHack), progression rate improves from a memory-as-file-system baseline (v0) of 25.0%/7.5%/0.42% to a fully optimized AUTOMEM agent of 51.36+-3.81% / 30.0+-7.25% / 1.85+-0.44% (x1.89-x4.4 gains), versus Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct baselines of 27.3%/5.0%/0.3% and Qwen2.5-32B with sliding-window (19.55%/2.50%/0.00%) or chain-of-thought (17.27%/10.00%/0.00%) context management. The optimized 32B model reaches near-parity with frontier proprietary systems: Claude-Opus-4.5 (49.5/27.5/2.0) and is within a few points of Gemini-3.1-Pro-Thinking (55.0/27.5/2.6) on these three metrics respectively. Scaffold optimization alone (Outer-loop 1) accounts for most of the gain (e.g., NetHack 0.42%->1.57%, x3.74); memory-specialist training (Outer-loop 2) adds a further +0.28 to +4.09 percentage points on top, comparable in magnitude to 1-2 scaffold iterations. Supporting behavioral metrics: unproductive action rate (stuck/oscillating) drops 32-65%, redundant memory writes drop 68-83%, empty-search rate falls 13-50%, and per-step input tokens shrink 3-30% after scaffold optimization; the LOG-phase write-to-search ratio falls 54-72% after specialist training, indicating a learned consult-before-write discipline. All environments are procedurally generated per episode (stochastic), limiting pretraining-knowledge transfer, and evaluation uses a fixed list of 10 seeds with 5-40 episodes per environment. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (treating memory management as an independently trainable skill decomposed into structure and proficiency axes, borrowing the metamemory concept from cognitive science) plus NEW METHOD (the two meta-LLM-driven outer loops: trajectory-level scaffold revision with acceptance gating, and LoRA data curation/training-config selection for a dedicated memory specialist). The individual mechanisms (file-system memory, LoRA fine-tuning, LLM-as-code-reviewer, meta-LLM-driven prompt/architecture search as in ADAS/AFlow/PromptBreeder) are known; the novel contribution is combining trajectory-level (not per-question) meta-LLM review to jointly automate both scaffold code revision and the curation-plus-configuration decision for training a memory-only model, applied to genuinely long-horizon (10^4-10^5 step) episodes. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present in a structural sense (v0 vs. scaffold-optimized vs. +trained, and per-iteration scaffold changes are itemized in Appendix B) but there is no ablation isolating which specific scaffold changes (e.g., the UPSERT_MAP dedup operation alone) drive which fraction of the gain. Results report mean +- standard error over a fixed list of 10 seeds per environment, consistent with the BALROG benchmark's own reporting convention, but this is a small sample for percentage-point differences of a few points (e.g., NetHack's 1.57%->1.85% gain has overlapping error bars, +-0.35 and +-0.44). No significance tests are reported. The scaffold is optimized and evaluated on the same fixed 10 seeds, which is a methodological concern: the gating criterion (accept only if mean progression on these seeds improves) risks overfitting the scaffold to this exact seed set, and the paper does not report held-out-seed generalization for the scaffold, though training-data collection episodes are explicitly seed-disjoint from evaluation. Code, prompts, and full implementation are released at a GitHub URL (github.com/autoLearnMem/AutoMem) plus a project page, which is a strong reproducibility positive. This is a preprint (arXiv, not yet peer-reviewed). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes, runs on a 32B open-weight model served locally via vLLM plus API calls to Claude for the meta-LLM roles; the meta-LLM cost/access requirement (Claude Opus with effort=max) is a deployment dependency not quantified in tokens or dollars. Efficient: The optimized scaffold reduces per-step input tokens by 3-30% relative to v0, and training the memory specialist uses modest LoRA configs (rank 128-256, 1-4 epochs, 444-1597 examples per environment) on two GPUs, but the outer-loop optimization cost itself (number of meta-LLM calls, iterations, and total tokens across 2-5 scaffold iterations plus 100-400-episode data collection) is not reported. Effective: Yes on the tested proxy metric (BALROG progression rate) across all three games, with qualitative trace evidence (Figure 6) corroborating the aggregate numbers (e.g., MiniHack Corridor-R3 task solved 0%->0%->100%). Scalable: Not tested. The paper explicitly limits scope to episodic (not persistent) memory and to game environments; whether the approach scales to real-world memory-intensive tasks, larger models, or shared scaffolds/specialists across environments is stated as future work, not evaluated. Strengths: - Cleanly isolates memory as an independent optimization axis by freezing task-action weights entirely, letting the paper make a specific quantitative claim ("memory management is higher-leverage than model scale") backed by a controlled comparison against a 72B same-family model. - The trajectory-level review workflow is a genuine methodological move: using a meta-LLM to read complete 10^4-10^5-step logs and localize delayed-consequence memory bugs (e.g., diagnosing an unbounded append-only map file causing duplicate entries) is a concrete instance of automating what the paper correctly identifies as intractable for human review. - The split between a frozen task model and a LoRA-trained memory specialist is a well-designed architectural choice that empirically avoids trading off task competence for memory gains, evidenced by the specialist gains stacking cleanly on top of scaffold gains rather than regressing them. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state three explicit limitations: (1) memory is episodic only, resetting each episode, with persistent cross-episode memory left as future work; (2) evaluation is restricted to game environments, chosen because they suit long-horizon/procedural-generation/information-management study, with real-world task transfer unverified; (3) because the three games differ structurally, a separate scaffold and memory specialist were trained per environment, and whether a single shared scaffold/specialist could generalize across environments is explicitly left open. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The scaffold-optimization gating criterion evaluates and accepts revisions on the same fixed 10-seed set used for final reported results, which the paper does not flag as a potential source of seed-specific overfitting (unlike the training loop, which explicitly uses disjoint seeds). The cost of the outer-loop optimization process itself (total meta-LLM tokens/dollars across iterations) is never quantified, which matters for anyone assessing practical adoption cost versus the reported gains. NetHack absolute progression remains very low (1.85%) even after both loops, a large residual gap versus frontier proprietary NetHack numbers reported elsewhere as substantially higher in absolute terms is not directly contextualized, likely reflecting NetHack's exceptional difficulty rather than a AUTOMEM-specific weakness, but the paper's framing ("approaching frontier proprietary systems") is more accurate for Crafter/MiniHack than for NetHack, where all methods including frontier proprietary systems remain in the low single digits. Bottom Line: A practitioner building long-horizon LLM agents gains a concrete, well-evidenced, and reproducible (code released) recipe for treating memory management as a separately optimizable skill, with the scaffold-revision loop being the more broadly applicable and lower-cost of the two contributions.
Recommended 2607.01224 PDF
#63 — Innovative (63)

OmniPilot: An Uncertainty-Aware LLM Inference Advisor for Heterogeneous GPU Clusters

D Balamurugan, TW Bush · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OmniPilot, a pre-submission launch advisor that predicts LLM inference serving costs across GPU type, tensor-parallel degree, and precision, and abstains when a request falls outside its measured support envelope. - Why it's important: Shared GPU clusters waste node-hours on bad first-attempt configurations; the authors report 80 of 561 benchmark runs (14%) failed immediately from hardware incompatibility or OOM, and static one-size-fits-all recipes miss non-monotonic precision and KV-cache interactions. - How it does it: A gradient-boosted quantile cost model over eight serving targets, calibrated with conformalized quantile regression (CQR) using held-out-cell residuals, ranks configurations by an economic utility (throughput value minus node-hour cost, queue wait, failure cost, and a KV-saturation penalty) and abstains via support-distance out-of-distribution (OOD) detection. - The good: On the frozen 460-row benchmark, the advisor reaches 95% top-1 placement accuracy with mean utility regret of 0.003, versus 0.020-2.71 for model-free baselines (size-threshold rule, H100/FP8 recipe, throughput-max), and the support-distance abstention layer correctly flags all five held-out OOD cells (24-46% prediction error) as low-confidence. - The missing: The entire evaluation comes from a single cluster (Kempner AI, 3 GPU kinds) with deployment-specific thresholds (0.85 KV knee, freshness windows), and the update-loop evidence is a single promotion cycle (447→460 rows), so neither cross-cluster generalization nor longitudinal drift behavior is demonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone operating a shared multi-tenant GPU cluster who needs a concrete pattern for calibrated, abstention-aware launch advising; the value-of-information benchmark gate section (Appendix C.4, a documented negative result) is also a useful cautionary case study on quantile-GBM limitations.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Completeness (75) and Rigor (70) are the strongest dimensions: leakage-free GroupKFold by workload cell, bootstrap confidence intervals on the headline top-1 and regret numbers, targeted ablations (dropping the quantization feature doubles FP8 error), and, unusually, two reported negative results (cluster-telemetry infeasibility and the benchmark gate's zero value-of-information) that the authors ran to failure rather than omitting. The held-out-cell versus random-fold conformal comparison (64-69% vs 80-81% coverage) is a transferable methodological finding beyond this system. Practical Impact (55) and Resilience (60) are the limiters: everything comes from one cluster with deployment-specific thresholds, the 95% top-1 headline is partly agreement with a single expert operator whose revealed preferences calibrated the utility function, and leave-one-family-out error blows out to 29-126% on structurally novel architectures. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*55 = 63.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Operators submitting LLM inference jobs to a shared, heterogeneous GPU cluster (here: A100/H100/H200 at an academic HPC center) must pick GPU type, tensor-parallel (TP) degree, and numerical precision before the job runs, without knowing how these choices interact with current cluster load. Prior submission-time decisions are made blind because traditional schedulers (Slurm) only match static resource requests, with no historical or behavioral awareness of workload performance. The interactions are counter-intuitive and costly to learn by trial: a 4-bit format can run slower than 16-bit on a given runtime, KV-cache pressure changes which precision is best per model, and multi-GPU launch success rates vary more than twofold across TP degrees. Existing serving systems (vLLM, Orca, Sarathi-Serve, SGLang, AlpaServe, DistServe, Splitwise) optimize execution within an already-chosen configuration; none of them chooses the configuration itself pre-submission. Key Idea: Treat configuration selection as a calibrated economic decision rather than a static rule: fit quantile regressions for the serving targets that actually enter the decision, calibrate their prediction intervals post hoc against held-out workload cells (not random folds), and use support-distance from the training envelope, not interval width, as the signal for when to abstain. The paper's own framing of this insight ("feature-before-data": a new operational axis, e.g. quantization format, must be represented in the featurizer before its data exists, or the axis silently defaults to uninformative) is a secondary but genuine methodological contribution. Methodology: The pipeline (Fig. 1) has four stages: (1) feature engineering over job + system features (~28 features: log-transformed numerics, one-hot GPU/model-family/quantization encodings); (2) an inference cost model — gradient-boosted regressors with quantile loss fitting {0.1, 0.5, 0.9} quantiles in log space across eight targets (six decision-driving: aggregate tokens/s, request throughput, TTFT p50, cold-start time, KV-cache usage, average power; two diagnostic-only: SM and tensor-core activity, excluded from the decision utility because the 60s DCGM sampling cadence under-resolves them, R²(log) 0.30-0.40); (3) a decision layer that computes expected economic utility (throughput × runtime × per-token value, minus compute cost, queue wait, expected-failure cost, SLO-violation cost, and a KV-saturation penalty applying a multiplicative throughput penalty above an 85% occupancy knee) and ranks configuration arms; (4) an OOD abstention layer using support-distance across seven training axes, independent of interval width. Calibration uses conformalized quantile regression with a split-conformal additive offset in log space, calibrated on held-out-cell residuals (grouped by workload cell: model × context × concurrency), which the paper shows is critical — random-fold calibration under-covers at 64-69% versus the 80% nominal target, while held-out-cell calibration restores 80-81% coverage at a 4-12% interval-width cost. Three design decisions stand out: (a) using held-out-cell rather than random-fold conformal calibration is well-justified empirically (Table B1 shows the coverage gap directly); (b) the value-per-token calibration (2×10⁻⁷) is fit by matching an expert operator's revealed anchors (7B→A100, 32B→2×A100) rather than derived independently, a soft, single-operator-derived choice; (c) the beta-binomial shrinkage of per-configuration launch-success priors toward the pooled rate is reasonable for sparse cells but is explicitly not causal (the cluster-wide harvest is observational and confounded, per §4.5). Key Results: On the frozen 460-row benchmark (6 model families, 3 GPU kinds, 4 precisions, TP 1-8, leakage-free 5-fold GroupKFold by workload cell), aggregate-throughput MAPE is 6.2% with log-space R² = 0.92 and 81% interval coverage (nominal 80%); TTFT p50 is 12% MAPE, R² 0.92. On 65 multi-arm joint (GPU+TP+precision) workload cells at the cost-conscious operating point, OmniPilot reaches 95% top-1 accuracy and 0.003 mean utility regret (bootstrap CI [89%,100%] top-1, [0.000,0.008] regret), versus 2.71 regret for a no-model mean-arm baseline, 1.97 for a size-threshold TP rule, 1.65 for an H100/FP8 recipe, and 4.73 for throughput-max; the closest competitive baseline, cheapest-fit, reaches 91% top-1 with 0.010 regret. On a re-run current dataset (66 cells, 476 rows) OmniPilot gets 92% top-1 / 0.006 regret, consistent with the frozen result. On five genuinely out-of-distribution cells (a new model family, OLMo-2-13B, and Qwen-7B at unseen context/concurrency settings), prediction error rises to 24-46%, conformal coverage drops to 0/5 points, and the abstention layer correctly flags all five as low-confidence — this is the paper's cleanest demonstration that the guardrail works, though the n=5 sample is too small to estimate an OOD error rate, a limitation the authors state directly. A leave-one-family-out generalization test (Table B7) shows related families predict at 11-19% error while structurally novel architectures (Phi, Gemma) degrade to 29-126%. A separate negative result: mining 500K Slurm jobs shows scheduler metadata alone cannot support a cluster-wide throughput advisor because it records GPU allocation state but not which workload ran (only 903 of 500K jobs self-identify as vLLM-like). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the OmniPilot advisor itself) combined with NEW METHOD (held-out-cell conformal calibration for grouped workload data; support-distance abstention independent of interval width) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the cluster-telemetry negative result; the value-of-information benchmark-gate failure mode in Appendix C.4). The core building blocks — gradient-boosted quantile regression, CQR, beta-binomial shrinkage, economic utility scoring — are all standard techniques; the novelty is in their specific combination and calibration choices applied to the pre-submission GPU configuration-selection problem, which the related-work section credibly argues is unaddressed by prior serving-system and scheduler literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and specific (Table B2): removing the quantization feature roughly doubles out-of-fold FP8 error (3.88%→7.36%) while leaving bf16 unchanged; removing the KV-saturation utility term reintroduces a 0.28-regret miss on one cell; removing the per-config success prior over-recommends multi-GPU configs whose real success rate is ~38%. Evaluation uses leakage-free GroupKFold grouped by workload cell (not by row), which is the correct discipline given the paper's own finding that random-fold splits leak and inflate apparent coverage. Bootstrap confidence intervals are reported for top-1 accuracy and regret at the primary operating point. No multi-seed variance is reported for the main quantile-GBM fits (only the data-efficiency curve in Fig. 3 uses 12 seeds). Code, benchmark dataset, and cost model are stated to be in a project repository (no URL given in the visible text); the cluster-wide harvest is explicitly not redistributable (salted-hash anonymization). A clear methodological strength: the paper reports its own negative/failure results (cluster telemetry infeasibility, benchmark-gate zero value-of-information) rather than omitting them, which is unusual and raises credibility. One concern: the value-per-token calibration is anchored to a single expert operator's stated preferences (n=1, informal), which is a soft empirical foundation for the utility function that everything else is optimized against. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built, evaluated advisor integrated with a production cluster). - Practical: Yes — it runs on standard cluster telemetry (Slurm accounting, DCGM) and commodity data center GPUs already in the deployment (A100/H100/H200); no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Not quantified. The paper does not report the compute/latency cost of running the advisor itself (gradient-boosted quantile models are cheap to score, but no numbers are given), nor training cost of the periodic refit loop. - Effective: Yes on the tested cluster and tested workload — 92-95% top-1 accuracy against real economic-regret baselines is a direct measure of the stated problem, not a proxy metric. - Scalable: Untested beyond the single cluster and single-node inference scope. The paper explicitly defers multi-node serving and training/recovery workloads to future work and states thresholds (0.85 KV knee, freshness windows) are deployment-specific and unvalidated elsewhere. The work delivers a software artifact (stated repository with dataset, cost model, and advisor code) though no URL is visible in the extracted text. Strengths: - The held-out-cell versus random-fold conformal calibration comparison (Table B1: 64-69% vs. 80-81% coverage) is a concrete, quantified methodological finding applicable beyond this system to any conformal-prediction pipeline over grouped/correlated data. - Honest reporting of two negative results (cluster-telemetry infeasibility from scheduler metadata alone; the benchmark-gate's zero value-of-information at the cost-conscious operating point, traced to a specific mechanism — conditioning that shrinks variance without updating the posterior mean) shows the authors ran mechanisms to failure rather than only reporting wins. - The support-distance abstention design is validated against its stated purpose: on genuine OOD cells where interval width alone fails to signal distrust (intervals cover 0/5 points but are not obviously wider), the support-distance check independently flags all five as low-confidence. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly (§4.5, §5.5): all results come from a single cluster, so absolute thresholds (KV knee, freshness windows, value-per-token) are deployment-specific and unvalidated elsewhere; the cluster-wide launch-success prior is observational and confounded, used only as a demand/failure prior rather than causal ground truth; the OOD evidence (n=5 cells) illustrates a failure regime rather than establishing an OOD error rate; the update-loop evidence is a single promotion cycle, not a longitudinal trend; training and fault-recovery workloads are out of scope; the benchmark gate is a known structural limitation (shrinks variance without updating posterior mean) with no fix yet implemented. This is an unusually direct and specific limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The economic utility function's value-per-token parameter is calibrated to match one expert operator's revealed preference; the 95% top-1 accuracy headline result is therefore partly a measure of agreement with that one operator's judgment, not an externally validated notion of "correct" placement. No sensitivity analysis on this parameter is shown outside the two named operating points (cost-conscious vs. throughput-first). - No wall-clock or resource cost is reported for running the advisor itself or for the periodic retraining loop, which matters for a system meant to sit in the critical path of every launch decision. - Table B7 (leave-one-family-out) shows some held-out families degrade to 56-126% MAPE (gemma, phi variants); the main-text headline numbers (6.2% MAPE, 92-95% top-1) describe in-distribution accuracy, and a reader skimming only the abstract/TL;DR could substantially overestimate general-purpose accuracy without reaching Appendix B. - The paper does not compare against any published pre-submission placement advisor as a baseline (Helix is cited as "closest comparator" but not benchmarked head-to-head); all quantitative baselines are model-free heuristics (size-threshold, cheapest-fit, throughput-max), which is a fair comparison set for this paper's stated gap but leaves open how much of the 95%-vs-91% (cheapest-fit) gap would matter to a real operator. Bottom Line: A practitioner running a shared multi-tenant GPU cluster gains a concrete, honestly-evaluated pattern (grouped conformal calibration, support-distance abstention, KV-saturation-aware utility) worth adapting, but the single-cluster scope and single-operator-calibrated utility function mean the specific numeric claims (95% top-1, 0.003 regret) should not be taken as portable performance guarantees for a different cluster.
Search Results 2607.01579 PDF
#64 — Innovative (63)

Jet-Long: Efficient Long-Context Extension with Dynamic Bifocal RoPE (7 ▲ )

Haozhan Tang, Zerui Wang, Yuxian Gu, Song Han, Han Cai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Jet-Long, a tuning-free zero-shot context-extension method that pairs a RoPE-faithful local attention window with a remote window whose position-rescaling factor adapts dynamically to the current sequence length, fused into a single CuTe kernel for near-free inference. - Why it's important: Open-weight LLMs are pretrained on 4K-32K windows but deployed on agentic, RAG, and coding workloads that push inputs to 100K+ tokens, and existing zero-shot extension methods fix a single rescaling factor that either sacrifices short-context fidelity or breaks down at long context. - How it does it: A discrete group size G = max(1, ceil(L/w_pretrained)) recomputed from the live sequence length L aliases remote-window positions onto the model's pretrained relative-angle grid, combined with an on-the-fly RoPE correction rotation that leaves the KV cache untouched and an inclusion-exclusion merge of three FlashAttention calls (stabilized via LogSumExp) to avoid materializing the full attention matrix. - The good: On Qwen3-1.7B/4B/8B up to 128K context, Jet-Long beats the strongest zero-shot baseline on RULER by +4.79/+2.18/+2.03 pp while matching the base model exactly within the 32K training window (Table 1, Table 2). - The missing: All evaluation is confined to Qwen3 (dense) and Jet-Nemotron (hybrid) checkpoints up to 8B parameters and 128K tokens; there is no result on larger models (70B+) or context lengths beyond 128K, and no non-NVIDIA model family is tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone deploying open-weight LLMs at long context without fine-tuning budget, especially engineers evaluating RoPE-extension tricks (YaRN, DNTK, Self-Extend, DCA) who want a method that is both more accurate and near-zero-overhead at inference.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) is driven by the correction-rotation trick that exploits RoPE's additive-angle composition to keep the KV cache untouched as the group size changes during generation, turning an apparently stateful problem into a stateless constant-time correction. Novelty (65) is real but bounded: dynamic length-adaptive group size is a direct evolution of Self-Extend's static dual-window design, and the paper's own ablation is what earns credit by isolating discrete aliasing from continuous interpolation (6.99 pp at 64K) rather than just asserting dynamism helps. Rigor (60) is the main drag: no seeds, no error bars, and no confirmed code release despite a listed GitHub URL, on benchmarks (RULER) where task-level accuracy is known to be noisy. The transfer to the hybrid Jet-Nemotron architecture (+10-11 pp RULER) is a generality check most zero-shot RoPE papers skip and lifts Resilience. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 = 63.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM deployments increasingly involve inputs an order of magnitude longer than the pretraining window: retrieval-augmented generation, repository-level coding, and multi-turn agentic traces routinely accumulate 100K+ tokens. Continued pretraining at long context is expensive (quadratic attention compute, scarce long-context data, risk of short-context regression), so open-weight checkpoints are typically extended at inference time without fine-tuning ("zero-shot context extension"). Two failure modes block naive extrapolation: RoPE rotation angles at unseen positions fall out of the training distribution (position-OOD), and softmax attention diffuses probability mass over a growing key set, degrading retrieval of centrally placed evidence. Prior zero-shot fixes (NTK, Dynamic NTK, YaRN, Self-Extend, Dual Chunk Attention) each fix a rescaling factor or group size up front, forcing a tradeoff between preserving short-context behavior and extrapolating cleanly to long context. Key Idea: Make the remote-window position-rescaling factor a function of the current sequence length rather than a fixed hyperparameter. The group size G = max(1, ceil(L/w_pretrained)) is recomputed as L grows, so remote positions are floor-divided by G before their RoPE angle is computed (f(x) = floor(x/G)), keeping every relative rotation angle for the remote window exactly on the pretrained model's discrete rotation grid at every length, rather than under- or over-compressing with a static factor. Methodology: Jet-Long computes a per-query, per-key attention score that is unmodified base RoPE for query-key pairs within a local window w_0 (default 2048), and RoPE with remapped positions f(q), f(k) for pairs beyond w_0 (Eq. 1). Three design decisions matter: (1) the group size is chosen as the smallest integer keeping the compressed sequence within the pretrained window (Eq. 2), which the paper frames as minimum-compression and justifies with an ablation against continuous frequency interpolation (aliasing wins by 6.99 pp at 64K, 4.30 pp at 128K on RULER); (2) the KV cache stores only exact base-position keys (Eq. 4), and at generation time a correction rotation with offsets Δq = f(q) - q, Δk = f(k) - k is applied on the fly to reconstruct the remote view via RoPE's additive-angle composition property (R_a R_b = R_{a+b}), so the physical cache is never rewritten as G changes during generation; (3) prefill avoids materializing a full quadratic attention matrix by combining three standard FlashAttention calls (local-only-base, full-causal-remote, local-only-remote) via an inclusion-exclusion identity stabilized with FlashAttention's own LogSumExp statistics in FP32 (Eq. 7), then fusing all three into a single CuTe kernel. All three decisions are reasonably justified via ablation (w_0 sweep, Table 5) or mechanism (RoPE's rotation-composition algebra), though the correction-rotation trick explicitly requires pure rotational RoPE and would not extend to per-dimension scaling variants. Key Results: On Qwen3-1.7B/4B/8B-Base (32K native window) extended to 128K, Jet-Long achieves the best RULER average across all three sizes (73.93/83.02/86.74 vs. best baseline 69.14-84.71) and leads the strongest per-size baseline (DNTK at 1.7B, Self-Extend at 4B/8B) by +4.79/+2.18/+2.03 pp (Table 1). On HELMET-RAG it is best at 4B/8B and 0.73 pp behind Self-Extend at 1.7B. On PG-19 perplexity, Jet-Long is mathematically equivalent to the base model within the 32K training window (by construction) and stays lowest past 32K (11.41/9.62/8.51 at 128K vs. bare-base collapse to 71.00/104.66/79.37). Per-task breakdown at L=65536 shows Jet-Long best or tied-best on 8/13 RULER tasks, with the largest margins on Multi-Key NIAH (61.80 vs. 20.00 at 1.7B) and Variable Tracking (73.56 vs. 54.24 at 1.7B). Transferred without retraining to the hybrid Jet-Nemotron-2B/4B architecture, Jet-Long lifts average RULER by +10.01/+11.31 pp, with the bare hybrid base collapsing to 8.54/5.65 at 128K versus 33.78/33.14 with Jet-Long. On inference efficiency (H100, Qwen3-8B), the fused CuTe kernel reaches 1.28-1.39x FlashAttention-2 prefill throughput past 32K (approaching Hopper-only FlashAttention-4's 1.53x) while generation throughput stays >=0.96x FA2 at every length; the unfused multi-launch variant, by contrast, drops generation throughput to 0.14-0.34x FA2, showing the kernel fusion is what makes the method practically free. Baselines are current (DNTK, YaRN, Self-Extend, DCA are all 2023-2024 zero-shot methods) and constitute a reasonable comparison set, though none of them are trained methods, so the comparison is scoped to the zero-shot regime only. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (dynamic, length-adaptive discrete group size for RoPE position aliasing, replacing the fixed group/scaling factor used in Self-Extend, DCA, YaRN, DNTK) plus ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (fused CuTe kernel realizing an inclusion-exclusion FlashAttention merge with an on-the-fly correction rotation that avoids KV cache rewrites). The core algorithmic move, discrete grouped-position aliasing, is a direct evolution of Self-Extend's static dual-window design; the genuine novelty is making the group size a function of live sequence length and proving (via the aliasing-vs-interpolation ablation) that discrete aliasing specifically, not just dynamism in general, is what wins. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper provides two ablations that isolate the method's key design choices: local window size w_0 (Table 5, showing hyperparameter resilience across {512, 1024, 2048, 4096} within 2 pp of per-row best) and aliasing vs. frequency interpolation (Table 6, isolating the discrete-vs-continuous axis while holding the dynamic-schedule idea fixed). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere; RULER, HELMET-RAG, and PG-19 numbers appear to be single-run figures, which is a real gap for benchmarks like RULER where task-level accuracy can be noisy at small sample counts. No code, weights, or data are released; a GitHub link (github.com/jet-ai-projects/jet-long) is listed in the header but the paper does not confirm code is live there. Evaluation uses standard external benchmarks (RULER, HELMET-RAG, PG-19), not an author-constructed dataset, which mitigates Goodhart's Law concerns. Baseline hyperparameters are disclosed in full (Appendix C), supporting fair-comparison reproducibility. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (a zero-shot inference-time method evaluated by direct experiment, with a systems-style efficiency component). - Practical: Yes; runs on a single NVIDIA H100 with no fine-tuning, and the fused kernel requires no exotic hardware (unlike FlashAttention-4, which is Hopper-only). - Efficient: Prefill reaches 1.28-1.39x FlashAttention-2 throughput past 32K and generation stays within 4% of FA2 at every tested length (Table 7, Table 8); the unfused variant is markedly worse (down to 0.14x FA2 generation), so the efficiency claim depends entirely on the custom CuTe kernel being adopted, not just the algorithm. - Effective: Evidence directly measures the target problem (long-context accuracy and perplexity extension) rather than a proxy, via RULER (synthetic recall), HELMET-RAG (application-grounded QA), and PG-19 (perplexity); this is a stronger evaluation triad than perplexity alone. - Scalable: Tested only up to 8B parameters and 128K context; the paper does not test 100x the evaluated scale (would require ~800B parameters or ~12.8M tokens) and makes no explicit claim about behavior beyond 128K, so scalability past the tested range is unstated rather than over-claimed. The work delivers no released software artifact confirmed in the paper text (a GitHub URL is listed but not verified as populated); its primary reusable contribution is the algorithmic construction (dynamic group-size aliasing, correction rotation, inclusion-exclusion kernel) plus the empirical efficiency numbers. Strengths: - The dynamic group-size schedule directly resolves a real tension in prior zero-shot methods (fixed factor forces a short-vs-long tradeoff), and the paper backs the specific design choice (discrete aliasing over continuous interpolation) with a targeted ablation rather than asserting it. - The correction-rotation trick exploiting RoPE's additive rotation composition (R_a R_b = R_{a+b}) to avoid ever rewriting the KV cache is a genuinely clever piece of engineering that turns a seemingly stateful problem (group size changes as generation proceeds) into a stateless, constant-time correction. - Demonstrated transfer to a hybrid softmax/linear-attention architecture (Jet-Nemotron) without modification is a meaningful generality check most zero-shot RoPE-extension papers skip, and the reported +10-11 pp RULER gains there are substantial. - The efficiency claim is validated with a real fused kernel and honest disclosure of the unfused variant's cost, rather than only presenting the best-case fused numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state plainly that Jet-Long addresses only the position-OOD failure mode, not the complementary attention-diffusion/softmax-dispersion failure mode, and suggest architectural alternatives (sparse attention, linear attention) as the path to address that. They note the method requires a softmax-with-RoPE base architecture, explicitly scoping out non-RoPE or non-softmax designs as future extensions (Multi-head Latent Attention, sparse attention). They also flag in the ablation section that the aliasing margin over frequency interpolation "shrinks at extreme lengths,
Recommended 2607.07740 PDF
#65 — Innovative (63)

Training and inference efficiency of large language models

M Pagliardini · infoscience.epfl.ch · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces four separate contributions to LLM efficiency: a dynamic sparse Flash-Attention kernel, the AdEMAMix optimizer (dual-EMA momentum), the DenseFormer architecture (depth-weighted residual averaging), and CoTFormer (a weight-tied recurrent architecture with token-adaptive compute at inference). - Why it's important: Brute-force scaling of LLMs is running into data exhaustion, quadratic attention cost, and GPU energy/water limits, so algorithmic efficiency gains are the stated alternative path to further capability gains. - How it does it: Each chapter attacks a different lifecycle stage: hardware-aware kernels for dynamic sparsity patterns (attention), a second, slow-decaying EMA on top of Adam's momentum (optimization), a learned weighted average over all previous transformer-block outputs (architecture), and recurrent block reuse with a learned per-token halting router (inference). - The good: AdEMAMix's headline result, a 1.3B model trained on 101B tokens matching an AdamW model trained on 197B tokens (95% more data), is a large, cleanly isolated compute-to-data trade documented across three model families (Transformer LM, Mamba, ViT). - The missing: None of the four chapters test their method jointly with the others (e.g., AdEMAMix + DenseFormer + CoTFormer combined), so the thesis does not establish whether these efficiency gains are additive or overlapping, and none of the four methods is validated beyond 1.3B-3B parameter models or research-scale token budgets (order 10-200B tokens), leaving frontier-scale generalization untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading in full for LLM pre-training practitioners weighing optimizer or architecture changes;
