TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Kairos, a proactive scheduler that deflects the prefill phase of select requests from saturated prefill nodes to underutilized decode nodes in disaggregated LLM serving, running the deflected prefill as chunked-prefill steps interleaved with each decode node's in-flight batches. - Why it's important: On a production-style 2-prefill/2-decode A100 cluster, prefill queuing and inter-node KV-cache transfer (not prefill compute itself) account for 77-98% of P95 time-to-first-token, so idle decode-side compute is going to waste while prefill nodes bottleneck tail latency. - How it does it: Kairos uses closed-form analytical step-latency predictors (trained once per model/hardware, MAPE < 10%) to greedily search per-decode-node chunk-size schedules that stay within the time-between-tokens (TBT) SLO, then deflects a request only if the resulting decode-node TTFT is both TBT-safe and at most alpha times the prefill-node TTFT. - The good: Evaluated on production-derived bursty traces with DeepSeek-V2-Lite, Kairos sustains 100% joint TTFT+TBT SLO attainment up to 9 RPS versus 7 RPS for PD disaggregation and worse for TaiChi, translating to a measured 28.5% higher sustainable request rate at equal SLO. - The missing: All results come from a single simulated/replayed production trace normalized to one MoE model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite) and one hardware/topology (2P2D A100 cluster); there is no evaluation across different model sizes, cluster ratios beyond a brief footnote, or dense (non-bursty) high-load regimes where decode headroom might vanish. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or operating disaggregated LLM serving infrastructure (Splitwise/DistServe/Mooncake-style systems); the empirical bottleneck characterization in Section 3 (Insight 1-3) is useful even independent of adopting Kairos itself.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (65) is the largest contributor: open-source code on vLLM 0.18.1, ~2000 lines, sub-millisecond scheduling overhead, and a 30-minute one-time profiling cost per model/hardware pair. The empirical diagnosis that prefill compute is only 2-23% of P95 TTFT (queue wait and KV transfer being the rest) is a genuinely actionable reframing, and the static-policy-versus-oracle comparison properly isolates the value of per-request state-aware deflection. Rigor (60) and Resilience (55) are held back by evaluation on exactly one model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite) and one topology (2P2D), no sensitivity analysis on the alpha=1.3 or beta knobs that drive every deflection decision, and no error bars anywhere, so the 1-2 RPS headroom gap over baselines could plausibly be trace-specific. Weighted: 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*65 = 60.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Disaggregated LLM serving splits prefill (compute-bound) and decode (memory-bandwidth-bound) onto separate GPU pools to avoid phase interference, a design used by Splitwise, DistServe, Mooncake, and TetriInfer. Under bursty, heavy-tailed real-world arrival patterns, prefill nodes saturate and requests queue while decode nodes sit with spare compute capacity, because decode phase latency is bound by memory bandwidth, not compute. Existing disaggregated schedulers assume a request's prefill always runs on the prefill node it arrived at, leaving this cross-pool compute imbalance unaddressed. The paper targets production operators running such clusters who need lower tail TTFT without breaking the TBT SLO for concurrent decode requests. Key Idea: Decode nodes have exploitable idle compute that can absorb another request's prefill phase as chunked-prefill steps injected into the decode batch, but the safe chunk size is not fixed (unlike Sarathi-style colocated serving): it must shrink adaptively as a function of the decode node's current batch size and KV-cache occupancy, computed per-request via a closed-form step-latency model rather than live trials. Methodology: Kairos has three components. (1) A Prefill-Node TTFT Estimator computes expected queue wait (FIFO, product of queue depth and average chunk execution time) plus prefill execution time for a candidate request, using a linear-regression step-latency model. (2) A Decode-Node Feasibility Checker builds, per decode node, a chunk schedule X = [chi^(1),...,chi^(m)] via a greedy largest-safe-chunk search: at each step it picks the largest chi from a candidate set such that injecting it into the current batch (size B_n, KV occupancy K_n) keeps the projected per-step latency under beta times the TBT SLO tau (Eq. 5), and sums per-step latencies to estimate decode-node TTFT (Eq. 6). (3) A Deflection Decision Algorithm (Algorithm 1) picks the fastest feasible decode node and deflects only if the decode-node TTFT is both TBT-safe and at most alpha times the prefill-node TTFT (alpha=1.3 chosen empirically; alpha=1 is strict, alpha>1 trades bounded TTFT regression on the deflected request for relieving downstream queue pressure). All three step-latency models (prefill-only, decode-only, mixed) are scikit-learn linear regressions trained on ~30 minutes of one-time profiling data per model/hardware pair. Design decisions: (a) using linear regression rather than a heavier simulator for O(1) prediction at scheduling time is well-justified by the sub-millisecond latency requirement, but its generalization beyond the profiled workload/hardware regime is untested; (b) the alpha=1.3 margin is a hand-tuned operator knob without a sensitivity study showing how sensitive results are across alpha values; (c) FIFO-only queue-wait modeling (Eq. 2) is a simplifying assumption the paper explicitly flags as excluding priority/prefix-aware scheduling. Key Results: On a Bursty workload trace (production-derived, Gamma inter-arrival, DeepSeek-V2-Lite 16B/2.4B-active MoE) run on 2 prefill + 2 decode NVIDIA A100 nodes, Kairos reduces P95 TTFT by up to 81% and improves SLO attainment by up to 79% versus PD Disaggregation (DistServe-style) and TaiChi baselines (abstract); the body reports up to 71% SLO attainment improvement and a 28.5% higher sustainable RPS at fixed SLO. At RPS 6-12, Kairos stays within the 4s P95 TTFT / 70ms P95 TBT joint SLO up to 9 RPS, while PD Disaggregation and TaiChi violate SLO earlier (7 and 8 RPS respectively); TaiChi violates both SLOs at high RPS. Kairos maintains zero TBT violations by construction (P95 TBT 5-10ms above baseline, still under 70ms). An oracle policy using the same analytical predictors but with perfect simulator-side ground truth achieves 2.6s P95 TTFT / 0% TBT violation / 99.5% SLO attainment on Bursty, versus Kairos's real deployment closing much of that gap versus static threshold policies (best static policy SL(l=2048): 3.6s TTFT / 1.5% TBT violation / 95.1% attainment). Throughput is comparable to or higher than both baselines. Step-latency predictors achieve MAPE < 10% on held-out traces. Scheduling overhead is sub-millisecond per request. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the TBT-safe adaptive chunk-schedule search and alpha-margin deflection decision) plus NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the P95 TTFT breakdown showing prefill compute is only 2-23% of tail latency, the rest being queue wait and KV transfer). The genuinely novel piece is the per-request, state-aware chunk-size schedule (adapting to current decode-node batch/KV state rather than a fixed chunk size, unlike Sarathi); routing prefill work to decode nodes when idle is a known idea (closest is PPD, discussed in Related Work), but PPD targets multi-turn Turn-2+ requests specifically while Kairos generalizes to any request with available decode-node headroom and adds explicit TBT-aware runtime monitoring. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports ablation-style comparisons against multiple static deflection policies (SQ(theta), SL(l)) and an oracle upper bound, which is a genuine effort to isolate the value of dynamic per-request decisions (Insight 3, Table 4). Step-latency predictor accuracy is quantified (MAPE < 10%) on held-out traces. However: no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed/multiple-trial reporting are given for any of the headline TTFT/SLO numbers in Figures 4-6; it is unclear whether single runs or averages are plotted. All evaluation uses one model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite) and one cluster topology (2P2D, with only a brief 1P3D/3P1D footnote comparison on one workload); no sensitivity analysis across model sizes, dense (non-bursty) workloads, or larger clusters is shown, despite the paper's own note that dispatcher CPU cost could become a bottleneck above 20x the tested RPS. Code is released at github.com/sudokara/Kairos, which is a strong reproducibility positive. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built scheduling layer evaluated by deployment on vLLM 0.18.1). - Practical: Yes, it runs on commercially available A100 hardware with roughly 2000 added lines of Python and no model modifications; profiling per model/hardware pair takes ~30 minutes. - Efficient: Per-request scheduling decision cost is sub-millisecond; the analytical predictors are O(1) linear regressions, cheap relative to any live-trial alternative, though the paper acknowledges the centralized dispatcher's O(N_d) per-request sweep could become a CPU bottleneck above ~20x current RPS. - Effective: Evidence directly measures the stated problem (tail TTFT and TBT under bursty load) rather than a proxy, and shows Kairos closing much (though not all) of the gap to the oracle upper bound (2.6s vs Kairos's TTFT staying within 4s SLO up to 9 RPS). - Scalable: Not tested. The paper only speculates about dispatcher sharding as future work and briefly notes an alternative node-ratio comparison (1P3D/3P1D) in a footnote; no experiment demonstrates behavior at 10x-100x cluster size or request rate. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source Kairos implementation on vLLM) rather than stopping at paper form. Strengths: - The P95 TTFT decomposition (Table 3, Insight 1) quantifying that prefill compute is only 2-23% of tail latency while queue wait and KV transfer dominate is a concrete, actionable empirical finding that reframes where disaggregated-serving optimization effort should go. - The chunk-schedule feasibility model (Eq. 4-6) correctly captures that successive chunks for the same deflected request are not TBT-equivalent as cached KV accumulates, motivating the greedy largest-safe-chunk construction rather than a fixed chunk size — a real technical distinction from Sarathi's static-chi colocated approach. - The static-policy-vs-oracle comparison (Table 4, Section 3.4) is a well-designed ablation that isolates the value of per-request, state-aware deflection decisions versus threshold heuristics, showing under-deflection and over-deflection are both real failure modes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly discuss three limitations: (1) analytical-model prediction error could push actual TBT above SLO in deployment (though they claim their <10% MAPE produced zero observed violations in their evaluation); (2) decode-node state is only reported every 100ms, so stale state could cause suboptimal deflection decisions, and each decode node is limited to a single deflected request as a mitigation; (3) deflecting a request to a decode node loses any prefix KV cache already built on a prefill node, and Kairos's TTFT estimate does not currently account for prefix cache residency. They also flag the centralized dispatcher as a potential CPU bottleneck at very high request rates (RPS exceeding |decode nodes|/0.1s ≈ 20 per decode node) and note this exceeds their tested SLO regime. This is a reasonably direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single-model, single-topology evaluation: all headline numbers come from one MoE model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite) and one 2P2D cluster configuration; the paper does not show results scale to dense (non-MoE) models, larger models, or different prefill:decode node ratios beyond a one-sentence footnote comparison. - No sensitivity analysis on alpha (fixed at 1.3) or beta (the TBT safety margin), despite both being described as tunable knobs central to the deflection decision; readers cannot assess how brittle results are to these choices. - No error bars or repeated-trial variance reported for any headline TTFT/SLO/throughput figure, making it hard to judge whether gaps between Kairos and baselines (e.g., 1-2 RPS extra headroom) are robust or trace-specific. - The comparison baselines (PD Disaggregation, TaiChi) are reimplemented/adapted by the authors rather than run as the original published systems on identical hardware, which is standard practice but still self-conducted and not independently verified. Bottom Line: A practitioner operating disaggregated LLM-serving infrastructure gains a concrete, code-available technique and a genuinely useful empirical diagnosis (prefill queue wait and KV transfer, not prefill compute, drive tail latency) that is not covered as precisely by prior disaggregation papers (Splitwise, DistServe, TaiChi), though the single-model, single-topology, no-error-bar evaluation means the reported 81% TTFT reduction should be read as a best-case number from one trace rather than a generalizable guarantee.
Search Results 2607.02043 PDF
#83 — Innovative (60)

Evaluating Direct Preference Optimization for Personalizing German Automatic Text Simplifications for Persons with Intellectual Disabilities

Y Gao, K Johnson, D Fröhlich, L Carrer, S Ebling · aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HF4ATS, the largest German-language human preference dataset for automatic text simplification (ATS), and uses it to evaluate whether direct preference optimization (DPO) can personalize LLM-based ATS models to the preferences of persons with intellectual disabilities. - Why it's important: Persons with intellectual disabilities are a target population for accessible text but are rarely consulted during AI system design, and preference-based personalization is one of the few HITL methods compatible with legal restrictions on building identifiable user profiles for this group. - How it does it: SFT on filtered DEPlain complex-simple pairs, followed by group-specific DPO post-training on pairwise preferences collected from 15 target-group and 4 expert annotators via a tablet-based annotation tool, with effectiveness measured through win rate, supremacy scores, and annotator agreement. - The good: The controlled comparison across six preference-data subsets (all, equal-information, model-matched, max-Intra-AA, max-Inter-AA) isolates preference consistency, not model backbone or data heuristics, as the dominant driver of DPO gains (up to +31.93% win rate for the max-Inter-AA subset). - The missing: The negative result for the target group (DPO underperforms SFT for most target annotators, Table 5) is only explained post hoc via low Intra-AA/Inter-AA scores; the paper does not test whether simpler interventions (larger annotator pool, longitudinal sessions, alternative elicitation formats) would raise consistency enough to make DPO work for this group. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ATS researchers and accessible-AI practitioners who care about participatory design methodology and preference-learning failure modes with vulnerable annotator populations; the core DPO mechanics are standard, so readers only interested in the algorithm can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (75) is the highest dimension and it is earned honestly: the paper reports that DPO fails for its primary intended beneficiaries (target-group annotators favor SFT in 11 of 12 combinations), analyzes why via preference-consistency mediation, and recommends alternatives rather than burying the negative result. Non-Obviousness (45) is the main drag because the DPO procedure is unmodified from Rafailov et al. and the contribution is a dataset plus an application. Rigor (62) is mixed: binomial significance testing and a six-subset factor ablation are above ATS-field norms, but the final human evaluation rests on 4 target and 4 expert annotators selected for highest agreement, which is the same variable the paper claims drives DPO success, creating partial circularity. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 = 60.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: German automatic text simplification systems built on LLMs are typically SFT-only and not adapted to the specific comprehension needs of persons with intellectual disabilities, a population with well-documented struggles with complex sentence structure, jargon, and implicit metaphor. Because building identifiable user profiles for this group is ethically and, in many jurisdictions, legally restricted, individualized personalization (e.g., via prompting on stored preferences) is not viable; the paper targets group-level personalization using only preference feedback, without user profiles, as the accessible alternative. Prior ATS research involving this population has mostly confined their input to late-stage evaluation rather than iterative preference collection that feeds back into model training. Key Idea: Apply the validate-annotate-evaluate human-in-the-loop principle to DPO post-training itself, treating annotator preference consistency (measured via Intra- and Inter-annotator agreement) as the variable that determines whether offline preference optimization succeeds, rather than treating DPO as a black-box alignment step. The paper's central empirical claim is that DPO's effectiveness for personalization is gated by preference-signal quality, not by which model backbone or which SFT checkpoint generated the candidate pairs. Methodology: Pipeline: (1) SFT phase — DEPlain-APA complex-simple pairs filtered by a four-step pipeline (many-to-many mapping removal, Sentence-BERT cosine-similarity threshold of 0.5, ROUGE-1/2/L F1 > 0.8 near-duplicate removal, >30-word simplification removal), reducing 13,122 pairs to a 5,200-pair stratified split (3,600 train/800 dev/800 test); four backbones (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, DiscoLeo-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct, LeoLM-Mistral-7B-Chat) fine-tuned with LoRA (rank 16) under AdamW, checkpoint-selected via SARI and WSTF4 on the dev set. (2) DPO pair creation — 20 inferences per SFT checkpoint per complex sentence (8 prompt templates x 4 decoding configs), reviewed by 13 trained German-speaking pair creators into preference pairs subject to entailment and equal-information rubrics, yielding HF4ATS-DPO (6,018 pairs, 3,009 unique). (3) DPO post-training — standard Bradley-Terry/DPO objective (Rafailov et al. 2023) with beta=0.1, frozen SFT reference model, trained separately per annotator group (target vs. expert) and per six preference-data subsets (all, all_=, LLM_=, max Intra-AA, max Inter-AA) to isolate factors. (4) Evaluation — automatic metrics (SARI, BERTScore, WSTF4, win rate via implicit reward margin) plus human evaluation via a DPO "supremacy score" (fraction of pairwise A/B choices favoring DPO over SFT output for the same input), computed per-annotator-group and tested for significance with one-sided binomial tests (scipy). Design decisions: using implicit-reward DPO instead of explicit reward modeling was well-justified given HITL cognitive burden and no explicit numeric preference elicitation from the target group; the choice of group-level (not individual-level) personalization is justified on legal/ethical grounds but forecloses any test of finer-grained personalization; the decision to run six independent DPO trainings per subset is well-suited to the ablation goal but multiplies noise given each expert/target group already has few annotators (4 each in final evaluation). Key Results: Automatic evaluation (Table 3): DPO improves WSTF4 readability over SFT for all backbones (e.g., DiscoLeo SFT WSTF4 6.515 -> DPO-expert 4.031) but BERTScore drops (0.9049 -> 0.7814), indicating semantic drift; SARI is mixed, largely preserved or improved for expert-supervised models, declining for target-supervised ones. Win rates against DPO test set are consistently higher for expert-trained checkpoints (0.6118-0.6118) than target-trained (0.4382-0.5211), with two of three target models falling below the 0.50 win-rate threshold entirely. Subset ablation (Table 4): training on maximum-agreement subsets yields the largest gains, up to +31.93% win rate for LeoLM-Mistral target-group DPO on the max Intra-AA subset; information-equality and model-matched subsets often degrade performance. Human evaluation (Table 5, supremacy scores): expert annotators favor DPO over SFT in 3/4 cases per backbone, with one result reaching significance (DiscoLeo-Llama expert, supremacy 0.74, p<0.05, double-asterisked as significant even excluding an outlier); target-group annotators favor SFT over DPO in most cases (only 1/12 annotator-model combinations favors DPO), and no target result reaches significance. All experiments run on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU with 8B-parameter backbones — a modest, standard-scale test, not a stress test. Annotator agreement (Table 1) is low to moderate for both groups (target Cohen's kappa mostly 0.00-0.30, several negative/near-zero; expert Krippendorff's alpha 0.42-0.76), underscoring the noisy-preference-signal explanation for the negative target-group result. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (HF4ATS, the first and largest German ATS human preference dataset collected directly from persons with intellectual disabilities) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (preference consistency, not model choice, gates DPO personalization success; DPO personalization fails for the target group specifically). The DPO training procedure itself is a standard application of existing DPO (Rafailov et al. 2023) to a new setting; no new formulation, method, or system is proposed. The genuinely novel contribution is the dataset plus the negative empirical result and its consistency-based explanation, not the algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and are the paper's main analytical contribution (six preference-data subsets x three backbones x two annotator groups = systematic factor isolation). Standard deviations are reported for automatic metrics (Table 3); significance testing (one-sided binomial, scipy, p<0.05) is applied to the human supremacy scores, a stronger standard than most ATS personalization papers. However: the final human evaluation set is only 300 ATS pairs (50 per winning checkpoint) drawn from only 4 target and 4 expert annotators (selected as the highest-agreement annotators, a selection that likely inflates apparent consistency benefits relative to the full 15-target-participant pool); Intra-AA is unavailable (NA) for several target annotators due to missing repeat pairs, weakening the consistency-measurement backbone of the paper's central claim. Dataset and code are stated as released (icons in abstract) but no URL is given in the excerpted text. Ethics approval was obtained from the University of Zurich ethics committee for target-group data collection, a genuine strength for work involving a vulnerable population. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (LLM-based ATS system evaluated by experiment). Practical: Yes — 8B-parameter backbones with LoRA fine-tuning run on a single A100 GPU, commercially accessible hardware. Efficient: Not quantified; no inference latency, throughput, or memory numbers are reported, only training compute (single GPU, LoRA rank 16). Effective: Partially — DPO measurably improves readability (WSTF4) and modestly improves SARI for expert-supervised models, but BERTScore declines indicate the improvement trades off semantic fidelity, and the target-group result shows DPO does not solve the stated personalization problem for its primary intended beneficiaries. Scalable: Not tested — the dataset (6,018 preference pairs from 19 total annotators) is small relative to typical RLHF-scale preference sets (tens of thousands to millions of pairs), and the paper explicitly flags target-group data collection at greater scale as impractical due to cognitive burden; no experiment at 10x-100x scale is attempted or claimed. The paper delivers open-source models and a dataset (per abstract icons) but the annotation web tool and pair-creation scripts are described but not benchmarked for reuse cost. Strengths: - The negative result for the target group is reported and analyzed rather than suppressed; the paper explicitly concludes DPO may be ill-suited for target-group ATS personalization and recommends alternatives (KTO), a genuine act of intellectual honesty uncommon in applied-DPO papers. - The preference-consistency-as-mediator analysis (Table 4 subset ablation, cross-referenced against Table 1 agreement scores) is a real methodological contribution: it isolates why DPO succeeds or fails, not just whether it does, which is more useful to downstream practitioners than a single win/loss number. - The inclusive annotation workflow (caretaker-mediated sessions, simplified-language consent, tablet UI, repeated pairs for agreement measurement) is carefully engineered for a population with intellectual disabilities and is documented in enough detail (Appendix A, Figure 5) to be reused by other accessibility-AI projects. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly: (1) the number of target-group annotators remains limited and preference variability within the group may not be fully captured; (2) the study addresses group-level, not individual-level, personalization, and stronger effects may emerge with finer-grained or longitudinal preference modeling; (3) DPO was evaluated as a representative offline method and findings may not generalize to online or alternative alignment frameworks; (4) the cognitive burden of pairwise annotation constrained data scale, which may have limited DPO's effectiveness, and lower-burden feedback paradigms are left to future work. This is a direct, non-minimizing confrontation of the study's central weakness. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The final human-evaluation annotator subset (4 target, 4 expert) is selected for highest Intra-AA, which is the same variable the paper claims drives DPO success; this creates a partial circularity where the reported target-group failure may be a floor rather than a ceiling estimate of what higher-consistency subsets could achieve, and the paper does not test whether the excluded, lower-agreement target annotators would have failed even more severely (or, symmetrically, whether the full target pool might show more promise under different consistency-boosting interventions). - Two of four expert annotators (ea01, ea03) could not see the complex source text during annotation to match target-group conditions, while the other two could; this creates a within-expert-group condition split that is acknowledged but not separately analyzed for whether it affects the reported win rates or supremacy scores. - No comparison against a non-DPO personalization baseline (e.g., personalized prompting, in-context few-shot from group preferences, as mentioned in Related Work) is run; the paper compares DPO-vs-SFT only, so the claim that DPO specifically (rather than any post-training signal) is what's driving expert-group gains is not isolated from simpler alternatives. - BERTScore decline under DPO (0.90 -> 0.78-0.84) is described as "semantic drift" but never manually inspected or reported with example outputs in the main text; a reviewer would want qualitative failure cases to distinguish benign paraphrase variation from actual meaning loss, especially given the target population's vulnerability to unfaithful simplifications. Bottom Line: The paper is a useful, honestly-reported case study on the limits of offline preference optimization when consulting a vulnerable, low-consistency annotator population, and its dataset and workflow have reuse value for accessible-AI researchers, but the underlying DPO technique is unmodified from prior work and practitioners seeking a working personalization method for intellectual-disability-focused ATS will not find one delivered here.
Search Results
#84 — Innovative (60)

Predicting Table Joinability in Data Lakes using a Metadata Knowledge Graph

S Shirai, O Hassanzadeh, D Bhattacharjya, G Rossiello · oktie.com · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MeGNN-Join, a two-phase heterogeneous GNN that predicts table joinability from a knowledge graph built purely out of table metadata (names, descriptions, columns, tags), with no access to cell contents. - Why it's important: Many data-lake governance policies prohibit or make expensive the content inspection that existing joinable-table-discovery methods require, so a metadata-only alternative unlocks join discovery under access restrictions and at lower storage/compute cost. - How it does it: It casts joinability as link prediction over a metadata KG, LLM-enriches missing metadata (column expansion, descriptions, tags), embeds entities with SBERT, and runs a two-phase GNN where phase 2 adds cross-table column edges so each table's columns can attend to the candidate table's columns before scoring via sigmoid(dot product). - The good: Across three real government-portal datasets plus a synthetic YAGO benchmark, MeGNN-Join beats KG-embedding, rule-learning, and GNN baselines by a wide margin in the inductive setting (MRR 0.500 vs. 0.458 for the closest GNN baseline, and Hits@10 0.679 vs. 0.646), the setting closest to real deployment on unseen tables. - The missing: The ground truth itself is generated by a content-based containment-ratio heuristic with hand-picked thresholds (0.6/0.3) and heuristic filters (drop numeric columns, drop low-cardinality columns), so the entire evaluation is only as trustworthy as that heuristic's correlation with true joinability, and this is not independently validated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building data-catalog or data-discovery tooling under access-restricted governance, especially the ablations on metadata sparsity and column under-sampling; less essential for readers focused on state-of-the-art content-based join discovery techniques, which this work explicitly does not try to beat.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (65) is the strongest driver: reframing content-free joinability as KG link prediction is a genuine new formulation, and the phase-2 cross-table column-connection mechanism is a real architectural idea validated against an ablated single-phase GNN (inductive Hits@10 0.679 vs. 0.646). Rigor (60) is held back by ground-truth labels generated from a containment-ratio heuristic with hand-picked thresholds, so all gains measure fidelity to a proxy rather than validated join utility, and no error bars or significance tests accompany the 5-fold means; code and data released via Zenodo and thorough ablations (enrichment on/off, per-metadata-type removal, column under-sampling) pull it back up. Non-Obviousness (60) reflects that column-to-column cross-attention before scoring is not what a competent practitioner would reach for first. Completeness (60) and Resilience (50) are limited by three same-domain government portals standing in for the enterprise data lakes the paper repeatedly invokes, and no wall-clock or storage comparison against the content-based methods (JOSIE, PEXESO, DeepJoin) the work positions itself against. Impact (60) for released artifact plus a first-of-its-kind benchmark. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*60 = 60.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Data lakes lack a unifying schema, so identifying which tables can be joined (i.e., share columns referring to the same real-world concept) typically requires inspecting table contents to find overlapping cell values. Content inspection is infeasible or costly when governance policies restrict access to table data, or when data-lake scale makes content processing and storage expensive. Business analysts, data engineers, and data scientists who need to discover joinable tables under these constraints have no adequate alternative; prior work on efficient joinable-table discovery (JOSIE, PEXESO, DeepJoin) all assumes content access is available. Key Idea: Table metadata, structured as a knowledge graph and processed with a two-phase GNN where the second phase explicitly wires columns of the two candidate tables together before producing final table embeddings, is a sufficient (if imperfect) signal for predicting joinability without ever touching cell values. The cross-table column-connection step in phase 2 is the specific mechanism that lets the model reason about column-to-column compatibility rather than treating each table's representation independently. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Metadata enrichment: an LLM (Llama 3.3 70B) performs column-name expansion, description generation, and tag generation to fill gaps in real-world metadata, run once offline and cached. (2) KG construction: each table, column, description, and tag becomes an entity with an SBERT-embedded text feature (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), connected by four relation types (table-name, table-description, column, column-description) plus a reflexive joinability relation recording known joins. (3) Phase-1 GNN update: a depth-2 heterogeneous GATv2 network updates query- and candidate-table embeddings independently over their 2-hop neighborhoods. (4) Phase-2 GNN update: new edges are added between every column of the query table and every column of the candidate table, and a second GATv2 pass propagates this cross-table column information into final table embeddings t_Q'' and t_C''; joinability is scored as sigmoid(t_Q'' · t_C'') and trained with binary cross-entropy. Three design decisions stand out: treating columns/tables as distinct entities even when names collide (well-justified, avoids conflating unrelated "name" columns across domains) but treating all tags within a data portal as referring to the same concept (a stated assumption, not validated); choosing GATv2 somewhat arbitrarily among GNN architectures ("any GNN architecture can be used" — no comparison against alternatives like GraphSAGE or R-GCN is run); and evaluating two model variants (with/without training-set join edges as features) to expose a generalizability/performance trade-off, which is a sound ablation choice. Key Results: Evaluated on three curated open-government datasets (CONY: 2,049 tables/40,986 columns; NYS: 714 tables; CANADA: 1,237 tables) plus a synthetic YAGO-4.5-derived dataset (880 tables, 59,892 joins), across transductive, semi-inductive, and inductive settings, using filtered MRR and Hits@10 against KG-embedding baselines (TransE, DistMult, ComplEx, RotatE — transductive only), rule learning (AnyBURL), GNN baselines (GraIL, a same-architecture GNN without phase 2), and text-similarity baselines (exact column match, SBERT column/table similarity). MeGNN-Join_J achieves MRR 0.836/Hits@10 0.951 (transductive) and 0.671/0.842 (semi-inductive), both best-in-table; plain MeGNN-Join wins inductive with MRR 0.500/Hits@10 0.679 versus the next-best GNN baseline's 0.458/0.646. On "difficult joins" (joinable columns with mismatched names), MeGNN-Join reaches Hits@10 0.369 inductive versus GNN's 0.336 and near-zero for text-similarity baselines. Ablations show enrichment adds ~7% MRR on CONY (0.784→0.833) but zero effect on NYS; removing table descriptions costs the most (0.833→0.684); sampling just 10 of ~20 average columns nearly matches full performance (MRR ≈0.78). Baselines are reasonably current (AnyBURL 2023, GraIL 2019) though the KG-embedding baselines (TransE 2013, DistMult/RotatE 2014-2019) are dated by KG-embedding-field standards; no comparison is made against actual content-based SOTA methods like DeepJoin, since the entire premise is operating without content access. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the two-phase GNN with cross-table column augmentation) and NEW FORMULATION (framing content-free, metadata-only table joinability as KG link prediction) are the genuine contributions; the LLM-based semantic enrichment step and the SBERT/GATv2 building blocks are standard components applied to a new setting rather than novel in themselves. The paper also contributes a NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (three curated real-world metadata datasets plus a synthetic YAGO-based one), which is a meaningful secondary contribution given the stated lack of prior metadata-joinability benchmarks. Rigor & Reproducibility: Results are averaged over 5-fold cross-validation, which is appropriate, but no error bars, variance, or significance tests are reported in the main tables, so the magnitude of true improvement over the closest baseline (GNN) cannot be statistically assessed. Ablations are reasonably thorough (enrichment on/off, per-metadata-type removal, column under-sampling) and do isolate which components matter. Code, data, and artifacts are stated as released at a Zenodo record. A significant methodological concern: the ground-truth labels are themselves generated by a heuristic (containment ratio above a hand-chosen threshold, with numeric-column and low-cardinality-column filtering), meaning the entire evaluation measures how well MeGNN-Join reproduces this particular content-based heuristic's judgments, not true joinability as verified by any external or human standard — a form of proxy-target risk the authors do not fully interrogate. There is no held-out human-annotated joinability set for validation. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes — the method uses only text embeddings and moderate-sized GNNs (GATv2, depth 2), runnable on standard hardware without needing table-content storage or processing. Efficient: Metadata KG construction and enrichment are one-time offline costs, and per-table embeddings can be cached; inference cost per query-candidate pair scales with 2-hop neighborhood size, but no wall-clock or memory benchmarks are reported against content-based baselines like JOSIE, so relative efficiency gains are asserted, not measured. Effective: The evidence shows the model reproduces a containment-ratio-based joinability proxy well (MRR up to 0.836), but effectiveness against genuine downstream join utility (e.g., whether joins recommended actually help an analyst's query) is not evaluated. Scalable: The largest dataset tested is 2,049 tables (CONY); the paper claims relevance to "scale of data lakes" but does not test at 10x-100x this table count, nor report how KG size affects 2-hop neighborhood retrieval cost as the dataset grows — this is an unevaluated extrapolation. The paper delivers a software artifact (code and data released via Zenodo per the artifact-availability statement) alongside the method. Strengths: - The phase-2 cross-table column-connection mechanism is a genuine, non-obvious architectural idea that directly targets the hardest sub-problem (matching columns with different names) and shows measurable benefit over the ablated single-phase GNN baseline (e.g., inductive Hits@10 0.679 vs. 0.646). - The difficult-joins evaluation (Table 3) is a meaningful stress test that isolates exactly the cases where naive text-similarity approaches fail (Hits@10 as low as 0.018-0.136), giving a credible signal that the KG/GNN approach captures more than surface lexical similarity. - The column under-sampling ablation (Figure 4) is a useful robustness check showing the model degrades gracefully with sparse metadata, directly relevant to the practical deployment concern the paper raises about incomplete real-world catalogs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note the semantic-enrichment ablation shows inconsistent benefit across datasets (helps CONY, neutral on NYS), and they explicitly flag that removing any single metadata type degrades performance, implying fragility to metadata sparsity in the worst case. They confront the limited domain diversity of their evaluation (three government open-data portals plus one synthetic set) and list expanding to more diverse domains, incorporating semantic table interpretation, and deeper study of automatic enrichment as explicit future work — a direct, credible acknowledgment of scope boundaries rather than a minimized treatment. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The ground-truth joinability labels are generated by the same class of set-similarity heuristic (containment ratio) that the paper's method is designed to avoid needing; there is no independent, human-validated notion of "true" joinability against which either the ground truth or MeGNN-Join's predictions are checked, so all reported gains reflect fidelity to a specific heuristic and threshold choice rather than to ground truth join utility. - All three real-world datasets are drawn from government open-data portals, a domain with relatively formulaic column-naming conventions (English/French bilingual columns, standardized government categories); no evidence is offered that findings transfer to enterprise data lakes, which the paper repeatedly invokes as the primary motivating use case. - No wall-clock, memory, or storage comparison is made against the content-based methods (JOSIE, PEXESO, DeepJoin) that the metadata-only approach is positioned as an alternative to, despite efficiency being one of the two central motivating claims (the other being privacy/access restriction). - The YAGO synthetic dataset's construction (treating KG classes as tables and properties as columns) may not faithfully replicate real tabular metadata structure or noise characteristics, limiting how much its results (which are not broken out separately in the main text) should be trusted as evidence of generalization. Bottom Line: Practitioners building data-catalog tooling under content-access restrictions gain a concrete, empirically validated architecture and a first-of-its-kind benchmark for metadata-only joinability prediction, but should treat the reported performance numbers as fidelity to a heuristic proxy for joinability rather than a validated measure of true join utility.
Citations
#85 — Moderately Innovative (59)

WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs

MF Argerich, J Fürst, M Patiño-Martínez · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two gradient-boosted regression models that predict mean GPU power draw and inter-token latency (ITL) for arbitrary LLM-GPU inference pairs, trained solely on publicly available LLM metadata and GPU specifications with zero hardware profiling. - Why it's important: Small- and medium-scale operators lack the hardware access and budget to exhaustively profile every candidate LLM-GPU combination, yet GPU choice alone can cut power draw by 43% for the same model under the same latency SLA (e.g., Llama 3.1 8B on an A30 vs. an H100). - How it does it: XGBoost regressors trained on GPU specs (TDP, memory bandwidth, clock rates) and Hugging Face metadata (parameter count, layers, attention heads), plus three engineered physically grounded features (boosting ratio, bandwidth latency, compute latency), with the power target normalized by each GPU's TDP to make the learned behavior scale-invariant across architectures. - The good: Under Leave-One-GPU-Out cross-validation on completely unseen GPUs, the power model reaches 3.4% (offline) / 13.5% (server) median absolute percentage error (MdAPE) and the ITL model reaches 8.5% (server), beating load-scaled TDP and roofline baselines by roughly 2-4x while preserving GPU ranking quality (Kendall tau greater than or equal to 0.76 for server scenarios). - The missing: Offline-scenario ITL prediction is markedly weaker (24.9% MdAPE under Leave-One-GPU-Out) and the entire evaluation is restricted to 8 dense-model-only NVIDIA GPUs and 42 dense LLMs up to 27B parameters, excluding Mixture-of-Experts, quantized, and multi-GPU deployments entirely. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building capacity-planning or energy-reporting tooling for LLM inference fleets; the ranking-quality results (Kendall tau) matter more than the point-error numbers for that use case, and the offline-ITL weakness should temper expectations for batch-serving deployments.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is the top contributor: the three-tier CV/LOGO/LOLO protocol directly targets the generalization question rather than reporting a single train-test split, code and the Watt Counts dataset are publicly released, and the analytical baselines are hyperparameter-free so cannot be strawmanned. Completeness (70) is helped by genuinely honest framing, including admitting the roofline baseline out-ranks the learned ITL model under LOGO despite 3x worse absolute error. Novelty (45) is the ceiling: TDP-normalization plus engineered physics features on an off-the-shelf XGBoost is a sensible but predictable modeling choice, and no learned baseline from the cited related work (Fu et al.'s GNN) is compared, so it is unclear how much of the gain is method versus being first to test LOGO/LOLO at all. Impact (60) reflects a real released tool whose 24.9% offline-ITL MdAPE and dense-only, single-GPU, NVIDIA-only scope limit deployment reach. Weighted: 0.20*45 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*60 = 59.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Operators running small- to medium-scale LLM inference deployments (as opposed to hyperscalers) need to select which GPU to pair with a given model to minimize power draw and meet latency service-level agreements (SLAs), but doing so today requires physically profiling each candidate (GPU, LLM) combination — a process requiring diverse hardware access, specialized expertise, and nontrivial cost that most such operators lack. Existing energy/latency benchmarking and prediction efforts (MLPerf Power, ML.ENERGY, TokenPowerBench, EcoLogits, and several regression/GNN-based predictors) all either require profiling the exact target hardware or have not been evaluated for generalization to GPUs unseen during training, leaving a gap for pre-deployment estimation on genuinely novel hardware. Key Idea: Physically motivated feature engineering plus target normalization lets a standard tabular regressor generalize across GPU architectures it has never seen: normalizing the power-draw target by each GPU's Thermal Design Power (TDP) converts an absolute, hardware-scale-dependent quantity into a relative, scale-invariant one the model can learn from a small set of training GPUs and extrapolate to new ones, while engineered features (bandwidth latency = model-weight bytes / memory bandwidth; compute latency = 2*params / FLOPS) inject known first-order physics (LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound) that the model can then correct with residual patterns. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages: (1) feature extraction, pulling GPU specs (TDP, memory bandwidth, clock rates, transistor count, memory type/size, FP16 TFLOPS) from manufacturer data and LLM architecture fields (parameter count, layer count, attention/KV heads, hidden size, model type) from Hugging Face metadata, then deriving boosting ratio, bandwidth latency, and compute latency; (2) two independent XGBoost regressors — one for mean power draw P-bar (target normalized by GPU TDP, max_depth=6, n_estimators=100), one for ITL (max_depth=5, n_estimators=200), both with reg_lambda=100 chosen via grid search over depth in {3..7}, lambda in {0.1,...,500}, and n_estimators in {50,...,500}; (3) evaluation via three splitting strategies of increasing difficulty — 5-fold GroupKFold CV (unseen GPU-LLM combination of known GPUs/LLMs), Leave-One-GPU-Out (LOGO, unseen GPU), and Leave-One-LLM-Out (LOLO, unseen LLM). Two design decisions stand out: normalizing power by TDP is well justified by the strong TDP-vs-max-power correlation and is empirically validated by the GPU-based features (memory bandwidth, TDP-related) dominating feature importance; algorithm selection (XGBoost over linear/elastic-net/MLP) is justified only by MdAPE/Pearson-r comparison during LOGO CV, not by a stated theoretical argument, so the choice is empirically but not mechanistically justified. Key Results: On the Watt Counts dataset (8 server-grade NVIDIA GPUs — V100, T4, A100, A30, L40S, L4, H100, H200 — and 42 dense LLMs from 0.124B to 27B parameters), the power model achieves MdAPE of 1.6% (CV), 3.4% (LOGO, unseen GPU), 2.0% (LOLO, unseen LLM) offline, and 5.5% / 13.5% / 6.7% server, versus 4.4% (offline) and 26.0% (server) for the best TDP-scaled baseline — a roughly 4x reduction on unseen (GPU, LLM) combinations for server scenarios and roughly 2x on completely unseen GPUs, with GPU Kendall tau greater than or equal to 0.86 versus 0.60 for the baseline. The ITL model achieves MdAPE of 12.8% (CV), 24.9% (LOGO), 15.6% (LOLO) offline, and 4.9% / 8.5% / 5.6% server, versus a roofline baseline at 80.2% (offline) and 29.6% (server); the roofline baseline actually matches or exceeds the learned model's GPU ranking quality (tau) under LOGO in both scenarios despite 3x higher absolute error, indicating the two approaches are complementary rather than one strictly dominating. Baselines are physically grounded (TDP-scaled, roofline-derived) rather than the strongest possible ML competitors — no comparison is made against the graph-neural-network approach of Fu et al. or other learned baselines from related work, only against non-learned analytical ones. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (feature engineering + TDP-normalized XGBoost regression for cross-hardware generalization) combined with a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the first quantified generalization results for LLM power/latency prediction on GPUs unseen during training); the underlying learning algorithm (XGBoost) and general regression-for-hardware-performance-prediction approach are standard, so the novelty is concentrated in the normalization strategy, the domain-specific derived features, and the rigorous LOGO/LOLO evaluation protocol rather than in the modeling technique itself. Rigor & Reproducibility: Feature importance analysis (Figure 3) substitutes for a formal ablation, showing scenario and memory-bandwidth features dominate for power while bandwidth latency dominates for ITL, but no leave-one-feature-out ablation quantifies each feature's marginal contribution to MdAPE. No error bars, seeds, or significance tests are reported for the XGBoost results themselves (only ±1 standard deviation bars on feature-importance and power-draw-distribution figures, not on the headline MdAPE/Pearson-r/Kendall-tau numbers), so it is not possible to assess whether a 3.4% vs. 5.5% MdAPE difference is statistically distinguishable from noise across folds. Code and the Watt Counts training/evaluation dataset are publicly released at https://github.com/maufadel/wattgpu, which is a genuine reproducibility positive. The baselines are analytical and hyperparameter-free, so there is no risk of the baseline being under-tuned relative to the proposed model; the evaluation strategy (LOGO/LOLO) directly targets and mitigates the standard risk of a technique being validated only on the same distribution it was fit to. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (regression models evaluated via cross-validated held-out prediction). Practical: Yes — the method requires only publicly available GPU spec sheets and Hugging Face metadata at inference time, no target hardware access, so it runs anywhere a spreadsheet of specs is available. Efficient: Training and inference cost is that of a small XGBoost model (100-200 trees, depth 5-6) over roughly 42 LLMs times 8 GPUs times a handful of scenarios — negligible compared to any actual profiling run, though the paper reports no explicit training-time or inference-time figures. Effective: The evidence (MdAPE, Pearson r, Kendall tau against held-out GPUs and LLMs) directly measures the stated problem (predicting power/latency for unseen hardware), not a proxy, though the offline-ITL result (24.9% MdAPE under LOGO) shows the method only partially solves the problem in that regime. Scalable: Not tested — the paper evaluates 8 GPUs and 42 dense models up to 27B parameters and explicitly defers Mixture-of-Experts, quantized, and multi-GPU configurations to future work, so behavior at 10x-100x model scale (e.g., 100B+ parameter or multi-GPU-sharded models) is unevaluated and unclaimed. Strengths: - Rigorous three-tier evaluation protocol (CV / LOGO / LOLO) that explicitly isolates generalization to unseen GPU-LLM pairs, unseen GPUs, and unseen LLMs, rather than a single train-test split, is a genuine methodological strength uncommon in prior LLM-energy-prediction work cited in Section 2. - The TDP-normalization trick for the power target is a simple, well-motivated design choice with a clear physical justification (TDP correlates with long-term max power) and is shown empirically to enable cross-architecture transfer. - Honest complementary-baseline framing: the paper does not oversell the learned model as strictly superior, explicitly noting the roofline baseline out-ranks the learned ITL model's GPU tau under LOGO despite worse absolute error. - Public release of code and the underlying Watt Counts dataset supports independent verification. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that offline-scenario ITL prediction is markedly harder (24.9% LOGO MdAPE) and attribute this to static metadata features being unable to capture vLLM's continuous-batching dynamics; they explicitly scope the work to dense models in single-GPU configurations, deferring Mixture-of-Experts, quantized models, and multi-GPU deployments as future work. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No comparison against any learned baseline from cited related work (e.g., the GNN-based approach of Fu et al. or the ML-based per-prompt predictor of Caravaca et al.), so it is unclear how much of the reported improvement is attributable to the modeling approach versus simply being the first to test LOGO/LOLO generalization at all. - The dataset spans only NVIDIA GPUs from a single vendor across 2018-2024 architectures (Volta through Hopper); generalization to AMD accelerators, TPUs, or genuinely next-generation architectures (e.g., Blackwell) with different power/frequency-scaling behavior is neither tested nor discussed as a limitation. - No confidence intervals or repeated-fold variance are reported for the headline MdAPE/tau numbers, so apparent differences between scenarios or against baselines cannot be checked for statistical robustness. - The claimed real-world energy-savings example (Figure 1, 43% power reduction by choosing A30 over H100 for Llama 3.1 8B) is presented as a demonstration rather than as part of the held-out evaluation, so its accuracy is not independently stress-tested beyond the aggregate CV/LOGO/LOLO numbers. Bottom Line: A practitioner selecting GPUs for LLM inference deployment gains a genuinely validated, code-released tool for ranking and estimating power/latency without profiling every candidate combination, though the offline-ITL weakness and the narrow (dense-model, single-GPU, NVIDIA-only) evaluation scope mean it should be treated as a strong first step rather than a deployment-ready universal predictor.
Search Results 2607.02391 PDF
#86 — Moderately Innovative (59)

ResearchStudio-Reel: Automate the Last Mile of Research from Paper to Poster, Video, and Blog (48 ▲ )

Lingao Xiao, Yalun Dai, Yangyu Huang, Qihao Zhao, Wenshan Wu, Hugo He, Ruishuo Chen, Jin Jiang, Qianli Ma, Jiahuan Zhang, Xin Zhang, Ying Xin, Yang Ou, Yan Xia, Scarlett Li, Longbo Huang, Zhipeng Zhang, Yang He, Yap Kim Hui, Yan Lu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ResearchStudio-Reel, a five-skill agentic composition (Claude Code/Codex skills) that turns one paper PDF into an editable print-ready poster, a synchronized narrated talk video, and a bilingual blog, then binds all three into a navigable interactive HTML viewer. - Why it's important: Authors, labs, and course instructors currently rebuild the poster, video, and blog for every accepted paper by hand, and each pipeline re-extracts the source paper from scratch and ships a one-way render the author can't reopen and fix. - How it does it: A single shared extractor (Paper2Assets) builds one reusable asset bundle consumed by three editable generators, each gated by a hard categorical pass/fail render check rather than a continuous VLM-preference score, with a fourth skill (Paper2Reel) stitching the three outputs into one section-aligned viewer. - The good: On the 100-paper Paper2Poster benchmark, the generated posters beat both prior automated poster systems and single-shot frontier LLMs (Claude-4.8 Opus, GPT-5.5, Gemini-3.1 Pro) on every aesthetic and information sub-criterion under two held-out VLM judges, and outscore the papers' own authored posters on aesthetics (3.52 vs. 2.94) while winning the overall pairwise comparison on 84-93% of papers. - The missing: The evaluation is proxy-bound and single-artifact: only the poster has a graded public benchmark; the video and blog are assessed solely via capability-coverage checklists (Tables 2-3), with no human-rater study, no reading-comprehension or engagement metric for video/blog, and no ablation isolating Paper2Reel's or Paper2Blog's individual quality contribution the way Table 1 isolates the poster's. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building agentic document-to-multimedia pipelines or paper-to-poster/video tools; the poster ablation methodology (isolating skill machinery from base-model quality) and the hard-gate-over-soft-score design are the transferable ideas, while the video/blog claims should be read as engineering-capability claims, not benchmarked quality claims.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (68) carries the composite: an MIT-licensed five-skill artifact producing natively editable PPTX/DOCX/HTML is a real deployable release, and the poster component beats prior systems and human-authored posters on aesthetics (3.52 vs. 2.94) on a 100-paper benchmark with a genuine skill-vs-base-model ablation. Resilience (50) and Completeness (55) pull it down hard: two of the three claimed artifacts (video, blog) have no graded evaluation at all, only self-defined capability checklists with no independent verification that competing systems lack the checked capabilities and no reader or viewer study, so the paper's headline is a four-artifact system supported by one-artifact evidence. Rigor (58) is further reduced by VLM-only judging using a model family (claude-opus-4.8) that also runs inside the pipeline, no error bars or seed variance on any table, and a headline cost figure averaged over only 5 papers. Weighted composite: 0.15*55 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*68 = 59.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Turning an accepted paper into a conference poster, a talk video, and a public-facing blog post is a manual, time-consuming last-mile task that authors, industrial research labs, and course instructors all face in the days immediately after camera-ready, when they have the least time available. Prior automation treats each of these three artifacts as a separate problem, so an industrial lab wanting a full communications pipeline, or a course wanting a paper-of-the-week briefing pack, must stitch together disconnected single-artifact tools that duplicate figure/caption/metadata extraction from the same PDF and ship non-editable renders (flat PDF, MP4, or plain-text blog) that cannot be corrected without regenerating everything from scratch. Key Idea: Reframe the last mile as an architectural composition problem rather than a modeling problem: one shared, reusable upstream extractor (Paper2Assets) that runs once per paper feeds three independent editable generators, and quality is enforced through deterministic categorical pass/fail render gates (e.g., a poster section must be 90-98% full, a video package must have non-empty audio and correctly timed subtitles) rather than a continuous VLM-as-judge aesthetic score that can rate a load-bearing but empty section as "good enough." Methodology: The pipeline has five Claude Code/Codex "skills." (1) Paper2Assets extracts a paper's PDF once into a shared bundle: full text with page breaks, per-figure manifest with a verifier-checked figure-cleanup chain (deterministic prefix stripping crop artifacts, then a fresh-context sub-agent verifier re-reading the crop against the original), metadata, a nine-section LLM-written summary (Problem/Motivation/Contribution/Method/Dataset/Key Result/Ablation/Headline Numbers/Takeaway), a narration script, and institution logos/QR codes. (2) Paper2Poster composes an HTML poster along four design axes (column layout, visual style, header arrangement, Scan-to-Read block) and runs a "staged-fill loop": each section is measured post-render as a categorical verdict (EMPTY/SPARSE/FULL/SPILLAGE/OVERFLOW based on a fullRatio = content-height/card-height scalar), edited one section per round until every panel reads FULL, then converted to native PowerPoint via DOM-walking (not pixel rasterization) so text, lists, equations (as OMML), and shapes remain editable. (3) Paper2Video delegates deck authoring to an existing tool (ppt-master), plans narration duration before TTS (Edge TTS) rather than post-hoc clipping, generates narration-aligned visual highlight cues via a semantic-anchor cue resolver, and outputs an editable PPTX plus captioned/uncaptioned MP4 variants bound by a timeline.json sidecar. (4) Paper2Blog builds one shared bilingual evidence map (facts, claims, quantitative results, source links) and writes separate Chinese (WeChat-register) and English (research-blog-register) DOCX articles from it, gated by a layout-aware DOCX check for near-blank pages and orphan tails. (5) Paper2Reel reads the completed outputs of the other three skills and their shared section IDs/figure handles/timeline sidecar to bind them into one interactive HTML viewer with section-level navigation across poster, video, and blog. Each skill's output is release-gated by a mandatory deterministic check (the poster's fill-gate, the video's check_video_package.py, the blog's package gate, Paper2Reel's browser-driven interaction gate) rather than a learned quality score. Two design decisions stand out: the choice to measure poster fullness as a discrete five-band categorical verdict rather than a continuous score is justified by an explicit engineering argument (continuous scores oscillate near the target and a tight 98% gate triples refinement rounds for negligible visual gain, addressed by a separate render-time whitespace expansion pass); the choice to reconstruct PowerPoint from live DOM geometry (getBoundingClientRect/getComputedStyle) rather than rasterizing the rendered page is well-justified as the mechanism that makes the poster truly re-editable. Key Results: On the 100-paper Paper2Poster benchmark, scored by two held-out VLM judges (claude-opus-4.8, gpt-5.5) against six aesthetic/information sub-criteria plus a PaperQuiz reading-comprehension probe, ResearchStudio-Reel (Claude Code setting) achieves the best mean score on every aesthetic sub-criterion (Elements 3.33, Engagement 3.02, Layout 3.97) and every information sub-criterion (Low 4.00, Logic 4.00, Content 3.71) among all compared systems, versus an aesthetic mean of 2.94 and information mean of 3.63 for the human authors' own ground-truth posters. It wins the overall pairwise comparison on 84-93% of papers and loses outright on at most four papers, against baselines including three single-shot frontier LLMs (aesthetic means 2.97-2.98) and three prior poster-pipeline systems (Paper2Poster tool, P2P, PosterGen; aesthetic means 1.82-2.93). On PaperQuiz reading comprehension, the ordering inverts: more text-dense systems (P2P at 75.40% Understanding, Paper2Poster tool at 95.65%) score higher than ResearchStudio-Reel (55.43% Detail, 91.32% Understanding), which the authors attribute to a genuine density-versus-legibility tradeoff. An ablation isolates the source of the gain: the same base model (claude-opus-4.8) prompted once for a full poster scores 2.76 aesthetic/2.83 layout, while the identical model wrapped in the staged-fill loop reaches 3.52/3.97, an improvement attributed to the skill machinery rather than the underlying LLM; the same +0.76 aesthetic gain appears when swapping in gpt-5.5 (2.76→3.36 not directly stated, but reported 3.03→3.36 aesthetic, 3.22→3.82 layout). For video and blog, no graded benchmark exists; evaluation is a capability-coverage checklist (Tables 2-3) showing ResearchStudio-Reel is the only system among compared categories (deck tools, video tools, NotebookLM Video, paper-to-video agents; Semantic Scholar TLDR, research assistants, Scholarcy, NotebookLM) that checks all listed capabilities (highlight, duration control, editable PPTX, subtitles, audio for video; layout, figures, DOCX, bilingual, summary for blog). Operational cost: full four-artifact pipeline averages ~89 minutes wall-clock and ~2.6M input / ~276K output tokens per paper on claude-opus-4.8, with the poster's fill loop and the video's ppt-master/QA stages the two heaviest sub-stages. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the five-skill composition and shared-extractor architecture) plus NEW METHOD for the poster generator specifically (the categorical staged-fill loop and DOM-to-PowerPoint reconstruction bridge). The paper-to-poster, paper-to-video, and paper-to-blog problems themselves are not new (surveyed against four prior threads in §2), and the underlying primitives (headless-browser rendering, Edge TTS, python-docx, ppt-master for slide authoring) are off-the-shelf; the genuine novelty is the shared-bundle/hard-gate architecture that unifies three previously isolated single-artifact pipelines and the interactive convergence layer (Paper2Reel), not any individual generator's core algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: The poster comparison is run under identical conditions (same benchmark, same downscaled resolution, two independent held-out VLM judges reporting consistent orderings) and includes a genuine ablation isolating skill machinery from base model choice (Table 1, ¶ Ablation), which is a meaningful rigor practice absent from many agentic-pipeline papers. However, no error bars, variance across seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported for any table; the judges are VLMs evaluating renders they never compare against the source PDF, so judge bias toward the system's own stylistic conventions cannot be ruled out. The video and blog generators have no graded benchmark and are evaluated only by capability checklists the authors themselves construct (self-defined criteria, not independently validated), which is a self-reported comparison. Code is released open-source (MIT license, https://aka.ms/ResearchStudio) with per-skill SKILL.md and requirements.txt, which supports reproducibility. The evaluation set (100 papers, ML/CV/NLP venues only per Appendix A) is explicitly scoped as domain-limited by the authors themselves, a credibility positive. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built multi-skill tool evaluated by deployment and benchmark). Practical: Yes, it runs as Claude Code/Codex sessions with standard dependencies (poppler-utils, LibreOffice, ffmpeg, headless Chromium) on commodity infrastructure with no GPU requirement stated. Efficient: ~89 minutes and ~2.6M input/~276K output tokens per paper for all three artifacts combined, with most traffic being ~10%-cost cached re-reads; no direct compute/memory comparison is given against any single-artifact competitor's cost. Effective: The poster's superiority is measured against a real benchmark with two judges and a human ground-truth comparison, giving reasonably strong evidence for that artifact; video and blog effectiveness rests only on checklist coverage, not on any measured quality or user-trust outcome, so the claim there is coverage, not proven quality. Scalable: Not tested at 10x-100x paper volume or under concurrent multi-user load; the authors note the domain scope is calibrated to ML/CV/NLP venues and explicitly flag that transfer to biomedicine, physics, or design-heavy fields is untested (Appendix A). The work delivers a full open-source software artifact (five Claude Code/Codex skills, MIT-licensed) plus native editable output files (PPTX, DOCX, HTML), not merely a paper-form proposal. Strengths: - The staged-fill categorical loop (EMPTY/SPARSE/FULL/SPILLAGE/OVERFLOW verdicts with a bounded discrete edit-per-round protocol) is a specific, well-motivated engineering solution to the discrete-editing oscillation problem that a naive continuous-score refinement loop would hit, backed by an explicit design-requirements analysis (A1-A5) and a measured convergence example (Figure 4). - The DOM-to-PowerPoint reconstruction bridge (walking node geometry via getBoundingClientRect/getComputedStyle rather than rasterizing pixels) is a concrete, non-obvious technical contribution that plausibly generalizes beyond this paper's use case to any HTML-to-native-office conversion problem. - The ablation in Table 1 (same model, with vs. without the skill loop) is a genuine attempt to isolate the source of the reported gains rather than just reporting an aggregate win, which is more rigorous than typical agentic-pipeline evaluations. - The shared-extraction, cross-artifact figure/caption/claim consistency design (G1 in the taxonomy) is a real architectural insight: previous systems each re-derive figure crops and metadata independently, risking silent cross-artifact drift, which this design eliminates by construction. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state their own evaluation is "proxy-bound": the aesthetic rubric and PaperQuiz metric pull in opposite directions and neither measures whether a reader actually absorbs the paper's content (§7). They acknowledge the gap to human posters is not just quality but generative: the pipeline can only reuse figures that already exist in the paper and cannot draw a bespoke overview diagram the way a human designer would, and note that closing this gap via figure synthesis would reintroduce the hallucination risk their categorical gates were built to suppress. They list five recurring failure modes (Appendix A: figure-cleanup residue, fill-loop non-convergence, slide-narration referential drift, bilingual blog numeric/claim drift, voice-mismatched narration) each with a stated mitigation, and explicitly flag the system is calibrated on ML/CV/NLP venues with untested transfer to biomedicine, physics, or design-heavy fields. This is a substantive, specific self-critique rather than boilerplate limitations language. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The video and blog generators receive no quantitative quality evaluation at all, only capability checklists that the authors themselves define and populate (Tables 2-3); there is no independent verification that a competing paper-to-video system (VideoAgent, Preacher, Paper2Video) actually lacks the checked capabilities, nor any reader/viewer study measuring whether the generated video or blog is actually good, only whether it exists in the claimed format. The PaperQuiz results (Table 1) show the method trailing older, worse-scoring systems on reading comprehension, which the paper frames as an acceptable density/legibility tradeoff, but no reader study confirms which tradeoff point actual poster viewers prefer. Aesthetic and information judging is done exclusively by VLMs (including one of the same model families, claude-opus-4.8, used inside the pipeline itself), raising a self-evaluation concern the paper does not address: a judge from the same model family as the generator could share stylistic biases not present in a human rating panel. The claimed cost/time figures (Table 4) are averaged over only 5 sampled papers for the full-pipeline breakdown, a small sample for a headline operational-cost claim presented alongside the 100-paper poster benchmark. Bottom Line: Worth reading for the poster-generation architecture and the categorical-gate design pattern, which are demonstrated with real benchmark evidence; the video and blog contributions are best read as engineering capability claims awaiting the same rigor of evaluation the poster component already received.

#87 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Understanding Why Language Models Hallucinate: Testing Reasoning Against Priors

Y Hu, X Tong, H Bai, X Ding, SM Bharadwaj, S Cao… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a latent key-task framework that formalizes hallucination as inference misalignment (a model choosing a statistically dominant shortcut inference path over the constraint-sensitive path the prompt supports), and builds TRAPQA, a two-part closed-book benchmark (SCIENTISTQA, 2,925 items; REAL-LIFE CONSTRAINED QA, 500 items) that isolates this failure from missing knowledge. - Why it's important: Hallucination is treated as a training-data or missing-fact problem in most benchmarks, but if models fail even when they possess the relevant facts, factual-coverage fixes (RAG, more pretraining data) will not close the gap. - How it does it: Pairs each disambiguation question with closed-book factual probes on the same decisive fact, so the paper can separate "doesn't know" from "knows but retrieves the wrong (higher-frequency) association." - The good: Across eight model-setting combinations (GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview, DeepSeek V3.2, each with a low/high reasoning mode), hallucination persists even in the "knows-both-probes-correct" bucket (2.01%-34.46% wrong-rate depending on model/mode), directly evidencing a retrieval/deployment failure rather than ignorance. - The missing: The theory's central object, the pretraining frequency π(k) of keys and key-task co-occurrences, is never measured; all frequency claims are inferred post hoc from behavior, not verified against actual pretraining corpora (which are unavailable for these closed-weight models). - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building hallucination-mitigation or RAG evaluation pipelines; the probe-conditioned tables (Table 2, Table 4) are the most actionable part, showing that supplying full profiles in-context (rather than just names) collapses error rates from up to 37.23% to at most 3.38%, which is a concrete, generalizable mitigation signal.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (55) pulls the composite down hardest: no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests on any primary hallucination rate, and three of the four evaluated model families (Gemini, GPT, Claude) participated in constructing the benchmark used to evaluate them, a circularity the paper does not resolve. Resilience (55) suffers because the theory's load-bearing quantity, pretraining frequency pi(k), is never measured for any closed-weight model and is inferred post hoc from behavior, so Theorem 3.4 is decorative rather than tested. The probe-conditioned design is a real methodological contribution (Novelty 65, Non-Obviousness 65) and the knows-both-yet-hallucinates rates of 2.01-34.46% are a clean result, but the paper diagnoses without benchmarking any mitigation. Weighted composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*50 = 59.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frontier LLMs are increasingly used in agentic settings (tool calls, transactions) where a confidently wrong answer under a benign, non-adversarial prompt can cause real damage. Existing hallucination benchmarks (TruthfulQA, HaluEval, FActScore, SimpleQA) measure whether output is factual or grounded but cannot distinguish two very different failure causes: the model never learned the fact, versus the model has the fact stored but applies the wrong inference path when the prompt requires comparing or discriminating between competing associations. This distinction matters for solution design: better retrieval/data coverage fixes the first cause but not the second. Key Idea: The paper's central insight is that many LLM prompts implicitly activate a latent key-task inference (identify a salient cue "key," then retrieve the task-relevant relation), and pretraining-frequency imbalance between the correct key-task pair and a competing high-frequency "shortcut" pair can make the shortcut's posterior dominate even when the prompt's evidence logically supports the correct pair. Crucially, this predicts hallucination without requiring the model to lack the relevant fact, since the failure is in inference-path selection, not fact storage. This is a genuine mechanistic hypothesis, not simply a benchmark-engineering contribution, though it is validated only indirectly through behavioral proxies. Methodology: The framework has three parts. (1) Theory: a Bayesian latent-variable model where a prompt z induces a posterior P(k,t|z) over key-task pairs, with three formal assumptions (Activated Key Restriction, Activated Task Restriction, Output Separation) used to derive Theorem 3.4 (Shortcut Probability Dominance): when the pretraining-frequency ratio π(k_s,t_s)/π(k,t) is large enough, the shortcut posterior dominates the correct posterior even under semantically correct prompt evidence. Theorem 3.6 then lower-bounds the resulting total-variation inference loss by (γ(z)+γ*(z))/2, connecting posterior dominance to output-level error. (2) SCIENTISTQA: built from 9,090 Wikipedia scientist profiles, name-removed and embedded (text-embedding-3-small), hard-paired via a sparsity-adjusted cosine similarity score (Eq. 1), then Gemini generates a biographical paragraph plus a single decisive constraint that rules out exactly one candidate, filtered by GPT, yielding 2,925 items each tested under names-only (retrieval-sensitive) and profiles-in-context (retrieval-relaxed) conditions, paired with two closed-book factual probes per item. (3) REAL-LIFE CONSTRAINED QA: seeded from SWOW word-association norms to find high-salience cue-response pairs, organized into 8 template families (e.g., vehicle_required, recording_medium), GPT-generated two-option everyday scenarios where one option matches the salient association but violates a stated constraint, filtered/proofread by Claude, yielding 500 items across 13 daily-life categories.
