Design decisions: (a) using probe pairs to separate ignorance from deployment failure is the paper's most defensible methodological choice; (b) using pretraining-frequency as an unmeasured, inferred construct is the weakest link, since the theory's key predictor is never directly observed for any evaluated closed-weight model; (c) restricting external tools (no web search) isolates internal retrieval but limits ecological validity for how these models are actually deployed. Key Results: Across 2,925 SCIENTISTQA questions in the names-only condition, hallucination rates range from 2.50% (Gemini-low) to 37.23% (DeepSeek-chat), collapsing to a maximum of 3.38% (six of eight settings hit 0%) when full profiles are supplied in-context (Table 1). Reasoning effort has inconsistent effects: high-thinking substantially helps Claude (23.90% to 6.22%) and DeepSeek (37.23% to 10.56%), only marginally helps GPT (11.76% to 10.26%), and slightly hurts Gemini (2.50% to 3.15%). Conditioning on the two factual probes (Table 2), hallucination still occurs 2.01%-34.46% of the time even when both probes are answered correctly, and complete probe-level ignorance explains at most 5.50% of errors, meaning most errors are deployment failures, not knowledge gaps. A fame-based control (Wikipedia page views, length, external links) rules out raw popularity as the explanation: hallucination rates are lower, not higher, when the wrong (distractor) candidate is more famous, across all eight settings. On REAL-LIFE CONSTRAINED QA (500 items), Claude, DeepSeek-chat, GPT, and Gemini make 81, 182, 44, and 18 errors respectively (16.20%, 36.40%, 8.80%, 3.60%), showing the same shortcut-vs-constraint pattern outside encyclopedic disambiguation. All baselines are the models' own no-context settings; there is no external hallucination-mitigation baseline (e.g., RAG, self-consistency, verifier re-ranking) for comparison, so the paper measures the phenomenon but does not benchmark against known mitigations. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (the latent key-task Bayesian model and its two formal theorems linking pretraining-frequency imbalance to inference loss) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (TRAPQA, combining SCIENTISTQA and REAL-LIFE CONSTRAINED QA with paired factual probes). What is genuinely novel is the probe-conditioned experimental design that operationally separates "knows" from "deploys"; the theoretical framework itself extends existing Bayesian in-context-learning views (Xie et al. 2022) and frequency-bias accounts (Sun et al. 2025b) rather than introducing a wholly new mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are effectively present via the probe-conditioning tables and the profiles-in-context control, which function as a well-designed knowledge-isolation manipulation. No error bars, confidence intervals, or repeated-seed variance are reported for any accuracy/hallucination-rate figure, and no statistical test accompanies the primary rate comparisons (only the fame-direction control in Table 9 reports p-values against a 50% baseline). Model versions and API identifiers are pinned in Table 3 for reproducibility, and the paper states the benchmark package (questions, labels, prompts, saved outputs) will be released, though no repository URL is given in the main text or appendix. A genuine methodological concern: the ground-truth constraints and biographical paragraphs were generated by Gemini and filtered by GPT/Claude, meaning three of the four evaluated model families participated in constructing the very benchmark used to evaluate them, which risks a subtle circularity (models may perform differently on constraint types their own family tends to phrase or interpret in idiosyncratic ways). The authors do flag distributional/temporal drift and provider-side model updates as reproducibility risks (Limitations section). Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (closed-book QA evaluation across four model families). Practical: Yes, evaluation requires only API access to commercial frontier models, no fine-tuning or special hardware. Efficient: Not applicable in a compute-cost sense; the benchmark itself is a fixed-size diagnostic (2,925 + 500 items) with modest inference cost, though high-thinking modes multiply the per-question cost. Effective: The evidence directly shows the diagnosed failure (probe-correct-yet-hallucinating rates of 2-34%), but the paper does not test any mitigation, so "solves the problem" is out of scope; it only demonstrates the problem exists and is not explained by missing facts or fame bias. Scalable: The paper explicitly cautions that its scientist-pair and everyday-scenario coverage is fixed at construction time and could be affected by temporal drift (new honors, changing biographies) or benchmark contamination if released publicly (Section I, Potential risks); no experiment tests generalization to other entity domains such as sports players or musicians, though the authors state this as planned future work. Delivered artifact: a benchmark dataset (TRAPQA) with saved evaluation outputs, not a mitigation tool, trained model, or reusable software library. Strengths: - The probe-pair design (eliminative probe + compatibility probe per item) is a genuinely useful methodological contribution for separating knowledge-storage failures from knowledge-deployment failures, and it is validated by the sizable knows-both-yet-hallucinates rates. - The names-only vs. profiles-in-context contrast is a clean natural experiment showing the shortcut effect is retrieval-sensitive: giving the model the same facts explicitly in-context nearly eliminates the error, which is strong support for the "inference misalignment,
Recommended 2607.00447 PDF
#88 — Moderately Innovative (58)

CoLT: Teaching Multi-Modal Models to Think with Chain of Latent Thoughts

L Hu, S Qin, Z Liao, Q Guo, L Wan, W Feng, Y Liu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CoLT, a framework that trains multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) to reason through 3 continuous latent thought vectors instead of verbose text chain-of-thought (CoT), using three step-level supervision signals (forward decoding, backward decoding, internal consistency) to keep the latent space meaningful. - Why it's important: Text-based CoT costs hundreds to thousands of autoregressive tokens per query, and naive latent-reasoning approaches produce meaningless representations and unstable training, so a method that regulates latent reasoning without expensive auxiliary image annotations addresses both a cost and a stability problem. - How it does it: A lightweight external decoder (Qwen3-0.6B) supervises each latent thought bidirectionally, forward-decoding it into the next text reasoning step and backward-aligning decoder hidden states to it via cosine loss, plus a small MLP predicts the next latent step from the current one. - The good: On 8 benchmarks with a Qwen3-VL-8B backbone, CoLT beats the strongest latent-reasoning baseline (SIM-CoT) by 5.1 points average (79.1 vs. 74.0) and the strongest latent-visual-reasoning baseline (LVR) by 5.5 points, while cutting generation time 22.6x versus text CoT. - The missing: All results come from a single backbone (Qwen3-VL-8B) and a single training corpus (OneThinker image subset) trained for one epoch, so generalization to other backbones, model scales, or CoT-annotation sources is untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building efficient multi-modal reasoning systems or evaluating latent-CoT methods; the ablations (Tables 2-5) are the most useful part since they isolate which supervision signal and which step count actually matter.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (62) is the strongest in the latent-reasoning subgroup: code is released on GitHub, all 2025-era baselines (CODI, SoftCoT, SIM-CoT, LVR) are rebuilt on the same Qwen3-VL-8B backbone, and four ablation axes are run (per-loss, decoder size, K sweep, cross-K), but no seeds or error bars appear anywhere and several claimed effects (decoder scaling, 0.1-0.3 points) sit inside plausible single-run noise. Novelty (55) is capped because latent CoT is established (CoCoNut, CODI, SIM-CoT) and the contribution is a specific bidirectional supervision architecture rather than a new reasoning paradigm. Completeness (60) is pulled down by the absence of any limitations section and by an interpretability claim resting on two hand-picked qualitative examples. Impact (62) reflects released code plus a real 22.6x generation-time reduction. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*62 = 58.2.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: MLLMs that use explicit text CoT incur heavy inference latency (hundreds to thousands of generated tokens), commit early to a single surface-form reasoning trajectory that forecloses alternative solving paths, and propagate early-step errors through the rest of the chain. Prior latent-reasoning work (CoCoNut, CODI, SIM-CoT) addresses this for text-only LLMs but does not target multi-modal reasoning, and the multi-modal-specific alternative (latent visual reasoning, e.g. LVR, MoNet) requires auxiliary image annotations or generation models, adding labeling cost and limiting applicability to domains lacking visual intermediates. Key Idea: Regulate an unconstrained continuous latent-thought space with a lightweight external decoder operating in two directions at once, forward (latent to text) and backward (text to latent), rather than relying on a single-direction alignment signal as prior latent-reasoning methods do; this bidirectional constraint plus an internal step-to-step consistency loss is what prevents the latent space from collapsing into semantically meaningless vectors, which the paper identifies as the core failure mode of naive latent reasoning. Methodology: Given image v and query x, the backbone MLLM (Qwen3-VL-8B) produces K=3 latent thought vectors h_1..h_K directly from its own last-layer hidden states at designated reasoning positions (Eq. 3), each fed back as the next input embedding, replacing L text reasoning tokens (K << L). Training combines four losses (Eq. 10): (1) task loss, standard next-token CE for the final answer conditioned on the latent thoughts; (2) forward decoding loss (Eq. 5), external decoder D_φ (Qwen3-0.6B, same family/tokenizer as backbone) takes h_k and is trained via CE to generate the text of reasoning step k+1; (3) backward decoding loss (Eq. 7), D_φ encodes the preceding text reasoning steps and its hidden state is aligned to h_k via a normalized (direction-only), stop-gradiented cosine/L2 loss; (4) internal supervision loss (Eq. 9), a 2-layer GELU MLP projection head f_θ predicts h_{k+1} from h_k, scored by cosine similarity with stop-gradient on the target. All CoT annotations come from the OneThinker dataset's image subset, split into K segments per sample. Three design decisions stand out: using stop-gradient + normalization in both backward and internal losses is justified as preventing representational collapse (a real risk given the unconstrained continuous space, and the paper's own ablation without stop-gradient is not shown, so this is asserted rather than demonstrated); fixing K=3 is empirically justified via Table 4 sweep; discarding the decoder entirely at inference (only 3 latent vectors are computed) is the source of the claimed efficiency and is architecturally clean since the decoder never touches the answer-generation path. Key Results: On 8 benchmarks (SeedBench, MMBench, ChartQA, TextVQA, ScienceQA, MMStar, AI2D, MMT-Bench) with Qwen3-VL-8B backbone, CoLT averages 79.1, beating direct-answer (no reasoning) by 9.6 points (69.5), text CoT by 3.4 points (75.7), and the strongest latent-reasoning baseline SIM-CoT by 5.1 points (74.0), with per-benchmark gains up to +9.6 (ChartQA) and +11.1% (TextVQA) over SIM-CoT. Against latent-visual-reasoning baselines that use auxiliary image annotations (LVR, 73.6 avg), CoLT wins by 5.5 points without any auxiliary visual supervision. Ablation (Table 2) shows all three losses are needed: backward decoding alone is the strongest single signal (67.2%), and the full three-way combination reaches 72.6% on the 4-benchmark ablation subset versus 61.4% with none. Inference-speed table (Table 7) reports CoLT completes generation in 0.32-0.33s versus 7.24-7.38s for text CoT (22.4-22.6x reduction in generation time, 10.1x end-to-end), using only 3 latent vectors versus ~139-142 text tokens for CoT, at higher accuracy (68.9 vs. 67.1 on MMStar; 67.4 vs. 63.3 on MMT-Bench). Noise-robustness experiments (Table 6) show CoLT degrades least under visual (Gaussian blur, occlusion) and text (spelling errors, key-token deletion) perturbations, 2.8-9.0% degradation versus 7.2-19.0% for Direct Answer and 5.0-13.8% for Text CoT. Baselines are current 2025-era latent-reasoning methods (CCoT, ICoT, CODI, SoftCoT, SIM-CoT) rebuilt on the same Qwen3-VL-8B backbone for fairness, which is a methodologically sound comparison design. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel element is the bidirectional (forward + backward) external-decoder supervision combined with an internal step-consistency loss, applied without auxiliary visual annotations; the underlying idea of latent-space CoT itself is not new (CoCoNut, CODI, SIM-CoT precede it in text-only settings), so the contribution is a specific supervision architecture and its extension to the multi-modal setting, not a new reasoning paradigm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and reasonably thorough: per-loss and pairwise-loss combinations (Table 2), decoder-size scaling from 0.6B to 8B (Table 3), latent-step-count sweep K=1..8 (Table 4), and cross-K generalization (Table 5). No error bars, multiple random seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the results tables; all numbers appear to be single-run point estimates, which is a real gap given some gaps between methods (e.g., decoder scaling 0.6B to 8B) are under 1 point. Code is stated as released (https://github.com/hulianyuyy/CoLT), which supports reproducibility. Training and evaluation both draw from the OneThinker dataset (or its image subset), and while evaluation is on held-out benchmarks distinct from training data, all comparison methods are retrained on the same corpus, mitigating direct benchmark-gaming concerns, though it also means results are conditional on this one CoT source having representative reasoning-chain structure. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and experiments the paper actually runs on real benchmarks). Practical: Yes, evaluated on a single H200 GPU with an 8B backbone, consistent with commercially available (if high-end) hardware. Efficient: Substantially cheaper than text CoT at inference (0.32-0.33s generation vs. 7.24-7.38s, per Table 7), plus adds a 0.6B decoder only during training (removed at inference), so the efficiency claim is well-supported for the tested configuration. Effective: Evidence directly measures the stated problem (answer accuracy on 8 diverse multi-modal benchmarks against strong same-backbone baselines), not just a proxy, and gains are consistent and specific (not cherry-picked single-benchmark wins). Scalable: Not tested beyond one backbone (8B) and one decoder family (Qwen3, up to 8B); Table 3 explores decoder scaling but backbone scaling (e.g., a 30B+ MLLM) is untested, so scalability to substantially larger backbones is claimed by extrapolation rather than measured. The work delivers a software artifact: code is released on GitHub, and the trained CoLT-8B model is described in detail sufficient for reproduction. Strengths: - The bidirectional (forward + backward) decoder supervision is a genuine mechanistic contribution beyond a straightforward reapplication of unidirectional latent-decoding schemes (CODI, SIM-CoT use single-direction supervision); the ablation in Table 2 credibly isolates each signal's marginal contribution. - Eliminating the need for auxiliary image annotations (versus LVR, MoNet) while still beating those methods is a meaningful practical simplification, not just parity. - The noise-robustness analysis (Table 6) is a non-obvious evaluation angle that most latent-reasoning papers skip, and the finding that continuous representations degrade more gracefully than discrete text tokens under input corruption is a genuine empirical contribution, not just an accuracy leaderboard entry. - The cross-K generalization study (Table 5) demonstrates the method is not brittle to test-time step-count mismatch, a practically relevant robustness check beyond the headline benchmark numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section; the conclusion frames "adaptive latent steps by problem difficulty" and "hybrid latent-text reasoning" as future directions, implicitly acknowledging that the fixed K=3 step count and pure-latent (no text fallback) design are current constraints, but this is stated as forward-looking work rather than as an explicit limitation discussion. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No error bars or repeated-seed results anywhere; several reported gaps (e.g., decoder-scaling improvements of 0.1-0.3 points in Table 3) are within plausible single-run noise, so claims like "even a lightweight 0.6B decoder provides effective supervision" rest on a single point estimate per configuration. - Single backbone family (Qwen3-VL) and single training corpus (OneThinker) throughout; no evidence the forward/backward decoder supervision transfers to a different MLLM architecture (e.g., LLaVA, InternVL) or a different CoT-annotation source. - The interpretability claim (Section 4.6) that decoded latent thoughts show "coherent step-by-step reasoning" rests on two hand-picked qualitative examples (Fig. 2) with no systematic or quantitative faithfulness evaluation of decoded-latent-to-actual-reasoning correspondence; this is a plausibility demonstration, not a rigorous evaluation of whether the decoder's forward projection reflects what the latent thoughts actually encode versus what the decoder is trained to hallucinate as plausible text. - Comparison against proprietary models (GPT-5-high, Claude-Opus-4.1, Gemini-2.5-Pro) in Table 1 is included for context but CoLT (79.1 avg on the 7-column subset it reports) is not directly comparable since those rows have several missing benchmark columns; the paper does not flag this incompleteness in-text. Bottom Line: A practitioner building efficient multi-modal reasoning systems gains a concrete, reproducible supervision recipe (bidirectional decoder plus internal consistency) that measurably beats prior latent-reasoning and latent-visual-reasoning baselines at a fixed backbone, though the single-backbone, single-corpus, no-variance evaluation means the generality of the 5+ point gains should be treated as provisional until validated on a second model family.
Search Results 2606.31986 PDF
#89 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Vidu S1: A Real-Time Interactive Video Generation Model (108 ▲ )

Jintao Zhang, Kai Jiang, Jintao Chen, Xu Wang, Yang Luo, Yuji Wang, Dechuang Chen, Jungang Li, Chengyang Ye, Marco Chen, Hongzhou Zhu, Min Zhao, Yuxuan Jiang, Zhengkun Huang, Chendong Xiang, Kaiwen Zheng, Haoxu Wang, Xiaohang Wang, Qi Jia, Xin Chen, Yimin Chen, Youhe Jiang, Fangcheng Fu, Zhijie Deng, Fan Bao, Jianfei Chen, Jun Zhu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Vidu S1, a real-time interactive video generation model that produces speech-controllable, infinite-length 540p avatar video at 42 FPS on a single consumer GPU. - Why it's important: Existing video generation models (Sora, Veo, Wan, Seedance) are offline, one-shot systems that force users to wait minutes for a fixed output, which blocks live streaming, gaming, and conversational avatar use cases that need immediate, ongoing user control. - How it does it: A three-stage training pipeline (bidirectional teacher, causal autoregressive adaptation via Teacher Forcing/Diffusion Forcing, then Distribution Matching Distillation with Phased Consistency Model regularization) paired with a sliding-window inference scheme (persistent reference context plus TwinCache dual noisy/clean history caching) that bounds error accumulation over arbitrarily long streams. - The good: On the authors' Vidu-StreamBench (500 samples), Vidu S1 hits 100% human-preference win rate on subject controllability against HeyGen and LemonSlice, and on HDTF it posts the best CSIM (0.9192), Sync-D (7.847), and DOVER (0.5660) among 8 compared methods while being the only one that is simultaneously instruction-following, real-time, and highest-resolution (540p at 42 FPS). - The missing: There is no ablation isolating the contribution of TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, or the PCM regularization term individually, so it is unclear how much each inference-side or training-side component contributes to the reported quality and speed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building real-time avatar or streaming-video systems who need a concrete recipe for causal distillation plus sliding-window caching; skip if the interest is offline high-fidelity video generation quality, since this paper trades some of that for latency.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (65) carries the score: a genuinely rare simultaneous combination of 540p, 42 FPS, and instruction-following on a single consumer GPU, but delivered only as a playable demo, with no code, weights, or dataset release. Rigor (50) is the main drag: no ablation isolates TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, or the PCM term, so no evidence attributes the results to the paper's claimed mechanisms; the primary benchmark (Vidu-StreamBench, 500 samples) is in-house, unreleased, and the source of the headline 100% subject-controllability win, which is a direct Goodhart's Law exposure. Resilience (50) and Completeness (50) are limited by an 'infinite-length, drift-free' claim demonstrated via a single 90-second qualitative figure rather than a drift metric over time, and by CSIM margins (0.9192 vs. 0.9191) reported without any variance. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*65 = 58.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Live streaming, gaming, video calls, and virtual-host applications need video that responds to user input as it is generated, but state-of-the-art video diffusion models (Sora, Veo, Wan, Seedance) generate offline in a single shot, with users passively waiting minutes for a complete clip and no ability to intervene mid-generation. The paper motivates the demand asymmetry with a simple argument: interactive-content demand scales as alphaN (N users) while offline-content demand scales as betaN/m because content gets reused m times, so when m > 100 the market for real-time interactive video vastly exceeds offline video. Prior autoregressive video models close part of the gap but still fall short in one of four ways: they lack live user interaction during generation, they don't use speech as a direct control signal, they suffer drift and visual collapse over long horizons, and they lack the inference/serving infrastructure needed to actually run in real time. Key Idea: The core trick is decoupling temporal propagation from appearance refinement during streaming inference via TwinCache: the historical context is represented by a noisy cache (preserving coarse temporal dynamics as an implicit low-pass filter that suppresses high-frequency artifact accumulation) during intermediate denoising steps, and swapped for a clean cache (restoring fine-grained visual detail) only at the final denoising step. Combined with a persistent first-frame reference context acting as an attention sink, this bounds drift over open-ended streams without requiring the model to re-observe the entire history. Methodology: The pipeline has three training stages and two inference-time components. Stage 1 trains a bidirectional "teacher" diffusion model on full video-audio sequences with a standard denoising-prediction loss (Eq. 3) to establish a generative prior. Stage 2 initializes a causal "student" from that teacher and imposes a causal attention mask, training with a stochastic mixture (Bernoulli(p)) of Teacher Forcing (clean ground-truth history, tau=0) and Diffusion Forcing (noisy history, tau>0) to close the train/inference gap for autoregressive rollout (Eq. 5). Stage 3 applies Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) to compress many-step generation into a few-step generator (Eq. 6), regularized by a Phased Consistency Model (PCM) perceptual-distance loss (Eq. 7) to counter the mode collapse, camera drift, and temporal inconsistency the authors report DMD alone produces. At inference, sliding-window attention restricts each step to three components (persistent reference, cached history within the window, current denoising target), with RoPE repositioning applied to cached historical key-value features to preserve positional consistency as the window advances, avoiding recomputation. The inference stack further layers on SageAttention/SpargeAttention/SLA kernels, custom per-block W8A8 quantized GEMM, Triton/CUDA kernel fusion, CUDA graphs, and Ulysses-style multi-GPU context parallelism (building on the authors' own TurboDiffusion and TurboServe systems). Critical design decisions: (1) the Teacher Forcing/Diffusion Forcing mixture is well-motivated as a standard fix for exposure bias in autoregressive diffusion, consistent with prior work (Diffusion Forcing, cited); (2) the DMD+PCM combination is empirically justified in-paper (the text states DMD alone caused instability, motivating PCM addition) but no ablation quantifies the PCM contribution; (3) the choice of per-block (rather than per-tensor/per-channel) quantization granularity is justified by an outlier-sensitivity argument but not benchmarked against alternatives in this paper. Key Results: On the authors' own Vidu-StreamBench (500 in-house samples with action instructions, reference frames, and audio), pairwise human A/B preference tests show Vidu S1 preferred overall 56% vs. 28% against HeyGen, 46% vs. 30% against LemonSlice, and 48% vs. 30% against Kling-Avatar-2.0, with a 100% preference win on subject controllability against HeyGen and LemonSlice (60% vs. 16% against Kling-Avatar-2.0). On the public HDTF benchmark, Vidu S1 reports CSIM 0.9192 (best among 8 methods, next best HeyGen at 0.9191), Sync-D 7.847 (best, next best LemonSlice at 7.921), and DOVER 0.5660 (best, next best LiveAvatar at 0.5639), while being the only method that is simultaneously instruction-following and real-time, at the highest reported resolution (540p, 960x540) and highest FPS (42, next fastest HeyGen at 25 FPS, measured on RTX 5090 GPUs). Baselines span both open-source autoregressive models (Wan2.2-S2V-14B, Hallo3, StableAvatar-1.3B, OmniAvatar-1.3B, LiveAvatar, LemonSlice) and commercial systems (HeyGen, Kling-Avatar-2.0), which is a reasonably current and strong comparison set for this narrow subfield as of the paper's writing. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD (TwinCache), ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION, NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (Vidu-StreamBench). The genuinely novel piece is TwinCache's noisy/clean dual-cache scheduling for streaming diffusion inference and the combination of DMD+PCM for causal distillation in this domain; the three-stage bidirectional-to-causal-to-distilled training recipe and the Teacher/Diffusion Forcing mixture are standard combinations of existing techniques (Diffusion Forcing, DMD, PCM, attention sinks) applied and integrated for a new real-time avatar setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation study isolates the individual contributions of TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, the PCM regularization term, or the choice of Teacher/Diffusion Forcing mixture ratio p; the paper asserts these design choices work but does not measure each independently. No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported for either the CSIM/Sync-D/DOVER metrics or the human preference percentages. The primary benchmark, Vidu-StreamBench, is authored in-house by the same team and not independently validated or released for external scrutiny (no stated public release); this is a Goodhart's Law risk since the model that wins 100% on subject controllability was evaluated on the developers' own instruction set. HDTF results provide a partial independent check, and the paper does report human A/B preference tests rather than relying solely on self-reported automatic metrics, which is a partial mitigation. No code, weights, or dataset release URL is stated beyond a playable online demo link (https://vidu.com/vidu-stream); this is a demo, not a reproducibility artifact. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built, deployed model plus serving stack, evaluated by real-time throughput and human preference on a streaming task). - Practical: Yes, demonstrated running on RTX 5090 consumer GPUs, not requiring a data-center cluster for single-user inference. - Efficient: The paper reports 42 FPS at 540p with a 3-step generator, aided by W8A8 quantization, kernel fusion, CUDA graphs, and sparse/low-bit attention, but gives no absolute latency, memory footprint, or cost-per-hour figures, nor a head-to-head compute-cost comparison against baselines beyond the FPS column in Table 1. - Effective: Human preference tests and standard identity/sync/quality metrics on HDTF and Vidu-StreamBench show the model beats compared systems on most axes, indicating it solves the stated interactive avatar problem rather than only a loosely related proxy, though the in-house benchmark limits independent verification. - Scalable: Multi-GPU Ulysses-style context parallelism is described for cluster-level serving, but no experiment reports throughput or latency at 10x-100x concurrent users or longer time horizons than tested; "infinite-length" generation is demonstrated qualitatively (Figure 1 shows a 90-second sequence) but not stress-tested at, e.g., multi-hour duration with quantitative drift measurements. The paper delivers a playable online demo (https://vidu.com/vidu-stream) as a product artifact rather than open code, weights, or the Vidu-StreamBench dataset. Strengths: - TwinCache's noisy/clean dual-cache decoupling is a concrete, well-motivated mechanism for balancing long-term temporal coherence against per-frame visual fidelity in streaming diffusion, not just a relabeling of existing KV-caching. - The demand-scaling argument in the introduction (alphaN vs. betaN/m) gives a clear, quantifiable business case for why real-time interactive generation is a distinct problem from offline generation, rather than an unsupported motivational claim. - The full-stack integration, from data pipeline through causal distillation to custom CUDA kernels and multi-GPU serving, is executed coherently enough to hit a genuinely rare combination: highest resolution, highest FPS, and instruction-following, simultaneously, among the compared systems. - The 6-stage data filtering pipeline (Figure 2) combining expert models with an omni-model semantic filter is a practically useful recipe for building clean, diarized, captioned single-subject video-audio training data. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that optimizing solely with the DMD objective causes mode collapse, camera drift, content degeneration, and temporal inconsistency, which motivated adding PCM regularization; this is a direct, credible acknowledgment of a real failure mode encountered during development. Beyond this training-stability limitation, the paper does not discuss failure cases of the final deployed model (e.g., under extreme poses, non-human subjects, adversarial or out-of-distribution speech) or state boundary conditions under which Vidu S1 itself might drift or fail. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No ablation isolates TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, or PCM's contribution to final quality; all evidence for these components is qualitative narrative, not controlled experiments. - Vidu-StreamBench is an in-house, unreleased benchmark authored by the same team that built the model being evaluated on it, which is the paper's primary quantitative testbed (500 samples) and the source of its most striking claim (100% preference win rate on subject controllability); no external benchmark corroborates this specific claim. - "Infinite-length" and "drift-free" claims are demonstrated by a 90-second qualitative example (Figure 1) and design arguments about the sliding window, not by a quantitative drift measurement (e.g., identity similarity or Sync-D) plotted over a multi-minute or multi-hour generation. - No compute cost, GPU-hour, or dollar-cost comparison against baselines is given despite "low-cost GPU" being a headline claim in the abstract and Figure 1. - The paper does not report variance or confidence intervals on any metric, making it hard to judge whether the narrow wins (e.g., CSIM 0.9192 vs. HeyGen's 0.9191) are meaningful. Bottom Line: Engineers building real-time, speech-driven avatar or streaming-video systems will find TwinCache and the causal-distillation training recipe directly useful and reasonably well-specified, but the headline benchmark numbers rest on an unreleased in-house dataset and lack the ablations needed to know which components are doing the work.
Recommended 2607.03118 PDF
#90 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Infinite Worlds with Versatile Interactions (21 ▲ )

Zelin Gao, Qiuyu Wang, Jiapeng Zhu, Jingye Chen, Zichen Liu, Qingyan Bai, Jiahao Wang, Yufeng Yuan, Hanlin Wang, Yichong Lu, Ka Leong Cheng, Haojie Zhang, Jian Gao, Tianrui Feng, Yuzheng Liu, Yao Yao, Yinghao Xu, Xing Zhu, Yujun Shen, Hao Ouyang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LingBot-World-Infinity, a causal video world model paired with a real-time distilled variant and a director-pilot agentic harness, that generates action-controllable, drift-free interactive worlds for over an hour at 720p/60fps. - Why it's important: Interactive world models today degrade visually within seconds to minutes of autoregressive rollout and offer only coarse camera navigation, which blocks their use as persistent, open-ended simulators for games and embodied training. - How it does it: A Mixture of Bidirectional and Autoregressive (MoBA) attention mask regularizes teacher-forcing training against overfitting to the growing context, and a two-stage distillation (consistency distillation plus distribution matching distillation applied to self-rollout trajectories) compresses the model into a real-time, drift-suppressed student. - The good: A single uninterrupted one-hour generation session (Fig. 10, 20 sampled scenarios) shows no perceptible visual quality decay, directly supporting the durability claim rather than only reporting short benchmark clips. - The missing: All comparisons against closed-source systems (HappyOyster, Genie 3) are qualitative side-by-side frame grids (Fig. 11-12) with no quantitative visual-fidelity or drift metrics (e.g., FID/FVD over time, PSNR decay curves), so the durability advantage rests on visual inspection rather than numbers. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building interactive world models or video-generation-based game engines; the MoBA attention design and DMD-over-self-rollout recipe for drift suppression are the transferable technical contributions, though practitioners should treat the comparative claims as qualitative until quantitative drift metrics are published.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) is nearly the entire case: full open release of a production-scale 14B interactive world model plus a 1.3B single-GPU variant, code, weights, and a working UI, which is rare against closed comparators like Genie 3. Rigor (35) is severely low: no ablation isolates the MoBA mask (the paper's central architectural claim), no quantitative metric of any kind appears (no FVD/FID, no drift curves, no latency table), the hour-long durability claim rests on N=1 session, and the comparison table is self-authored with categorical bins and no measurement protocol. Completeness (45) gets partial credit for an unusually direct limitations section (no long-term memory, identity drift, no explicit physics) but that honesty does not substitute for the missing evidence. Resilience (40) reflects that 'matches or exceeds the strongest closed-source systems' would not survive a reviewer asking for a single number. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*35 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.30*80 = 58.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive world models generate video frame-by-frame conditioned on a stream of user or agent actions, aiming to serve as explorable substrates for game generation and embodied simulation. Two problems block deployment: (1) long-horizon stability, since autoregressive conditioning feeds each frame's errors back into the model, causing texture smearing, geometry warping, and scene drift within seconds to a few minutes; and (2) interactivity at high fidelity, since rendering detailed video while reacting to live input is compute-expensive, forcing prior systems to trade off resolution, smoothness, or control richness. Existing systems also expose only coarse navigation (camera movement through an otherwise inert scene) rather than a rich, semantically meaningful action space (combat, object interaction, environmental events). Key Idea: The core insight is that pure teacher-forcing training causes autoregressive video models to overfit to their growing conditioning context rather than learning to predict genuinely novel future frames, and that appending a bidirectional attention component to the teacher-forcing self-attention mask (MoBA) acts as a regularizer that eases the transition from bidirectional to autoregressive generation and suppresses this overfitting-driven drift. A second idea, applied at distillation time, is to run distribution matching distillation (DMD) over the student's own long self-rollout trajectories rather than only on teacher-forced states, so the student is optimized on the same error distribution it will encounter at deployment. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Data engine: heterogeneous raw video (egocentric, synthetic game/Unreal Engine, web video) is acquired, profiled (TransNet V2 shot segmentation, technical scoring filters, VLM-based quality/semantic tagging), and annotated with multi-track event-level captions (motion, interaction, scene dynamics, static state) that are composed into chunk-wise, temporally-aligned captions, avoiding a train/inference mismatch that would arise from training only on global per-video captions. (2) Pre-training: a causal video model (DiT backbone) is trained with a conditional flow-matching objective (Eq. 2, predicting velocity ε−x_i) under a causal factorization p_θ(x_1:T|a_1:T)=∏p_θ(x_t|x_<t,a_≤t), conditioned on Plücker-embedding camera poses and chunk-wise text prompts injected via AdaLN and cross-attention; the MoBA self-attention mask blends a teacher-forcing lower-triangular block with an appended bidirectional block, paired with a matching causal cross-attention mask (teacher-forcing branch attends to background + causal prompt history, bidirectional branch attends to a global prompt). (3) Post-training distillation: consistency distillation (Eq. 3) collapses the multi-step teacher into a few-step consistency-model student by enforcing local consistency between adjacent points on the same teacher PF-ODE trajectory, then DMD (Eq. 4) further refines the student by matching a KL-divergence gradient between real and student score estimates, applied over long self-rollout trajectories to reduce accumulated drift under deployment-time dynamics. Deployment adds a director-pilot agentic harness: a VLM "Director" analyzes scene state and proposes discrete event cards (Mode A: direct semantic interaction with no object masks; Mode B: SAM-based object tracking with per-object action proposals), and a "Pilot" (the video generator) renders these into physically consistent rollouts. Three design decisions stand out: the MoBA mask is justified empirically (ablation-free but motivated by observed overfitting) but no controlled ablation isolating its individual contribution to drift reduction is presented in the visible text; the choice to apply DMD over self-rollout rather than teacher-forced states is well-justified as addressing train/inference mismatch, consistent with known exposure-bias literature; the dynamic KV-cache scheduling that discards low-relevance history is described only qualitatively, with no quantitative characterization of the accuracy/efficiency trade-off it introduces. Key Results: The paper reports a distilled model that renders 720p video at 60fps with an hour-long uninterrupted rollout (Fig. 10) showing no perceptible quality decay across 20 sampled scenarios, and claims it is the only system in its comparison table (Table 1, against Matrix-Game 3.0, DreamWorld, LingBot-World 1.0, HappyOyster, Genie 3) to combine hour-level ("infinite") generation duration, "infinite" semantic interaction, general domain, high dynamic degree, real-time performance, and full open-source release. Qualitative comparisons (Fig. 11, 12) show the causal pretrained backbone retaining sharp textures and stable geometry over 60-second rollouts where baselines (HY-World 1.5, SANA-WM, Matrix-Game 3.0, DreamWorld) visibly degrade into noise or color drift by 15-45 seconds. No quantitative benchmark tables (FVD, FID, CLIP-similarity, human preference scores, or latency/throughput numbers) appear in the visible text; all comparative evidence is qualitative frame grids and prose claims of matching or exceeding closed-source visual quality. Model sizes are named (14B primary, 1.3B lightweight variant for single-GPU deployment) but no benchmark accuracy/quality numbers are attached to either. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (MoBA attention mask combining teacher-forcing and bidirectional attention for drift-resistant causal video generation; DMD applied over self-rollout trajectories rather than teacher-forced states) and NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the director-pilot agentic harness for world modeling, plus the full open-sourced model, data engine, and interactive UI). The genuinely novel piece is applying a bidirectional-regularization mask inside a causal-attention training scheme specifically to counter context-length-driven overfitting in video world models, and extending DMD's error-correction rationale to long self-rollout trajectories at deployment; the data pipeline, camera control via Plücker embeddings, and consistency distillation are standard techniques from prior video generation and world-model literature (cited directly) applied and combined at larger production scale. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation table isolating the individual contribution of the MoBA mask, the DMD-over-self-rollout choice, or the KV-cache scheduling mechanism appears in the visible sections; the argument for their necessity is motivated narratively ("we find that... resulting in overfitting") rather than shown via a controlled comparison with numbers. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere. The long-horizon stability claim rests on one hour-long qualitative session rather than repeated runs or a quantitative drift metric over time. Model checkpoints, code, and a website are released (URLs given: technology.robbyant.com/lingbot-world-v2, github.com/robbyant/lingbot-world-v2, huggingface.co/robbyant/lingbot-world-v2), which is a genuine reproducibility positive, though the evaluation numbers needed to independently verify the comparative claims are not included in the visible portion of the paper. Comparisons against closed-source systems (HappyOyster, Genie 3) necessarily rely on those systems' outputs as observed by the authors, which is a standard but real limitation given no shared benchmark protocol is described. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built, open-sourced tool evaluated primarily by deployment characteristics). Practical: Yes for the 1.3B variant, explicitly designed for effortless single-GPU deployment; the 14B primary model's hardware requirements are not stated. Efficient: The paper describes compiler optimization, efficient attention kernels, hybrid parallel inference, asynchronous VAE decoding, and dynamic KV-cache scheduling to hit real-time throughput, but reports no absolute compute/memory numbers (GPU-hours, VRAM, cost per generated minute) relative to alternatives. Effective: The one-hour no-decay session and side-by-side frame comparisons support the stated problem (drift-free long-horizon generation) qualitatively; no quantitative metric confirms it beyond visual inspection, so this is a proxy demonstration rather than a measured one. Scalable: The paper tests one hour of continuous generation as its stress test; behavior at 10x-100x that horizon (10-100 hours) is neither tested nor claimed, and the authors themselves flag long-term memory as an unsolved problem (regions leaving the context window are regenerated, not recalled), which is a direct admission that identity persistence does not scale with horizon length under the current design. Strengths: - The MoBA attention mask is a specific, mechanistically-motivated architectural fix (not a generic "more data/more compute" claim) targeting a named failure mode (overfitting to conditioning context in teacher-forced causal video training). - Applying DMD distillation over self-rollout trajectories rather than only teacher-forced states directly targets exposure bias, a well-known but often-unaddressed problem in autoregressive generation. - Full open release of code, weights, and a working interactive UI (Fig. 9) for a production-scale (14B parameter) interactive world model is a genuine execution strength, rare among comparably capable closed systems (Genie 3, HappyOyster) per the authors' own comparison table. - The director-pilot harness operationalizes a real architectural idea (decoupling causal/semantic reasoning from low-level visual dynamics generation, mirroring agentic coding harnesses) rather than being a thin wrapper around the base model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state three limitations directly and specifically: (1) long-term memory is absent, meaning revisited regions are regenerated rather than recalled, so the world is persistent in appearance but not identity, an inherent tension since memory cost grows with horizon under a bounded budget; (2) identity and style consistency can subtly drift for specific characters and overall art style even when the scene as a whole stays coherent; (3) physical understanding is imperfect because the model learns dynamics purely from pixels without explicit geometry or collision modeling, producing implausible artifacts like objects intersecting rather than colliding; and (4) despite running in real time, the distilled model still requires substantial compute, and reaching real-time high-fidelity world modeling on commodity hardware needs further efficiency gains. This is a direct, unhedged limitations section that confronts structural weaknesses rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No quantitative evaluation metrics (FVD/FID, human preference study, latency/throughput tables, drift-over-time curves) appear anywhere in the visible text; every comparative claim against baselines is supported by side-by-side qualitative frame grids only, which is a significant gap for claims this specific ("matches or exceeds the visual quality of the strongest closed-source systems"). The paper does not report an ablation isolating the MoBA mask's contribution versus a pure teacher-forcing baseline, despite this being the paper's central architectural claim. The one-hour stress test is a single session; repeatability (multiple hour-long runs, or runs seeded differently) is not reported, so the strength of the durability claim rests on N=1 rather than a distribution of outcomes. The comparison table (Table 1) is authored by the paper itself and rates competitor systems (including a same-lab prior version, LingBot-World 1.0) on qualitative categorical bins ("Medium"/"High" dynamic degree, "None"/"Few"/"Infinite" semantic interaction) without a defined measurement protocol, which risks favorable self-scoring. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating interactive world models gains a concrete, mechanistically-motivated technique (MoBA attention regularization plus self-rollout DMD) and a fully open, production-scale reference system, but should treat the durability and quality-parity claims as qualitatively demonstrated rather than quantitatively established until the authors or independent replicators supply drift metrics and head-to-head numeric benchmarks.
Recommended 2607.07534 PDF
#91 — Moderately Innovative (58)

OpenCoF: Learning to Reason Through Video Generation (6 ▲ )

Xinyan Chen, Ziyu Guo, Renrui Zhang, Dongzhi Jiang, Hongsheng Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenCoF, a framework comprising the OpenCoF-17K reasoning-video dataset (17,312 videos, 11 task families) and Wan-CoF, a LoRA fine-tune of Wan2.2-I2V-A14B, plus two reasoning-token designs (visual and textual) for Chain-of-Frame reasoning in video generators. - Why it's important: Visual chain-of-thought pipelines are stuck reasoning over static images or external tools; video generation offers a native way to reason through temporally connected frames, but no dataset or architecture exists to train this capability directly. - How it does it: Diverse temporal supervision from four curation pipelines (instance-based rendering, expert-guided rendering, procedural scene synthesis, external video repurposing) combined with learnable reasoning tokens inserted either into the visual latent sequence (self-attention) or the text-conditioning sequence (cross-attention). - The good: Wan-CoF shows consistent gains over the Wan2.2-I2V-A14B baseline across all four independent, externally curated benchmarks (MME-CoF +0.30, Gen-ViRe +0.087, VIPER POC +4.2, RULER-Bench +1.0), with the largest jumps on reasoning sub-dimensions rather than visual-quality metrics, and the per-category transfer pattern plausibly maps to the training data's task composition. - The missing: No ablation isolates which of the 11 OpenCoF-17K task families or which curation pipeline drives the external-benchmark gains, so the "diverse supervision generalizes" claim rests on correlation across four benchmarks rather than a controlled leave-one-task-out test. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating video-generation-as-reasoning systems; the dataset and reasoning-token ablations are useful data points, but the absolute performance numbers (e.g., VIPER POC of 7.5-8.8 out of presumably 100) remain far below closed-source frontier models, so this is an incremental open-source contribution rather than a new capability class.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (62) and Impact (62) are the strongest dimensions: evaluating exclusively on four external, independently curated benchmarks (MME-CoF, Gen-ViRe, VIPER, RULER-Bench) that the training data was not designed for is a real Goodhart safeguard, and dataset/model/code are open-sourced. Non-Obviousness (52) is low because the visual-vs-textual reasoning-token split is a straightforward application of learnable prepended tokens to a video DiT rather than a new mechanism. Completeness (58) is limited by the absence of any leave-one-task-out ablation, so the central data-centric claim ('diverse supervision generalizes') rests on cross-benchmark correlation rather than controlled causal evidence, and several reported deltas (Gen-ViRe Spatio-Temporal at -0.001) are indistinguishable from noise with no seeds or error bars. Weighted: 0.20*58 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*52 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*62 = 58.
empirical Novelty 58 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 52 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language reasoning pipelines (visual Chain-of-Thought) are largely anchored to static visual observations, extracting localized evidence, invoking external tools, or generating auxiliary images. This limits their ability to model dynamic transitions and multi-step visual consequences (e.g., a chess move, a folding sequence, a physics trajectory). Video generation models offer a distinct reasoning path, termed Chain-of-Frame (CoF) reasoning, where logical/physical consequences unfold across temporally connected frames. However, existing video generators are trained on general video corpora without dedicated supervision or architectural support for this kind of stepwise reasoning, and prior domain-specific efforts (VR-Bench for mazes, Thinking in Frames, VBVR) are narrow and internally coupled to their own evaluation suites, leaving generalization to independent benchmarks untested. Key Idea: There are two distinct ideas bundled together. First, a data-centric claim: diverse temporal supervision alone (without any architectural reasoning mechanism) can elicit CoF behavior that transfers to unseen benchmarks. Second, an architectural idea: reasoning state in a DiT video generator can be hosted in two complementary locations, learnable tokens prepended to the visual latent sequence (captures low-level, spatially-grounded visual cues via bidirectional self-attention) versus learnable tokens prepended to the text-conditioning sequence (captures high-level, prompt-independent semantic priors via unidirectional cross-attention). The paper's contribution is demonstrating and characterizing this dichotomy, not inventing a fundamentally new attention mechanism. Methodology: The OpenCoF-17K dataset is built via four pipelines: (1) instance-based rendering — sampling pre-existing structured assets (chess puzzles from a Kaggle mate-in-one dataset, Sudoku-Easy) and deterministically rendering them to video via code; (2) expert-guided rendering — using human annotators, LLM-generated concepts, or T2I model outputs to define intermediate structures (2D geometry from Geo170K, dot-to-dot from LLM-generated categories + T2I + edge detection), then code-rendering sequences; (3) procedural scene synthesis — physics engines/graphics pipelines instantiating scenes from random seeds (tangram, cube folding, 3D polycube rotation, physics motion); (4) external video repurposing — sampling and standardizing existing datasets (maze from VR-Bench, VBVR subset of 30 subtasks, embodied manipulation from BridgeData V2). All videos are standardized to 480p, 15 fps, 81 frames. Training: Wan2.2-I2V-A14B (a Mixture-of-Experts DiT with separate high-noise/low-noise experts, umT5 text encoder, Wan-VAE) is fine-tuned with LoRA (rank 32, targeting q/k/v/o projections and two MLP layers per DiT block, lr 2e-5), keeping the text encoder and VAE frozen, to produce Wan-CoF. For the reasoning-token exploration, Visual Reasoning Tokens (vt, N_r=16) are prepended to the visual token sequence and interact with visual tokens via self-attention, discarded before final readout; Textual Reasoning Tokens (tt, N_t=16) are prepended to the text-conditioning sequence and act only as key/value context in cross-attention, never refreshed by the visual stream. Wan-CoF trains 2 epochs; Wan-CoF_vt and Wan-CoF_tt train 5 epochs. Design decisions: (1) using LoRA rather than full fine-tuning is well-justified for attributing gains cleanly to data versus architecture with a fixed compute budget, though it likely caps the achievable improvement ceiling; (2) keeping vt and tt separate rather than combining them is explicitly flagged as unexplored, which limits the paper's ability to claim an optimal reasoning-token design; (3) the choice of N_r=N_t=16 is validated by one ablation (n=32) that shows no consistent improvement, a reasonably rigorous check for a single hyperparameter. Key Results: Across four external, independently curated benchmarks not seen during training: MME-CoF (0-4 scale) overall improves from 1.00 (baseline) to 1.30 (Wan-CoF), reaching 1.34-1.35 with reasoning tokens, versus 1.80 for the closed-source Sora-2 and 1.53 for Veo-3.0-Preview. Gen-ViRe (0-1 scale) improves from 0.304 to 0.391 (Wan-CoF), up to 0.441 (Wan-CoF_vt), versus 0.560 for Sora-2. VIPER (POC@1.0) improves from 3.3 to 7.5 (Wan-CoF), up to 8.8 (Wan-CoF_tt), versus 23.3 for Sora-2 and 20.3 for Veo-3.1 — still far below closed-source and comparable to open-source Hunyuan-1.5 (8.1). RULER-Bench (0-100) improves from 55.8 to 56.8 (Wan-CoF), up to 59.6 (Wan-CoF_vt). The largest gains cluster on reasoning-oriented sub-dimensions (Temporal Consistency, Physics, Spatial, Perceptual, Algorithmic & Logical) rather than visual-fidelity metrics, which the authors use to argue the training strengthens reasoning rather than just visual quality — a reasonable but not definitively causal inference. vt and tt specialize: vt wins on Planning/Spatial/global-plan-persistence tasks, tt wins on Instruction Alignment/Structural tasks, consistent with their architectural roles. Baselines compared span closed-source frontier (Kling-v1, Seedance, Veo-3, Sora-2) and open-source (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B, HunyuanVideo-I2V/1.5) systems, which is a reasonably current and strong comparison set as of the paper's writing. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (OpenCoF-17K, a 17K-video, 11-task-family CoF training corpus with a four-pipeline curation protocol) and ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION/NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (LoRA fine-tuning plus two reasoning-token designs, evaluated via performance and attention analysis). The visual/textual reasoning-token split is a straightforward application of existing ideas (learnable prepended tokens, self-attention vs. cross-attention) to the video-DiT setting rather than a new mechanism; the genuine novelty is the systematic characterization of where and when these two token types operate (depth, denoising step, space, time) via attention analysis, and the demonstration that pure data-centric diverse supervision transfers across independently curated benchmarks — a claim not established by prior domain-specific efforts (VR-Bench, Thinking in Frames, VBVR). Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports one number per benchmark/model configuration, no error bars, no multiple seeds, and no significance testing across the four benchmark tables — a notable gap for empirical claims stated as improvements of a few tenths of a point (e.g., Gen-ViRe RULER Spatio-Temporal shows a -0.001 delta, within likely noise, which the authors acknowledge). Ablations exist for the reasoning-token count (n=16 vs 32, Table 6) and are used to justify the default setting, a genuine ablation. No ablation isolates which of the four curation pipelines or which of the 11 task families drives external-benchmark transfer, despite this being the paper's central data-centric claim. Evaluation is on four external, independently curated benchmarks (MME-CoF, Gen-ViRe, VIPER, RULER-Bench) rather than the authors' own training distribution, which is a genuine strength against Goodhart's Law concerns — this is explicitly the paper's stated advantage over prior domain-coupled work. Dataset, model, and code are stated as open-sourced with a project page (https://opencof.github.io/), though exact release completeness (weights, full training scripts) is not verifiable from the text alone. Every benchmark is evaluated with its official judge model per the appendix, which is good practice, but "official judge" protocols (especially LLM-based judges) themselves carry unquantified variance not addressed here. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (the paper's primary claims rest on running a fine-tuned model against benchmarks). Practical: The model builds on an open 14B-parameter backbone (Wan2.2-I2V-A14B) fine-tuned via LoRA, which is deployable on standard GPU inference/training setups without proprietary infrastructure, though a 14B video DiT still requires substantial VRAM. Efficient: LoRA fine-tuning (rank 32, targeting a subset of projections) keeps training cost low relative to full fine-tuning, but the paper does not report absolute training compute (GPU-hours), inference latency, or memory overhead of the added reasoning tokens versus the baseline. Effective: Evidence shows headline-metric gains on four independent benchmarks, addressing the stated problem of CoF reasoning transfer, but absolute scores (e.g., VIPER POC 7.5-8.8) remain far below closed-source systems (20-23), meaning the method narrows but does not close the gap. Scalable: Not tested. The dataset is fixed at 17,312 videos and training data was not scaled up or down to test whether gains saturate or continue; no claim is made about 10x-100x dataset or model scale. Strengths: - Evaluating exclusively on external, independently curated benchmarks (four suites the training data was not designed for) is a genuine methodological strength that directly addresses a limitation the authors identify in prior domain-specific reasoning-video work. - The attention analysis (Figures 8-9) decomposing vt/tt behavior across DiT depth, denoising step, spatial position, and temporal position is a substantive mechanistic investigation beyond a black-box performance comparison, and produces a coherent, falsifiable story (vt = spatially-grounded bidirectional low-level cues that peak early/late in the temporal sequence; tt = steadier prompt-level semantic prior). - The four-pipeline dataset curation strategy (instance-based, expert-guided, procedural, repurposed) is more diverse than prior single-source approaches (VR-Bench, VBVR rely predominantly on procedural synthesis), giving the resulting corpus broader task coverage (11 families) at a moderate scale (17K videos). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state one limitation: vt and tt are investigated separately, and how to effectively combine them remains future work. This is a direct, non-minimized acknowledgment, though it is also the paper's single stated limitation, which likely understates the full scope of open questions. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No ablation isolates the contribution of individual OpenCoF-17K task families or curation pipelines to the observed cross-benchmark transfer; the correlational argument in the Out-of-Distribution Analysis section (e.g., "training on Physics motion transfers to VIPER Physics") is suggestive but not causally tested via held-out task removal. - No error bars, seeds, or statistical significance are reported anywhere, despite several reported deltas being small (e.g., Gen-ViRe Spatio-Temporal -0.001, RULER-Bench VC deltas near zero), making it hard to distinguish genuine effects from evaluation noise. - The reasoning-token designs add trainable parameters and prepended tokens per forward pass; the paper reports no inference-time latency or memory overhead versus the Wan-CoF data-only baseline, which matters for practical adoption. - The paper does not report how OpenCoF-17K's own held-out test performance compares to external-benchmark performance, so it is not possible to assess overfitting to the training data itself independent of the transfer story. - The comparison to closed-source frontier models (Sora-2, Veo-3.1) is framed generously ("approaching," "moving closer to") but the absolute gaps remain large (e.g., 7.5-8.8 vs 20.3-23.3 POC on VIPER, roughly 3x), and this magnitude is somewhat underweighted in the prose relative to the tables. Bottom Line: A useful, if incremental, open-source contribution: the OpenCoF-17K dataset and the vt/tt attention analysis are genuinely useful artifacts for researchers building CoF-reasoning video systems, but the absence of task-level ablations and statistical rigor means the central transfer claim is better supported by pattern-consistency than by controlled causal evidence, and the reported improvements still leave a roughly 3x gap to closed-source frontier models on the hardest benchmark (VIPER).
Recommended 2607.08763 PDF
#92 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Is Your NPU Ready for LLMs? Dissecting the Hidden Efficiency Bottlenecks in Mobile LLM Inference

G Cai, R Tian, L Yang, Z Ren, J Yuan, L Li, J Wang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents the first cross-layer measurement study of mobile LLM inference spanning five frameworks (llama.cpp, MNN, MLC-LLM, MLLM, GENIE), three hardware backends (CPU, GPU, NPU), and resource scheduling, built on a custom energy-profiling tool called PowerBench. - Why it's important: Mobile NPUs are marketed as the energy-efficient path for on-device LLMs, but the paper shows measured NPU decode energy is 2-4x higher than CPU/GPU alternatives, meaning current deployment defaults waste substantial battery on the exact hardware meant to save it. - How it does it: PowerBench attributes per-compute-unit energy (CPU/GPU/NPU rails via Qualcomm Power Telemetry) at operator granularity across 400+ configurations, isolating framework offloading strategy, operator scaling, activation quantization, and scheduling parameters as root causes of performance gaps. - The good: The operator-level microbenchmark (Figure 3) isolates that QNN's MatMul reduces compute cycles by 26.9x-30.7x over llama.cpp's implementation at N=256-1024, directly explaining a 15x prefill throughput gap between GENIE and llama.cpp on identical hardware and model. - The missing: All energy and throughput results are averaged over at least three trials with no reported variance, confidence intervals, or statistical significance testing, so the magnitude of measurement noise behind claims like "up to 54.8% energy reduction" is unstated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone deploying LLMs on Qualcomm mobile NPUs (systems/mobile-ML engineers), since it identifies concrete, actionable scheduling knobs (RPC polling interval, NPU sleep latency, thread-core affinity, DVFS); less relevant to those working purely on model-level (non-systems) LLM research.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (55) is the main drag: no error bars, standard deviations, or variance anywhere despite power telemetry being known-noisy across runs, and the headline 53-55% end-to-end energy savings are estimated by composing phase-level measurements rather than measured on actual pipeline runs. Resilience (50) is low because all findings come from a single vendor's stack (Qualcomm QNN), so 'mobile NPU' conclusions are evidence for one NPU architecture only. Non-Obviousness (65) credits the phase-split insight (NPU wins prefill, loses decode) and the finding that the host CPU consumes up to 30% of 'NPU-accelerated' inference energy, which whole-device measurement cannot see. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*65 = 58.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLMs on smartphones is attractive for privacy, latency, and offline use, but mobile SoCs expose three heterogeneous compute backends (CPU, GPU, NPU) and at least five actively used inference frameworks, and practitioners currently have no unified view of which framework-backend combination is efficient for a given inference phase. Prior measurement studies (MELTing Point, MobileAIBench, and others cited in Table 1) examine only CPU/GPU or a narrow subset of frameworks, and none jointly measure NPU execution, backend-specific energy at compute-unit granularity, and resource scheduling (DVFS, thread affinity, RPC polling) together. This leaves engineers guessing about NPU deployment despite NPUs being pushed as the primary target for on-device LLM acceleration. Key Idea: The paper's organizing insight is that mobile LLM inference exhibits a "phase split": NPUs win prefill (compute-bound, large token-parallel GEMM, where NPU operator implementations scale far better than CPU/GPU) but lose decode (memory-bound, single-token GEMV, where the NPU's preference for large fixed-shape static graphs mismatches the small, dynamic per-token kernel and incurs invocation overhead). This reframes "NPU vs CPU vs GPU" from a single best-backend question into a phase-aware backend-selection problem, and further shows that even when an accelerator does the compute, the host CPU's RPC polling and synchronization overhead (up to 30% of total system energy) is itself a controllable second-order bottleneck. Methodology: The pipeline has three layers: (1) PowerBench, a header-only C++ instrumentation library on Qualcomm Power Telemetry (QPT) that reads PMIC power-zone counters per compute unit (CPU/GPU/NPU/SoC total) via start()/stop() deltas, integrated into each framework's benchmark harness; (2) a standardized throughput harness per framework that controls prompt injection and end-of-sequence token replacement so prefill/decode are measured under consistent token lengths; (3) a benchmark spanning four Qualcomm devices (Snapdragon SM8650/SM8750/SM8850 across 2023-2025), five frameworks, four model sizes (1B-7B, 4-bit weight quantization, w4a16 where noted), and >400 configurations, plus isolated operator microbenchmarks (attention, FFN, prefill GEMM, decode GEMV) at matched tensor shapes to attribute framework gaps to specific operators. Three design decisions stand out: using per-compute-unit power-zone attribution rather than whole-device energy is the paper's core methodological advance and is well-justified by the finding that CPU accounts for up to 30% of "NPU-accelerated" inference energy, invisible to device-level measurement; standardizing token injection across frameworks with disparate APIs is necessary for a fair comparison but relies on the assumption that this harness doesn't itself perturb each framework's native scheduling (not tested); and sweeping scheduling parameters (RPC polling 5-65535us, NPU sleep latency 5-65535us, thread-core affinity, DVFS) one-at-a-time rather than jointly, which the paper itself flags as leaving "holistic" joint tuning to future work. Key Results: On OnePlus 15 with Qwen2.5-1.5B, GENIE reaches 1463.7 tokens/s NPU prefill vs. llama.cpp's 115.1 tokens/s (12.7x) and MNN's 700.9 tokens/s, while decode throughputs converge (~23 tokens/s) across the same frameworks. Decode throughput is dominated by context size, not generation length: for Llama-3.2-1B it drops from 61.6 to 47.0 tokens/s (23.8%) as context grows 16->4096 tokens at fixed output length. CPU decode (55.7-76.1 tokens/s across models/devices) generally beats or matches GPU and exceeds NPU. Activation quantization (W4A16 vs W4) gives 2.16x-2.63x prefill/decode throughput gains and 53.5%-66.9% energy reduction on Llama models but increases WikiText-2 perplexity by only 1.04% on Llama-3.2-3B versus 30.36% on Llama-3.2-1B, showing strong model-size dependence in the accuracy cost. Scheduling tuning: RPC polling interval reduction (default 9999us to 5-20us) cuts energy per token 30.9%-37.8% with throughput change of only -7.2% to +0.1%; NPU sleep latency retuning (default 40us to 1280-65535us) cuts energy 44.6%-50.9%; thread-core affinity misconfiguration causes up to 35% throughput variance; fine-grained DVFS recovers ~50% of wasted energy on certain backends. The paper's proposed best-practice NPU configuration (full-graph QNN offload + tuned RPC polling/CPU frequency/NPU sleep latency) is estimated (not directly measured end-to-end on real workloads beyond the reported datasets) to cut prefill energy 53.6% and decode energy 54.8% on MathQA, RoleBench, and LongBench, though MathQA/RoleBench latency simultaneously rises 15.1%-15.5%. Baselines are the frameworks' own default settings rather than external prior-art configurations, since this is a measurement study rather than a method proposing to beat SOTA. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contributions are (a) the empirical "phase split" and framework-gap-amplification-on-NPU findings, which required backend- and operator-level granularity that Table 1 shows no prior work provides, and (b) PowerBench itself as a reusable fine-grained energy-attribution tool. The five frameworks, three backends, and scheduling knobs (DVFS, thread affinity, RPC polling) are all pre-existing artifacts; the paper's contribution is measuring and cross-referencing them systematically, not inventing new backends or scheduling algorithms. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports means across "at least three recorded trials" after a warm-up run, with device thermal conditioning (<28C) and airplane-mode isolation to control for confounds, which is a defensible protocol for hardware measurement. However, no error bars, standard deviations, or seed-level variance are reported anywhere in the main tables or figures (Tables 5, 9; Figures 3-13), so readers cannot distinguish a genuine 0.8%-16.2% throughput change from measurement noise. There is no public code/data/PowerBench release URL stated in the visible text. Evaluation is entirely on the authors' own benchmark harness across models/devices they selected (not an external community benchmark), which is appropriate for a measurement study but means comparisons are self-contained. Table 8's end-to-end dataset estimates ($MathQA$, RoleBench, LongBench) are described as "estimated," not directly measured pipeline runs, which is a meaningful rigor caveat given the magnitude of the headline 53-55% energy claims. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurement study). Practical: Yes, all four test devices (Xiaomi 17/14, OnePlus 15, plus SM8750/SM8850/SM8650 SoCs) are commercially available flagship phones, and PowerBench is a lightweight instrumentation library requiring only vendor power-telemetry sysfs access. Efficient: The paper reports the profiling tool itself as "lightweight" but does not quantify PowerBench's own instrumentation overhead (CPU cycles or energy added by measurement) anywhere in the visible text. Effective: Yes for the stated goal (measuring where energy and throughput go); the proposed best-practice configuration is validated only on 3 datasets with estimated rather than directly-measured end-to-end deployment. Scalable: Not tested beyond 8192-token context (Figure 8) and four device SoCs from a single vendor (Qualcomm); the paper does not test or claim behavior on non-Qualcomm NPUs (e.g., Apple, MediaTek, despite listing their peak TOPS in Table 2), so cross-vendor generalization is unevaluated. Strengths: - The per-compute-unit energy attribution (PowerBench) is a genuine methodological advance over prior whole-device power measurement (Android batterystats, external power monitors) cited as the state of practice in Table 1, and it is what makes the "CPU accounts for 30% of NPU-inference energy" finding visible at all. - The matmul-cycle microbenchmark (Figure 3) isolating operator-level scaling (26.9x-30.7x QNN advantage at large N, parity at small N) is a well-controlled causal experiment that cleanly explains the observed prefill/decode framework-gap asymmetry rather than just reporting the gap. - The scheduling sweeps (RPC polling, NPU sleep latency, thread-core affinity, DVFS) each isolate one knob at commercially meaningful granularity and report consistent, large effect sizes (30-50% energy reductions) across multiple models and devices, giving practitioners concrete, actionable tuning targets. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly flags that its scheduling parameters were swept largely one-at-a-time rather than jointly optimized ("future work may jointly tune NPU frequency with workload features"), and separately proposes "holistic resource scheduling" and "multi-graph switching on NPU" as unsolved future directions rather than claiming its best-practice configuration is globally optimal. It also states the LongBench dataset shows a smaller energy benefit (0.8% decrease vs. 53-55% for MathQA/RoleBench) because it is more prefill-dominated, directly confronting a case where its proposed configuration underperforms rather than hiding it. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All results are confined to Qualcomm SoCs and the Qualcomm QNN NPU stack; despite Table 2 listing MediaTek and Apple NPU peak TOPS, no MediaTek/Apple/Samsung NPU is measured, so claims about "mobile NPU" behavior generally are evidence only for one vendor's NPU architecture. - The estimated end-to-end dataset impact (Table 8) is derived from combining phase-level measurements rather than running the actual best-practice configuration through the full MathQA/RoleBench/LongBench pipelines, which is a weaker form of evidence than the paper's headline framing ("we estimate... could reduce energy consumption by up to 54.8%") suggests at first read. - No error bars or variance reporting anywhere means several finer-grained claims (e.g., "throughput change of only -7.2% to +0.1%") cannot be checked against measurement noise, which matters more for a hardware-measurement paper than for typical ML-accuracy papers since power telemetry is known to be noisy across runs. - Model coverage stops at 7B parameters (Qwen2.5-7B) with no test of larger on-device-relevant models (e.g., 8B-13B class), so the paper's phase-split conclusions are not verified to hold as models scale toward the upper end of what mobile NPUs are being marketed for. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or configuring an NPU-accelerated mobile LLM inference stack on Qualcomm hardware gains directly actionable findings (RPC polling interval, NPU sleep latency, thread affinity, DVFS settings) not available in prior device-level measurement work, but should treat the headline 53-55% end-to-end energy claims as estimated rather than directly measured, and should not generalize the NPU-specific findings beyond the Qualcomm QNN stack this paper tests.

#93 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Gemma 4 Technical Report (21 ▲ )

Gemma Team, Sherif El Abd, Vaibhav Aggarwal, Robin Algayres, Alek Andreev, Olivier Bachem, Ian Ballantyne, Cormac Brick, Victor Cărbune, Michelle Casbon, Mayank Chaturvedi, Victor Cotruta, Alice Coucke, Phil Culliton, Robert Dadashi, Lucas Dixon, Mohamed Elhawaty, Utku Evci, Clément Farabet, Johan Ferret, Filippo Galgani, Sertan Girgin, Jean-Bastien Grill, Maarten Grootendorst, Jiaxian Guo, Cassidy Hardin, Yanzhang He, Steven M.
