Hernandez, Omri Homburger, Léonard Hussenot, Juyeong Ji, Armand Joulin, Aishwarya Kamath, Parnian Kassraie, Olivier Lacombe, Preethi Lahoti, Gaël Liu, Gus Martins, Luciano Martins, Tatiana Matejovicova, Ramona Merhej, Nikola Momchev, Sneha Mondal, Ryan Mullins, Sindhu Raghuram Panyam, Shreya Pathak, Sarah Perrin, André Susano Pinto, Etienne Pot, Angéline Pouget, Alexandre Ramé, Sabela Ramos, Douglas Reid, David Rim, Morgane Rivière, Karsten Roth, Louis Rouillard, Omar Sanseviero, Pier Giuseppe Sessa, Shane Settle, Danila Sinopalnikov, Sara Smoot, Piotr Stanczyk, Andreas Steiner, Lawrence Stewart, Ilya Tolstikhin, Michael Tschannen, Anton Tsitsulin, Nino Vieillard, Renjie Wu, Pingmei Xu, Haichuan Yang, Edouard Yvinec, Li Zhang, Joe Zou, Nicolas Aagnes, Abdelrahman Abdelhamed, Shivani Agrawal, Shubham Agrawal, Ibrahim Alabdulmohsin, Jean Baptiste Alayrac, Uri Alon, Chandramouli Amarnath, Ankesh Anand, Chrysovalantis Anastasiou, Setareh Ariafar, François-Xavier Aubet, Kyriakos Axiotis, Federico Barbero, Joelle Barral, Alexei Bendebury, Urs Bergmann, Stanley Bileschi, Kat Black, Mathieu Blondel, Sebastian Borgeaud, Arthur Bražinskas, Ryan Burnell, Robert Busa-Fekete, Mu Cai, Glenn Cameron, Charlotte Caucheteux, Garima Chadha, Jetha Chan, Aditya Chawla, Blake Jianhang Chen, Jesse Chen, Lin Chen, Xu Chen, Derek Cheng, Tzu-hsiang Chien, Nikolai Chinaev, Yi Chou, Zhaohui Chu, Benjamin Coleman, Pooja Consul, Sam Conway-Rahman, Scott Crowell, Dylan Cutler, Vivek Dani, Samira Daruki, Anil Das, Daniel Deutsch, Nishanth Dikkala, Li Ding, Qiuhan Ding, Shenil Dodhia, Konstantin Donhauser, Tulsee Doshi, Anca Dragan, Alex Druinsky, Sahil Dua, Zoltan Egyed, Danielle Eisenbud, Daniel Eppens, Cindy Fan, Bahare Fatemi, Yassir Fathullah, Vlad Feinberg, Milen Ferev, Takumi Fujimoto, Isaac Galatzer-Levy, João Gante, Simon Geisler, Soham Ghosal, Antonious M. Girgis, Alec Go, Alhaad Gokhale, Alex Grills, Yiming Gu, Pramod Gupta, Guru Guruganesh, Raia Hadsell, Hamza Harkous, Jitendra Harlalka, Demis Hassabis, Anja Hauth, Joe Heyward, Arian Hosseini, Chih-Yang Hsia, I-Hung Hsu, Xiaopeng Huang, Yangsibo Huang, Kevin Hui, Adrian Hutter, Te I, Fotis Iliopoulos, Advait Jain, Ganesh Jawahar, Ziwei Ji, Qilin Jin, Melvin Johnson, Kandarp Joshi, Arun Kandoor, Wang-Cheng Kang, Koray Kavukcuoglu, Mehran Kazemi, Kathleen Kenealy, Amr Khalifa, Phoebe Kirk, Suraj Kothawade, Vitaly Kovalev, Neel Kovelamudi, Adam Kraft, Ravin Kumar, Harish Kuppam, Justin Lannin, Chen-Yu Lee, Seungji Lee, Dmitry Lepikhin, Dongdong Li, Qiujia Li, Valentin Liévin, Ethan Lin, Ziqian Lin, Casper Liu, Tianlin Liu, Tianqi Liu, Xin Liu, Mayank Lunayach, Min Ma, Gagan Madan, Andrii Maksai, Eric Malmi, Michal Matuszak, Daniel McDuff, Gaurav Menghani, Daniil Mirylenka, Karolis Misiunas, Vedant Misra, Andreea Mitran, Kareem Mohamed, Maksim Mukha, Eric Noland, James O'Donnell, Kate Olszewska, Bernett Orlando, Wanqiong Pan, Rina Panigrahy, Unnati Parekh, Chunjong Park, Eric Paskie, Liqian Peng, Bryce Petrini, Slav Petrov, Jonas Pfeiffer, Bilal Piot, Martyna Plomecka, Siim Poder, Octavio Ponce, Arijit Pramanik, David Racz, Anish Rajan, Michelle Ramanovich, Anand Rao, Marvin Ritter, Vitor Rodrigues, Evan Rosen, Mikołaj Rybiński, Noveen Sachdeva, Michaël E. Sander, Rohit Sathyanarayana, Sagar Savla, Samuel Schmidgall, Tal Schuster, Benoit Seguin, Andrew Sellergren, Aliaksei Severyn, Izhak Shafran, Dhruv Shah, Yuan Shangguan, Ashish Shenoy, Pradeep Shenoy, Rakesh Shivanna, Pauline Sho, Lucas Spangher, Wojciech Stokowiec, Tim Strother, Yao Su, Yinghao Sun, Mukund Sundararajan, Andrea Tacchetti, Mor Hazan Taege, Pouya Tafti, Chetan Tekur, Rahul Thapa, Madeleine Traverse, Lenart Treven, Tao Tu, Chien Te Tung, Petar Veličković, Malini Pooni Venkat, Sagar Gubbi Venkatesh, Vidya Venkiteswaran, Francesco Visin, Alex Vitvitskyi, Kiran Vodrahalli, Weiyi Wang, Xin Wang, Tris Warkentin, Jan Wassenberg, John Wieting, Lechao Xiao, Hao Xu, Yuhui Xu, Fuzhao Xue, Arun Yadav, Jun Yan, Antoine Yang, Lin Yang, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Ziyu Ying, Jae Hyeon Yoo, Sajjad Zafar, Fred Zhang, Jiageng Zhang, Jianyi Zhang, Xiaofan Zhang, Chao Zhao, David Zhou, Chen Zou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jul 2026
TL;DR: - What it does: This paper introduces Gemma 4, an open-weight multimodal language model family (2.3B-31B dense, plus a 26B-A4B MoE) with a thinking mode, an encoder-free unified vision/audio architecture for the 12B model, and an autoregressive multi-token-prediction (MTP) drafter for speculative decoding. - Why it's important: Open-weight models that match much larger frontier systems on reasoning and multimodal benchmarks while running on commodity/on-device hardware lower the cost of deploying capable LLMs outside hyperscaler infrastructure. - How it does it: A 5:1 local-to-global attention ratio, p-RoPE, and KV-cache key/value sharing cut KV-cache memory by 37.5%, while a raw-patch/raw-audio encoder-free 12B variant and quantization-aware training (QAT) reduce parameter and activation memory further. - The good: Gemma 4 31B reaches Elo 1451 (95% CI ±8) on Arena Text, ranked above several much larger MoE models (e.g., Kimi K2.5 Thinking at 1T/32B active) despite being a 31B dense model. - The missing: There are no ablations isolating the contribution of individual architectural changes (p-RoPE, KV sharing, encoder-free design, MTP drafter) to the reported gains — all comparisons are end-to-end against prior Gemma generations or other vendors' models. - Read it or not: Worth a skim for practitioners deploying on-device multimodal LLMs who care about KV-cache and quantization engineering specifics; skip if looking for algorithmic novelty or theoretical contributions, since this is a systems/engineering report, not a new method paper.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (a built and released open-weight model family evaluated on deployment-relevant memory, latency, and capability benchmarks). Practical Impact (90) is by far the largest contributor and is what keeps the composite respectable: Apache 2.0 open weights across 2.3B-31B plus a 26B-A4B MoE, with QAT recipes and a 0.8GB on-device footprint, is an artifact practitioners can deploy today. Rigor (40) is the severe drag: zero ablations isolate p-RoPE, KV sharing, the encoder-free design, or the MTP drafter, so no per-change delta is attributable; all static-benchmark comparisons are self-reported against the organization's own prior generation, and no error bars are given outside Arena Elo. Novelty (40) and Non-Obviousness (30) reflect that the local/global ratio, p-RoPE, KV sharing, and QAT are refinements of Gemma 3/3n techniques, with only the encoder-free 12B raw-patch/raw-audio path being a real architectural departure. Weighted composite: 0.15*40 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*30 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*45 + 0.30*90 = 57.
systems Novelty 40 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 45 Resilience 45 Impact 90
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Developers building on-device or edge-deployed multimodal applications face a tension between model capability and hardware constraints: long-context KV cache grows memory footprint substantially, separate vision/audio encoders add memory fragmentation and parameter overhead, and autoregressive decoding is latency-bound by sequential token generation. The paper targets teams needing frontier-adjacent reasoning, vision, and audio understanding within a fixed on-device memory and compute budget (2.3B-31B parameter range), building on the Gemma 3/3n lineage. Key Idea: There isn't a single unifying algorithmic insight; the report bundles several independent engineering choices, the most distinctive being the encoder-free 12B architecture that projects raw 48x48 RGB patches and raw 40ms audio chunks directly into the LLM embedding space via lightweight projection matrices, eliminating the 550M vision and 305M audio encoders used in the other model sizes. This trades encoder capacity for reduced memory fragmentation and lets the LLM backbone absorb representation learning for both modalities jointly. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages: (1) pre-training on a large web/code/image/audio corpus (cutoff January 2025) using a decoder-only Transformer with pre/post-norm RMSNorm and QKNorm, a 5:1 (4:1 for the 2.3B model) local-to-global attention ratio, p-RoPE (p=0.25) on global layers and standard RoPE on local layers, and KV-cache key=value sharing on global layers for models above E4B; (2) instruction tuning that adds a "thinking mode" producing a reasoning trace before the response, using filtered post-training data optimized to reduce hallucination and unjustified refusals; (3) an autoregressive MTP drafter head (4-layer Transformer, cross-attending to the main model's KV cache) trained for speculative decoding, with a decoding-time optimization that replaces the E2B/E4B drafter's full-vocabulary projection (d x 262,000) with a top-k cluster projection (d x 4096). Design decisions: the encoder-free 12B design is well-justified by the reported 78% on-disk footprint reduction for the audio path (390MB to 87MB) and reasonable transcription/translation results, though it is applied to only one model size, leaving generalizability to other sizes untested; the p-RoPE + KV-sharing combination is justified by a stated 37.5% global KV-cache reduction but the interaction with p-RoPE's tightened frequency range (1M/10k) is not separately validated; QAT is applied to the LLM and both encoders in two bit-width recipes (mobile int2/int4/int8, and Q4_0), each reported with memory numbers but no accuracy-quality tradeoff at each precision level beyond a single "similar acceptance rate" claim for MTP. Key Results: Gemma 4 31B posts MMLU Pro 85.2, AIME 2026 (no tools) 89.2, LiveCodeBench v6 72.0-80.0 range, GPQA Diamond 84.3, and Big Bench Extra Hard 74.4, all substantial jumps over Gemma 3 27B (67.6, 20.8, 29.1, 42.4, 19.3 respectively). On Arena Text (human blind pairwise evals, as of 2026-06-19), Gemma 4 31B scores Elo 1451 (rank 43, above Kimi K2.5 Thinking's 1T/32B-active model at 1450) and 26B-A4B scores 1438 (rank 61); the top closed model (Fable 5) sits at 1508. Vision benchmarks show E4B (4.5B effective) matching or beating Gemma 3 27B on all evals despite ~6x fewer parameters (e.g., MMMU Pro 52.6 vs 49.7). Audio: Gemma 4 E4B improves CoVoST translation BLEU by 12% and FLEURS transcription WER by 12% relative to Gemma 3n E4B, while cutting the audio encoder's on-disk footprint 78% (390MB to 87MB). Long-context: GraphWalks F1 drops from 82.3 (31B) to 4.1 (E2B) at <128k context, showing severe degradation at the smallest model size. All numbers are self-reported by the developing organization with no independent replication, and comparisons rely on the same organization's own prior-generation baselines (Gemma 3/3n) plus a public leaderboard (Arena) for external comparison. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The genuinely new elements are the encoder-free unified multimodal architecture for the 12B model and the MTP drafter's clustered top-k projection; the local/global attention ratio, p-RoPE, KV-cache sharing, and QAT are incremental refinements of techniques already used in Gemma 3/3n and cited prior work (Shazeer 2019, Barbero et al. 2025, Jacob et al. 2018). Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolate individual architectural contributions (the paper reports end-to-end gains only, not per-change deltas). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported for any benchmark except Arena Elo scores, which do include 95% CIs (±4 to ±10). All static-benchmark comparisons are against the authors' own prior model (Gemma 3 27B) rather than a broad current external baseline set, and the paper is a preprint/technical report, not peer-reviewed. Weights are released under Apache 2.0 (stated but no repository URL given in the visible text), which does allow external verification of the reported numbers, a meaningful reproducibility positive despite the lack of ablations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built, released model family evaluated via deployment-relevant benchmarks and memory/latency measurements). - Practical: Yes; models target commercial TPU (v5p/v6e) for training and are explicitly designed and quantized for on-device/edge inference, with memory footprints reported down to 0.8GB (E2B, mobile-quantized, no KV cache). - Efficient: The paper reports concrete relative gains, 37.5% global KV-cache reduction, 78% audio-encoder disk-footprint reduction, 44% latency reduction on the 150M vision encoder under W8A8 quantization, but does not report wall-clock inference latency or throughput numbers for the full model stack, only encoder-level and drafter-level pieces. - Effective: Static and human-eval benchmarks show real task performance gains over the prior generation and against listed competitors, but all evaluation is on standard/public benchmarks (MMLU Pro, GPQA, Arena) rather than a downstream deployed application, so effectiveness is measured on proxies for real-world use, not real-world use itself. - Scalable: The model suite already spans 2.3B to 31B (and a 26B-total MoE), so multi-scale behavior is tested directly rather than extrapolated; the paper does not claim or test beyond 31B, so scaling behavior at 10x today's largest Gemma 4 model is unaddressed. The work delivers a released software artifact: open weights (raw and QAT-quantized) for the full model suite under Apache 2.0. Strengths: - Concrete, quantified memory-efficiency engineering: 37.5% KV-cache reduction, 78% audio-encoder footprint reduction, and detailed per-model memory tables (Table 3) rather than vague efficiency claims. - The encoder-free 12B design is a genuine architectural departure (raw patches/raw audio direct to LLM embedding space) rather than a scaled repeat of the Gemma 3n encoder-based approach. - Broad, multi-modal, multi-scale evaluation (text, vision, audio, long-context) across five model sizes gives a wide surface for readers to locate the size/capability tradeoff relevant to their deployment target. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state E2B's long-context performance drops sharply relative to larger models (implicit in Table 9's numbers, e.g., GraphWalks F1 82.3 at 31B vs 4.1 at E2B) but do not explicitly discuss this as a limitation in prose; they do note that the encoder-free design is applied only to the 12B model, implicitly acknowledging it as unproven at other sizes. Safety section states testing was conducted "without safety filters" to measure inherent capability, an explicit and transparent methodological disclosure. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No ablation table isolates which architectural change (p-RoPE, KV sharing, encoder-free design, MTP drafter) drives which fraction of the benchmark gains; a reader cannot tell if the encoder-free 12B's benchmark competitiveness comes from the new architecture or simply more/better training data. - Arena Elo comparisons list only rank and CI, with no discussion of potential contamination or overlap between Arena prompts and training data, despite the paper's own mention of benchmark decontamination filtering only for "training data,
Recommended 2607.02770 PDF
#94 — Moderately Innovative (57)

MultAttnAttrib: Training-Free Multimodal Attribution in Long Document Question Answering

DQT Tran, QV Dang, V Tyagi, SSR Veeravalli… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MultAttnAttrib, a training-free method that locates the text spans and image regions supporting a generated answer by aggregating attention signals from a small set of calibrated cross-modal retrieval heads during a single prefill forward pass. - Why it's important: Grounded QA systems deployed in agentic and enterprise settings need per-answer citations to establish trust and catch hallucination, but multimodal attribution across long documents with interleaved text, charts, and images is comparatively unaddressed relative to text-only attribution. - How it does it: A small labeled probe set is used to identify a fixed subset of attention heads (via Causal Mediation Analysis) that reliably attend to ground-truth evidence across modalities, then their attention is aggregated and thresholded at inference to score and cite text spans and image slots in one forward pass. - The good: MultAttnAttrib beats every prompting/RAG baseline on the same Qwen3-VL-30B backbone by over 20 F1 points on average across text-only, image-only, and text+image regimes (Table 2), while cutting peak VRAM by ~15GB and latency by 7.3x versus direct VLM prompting (Table 1). - The missing: The method requires a labeled probe set (90 QAA triplets) for head identification and threshold calibration per backbone, and the paper does not test whether heads or thresholds transfer to a different model family or a materially different document distribution. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building retrieval-augmented or agentic document QA systems who needs cheap, interpretable multimodal citations; less essential for readers focused purely on mechanistic interpretability theory since the head-identification recipe (CMA) is adapted from prior text-only work rather than newly derived.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) carry the score: the authors state directly that multimodal attribution 'resists frontier gains' and report the mixed GPT-5.4 comparison (losing text-only F1 by 25.1%) rather than burying it, which is honest limitation acknowledgment. Rigor (55) is held back by single-run point estimates on a 608-item test split, unablated hyperparameters (head count k, entropy weighting, fallback rule, window length W), an efficiency claim measured only on the subset where the baseline does not OOM, and benchmark ground truth generated and judged entirely by LLMs with no human-agreement check. Novelty (55) reflects that CMA-based retrieval-head identification is imported from prior text-only work and extended to multimodal, with the new benchmark (MultAttrEval) as the more durable contribution. Impact (60) reflects a reusable benchmark and a 7.3x latency advantage, discounted by the per-backbone labeled-probe-set calibration requirement that is never shown to transfer across model families. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*60 = 57.0.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deployed grounded-QA and agentic systems need to attribute each generated answer to its supporting evidence so users can verify correctness before acting on it; fewer than 52% of generated statements in modern systems carry accurate citations per prior audits. Existing attribution methods (citation-style fine-tuning, retrieval-then-verify pipelines, decomposition methods) were built for text-only QA and, when extended naively to multimodal long documents (PDFs mixing text, charts, and images), either omit visual evidence entirely or require expensive multi-call verification. No prior benchmark evaluates fine-grained localization (as opposed to selection from a small pre-curated candidate pool) for full multimodal long documents, so both the attribution method and its evaluation are underdeveloped for this setting. Key Idea: Retrieval heads (attention heads that causally mediate copying evidence into the answer) are largely shared across text and image modalities in the broad population but modality-specific at the very top ranks; identifying a small set of these shared cross-modal heads from a labeled probe set and reading their attention directly off a single prefill pass yields citations without any additional generation, verification, or fine-tuning. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Head identification: for each of 1536 heads (48 layers x 32 heads) in Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B, an Indirect Effect score is computed via an adapted Causal Mediation Analysis (CMA) — one clean forward pass and one corrupted pass per probe item where ground-truth text is replaced with an equal-length span from another document and images are resized to preserve patch geometry — measuring how much each head's attention to ground-truth positions drops under corruption, weighted by a Shannon-entropy term that penalizes heads that spread attention uniformly; the top-k heads by this score are retained. (2) Calibration: sliding-window length W is set to the median chunk length in the probe set, then attribution is run on all probe items and F1-maximizing thresholds T_img and T_txt are swept separately for image and text modalities (Algorithm 4). (3) Attribution: a single forward pass produces a mean attention vector over the selected heads; image slots are scored by mean attention over their patch tokens and text is scored via sliding windows, both min-max normalized, then thresholded against T_img/T_txt, with a fallback to the highest-scoring modality if nothing clears threshold. Three design choices stand out: using CMA instead of correlational Mean Attention Scoring (MAS) is justified empirically (Section 6.3, IoU/Spearman comparisons) as producing more cross-modal-consistent head rankings; the corruption scheme for CMA is a genuine multimodal extension of prior text-only work (Basu et al. 2025) but its equal-length/equal-shape substitution is an assumption not independently validated as causally clean; and the fallback-to-highest-score rule when no threshold is cleared is a heuristic patch whose effect on precision/recall is not separately ablated. Key Results: On the introduced MultAttrEval benchmark (608 test QAA triplets across 5 domains: academic, business, health, legal, marketing), using Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct as backbone, MultAttnAttrib (full-document variant) reaches F1 of 0.596 (text-only), 0.776 (image-only), and 0.582 (text+image), versus the best same-backbone prompting baseline (Cohere+LLM RAG) at 0.563, 0.604, 0.528 respectively — a >20% relative F1 gain claimed and roughly matched in the reported deltas (Table 2). Against the frontier closed-source GPT-5.4 with Cohere RAG (the strongest baseline overall), MultAttnAttrib+Cohere is competitive but not uniformly better: it wins on image-only F1 (0.786 vs 0.763, +3.0%) but loses on text-only F1 (0.665 vs 0.888, -25.1%) and multimodal F1 (0.601 vs 0.663, -9.4%) (Tables 8-10). Latency and memory: on an A100 GPU, non-vLLM batch-1 inference shows MultAttnAttrib at 2.16±0.17s and 63.41GB peak VRAM versus VLM prompting at 15.67±14.38s and 78.28GB, a 7.3x latency reduction and ~15GB memory reduction, measured only on valid non-OOM QA inputs (an unspecified fraction of the dataset, since direct Qwen inference OOMs frequently on long documents). LLM-judge relevance/support scores (GPT-4o panel) show a more mixed picture: MultAttnAttrib is not the top performer on text-regime relevance (0.691 vs LLM baseline's 0.741) though it wins on support (0.741) and modestly leads on image and multimodal relevance/support. Sparsity finding: about 80% of all heads score below 0.1 after min-max normalization and fewer than 2% score above 0.6 in any modality category, confirming a genuinely small retrieval circuit (Figure 5b). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (training-free multimodal attribution via calibrated cross-modal retrieval heads) and NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (MultAttrEval, the first fine-grained multimodal attribution benchmark for long documents with free-form localization rather than pre-curated candidate selection). The genuinely novel piece is the multimodal extension of CMA-based retrieval-head identification (prior CMA work was text-only) plus the min-max-normalized joint text/image thresholding scheme; the underlying idea that attention heads mechanistically explain retrieval/copying behavior is a direct application of prior text-only findings (Wu et al. 2025) to a new (multimodal) setting, not a new mechanistic claim. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present but partial: the paper compares CMA vs. MAS for head scoring (Section 6.3, Figures 4-5) and reports domain-difficulty breakdowns (Figure 9), but does not ablate k (number of heads), the entropy-weighting term, the fallback rule, or the sliding-window length W independently. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any table; all F1/precision/recall numbers appear to be single-run point estimates on a fixed 608-item test split drawn from a probe/test split of only 698 total QAA items, a comparatively small evaluation set for five-domain claims. No code, data, or model weight release URL is stated in the excerpted sections. The probe set used for calibration (90 items, 30 per regime) is drawn from the same MultAttrEval distribution used for test-set evaluation, which is standard for a training-free calibration method but does mean threshold generalization to genuinely out-of-distribution documents is untested. The benchmark's own construction pipeline uses an LLM (unspecified model, appears to be a Gemini-family model per Figure 2's icon) for QAA generation and an LLM-judge panel (GPT-4o, 3-persona) for a secondary evaluation, both of which introduce LLM-annotator bias that is not cross-validated against human annotation. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes on a single A100 GPU at batch size 1; peak VRAM of 63GB still exceeds consumer GPU memory (24-48GB), so "commercially available hardware" holds only for datacenter-class accelerators. Efficient: Substantially cheaper than direct VLM prompting (2 forward passes for CMA calibration, 1 forward pass at inference vs. iterative generation), with a stated 7.3x latency and ~15GB memory advantage, though this comparison excludes the fraction of QA pairs where direct Qwen inference OOMs and is silently dropped from the latency measurement. Effective: Evidence shows consistent F1 gains over same-backbone prompting baselines on the paper's own benchmark; effectiveness relative to the strongest overall system (GPT-5.4+RAG) is mixed, and the benchmark itself is authored by the same research effort, so the evidence is not yet independently replicated. Scalable: Not tested beyond documents in MultAttrEval (up to ~90k OCR words, 253 images total across 20 documents per Table 3); scaling to much longer documents or many more images per document is untested, and the fixed sliding-window/patch-token scoring scheme has no stated complexity analysis for larger inputs. The paper delivers a benchmark dataset (MultAttrEval) as a reusable artifact; the attribution method itself is a procedure (head identification + calibration) rather than a standalone software release, and no repository link appears in the excerpted text. Strengths: - The CMA-based multimodal corruption scheme (replacing ground-truth text/image evidence with equal-length/equal-shape distractors from another document) is a real methodological extension of prior text-only causal head-identification work, not a relabeling of an existing technique. - The layer-wise and modality-specialization analysis (Figures 4-5, 10-13) is a genuine mechanistic contribution independent of the attribution method's downstream performance, showing image heads concentrate in layers 22-36 while text heads span early-to-late layers, with crossmodal heads clustering in the transition zone. - The latency/memory comparison (Table 1) isolates a concrete deployment advantage (single forward pass vs. iterative generation) that is mechanistically well-motivated, not just an empirical coincidence. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - MultAttrEval documents are image-dense with near-duplicate/decorative images and single-source ground truth, which the authors say depresses baseline scores against visually similar alternative images and calls for stricter image-relevance filtering or multi-image attribution support in future work. - The method requires a labeled probe set for both head identification and threshold calibration; the authors note unsupervised head scoring or fixed (non-swept) thresholding could remove this requirement but at unknown performance cost, and flag that correlational scoring (MAS) can rank heads highly without those heads causally mediating retrieval. - The authors state directly that multimodal attribution "resists frontier gains" — even GPT-5.4 only narrows the combined-attribution gap to 1.8 F1 points versus Qwen while dominating on unimodal splits by 11-12 points, meaning no method (including their own) has solved joint text-image evidence arbitration. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No cross-model transfer experiment: heads and thresholds are identified/calibrated per backbone (Qwen3-VL-30B only for the full method), so it is unknown whether the identified retrieval-head set is stable across model checkpoints, sizes, or families, which matters directly for deployability since re-running CMA calibration for every new backbone is nontrivial engineering overhead. - The claimed 7.3x latency / 15GB memory advantage (Table 1) is computed only on QA pairs where direct Qwen inference does not OOM; the fraction of the test set excluded by this filter is not stated, so the aggregate efficiency advantage across the full workload (including OOM cases the baseline cannot even complete) is understated in the paper's own favor rather than reported. - The image-only regime shows the VLM baseline achieving the highest recall of any method (0.871, Table 2) while MultAttnAttrib trades recall for precision; the paper frames this as a strength ("universal F1 gains") without acknowledging that for use cases prioritizing recall (e.g. compliance/audit citation completeness) the baseline may still be preferable. - The benchmark's QAA generation and quality-verification pipeline both rely on LLM judges (an unnamed generator LLM plus a 3-persona GPT-4o panel) with no reported human-annotation agreement check, so ground-truth attribution quality in MultAttrEval itself is not independently validated against human raters. Bottom Line: A practitioner building multimodal document-QA systems who needs cheap, single-pass citations gains a concrete, well-benchmarked training-free method and a genuinely new evaluation resource, but should treat the frontier-model comparison as mixed rather than a clear win and budget for the labeled-probe-set calibration step per deployment backbone.
Citations 2607.01420 PDF
#95 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Enhancing In-context Panoramic Generation via Geometric-aware Pretraining (14 ▲ )

Haoran Feng, Ruiyang Zhang, Longyi Zhang, Dizhe Zhang, Lu Qi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Canvas360, a two-stage flow-transformer framework that pretrains on parallel RGB-depth panorama generation and then fine-tunes a single model to jointly perform text-to-panorama style transfer, inpainting, outpainting, and editing via token-level concatenation. - Why it's important: Equirectangular panoramas have latitude-dependent distortion that breaks geometric consistency during editing, and existing panorama methods either need per-task models or distortion-specific tricks (cube maps, spherical positional embeddings) that the authors show still fail to preserve 3D scene structure. - How it does it: It regresses velocity fields for RGB and depth latents jointly (via 3D RoPE positional offsets and a correlation-penalizing similarity loss to keep the two modalities distinct) and adds velocity circular padding that synchronizes ghost-column features across the 0/360-degree seam so the model learns wrap-around adjacency explicitly. - The good: Canvas360 achieves the best panorama-specific FAED (2.33 vs. 2.91 for the next-best, DiT360) and the lowest LRCE-RGB boundary-consistency score (0.0063 vs. 0.0094-0.0188 for six baselines), both supporting the geometric-consistency claim directly. - The missing: There is no ablation isolating the fine-tuning stage's task-unification benefit versus training four separate task-specific models on the same data, so the paper does not show unified fine-tuning outperforms (rather than merely matching) per-task alternatives. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building panoramic generation or editing pipelines who needs a concrete boundary-continuity and depth-conditioning recipe; skip if the interest is purely in perspective-image in-context editing, where the geometric machinery here does not apply.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (58) is the pivotal dimension: cumulative ablations (Table 7) do isolate velocity circular padding, depth supervision, positional offset, and the similarity loss, and the paper honestly reports that depth supervision alone destabilizes training, but there are no error bars on any table, no release confirmation, and the 900K fine-tuning corpus is largely self-synthesized with FLUX.2-dev/NanoBanana, sharing provenance with the comparison images. Completeness (55) is pulled down by the paper's second headline claim ('unified framework') never being tested against four independently fine-tuned task-specific models on identical data, and by editing evaluation being entirely qualitative. Novelty (60) is moderate: velocity circular padding is a real mechanistic advance over prior boundary-column duplication, but the flow-matching, DiT, LoRA, and token-concatenation machinery is standard. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*50 = 57.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Panoramic (360-degree) image generation and in-context editing are needed for VR/AR content creation, immersive scene manipulation, and virtual production, but the dominant equirectangular projection (ERP) representation distorts scene geometry non-uniformly by latitude (objects near the poles are stretched, and the left/right image edges are physically the same seam). Existing in-context panorama editing methods (cube-map decompositions, 3D spherical positional embeddings) attempt to compensate for this but, per the authors' own qualitative inspection, still fail to preserve consistent 3D scene structure across edits. Training data for in-context panoramic tasks (paired before/after panoramas for style transfer, inpainting, outpainting, editing) is also scarce at scale, unlike the abundant paired data available for perspective-image in-context editing. Key Idea: Depth in panoramic space is naturally spherical (radial distance from the camera center) rather than Cartesian, so training the model to jointly generate RGB and depth panoramas (as two token sequences distinguished only by a constant positional offset in 3D RoPE, plus a similarity loss to prevent the two branches from collapsing into a degenerate similar output) forces the shared flow transformer to internalize spherical geometry as a side effect. This depth-conditioned prior is then transferred, depth-free, into unified in-context fine-tuning by simply discarding the depth branch and reusing the same token-concatenation mechanism for style/inpaint/outpaint/edit contexts. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. Stage 1 (geometry-aware pretraining) takes 100K RGB panoramas (10K from Matterport3D, 90K newly curated), estimates pseudo-depth via DAP (a panoramic depth foundation model, with depth truncated at 100/10 for outdoor/indoor scenes to avoid instability), and trains a Flow Transformer (built on FLUX.1-dev, fine-tuned via LoRA rank 64) with a rectified-flow matching objective computed independently on RGB and depth token sequences that are concatenated along the token dimension (following FLUX.1-Kontext's sequence-concatenation design) and separated in 3D RoPE via a constant coordinate offset T_d. A similarity loss (squared correlation between predicted RGB and depth velocity fields) regularizes against degenerate over-coupling. Velocity circular padding synchronizes two ghost columns at the longitude 0/360 boundary with their circular counterparts before computing the flow-matching loss, making the model explicitly aware of spherical wrap-around adjacency (distinguishing this from prior circular-padding work that only duplicates boundary columns without informing the loss of the adjacency). Stage 2 (unified in-context fine-tuning) drops the depth branch and instead concatenates context and target latents ([x_tgt; x_cxt]) with a different positional offset T_c, training on the 900K-sample Canvas360Dataset (200K style transfer via FLUX.2-dev stylization, 250K outpainting via sampled camera reprojection, 250K inpainting via random rectangular masks with global/local VLM-generated prompts, 200K editing via VLM+GroundingDINO object localization and FLUX.2-dev/NanoBanana-based erasure). Three design decisions stand out: (1) using token-level concatenation with positional offsets rather than channel-wise fusion, chosen explicitly to allow reuse of pretrained FLUX weights, which is well-justified by the backbone ablation (Fig. 9); (2) the similarity loss to prevent RGB-depth collapse, justified qualitatively via Fig. 11/Table 7 but not via a mechanistic explanation of why collapse occurs; (3) discarding depth entirely at fine-tuning time on the assumption that the pretrained backbone retains the geometric prior implicitly, supported by Fig. 7's cubemap-depth-consistency comparison but not by any inference-time metric that isolates the depth-retention question from generation quality. Key Results: On text-to-panorama generation (Matterport3D benchmark, Table 1), Canvas360 achieves the best FAED (2.33, next-best DiT360 at 2.91) and best IS (1.76) and QA-aesthetic (4.20) among 7 baselines (PanFusion, SMGD, PAR, WorldGen, LayerPano3D, HunyuanWorld, DiT360), while ranking second on FID (44.17 vs. DiT360's 42.88), FID_pole, FID_equ, QA_quality, and BRISQUE. A 71-participant user study (Table 2) shows Canvas360 preferred most often on boundary continuity (31.1%), panorama awareness (34.1%), and overall quality (35.9%), though HunyuanWorld edges it out slightly on text alignment (26.9% vs 26.2%). Boundary consistency (Table 6, LRCE-RGB extended from PanoFormer) shows Canvas360 at 0.0063, roughly 1.5-3x lower (better) than all six compared methods (0.0094-0.0188). Ablations (Table 7) show depth supervision alone drops FAED from 5.37 to 4.74 but destabilizes QA_qua/QA_ae/BRISQUE (over-darkened outputs); adding positional offset and the similarity loss recovers most of that stability. A small manual data-cleaning check (48K/50K samples judged clean) shows negligible difference between cleaned and raw-sampled 20K editing subsets (LPIPS 0.093 vs 0.096, PSNR 25.70 vs 25.34), suggesting the synthetic data pipeline's noise floor is low. All quantitative comparisons are against the authors' own selected recent baselines (2023-2025) on a Matterport3D-derived validation set; no comparison against a non-panoramic (perspective-model-adapted) baseline is reported for the in-context tasks beyond FLUX.1-Fill-dev and NanoBanana. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (parallel RGB-depth pretraining with positional-offset token separation and velocity circular padding for panoramic flow transformers) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (Canvas360Dataset, 1M paired samples spanning four in-context tasks). The core novelty is combining spherical-depth-as-auxiliary-supervision with an explicit wrap-around-aware padding scheme inside a token-concatenation in-context framework; the underlying flow-matching, DiT, and LoRA fine-tuning machinery is a standard combination of existing techniques (Rectified Flow, FLUX.1-Kontext-style concatenation, 3D RoPE) applied to a new spherical setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and reasonably thorough (Table 7 isolates baseline, +VCP, +depth, +position-offset, +similarity-loss cumulatively; Fig. 9-11 provide qualitative counterparts). No error bars, multiple random seeds, or significance tests are reported for any quantitative table, so it is unclear whether the FID/FAED/LRCE differences (e.g., 44.17 vs. 42.88 FID against DiT360) are within noise. No code, model weights, or dataset release is confirmed in the paper text; a project page URL is given but the paper does not state whether Canvas360Dataset or model weights will be publicly released. Evaluation is on a Matterport3D-derived validation set that the paper itself curated and extended (100K pilot set, 900K downstream samples largely self-synthesized via FLUX.2-dev/NanoBanana), which raises a mild Goodhart concern: the training data and much of the evaluation protocol share provenance with the same generative models used to build baselines' comparison images, and quality control for AI-generated training pairs relies on the authors' own manual 50K-sample spot check rather than an independent audit. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (built and evaluated system for panorama generation and editing). Practical: Yes, built on FLUX.1-dev with LoRA fine-tuning (rank 64) on 8 NVIDIA H20 GPUs, a configuration reproducible in a well-resourced lab, though full pretraining-plus-fine-tuning compute cost across 100K+900K samples for 25 epochs is not stated in GPU-hours. Efficient: Not directly measured; no inference latency, throughput, or memory comparison against baselines is reported despite the framework requiring depth-branch computation during pretraining (removed at fine-tuning/inference). Effective: The evidence (FAED, LRCE-RGB, user study) directly targets the stated problem of geometric/boundary consistency and shows consistent wins on those specific metrics, though general FID performance is only second-best, indicating the method trades some standard-fidelity ground for panorama-specific consistency gains. Scalable: Not tested. All experiments use a fixed 1024x2048 resolution (ablations at 512x1024) and the dataset sizes (100K pretraining, 900K fine-tuning) are fixed; the paper claims the data pipeline provides "a foundation for large-scale model scaling" but presents no experiment at 10x data or resolution to support this. Strengths: - The velocity circular padding formulation (Eq. in Sec 3.2, synchronizing ghost-column features and target velocities across the seam before loss computation) is a concrete, mechanistically distinct improvement over prior circular-padding approaches that only duplicate boundary tokens, and the LRCE-RGB result (0.0063 vs. next-best 0.0094) is the paper's most convincing quantitative signal. - The Canvas360Dataset data-synthesis pipeline (Fig. 3) is a genuine engineering contribution: distinct, well-specified procedures for each of four tasks (FLUX.2-dev stylization, camera-reprojection outpainting masks, dual global/local-prompt inpainting, VLM+grounding-based object erasure/insertion) producing 900K samples, filling a real gap versus prior panorama datasets (Table 4 comparison to H*Bench, Puffin-4M, 360+x). - The ablation in Table 7 honestly reports that depth supervision alone destabilizes training (over-darkened, artifact-prone outputs) rather than only presenting the final, cherry-picked configuration, which is a credibility positive. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that adding depth supervision alone is not sufficiently stable (causes over-darkened regions and near-black failure cases in certain configurations) and that the positional offset and similarity loss are stabilization mechanisms rather than components designed to monotonically improve every metric. They also note velocity circular padding targets local boundary continuity specifically and does not necessarily improve global distribution-matching metrics like FID/FAED. The paper does not otherwise discuss broader limitations (failure modes on out-of-domain scene types, compute cost, or dataset release status) in a dedicated limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No ablation isolates whether the four-task unified fine-tuning framework actually benefits from joint training versus four independently fine-tuned task-specific models on identical data, which is the paper's second headline claim ("unified... framework") but is never directly tested against a non-unified control. The evaluation set is Matterport3D-derived (predominantly indoor scenes, per Figs. 1/4/8), and outdoor generalization (desert/mountain scenes appear only in the depth-visualization appendix, Fig. 6) is not quantitatively evaluated. The pseudo-depth supervision itself comes from DAP, another recent model, so any systematic bias in DAP's depth estimates (per-scene-type or per-distance) propagates into the geometric prior without an ablation checking sensitivity to depth-estimator choice. Editing-task evaluation is entirely qualitative (Fig. 5, Appendix F) with no LPIPS/PSNR/FAED numbers reported for editing outside the small 20K data-cleaning ablation (Table 5), leaving the headline editing capability under-quantified relative to generation and inpainting/outpainting. Bottom Line: A practitioner building panorama generation or in-context editing systems gains a concrete, empirically validated boundary-consistency technique (velocity circular padding) and a large synthetic dataset recipe, but should treat the unified-fine-tuning and general-fidelity claims as less rigorously supported than the geometric-consistency claims that dominate the paper's evidence.
Recommended 2607.08765 PDF
#96 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Evaluating Time Series Foundation Models for Electricity Price Forecasting: Contamination Risk, Distributional Shifts, and Covariate Dependence

Z Pan, AA Ezzat · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-dataset benchmarking framework that evaluates four time series foundation models (TSFMs) — Chronos 2, TimesFM 2.5, TabPFN-TS, TOTO 1.0 — against statistical, deep learning, and domain-specific electricity price forecasting (EPF) methods, examining covariate support, tail behavior, and robustness to price spikes. - Why it's important: EPF drives real financial risk in power system operations, and practitioners need to know whether large-scale pretrained models can replace or complement domain-specific forecasting pipelines built over decades of EPF-specific research. - How it does it: The paper pairs an established competition benchmark (GEFCom2014-P) with a newly curated, low-contamination-risk dataset (GridStatus2025) and evaluates all models under a common protocol across point accuracy (MAE/RMSE), probabilistic accuracy (average quantile loss), tail quantiles, and spike/non-spike subsets, using Diebold-Mariano tests to check whether covariate use is a statistically significant improvement. - The good: The Diebold-Mariano test results (Table 7) give a rare instance of statistical rigor in this literature, showing covariate incorporation significantly reduces loss for Chronos 2 and TabPFN-TS (DM statistics -6.34 to -6.74, p<0.05) but not for TimesFM 2.5 or TOTO 1.0, a genuinely informative and testable finding rather than a raw leaderboard number. - The missing: The paper never explains why TOTO 1.0 wins on GEFCom2014-P (rank 1, aggregate rating 71.7%) yet degrades to worst-in-class on GridStatus2025 (RMSE 16.208, rank 21.00) beyond a one-sentence gesture at "market conditions and data characteristics" — a discrepancy this large deserves a dedicated ablation, not a shrug. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or selecting EPF forecasting pipelines who wants a covariate-aware, spike-aware comparison of TSFMs against domain-specific baselines (LEAR, CING-LEAR); skippable for readers wanting deep architectural insight into TSFMs themselves, since this is purely an evaluation study with no new model.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (65) is the highest dimension and the main thing keeping this above the midline: Diebold-Mariano tests on the covariate-support claim are a real statistical upgrade over the raw leaderboards typical of this literature, and the two-dataset contamination-control design (pairing GEFCom2014-P with the post-cutoff GridStatus2025) addresses a documented Goodhart-style weakness that seven prior TSFM benchmarks ignore. But no error bars, seeds, or confidence intervals accompany the point/quantile tables that carry the rankings, no code or data release is stated, and the contamination claim itself rests on pretraining cutoffs the authors admit are not verifiable for TOTO 1.0. Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (50) are limited by there being no new model, loss, or algorithm — the methodology is a standard toolkit applied to an underexplored domain — and Practical Impact (45) is low because the work ships neither artifact nor library, and omits any compute/latency comparison despite efficiency being one of its own four stated evaluation axes. Weighted composite: 0.20*55 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*45 = 57.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Short-term electricity price forecasting (day-ahead, 24-hour horizon) underlies power system market operations, and it is unusually hard because prices exhibit strong nonstationarity, multi-scale seasonality, sudden spikes, and heavy dependence on exogenous covariates (load, fuel prices, weather). Time series foundation models pretrained on broad time series corpora have shown strong zero-shot performance on generic forecasting benchmarks, but nobody had systematically asked whether that generalization holds in a covariate-driven, spike-prone, nonstationary domain like EPF, and whether it holds up against methods purpose-built for this domain (LEAR, CING-LEAR, DNN-based EPF models). A further confound specific to TSFM evaluation is data contamination: TSFM pretraining corpora are large, heterogeneous, and rarely fully disclosed, so a TSFM's strong score on a public benchmark dataset could just reflect memorized test data rather than genuine generalization. Key Idea: There is no single new algorithmic idea; the paper's core methodological device is the two-dataset design that pairs an established, unavoidably-contamination-suspect benchmark (GEFCom2014-P, 2014 data, likely seen during TSFM pretraining) with a newly curated 2022-2025 dataset (GridStatus2025) chosen specifically because its evaluation window falls after the disclosed pretraining cutoffs of most benchmarked TSFMs, letting the authors triangulate whether observed TSFM strength survives when contamination is unlikely. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages: (1) data curation — GEFCom2014-P used via its official rolling-window competition protocol (nine quantiles, 12 tasks); GridStatus2025 newly assembled from the GridStatus API with three years training (2022-2024) and one year testing (2025), covariates = load, gas, and fuel prices; (2) model benchmarking — four TSFMs each run in covariate-free and covariate-supported ("w.") variants, zero-shot only (no few-shot/fine-tuning), against three statistical baselines (Auto-ARIMA, Auto-ETS, Auto-Theta), five deep learning baselines (DeepAR, TFT, TiDE, TSMixer, DLinear/NLinear) tuned via Optuna hyperparameter search over up to 100 epochs, and three domain-specific EPF methods (LEAR, DNN, CING-LEAR) using EPF-standard asinh transforms; (3) multi-axis evaluation — point accuracy (MAE, RMSE), probabilistic accuracy (average quantile/pinball loss over nine quantiles), tail-quantile breakdown (Q0.01 through Q0.99), and spike-vs-non-spike stratified performance (spikes defined as observations outside the 5th-95th percentile of the test distribution), plus one-sided Diebold-Mariano tests for covariate-support significance. Three design decisions stand out: fixing TSFM context length at 2048 for fair comparison (justified by citing Chronos 2's own finding that gains beyond 2048 are marginal, though this wasn't independently re-verified by the authors); constructing a simple unweighted average ensemble of the best TSFM (Chronos 2 w.) and best domain-specific model (CING-LEAR) as an ad hoc combination rather than a learned meta-model; and using GEFCom2014-P purely as an external sanity check (no diagnostic breakdowns possible there) since competitor prediction outputs are not public, reserving all tail/spike diagnostics for GridStatus2025 alone. Key Results: On GridStatus2025, covariate-supported Chronos 2 w. and TabPFN-TS w. rank among the strongest TSFMs (Chronos 2 w. MAE 4.105, RMSE 5.719, aQL 1.631, average rank 2.00), but CING-LEAR (a domain-specific method) ranks second overall individually (MAE 4.202, RMSE 5.843, aQL 1.541, rank 3.00) and the Chronos 2 w. + CING-LEAR ensemble is the best performer on every metric (MAE 3.922, RMSE 5.470, aQL 1.541, rank 1.00). On GEFCom2014-P, the best TSFM (TOTO 1.0) ranks 8th overall against the 16 competition entries plus baselines, below the top-5 human/team submissions. On tail quantiles (Table 5), the ensemble wins or ties best at Q(0.01), Q(0.50), and Q(0.99); statistical models show pronounced degradation at low tail quantiles (Auto-ARIMA Q(0.01)=22.97 vs Q(0.50)=26.12) while TSFMs and domain-aware methods stay comparatively balanced. Under spike conditions (Table 6), CING-LEAR shows the smallest MAE degradation (99.3%) among all model classes, and the ensemble again performs best in absolute terms on both spike and non-spike MAE (3.555 / 7.220). Diebold-Mariano tests (Table 7, alpha=0.05) confirm covariate support significantly reduces loss for Chronos 2 and TabPFN-TS across all three metrics (DM statistics -5.09 to -7.04) but not for TimesFM 2.5 (DM +0.16 to -0.88, no rejection) or TOTO 1.0 (DM statistics are strongly positive, +9.98 to +12.02, meaning covariates actively hurt TOTO 1.0). Baselines span classical statistical models, tuned deep learning, and hand-designed EPF methods, which is a reasonably strong and current baseline set for this literature, though TSFM baselines are limited to four models and exclude several other published TSFMs (e.g., TimeGPT-1, Moirai) cited in the related-work table. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (GridStatus2025, curated to reduce pretraining-contamination risk) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (systematic quantification of covariate dependence, tail asymmetry, and spike robustness for TSFMs in EPF, plus the counterintuitive finding that a naive TSFM+domain-model ensemble beats both constituents). The core evaluation methodology (rolling-window MAE/RMSE/quantile loss, DM tests) is a standard combination of existing tools applied to a genuinely underexplored setting; there is no new model, loss function, or algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes appendix-level detail on hyperparameter search spaces (Table 3, Optuna-tuned over input length, hidden size, layers, dropout, batch size, learning rate) and reports Diebold-Mariano statistical tests for the covariate-support claim, which is a genuine ablation with a formal significance test rather than a raw score comparison. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed variance are reported for the point/quantile-loss tables (Tables 1, 4, 5, 6), so it is unclear whether the reported rank differences (e.g., TOTO 1.0 w. rank 20.00 vs Chronos 2 w. rank 6.00 on GridStatus2025) are robust to resampling or rolling-window selection. Code, data, and trained model artifacts are not stated as released. The two-dataset design directly addresses Goodhart's-Law-style contamination risk, which is commendable, but the contamination-avoidance claim for GridStatus2025 rests on publicly disclosed pretraining cutoffs that the authors themselves note are not fully verifiable (TOTO 1.0's pretraining data coverage is explicitly stated as "not publicly disclosed"). Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (comparative benchmarking of already-built models and pipelines, no new system released). - Practical: Yes — all evaluated methods (TSFMs plus statistical/deep learning/domain baselines) run on standard commercial hardware; TSFMs are used zero-shot, avoiding fine-tuning cost. - Efficient: Not stated — no wall-clock time, memory footprint, or parameter count comparison is reported for any of the four TSFMs or their competitors, a significant omission for a paper whose stated appeal of TSFMs is practical deployability. - Effective: Partially — TSFMs are competitive on point and probabilistic accuracy against domain-specific methods on GridStatus2025 but do not consistently surpass them (CING-LEAR beats most TSFMs individually), and on GEFCom2014-P no TSFM reaches the top-5 competition rank, so the paper's own evidence shows TSFMs solve a proxy of the EPF problem, not the full problem better than specialized methods. - Scalable: Not tested — both datasets are single-market, single-region day-ahead EPF tasks (GEFCom2014-P: one US market from 2014; GridStatus2025: one grid over 2022-2025); no cross-market or multi-year-ahead scaling experiment is run, so generalization to other electricity markets, longer horizons, or larger covariate sets is unverified. The work delivers a benchmarking methodology and one new dataset (GridStatus2025), not a software artifact, trained model, or reusable library; no repository or dataset release URL is given in the excerpted text. Strengths: - The two-dataset contamination-control design is a genuine methodological contribution to TSFM evaluation, addressing a documented weakness (undisclosed pretraining corpora) that most prior TSFM benchmarks (Table 2: ProbTS, TFB, GIFT-Eval, TSFM-Bench, FEV-Bench, TS-ARENA, TIME) do not address at all. - The Diebold-Mariano significance testing for covariate-support claims (Table 7) is a real statistical rigor upgrade over the raw-score leaderboards common in this space. - The spike-stratified and tail-quantile analyses (Tables 5, 6) surface a genuinely useful and non-obvious finding: average-metric rankings mask large asymmetric degradation in the lower tail for statistical models, information directly relevant to risk-aware EPF deployment decisions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that GEFCom2014-P cannot support the detailed diagnostic analyses (tail, spike) because competitor prediction outputs are not public, restricting those analyses to GridStatus2025 alone. They acknowledge TOTO 1.0's degradation from GEFCom2014-P to GridStatus2025 is not fully explained ("several factors may contribute to this discrepancy") and flag that TOTO 1.0's pretraining data provenance is not publicly disclosed, which weakens their own contamination-mitigation claim for that specific model. They also state smaller relative degradation under spikes for deep learning models should not be conflated with smaller absolute error, an appropriately self-critical caveat. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No compute/efficiency comparison (inference latency, memory, parameter count) despite this being one of four core evaluation axes the introduction promises ("what is the compute/memory cost relative to alternatives?" in the deployability framing) — this is a dog that doesn't bark given the paper's own stated framework. - Only one grid/market (GridStatus2025 covers a single grid operator) is used for the low-contamination dataset; a genuine test of TSFM generalization would need multiple independent markets, since a single market's idiosyncrasies (fuel mix, regulatory structure) could dominate results. - The ensemble result (Chronos 2 w. + CING-LEAR beats both constituents everywhere) is presented as a headline finding but tested with only one ensembling scheme (unweighted average of two specific models); no ablation shows whether this generalizes to other TSFM/domain-method pairings or whether a learned combination weight would do even better, so the "complementary information" claim rests on an n=1 combination. - The paper does not report whether the same TSFM checkpoints/versions were used consistently, or whether GridStatus2025's one-year test window (2025) captures enough distinct price regimes (e.g., seasonal extremes) to generalize the tail/spike findings beyond that specific year. Bottom Line: A practitioner deciding whether to adopt TSFMs for EPF gains a concrete, contamination-aware answer, TSFMs are competitive but not dominant, covariate support matters more for some TSFMs than others, and a simple TSFM+domain-model ensemble currently outperforms either alone, though the missing efficiency comparison and single-market scope mean the practical deployment case is not yet fully made.
Search Results 2607.02623 PDF
#97 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Inspectable Tabular Foundation Models via In-Context Kernel Learning

R Miftachov, B Charron, S Valentin · cdn.amazon.science · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KernelICL, a framework that replaces the opaque MLP prediction head of tabular foundation models like TabICL with explicit kernel functions (Gaussian, dot-product, kNN), making every prediction a transparent weighted average of retrieved training labels. - Why it's important: Regulated domains (healthcare, finance) require practitioners to inspect which training cases drove a prediction before acting on it, and current tabular foundation models offer only opaque MLP outputs or unfaithful post-hoc explanations like SHAP. - How it does it: The method fine-tunes TabICL's embedding module end-to-end with a Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression head instead of an MLP, and achieves geometrically meaningful distance-based kernels by reprocessing training samples as additional queries through the third transformer stage to obtain symmetric query/key embeddings. - The good: On 55 TALENT benchmark datasets, KernelICL variants land within the statistical critical-difference threshold of TabICL and TabPFN (mean accuracy 82.79-82.91% vs. TabICL ensemble's 83.33%), showing transparency costs under 0.5 accuracy points. - The missing: The paper never evaluates whether the exposed nearest-neighbor evidence is actually faithful to the model's true decision process (only that it is inspectable by construction) or whether practitioners, in a user study, correctly use it to catch errors. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building interpretable tabular ML pipelines in regulated domains; skip if you only need raw accuracy, since TabICL ensemble still wins outright and the interpretability payoff here is architectural rather than validated with human subjects.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is the high point: ablations isolate every design choice (symmetric embeddings, calibration, projection dimension) with Friedman/Nemenyi critical-difference testing across all 55 TALENT datasets, which is above the norm for tabular ML, though no per-dataset seeds or variance are reported and no code is released. Non-Obviousness (65) credits the symmetric-embedding construction (reprocessing training samples as queries through TF_icl) as a real architectural insight, but the attention-as-kernel-regression equivalence is prior work and the kernel families are standard. The two dimensions that pull the composite down are Resilience (45) and Impact (40): the headline 'no performance sacrifice' claim rests on statistical indistinguishability rather than parity (TabICL ensemble still wins outright at mean rank 4.95 vs 6.25-6.75), the entire clinical motivation is untested (no user study, one qualitative Pima case study), and no artifact is released despite the contribution being interpretability tooling, with 69.4s inference vs 2.9s for TabICL. Weighted composite: 0.20*55 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*40 = 57.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 62 Resilience 45 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tabular foundation models (TabPFN, TabICL, MITRA) achieve strong accuracy via in-context learning but function as black boxes: an MLP consumes learned embeddings and outputs predictions with no exposed reasoning trace. In healthcare, clinicians need to validate model reasoning against domain knowledge before acting on diagnoses; in finance, regulators increasingly require transparency in automated decisions. Post-hoc explanation tools (SHAP, LIME) only reveal which input features mattered, not which training cases the model implicitly relied on, and multiple cited works (Rudin 2019, Lipton 2018, Miftachov et al. 2025) argue such post-hoc attributions can be unfaithful to actual model reasoning. The gap: no existing tabular foundation model exposes case-based, sample-level evidence for its predictions while retaining competitive accuracy. Key Idea: Standard scaled dot-product attention is mathematically a special case of Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression with an exponential kernel; the paper's insight is that this equivalence can be made explicit and constrained rather than left implicit in an opaque MLP head, and that TabICL's three-stage embedding architecture (column-wise, row-wise, in-context) can be repurposed to produce symmetric query/key embeddings (by reprocessing training samples as queries through the third stage) so that distance-based kernels are geometrically meaningful rather than defined over asymmetric, incomparable spaces. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) TabICL's embedding module processes training and test samples through column-wise (TF_col, Set Transformer-based), row-wise (TF_row), and in-context (TF_icl) transformer stages, the last introducing train/test asymmetry via attention masking. (2) To achieve symmetric embeddings, training samples are reprocessed as additional queries through TF_icl (Equation 5), incurring roughly a 2x runtime overhead on the ICL stage in the large-sample limit (Appendix H), then projected via a learnable W to a d_k-dimensional space (default d_k=512). (3) The 512-dim embeddings feed one of three kernel functions in place of TabICL's MLP: Gaussian (exp(-γ||q-k||²)), dot-product (exp(γq^Tk)), or kNN (binary indicator over γ-nearest neighbors), producing predictions as weighted label averages via Nadaraya-Watson estimation, with cross-entropy loss on kernel outputs used for end-to-end fine-tuning over 5,000 synthetic batches (kNN trained via embeddings borrowed from the Gaussian-trained model, since sorting is non-differentiable). Critical design decisions: (a) symmetric embeddings via query reprocessing, well-justified as necessary for geometric interpretation of distance-based kernels and empirically shown to improve both accuracy and perplexity (Table 8) at 9-16% runtime cost; (b) kernel scale γ selected via 5-fold cross-validation, justified by Table 7 showing accuracy/perplexity gains, though the paper itself flags that calibration's 21-24x runtime overhead is not justified for soft kernels since gains are not statistically significant; (c) using perplexity of the weight distribution, exp(-Σw_i log w_i), as the sparsity/inspectability metric, a reasonable choice (lower PPL approximates a k-nearest-neighbor equivalent) but not validated against any human-judged notion of "how many cases a practitioner can actually review." Key Results: On 55 TALENT binary classification datasets (645 to 109,099 samples, 3 to 970 features), KernelICL-Gaussian/Dot/kNN achieve 82.79-82.91% mean accuracy versus TabICL ensemble 83.33%, TabICL single 83.05%, TabPFN v2 83.16%, Real-TabPFN 83.28% (Table 6). Mean ranks (Wilcoxon signed-rank, Friedman+Nemenyi) place all KernelICL variants (6.25-6.75) within the critical-difference threshold of 2.68 relative to TabICL ensemble (4.95), meaning no statistically significant accuracy gap. Against TabICL-MLP, a controlled baseline sharing the same fine-tuning procedure but retaining an MLP head, all three KernelICL variants match within 0.12 accuracy points, isolating the kernel-head cost as negligible. KernelICL-kNN beats standard kNN (Euclidean distance in raw input space) by ~5 percentage points at matched sparsity (Figure 3), and on Pima Indians Diabetes specifically, KernelICL-kNN reaches 75.3% vs. 68.8% for standard kNN (Appendix D), with learned embeddings concentrating similarity on glucose (+61% relative neighborhood compactness) and BMI (+35%), the clinically established primary risk factors (DeFronzo et al., 2015). At 10% relative perplexity, accuracy is close to the TabPFN/TabICL cluster; cross-validated scale selection alone yields 11-29% relative perplexity, which the authors themselves state may not provide adequate inspectability for large datasets. Baselines are current (TabPFN v2, TabICL, MITRA, published within the last year) and appropriately strong; traditional ML baselines (CatBoost, XGBoost, RandomForest, LightGBM) rank substantially lower (13.24-19.54 mean rank), confirming foundation models' edge is preserved. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (kernel-constrained prediction head fine-tuned into an existing foundation model architecture) and NEW FORMULATION (a four-level taxonomy of prediction-layer transparency: opaque, mechanistic, geometric, distance-based, in Appendix B). The genuinely novel contribution is the symmetric-embedding mechanism that makes distance-based kernels geometrically valid within TabICL's inherently asymmetric train/test embedding architecture; the underlying attention-as-kernel-regression equivalence is prior theoretical work (Han et al., 2025, cited), and Gaussian/dot-product/kNN kernels are standard kernel families (Genton, 2001) applied in a new architectural setting rather than newly invented. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are extensive and directly isolate each design choice: symmetric vs. non-symmetric embeddings (Table 8), calibrated vs. non-calibrated scales (Table 7), projection dimension sweep from 16 to 512 (Table 9), and a comprehensive 23-configuration comparison (Table 10) with critical-difference diagrams (Figures 2 and 6) using standard Friedman/Nemenyi statistical testing across 55 datasets, which is methodologically sound and above the norm for most tabular ML papers. No error bars, seeds, or variance are reported for individual accuracy numbers, only means across 55 datasets, so within-dataset stability is unknown; the Wilcoxon-based mean rank partially substitutes but does not quantify per-run variance from training stochasticity. Code, data, and model weights are not stated as released in the paper (no URL or repo mentioned), which limits reproducibility. Evaluation uses the standard TALENT benchmark rather than an author-curated dataset, avoiding a key self-serving-benchmark risk, and baselines follow the TALENT library's published defaults, which is fair procedure. This is a preprint/workshop paper (ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data 2026), not yet peer-reviewed at a full venue. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (the paper runs classification experiments across 55 real datasets and reports accuracy/runtime/perplexity trade-offs). - Practical: Yes; trained on a single H100 GPU (~40GB) with inference and cross-validated calibration runnable on standard research GPU hardware, though the 21-24x calibration overhead and up to 69.4s mean inference time (vs. 0.6-2.9s for TabICL/TabPFN variants, Table 6) are a real practical cost. - Efficient: Symmetric embeddings cost 9-16% extra runtime over non-symmetric at calibrated settings (Table 8) but approach a 2x embedding-time overhead asymptotically for large training sets (Figure 5), and KernelICL-kNN's mean inference time (69.4s) is roughly 24x that of TabICL ensemble (2.9s). - Effective: Yes for the accuracy claim (within CD threshold of TabPFN/TabICL across 55 datasets) and for the inspectability claim on one case study (Pima Diabetes neighborhood compactness aligning with known clinical risk factors), but the paper does not test whether inspected neighbors actually help a human practitioner catch errors or improve trust, only that the neighbors exist and are computable. - Scalable: Tested up to 109,099 samples and 970 features (Airline Satisfaction, Gina Agnostic datasets in Table 4), which is a standard-scale test, not stress-scale; the paper explicitly flags that symmetric-mode overhead approaches 2x for large n and that "setups running out of memory are skipped" in the embedding-overhead analysis (Figure 5 caption), meaning true scaling limits at 10x-100x this scale (millions of samples, TabPFN-2.5's stated 50,000-sample ceiling for comparison) are not established. The work delivers no stated software artifact (no code/model release mentioned); it is a fine-tuning procedure and architectural modification applied on top of TabICL's published architecture. Strengths: - The symmetric-embedding construction (Equation 5, reprocessing training samples as queries through TF_icl) is a genuine, non-obvious architectural insight for making distance-based kernels valid within an inherently asymmetric ICL architecture, not just a straightforward kernel swap. - The four-level interpretability taxonomy (Appendix B: opaque → mechanistic → geometric → distance-based) gives a precise vocabulary for comparing prediction-head transparency across the broader tabular foundation model literature, useful beyond this paper. - The ablation methodology is unusually thorough for a workshop paper: every design choice (symmetric embeddings, calibration, projection dimension) is isolated with its own controlled comparison and statistical significance testing across all 55 benchmark datasets. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state that cross-validated scale calibration, while maximizing accuracy, yields only 11-29% relative perplexity, which "may not provide adequate inspectability" for large datasets, since even 10% relative perplexity corresponds to many samples being examined in absolute terms. They also note the kNN kernel sacrifices differentiability due to the sorting operation, requiring embeddings borrowed from Gaussian-kernel training rather than true end-to-end kNN optimization. They acknowledge the ~2x runtime overhead of symmetric embeddings in the large-sample limit and flag that calibration's 21-24x overhead is not justified for soft kernels given statistically insignificant accuracy gains. This is a direct, quantified confrontation of limitations, a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No human-subject or user study validates that exposed nearest-neighbor evidence actually helps practitioners detect errors, calibrate trust, or make better decisions; the entire motivation (clinicians validating predictions) is untested empirically, only illustrated via one qualitative case study (Table 1, Pima Diabetes). - The claim that inspectability is achieved "without sacrificing performance" rests on statistical indistinguishability (CD threshold) rather than KernelICL matching or beating TabICL outright; TabICL ensemble still has the best raw mean rank (4.95 vs. 6.25-6.75), a distinction the abstract elides. - No code, data, or model weight release is mentioned anywhere in the paper, undermining reproducibility for a paper whose stated contribution is community-facing inspectability tooling. - The neighborhood-compactness alignment with clinical risk factors (glucose, BMI) is demonstrated on exactly one dataset (Pima Diabetes, 645 samples); whether this alignment generalizes or is dataset-specific/coincidental is explicitly left as future work by the authors themselves ("requires further clinical validation"), yet it's used as a headline illustrative claim. - kNN's binary weights are stated to offer "maximum inspectability," but the paper does not quantify inspectability from a human-factors angle (e.g., how many neighbors is actually tractable for a clinician to review in a time-constrained setting) beyond the informal perplexity proxy. Bottom Line: Practitioners building tabular ML systems for regulated domains gain a concrete, well-ablated architectural recipe for near-lossless case-based interpretability grafted onto TabICL, but the paper stops short of proving the interpretability actually helps anyone, so treat it as an engineering contribution with promising but unvalidated downstream value.
Citations
#98 — Moderately Innovative (56)

A Comprehensive Study of Implementation Bugs in Multi-modal Agents

S Li, L Bu, S Liu, Y Wang, G Bai, F Xie, K Chen, C Yue · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper builds a taxonomy of implementation bugs specific to multi-modal LLM agents (M-agents) from 158 bugs mined across 34 GitHub repositories, then implements MATester, a runtime tool that flags bugs by comparing Perceptor/Planner/Executor outputs against the environment and task. - Why it's important: M-agents are already deployed in safety-critical settings (autonomous driving, robotics, GUI automation) where crashes or misbehavior cause traffic accidents, financial loss, or privacy leaks, yet no prior study isolates bugs unique to multi-modal perception and cross-modal action. - How it does it: A top-down taxonomy splits bugs into 6 end-user symptoms, 16 component-level symptoms (Perceptor/Planner/Executor), and 7 root causes, derived by manually coding 412 bug reports down to 158 agent-specific bugs (Cohen's Kappa 0.84), then MATester runs a GPT-4o judging LLM over instrumented inter-component logs to detect these symptoms automatically. - The good: MATester, built purely from the paper's own taxonomy, recovers 61.4% of already-known open issues across 12 held-out agents and surfaces 31 previously unreported bugs, giving the taxonomy a validation beyond post-hoc labeling of the same data it was derived from. - The missing: The taxonomy and MATester both depend on manual instrumentation of each agent to emit snapshot/plan/action/reflect logs, and the paper does not report false-positive rates for MATester (i.e., how many detector-flagged symptoms are not real bugs) beyond a single-run 97.6% manual-validation accuracy figure on 41 flagged bugs. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building, testing, or debugging LLM agents that consume non-textual (image/audio/video) environment state, since it is the first systematic empirical catalogue of where these agents fail and why; less relevant to text-only agent researchers.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (55) and Non-Obviousness (45) pull the composite down. The taxonomy is standard empirical-software-bug methodology (open coding, Cohen's Kappa, quasi-gold-standard sampling) applied to a new artifact class, and MATester's LLM-as-judge-over-logs is a stock pattern aimed at a new target rather than a new detection technique. MATester's precision rests on manual validation of only 41 flagged bugs with no false-positive rate, no baseline comparison, and no ablation on its own design choices (majority-of-3, GPT-4o, temperature 0.2), while GPT-4o both drives some benchmarked agents and judges them, an unexamined self-referential risk. Completeness (70) is a genuine positive: the authors confront subjectivity, agent-selection coverage, and the inter-component-only blind spot directly, and the 158-bug dataset plus tool are publicly released. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 = 56.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based agents that must perceive and act in dynamic, multi-modal environments (autonomous driving, robotics, GUI automation) face implementation failure modes that single-modal (text-only) agents do not: cross-modal perception errors, dynamic-object handling, and action/tool mismatches with heterogeneous environments.
