#137 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Progressive Visual Grounding for Medical Reasoning via Automated Chain-of-Thought Data

SY Kim, S Cho, VD Yun, G Hwang · linklings.s3.amazonaws.com · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MedCLM, a pipeline that converts lesion-detection datasets into 125k anatomically grounded medical VQA chain-of-thought (CoT) samples, paired with an Easy-to-Medium curriculum for fine-tuning a 7B vision-language model (VLM). - Why it's important: Manually annotated medical CoT data is expensive and scarce, and models trained without anatomical grounding hallucinate diagnostic rationales that could mislead clinicians. - How it does it: Lesion boxes from six existing detection datasets are automatically linked to organ segmentation masks (TotalSegmentator, CXAS) to form factual seeds that condition a teacher VLM (HuatuoGPT-Vision-34B) to generate rationales, filtered by IoU consistency and log-probability confidence, then used in a curriculum that removes explicit box overlays while keeping localization supervision. - The good: MedCLM outperforms MedVP-LLaVA, an identical-backbone baseline, across six benchmarks (VQA-RAD, SLAKE, PMC-VQA, OmniMedVQA, MedXpertQA, BESTMVQA) and wins 72.6% of GPT-5 judged head-to-head comparisons on radiology captioning. - The missing: Human evaluation of data quality is performed by the paper's own authors with unspecified radiologist involvement ('we authors with help of certified radiologist'), and no inter-rater agreement, radiologist count, or independent blind evaluation is reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building medical VQA training pipelines who need a template for organ-grounded CoT data synthesis and curriculum scheduling; the core empirical claims rest on a single 7B backbone and an unusually thin description of the human evaluation protocol.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (40) pulls the composite down hardest: no error bars, seeds, or significance tests anywhere despite sub-2-point margins over MedVP-LLaVA on the saturated benchmarks; the 91.2% clinical-correctness claim is a self-evaluation by the paper's own authors with one unspecified radiologist and no inter-rater agreement; the 72.6% win rate comes from a single GPT-5 judge with no cross-validation; and no code, weights, or data are released. Completeness (45) and Resilience (40) follow from the same gap, since the two headline validation numbers are both self-administered and would not survive a tough reviewer asking who actually graded the rationales. Novelty (55) credits the organ-segmentation-as-verification-anchor loop, which is a specific and reusable idea, while the curriculum scheduling is standard technique adapted to a new setting. Weighted composite: 0.20*55 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*40 = 45.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Medical visual question answering (VQA) systems need transparent, anatomically grounded reasoning chains for clinicians to trust their outputs, but constructing large-scale chain-of-thought (CoT) training data is costly. Existing approaches rely on manual curation (expensive, doesn't scale) or few-shot generation from proprietary models without anatomical verification (prone to hallucinated organ/location claims that propagate into trained models). Detection datasets with lesion bounding boxes exist at scale (DeepLesion, VinDr-CXR, CBIS-DDSM, etc.) but lack the structured rationales needed for CoT supervision. Key Idea: Automated organ segmentation supplies a verifiable anchor for CoT generation: assign each lesion box to its host organ via IoU-maximizing overlap with a segmentation mask, then use that (lesion, organ) pair as a factual seed to constrain what the generation model can claim, and re-verify after generation that any organ mentioned in the rationale actually overlaps the lesion box (IoU > 0.5). This closes the loop between generation and verification using the same segmentation signal, rather than relying on the generator's self-consistency alone. Methodology: Two-stage framework. (1) Data generation: a detection dataset D_det of (image, box, class) triples is passed through an organ segmentor S per image; each lesion box is assigned its highest-IoU organ mask, forming a factual seed (e.g., "pneumonia in the right lung"); HuatuoGPT-Vision-34B generates (question, answer, CoT) conditioned on the image and seed. Two filters follow: an anatomical consistency check (regenerated organ mentions must have IoU > 0.5 with the lesion box) and a confidence filter (average token log-probability > τ=0.8), plus class-aware capping to prevent pathology over-representation — this reduces 156k raw generations to 125k retained samples. (2) Curriculum fine-tuning: a 7B ViP-LLaVA backbone is trained through Easy (explicit box overlay on the image, teacher-forced rationale, combined answer/rationale/grounding loss), Medium (box overlay removed but box coordinates retained in the text supervision, plus an attention loss aligning visual attention to a Gaussian-blurred soft mask of the box), and an experimental Hard stage (answer-only supervision via self-consistency selection over K candidate rationales). A domain-aware scheduler tracks per-(lesion-class, modality) exponential moving average (EMA) training loss to set the per-epoch Easy/Medium mixing probability and gates Hard-stage activation on loss plateau and rationale-loss-gap thresholds. Design decisions: (a) using IoU-based re-verification as a post-hoc hallucination filter is well-justified given the ablation in Table V showing correctness rising from 84.2% to 91.2% through filtering stages; (b) demoting the Hard stage to an optional, rarely-active component is justified by Table IV showing it degrades VQA-RAD and PMC-VQA scores; (c) the specific token log-probability threshold (τ=0.8) and IoU threshold (0.5) for filtering are not ablated individually, so their sensitivity is unverified. Key Results: On standard benchmarks (Table II), MedCLM's Easy→Medium model scores 90.4/97.1 (Open/Closed) on VQA-RAD, 92.2/93.4 on SLAKE, and 61.2 (Closed) on PMC-VQA, versus the strongest baseline MedVP-LLaVA at 89.3/97.3, 91.6/92.9, 58.3 — gains are small (0.2-1.1 points) and mostly within a plausible margin of noise, since no error bars or multiple seeds are reported. On challenging zero-shot benchmarks (Table III), MedCLM reaches 60.2% (OmniMedVQA), 47.5% (MedXpertQA), 69.2% (BESTMVQA) against MedVP-LLaVA's 56.3%, 43.8%, 67.9% — larger absolute gaps (2.9-3.9 points) than on saturated benchmarks. The LLM-judge win-rate of 72.6% against MedVP-LLaVA (evaluated by GPT-5 on 500 IU-Xray/MIMIC-CXR samples) is the single most eye-catching number but is a pairwise preference metric from one judge model, not a task-accuracy metric. Ablations (Table IV) show anatomical context contributes roughly 1-2 points on Open-VQA-RAD (86.9→89.0 without AC, 90.4 with AC and curriculum combined), and the Easy→Medium curriculum adds roughly 1-2 points over single-stage training. Baselines (PMC-CLIP, MedVInT, LLaVA-Med, LLaVA-Med++, MedVP-LLaVA) are standard for this subfield but not the newest possible comparators; the paper does not compare against any 2025 medical CoT-specific method beyond MedVP-LLaVA (Apr. 2025). Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/METHOD (automated organ-grounded CoT-VQA generation pipeline) plus ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (curriculum scheduling). The organ-segmentation-as-verification-anchor idea for CoT generation is a genuine, specific technical contribution; the curriculum learning framework (easy-to-hard staged supervision) is a standard technique from prior work [38], [39] adapted to this setting rather than a new formulation. The three-stage curriculum with domain-aware EMA-based scheduling is a nontrivial engineering combination but builds directly on established curriculum learning and Grad-CAM-style attention supervision literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and reasonably thorough (anatomical context on/off, curriculum stage combinations, filtering pipeline stages) and support the paper's core claims directionally. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, multiple random seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere — all numbers appear to be single-run point estimates, which is a meaningful gap given the sub-2-point margins over the strongest baseline on several benchmarks. No code, data, or model weights release URL is stated anywhere in the paper. The central human evaluation (91.2% correctness, 500 samples) is described only as conducted by "we authors with help of certified radiologist" (singular, unclear count, unclear division of labor, no inter-annotator agreement reported) — this is a serious methodological weak point since it is the paper's primary claim of data quality and clinical validity, and author self-evaluation of one's own generated data is a conflict-of-interest risk not addressed. The LLM-judge evaluation uses GPT-5 as judge with no cross-validation against a second judge model or human raters, which is a known unreliable practice (LLM-judge bias toward verbose or stylistically similar outputs is a documented failure mode not discussed). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (trained VLM evaluated on held-out benchmarks). - Practical: Yes — a 7B parameter VLM (ViP-LLaVA backbone) is trainable and runnable on a single high-end GPU (e.g., A100), consistent with prior work at this scale (MedVP-LLaVA, LLaVA-Med). - Efficient: Not stated — no training wall-clock time, GPU-hours, or memory footprint is reported; the paper only reports epoch counts (35 pre-train, 3 downstream SFT) without cost figures, so relative compute efficiency against baselines is unverifiable. - Effective: Partially — the method solves a proxy (benchmark accuracy and LLM-judge preference) rather than being validated in a real clinical deployment or against practicing radiologists' diagnostic accuracy; the fairness study (stratified accuracy 89.2%-91.5% across modalities) and single-judge-model win-rate are evidence of a research-stage capability, not clinical readiness. - Scalable: Not tested — the paper does not report behavior at 10x-100x the current 125k sample corpus size or at larger backbone scale (e.g., 34B+); given the pipeline is fully automated, scaling to more detection datasets or modalities is plausible but unverified. Delivers: a training data generation pipeline and a trained model, but no software artifact (repo, weights, or dataset) is released per the text of the paper, so reproducibility depends entirely on re-implementing Algorithm 1/2 and re-running the (also unreleased) teacher-model prompts. Strengths: - The organ-segmentation-as-verification-loop design (generate rationale, then re-extract organ mentions and check IoU against the lesion box) is a concrete, well-motivated mechanism for catching a specific hallucination failure mode (anatomically inconsistent rationales), not just a generic filtering step. - The ablation in Table V isolates the incremental value of each filtering stage (IoU/confidence, then class balancing) on both data-quality (human eval) and downstream task accuracy, which is a genuinely useful methodological demonstration that data curation, not just data volume, drives the improvement. - The fairness study explicitly measures cross-modality performance parity (89.2%-91.5% across CT/X-ray/mammography/MRI) and reports the effect of removing class balancing (>15% accuracy drop on underrepresented lesion classes), which is more scrutiny of subgroup robustness than most papers in this space provide. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that the Hard (answer-only) curriculum stage gives "mixed results" — marginal gains on SLAKE but degradation on VQA-RAD and PMC-VQA — and present it as an optional, non-mandatory experimental extension rather than overstating it as a contribution. This is a credibility positive; the paper does not claim victory on a component that its own data contradicts. No other limitations (e.g., single-backbone generalization, judge-model bias, human-eval author involvement) are explicitly flagged by the authors. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The 91.2% "human evaluation" of clinical correctness is performed by the paper's own authors with unspecified radiologist support — no inter-rater agreement, no independent (blinded, non-author) evaluation, and no description of how many radiologists were involved or what fraction of the 500 samples they personally reviewed. This is the paper's central data-quality claim and is the weakest-substantiated part of the entire submission. - The GPT-5 LLM-judge win-rate (72.6%) uses a single judge model with no cross-validation against a second judge or human radiologist preference study; LLM-judges are known to favor certain stylistic patterns (verbosity, specific phrasing) independent of clinical correctness, and this risk is not discussed or controlled for. - All benchmark comparisons use a single 7B backbone and single training run per configuration; the absence of seeds/error bars means several of the headline gains (e.g., 90.4 vs 89.3 on VQA-RAD Open, a 1.1-point gap) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run noise. - No comparison against any CoT-specific medical VLM method published in 2025 beyond MedVP-LLaVA (Apr. 2025) — the field has moved quickly and other reasoning-focused medical VLMs may exist that are not benchmarked here. - Table I compares pre-training epoch counts across methods (PMC-CLIP 50, LLaVA-Med++ 20, MedVP-LLaVA 45, MedCLM 35) without justifying why fewer pre-training epochs than PMC-CLIP or MedVP-LLaVA constitutes a meaningful efficiency claim, nor does it control for total compute (batch size, dataset size per epoch) needed to make epoch counts comparable. Bottom Line: The organ-grounded data generation and verification pipeline is a specific, reusable idea worth reading for anyone building medical CoT training data, but the paper's two headline validation claims (91.2% author-conducted human correctness, 72.6% single-judge-model win-rate) are both self-administered evaluations without independent verification, which caps how much confidence a practitioner should place in the clinical-quality claims versus the underlying data-engineering technique.
Citations
#138 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Uncovering gaps and opportunities in water distribution system operations through graph-database-driven data utilization and process mapping

IR Perera, V Speight, S Young, J Boxall · eprints.whiterose.ac.uk · simulation environments for business processes - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a three-layer graph-database framework (Data, Intermediary Processes, Business Applications) that maps how a water utility's datasets flow into analytical processes and downstream operational decisions, and validates it on Scottish Water's drinking water distribution system (DWDS). - Why it's important: Water utilities generate large volumes of sensor, asset, and operational data but store it in disconnected silos, which blocks cross-functional analysis and leaves managers making decisions on incomplete information. - How it does it: It represents datasets, analytical processes, and business applications as nodes and typed relationships (FEEDS_INTO, SUPPORTS) in a Neo4j property graph, then applies degree centrality, attribute-filtered querying, and node-removal failure simulation to surface underused data and critical dependencies. - The good: The Scottish Water case study builds a concrete graph (127 nodes, 911 relationships across 60 data, 54 process, and 13 application nodes) from structured stakeholder interviews and shows specific, actionable findings, e.g., flow data feeds 21/54 intermediary processes (38.9%) and pipe network layout underpins 48/54 processes (88.9%). - The missing: current_usage and potential_usage attribute values are assigned by researcher judgment from qualitative interview responses translated into Low/Medium/High categories, with no inter-rater reliability check, second-utility validation, or sensitivity analysis showing how much the findings depend on these subjective calls. - Read it or not: Worth reading for utility data-governance managers or infrastructure-informatics researchers evaluating graph-database approaches to data-silo diagnosis; skip if looking for a hydraulic modeling, leak-detection, or machine-learning technique, since this paper does not propose any new predictive model.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (40) and Non-Obviousness (40) pull the composite down: every finding in the Results section rests on current_usage and potential_usage attributes assigned by researcher judgment from interview transcripts, with no inter-rater reliability check, no sensitivity analysis, and no released graph or interview data; the graph algorithms applied (degree centrality, subgraph filtering, node removal) are entirely standard. Completeness (55) is the strongest dimension because the authors confront the subjectivity of their attribute assignment and the single-utility scope directly rather than minimizing them. Practical Impact (45) reflects a reusable ontology and interview protocol but no released artifact, and no baseline showing the graph database beats a spreadsheet inventory at the stated diagnostic task. Weighted: 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*40 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.30*45 = 45.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Water utilities such as Scottish Water (2.6 million households, 30,586 miles of mains, 229 treatment works) collect data across sensors, GIS asset records, work-management logs, customer complaints, and external sources like weather feeds, but these are stored in siloed relational systems built around individual departmental functions and regulatory reporting rather than integrative analysis. This fragmentation, documented across the water sector (Grueau et al. 2019; Weintraut et al. 2022; Kyritsakas et al. 2023), prevents utilities from identifying which data sources are underused, which processes are bottlenecked, and which business decisions rest on weak evidentiary foundations. Prior graph-database applications in water (Bollen et al. 2021 on Flemish rivers; Qiao et al. 2024; Rose 2015) target narrow technical tasks such as leak localization or hydraulic connectivity, leaving a gap in methodology for mapping the full data-to-decision ecosystem across an entire utility's operations. Key Idea: Model a utility's information ecosystem (not its physical pipe network) as a three-layer property graph, Data feeds into Intermediary Processes which support Business Applications, with edges carrying current_usage and potential_usage attributes, so that standard graph algorithms (centrality, filtered queries, node removal) can be repurposed as a data-governance diagnostic rather than a hydraulic or fraud-detection tool. Methodology: The three-layer framework was built through semi-structured interviews with Scottish Water personnel across asset management, network operations, hydraulic modelling, water quality, IT/data management, and planning, using structured question sets (Tables 1-3) sequentially covering Layer 1 (available datasets), Layer 2 (intermediary processes such as minimum night flow analysis or water balance assessments), and Layer 3 (business applications such as burst detection or mains replacement). The graph was implemented in Neo4j, with node attributes (category, type, business_impact) and two key edge attributes, current_usage and potential_usage, categorized into Low/Medium/High/None bins based on how interviewees described dataset/process usage frequency and breadth. Three analytical techniques were applied: (1) degree centrality to rank datasets/processes by downstream connectivity, (2) relationship-attribute-filtered graph querying (e.g., current_usage=Low AND potential_usage=High/Moderate) to surface underexploited data-process links, and (3) node-removal impact simulation to trace how disruption of a data source (e.g., pressure data becoming unavailable) propagates through dependent processes and applications. Three design decisions stand out: (a) using only two relationship types (FEEDS_INTO, SUPPORTS) keeps the graph tractable but forecloses intra-layer relationships (e.g., dataset-to-dataset dependencies) that the authors acknowledge could add richer structure; (b) categorizing usage into ordinal bins (Low/Medium/High) rather than continuous scores trades precision for interpretability, reasonable for an interview-derived dataset but unvalidated against any ground truth; (c) restricting scope to DWDS operations at a single utility is well-justified as a tractable proof-of-concept but limits generalizability claims. Key Results: The Scottish Water graph comprises 127 nodes (60 data, 54 intermediary process, 13 business application) and 911 relationships. Centrality analysis found flow data feeds into 21/54 intermediary processes (38.9%) supporting 11 business applications, pressure data feeds into 17/54 processes (31.5%) supporting 11 applications, and pipe network layout (GIS asset data) underpins 48/54 intermediary processes (88.9%), the single most connected dataset in the graph. Filtered querying on the burst detection and localization application identified four underutilized intermediary processes (satellite-based monitoring, ML anomaly detection, ML time-series prediction, pipe condition assessment) with Low current_usage but High/Moderate potential_usage, and flagged water quality data, power/energy usage data, and satellite imagery as underused data inputs. The failure simulation for pressure-data removal found that no intermediary process depends exclusively on pressure data (none became fully non-functional), but several processes such as minimum night flow analysis showed reduced robustness/confidence. All results are qualitative/structural findings from a single case study; no quantitative baseline, comparison utility, or ground-truth validation of the usage classifications is reported. No datasets or graph exports are released; the paper states "all relevant data are included in the paper," but the underlying interview data and full graph are not shared. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the three-layer Data/Intermediary-Process/Business-Application graph schema applied to utility-wide, cross-functional data governance) and NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the Neo4j implementation and associated query patterns), applied via ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION of well-established graph analytics (degree centrality, subgraph filtering, node-removal simulation) to a domain (holistic water-utility data-process mapping) that prior work (Bollen et al. 2021; Qiao et al. 2024; Rose 2015) has only applied to narrow hydraulic-network representation. The genuinely new contribution is the layered ontology and its application as an organizational diagnostic; the graph algorithms themselves are standard and not novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are possible or presented, since this is a descriptive case-study framework rather than a predictive model with tunable components. No error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests are reported, appropriate given the qualitative, interview-derived nature of the data but meaning all quantitative claims (e.g., 38.9%, 88.9%) describe a single fixed graph rather than a distribution. Code, the Neo4j graph instance, and interview data are not released; the paper states data availability as "included in the paper," but the full node/edge attribute set (127 nodes, 911 relationships) is not provided in machine-readable form, so the graph cannot be independently queried or the centrality figures independently verified. The central methodological concern is that current_usage and potential_usage values, the two attributes driving every finding in Results, are assigned by "researcher judgement" synthesizing tentative interviewee statements into three-way categorical bins, with no inter-annotator agreement check, blind coding, or sensitivity analysis testing whether findings change under alternative categorization thresholds. The study is also evaluated only on the same single utility (Scottish Water) that co-designed the framework through the interview process, so there is no held-out or independent validation of whether the framework generalizes to a utility with different organizational structure. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a graph-database tool/methodology evaluated via a deployment case study rather than a controlled experiment). - Practical: Yes; built on Neo4j, a widely available commercial/open-source graph database requiring no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Not evaluated; no query latency, memory footprint, or scaling benchmarks are reported for the 127-node/911-edge graph, though this scale is modest by graph-database standards. - Effective: Partially; the case study demonstrates the framework surfaces plausible, interpretable findings (underutilized satellite data, high centrality of pipe-layout and flow data) that align with domain literature, but there is no comparison against a non-graph baseline (e.g., a simple spreadsheet-based data inventory) showing the graph approach outperforms simpler alternatives at the stated goal. - Scalable: Not tested; the paper claims generalizability to other utility types and infrastructure sectors (sewerage, transportation) but provides no evidence at 10x-100x the node/edge count, nor any test on a second utility. The work delivers a methodology and a Neo4j schema/query pattern (node/edge attribute templates in Figure 2), not a released software artifact, trained model, or public dataset; a practitioner would need to rebuild the graph from scratch following the paper's interview protocol. Strengths: - The three-layer schema (Data / Intermediary Processes / Business Applications) with FEEDS_INTO / SUPPORTS typed relationships is a clean, reusable ontology for a problem, cross-departmental data silo diagnosis, that prior water-sector graph work had not addressed at this scope. - Grounding the graph in structured, multi-department interviews (Tables 1-3 guiding questions) is a credible and repeatable elicitation protocol, not an ad hoc data dump. - The node-removal failure simulation (Section 4.3, Figure 9) is a genuinely useful diagnostic pattern, showing not just what depends on a data source but the graded severity of dependency (exclusive vs. degraded vs. unaffected), which goes beyond simple connectivity counting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The authors explicitly flag that attribute assignment (current_usage, potential_usage) is subjective and depends on researcher judgment and expert interpretation of qualitative interview responses. - They note the framework requires that connections already be known/identified by interviewees to be captured, so entirely unknown or unrecognized data relationships cannot be discovered by the graph itself. - They acknowledge the framework is a diagnostic tool, not a solution generator; it does not automatically prescribe fixes for identified gaps. - They note the current implementation uses only two relationship types (FEEDS_INTO, SUPPORTS) and could be extended with intra-layer relationships for deeper interdependency modeling. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No second utility or cross-utility comparison is attempted despite the paper's repeated claims of generalizability to "the wider water sector and other industries"; this is an extrapolation beyond the evidence presented. - No comparison against a simpler baseline (e.g., a relational data inventory spreadsheet or an informal stakeholder workshop) that could test whether the graph-database structure itself adds value over a lighter-weight mapping exercise. - The interview sample size and number of participants per department are not stated, making it impossible to assess whether the usage-attribute judgments reflect broad organizational consensus or a small number of individual opinions. - The paper does not report how disagreements between interviewees about a dataset's current_usage or potential_usage were resolved when they conflicted, beyond a general statement that the graph structure "allows for quick updates." - Given the EPSRC/Scottish Water funding relationship (an industrial CDT partnership), the case study utility is also the funding partner, which is disclosed but raises a mild conflict-of-interest consideration for how critically underutilization findings might have been framed. Bottom Line: A practitioner responsible for utility data governance gains a concrete, reusable interview protocol and graph schema for auditing data silos, but should treat the specific Scottish Water findings as an illustrative single-site case study rather than validated evidence that graph databases outperform simpler data-inventory methods.
Search Results
#139 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Insight generation using language models in enterprise settings

F Appelblad, L Nordberg · odr.chalmers.se · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper builds and empirically compares two enterprise insight-generation pipelines, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting versus a Recursive Language Model (RLM)-inspired, tool-based iterative retrieval system, evaluated on a real internal document corpus at a large manufacturing company (Essity). - Why it's important: Enterprises hold 80-90% of their data as unstructured text spread across incompatible repositories, and naive LLM deployment on this data faces context-window limits and weak multi-step reasoning, so picking the right retrieval-and-reasoning architecture has direct cost and trust consequences for real deployments. - How it does it: The RLM pipeline replaces RAG's single-pass retrieve-then-generate step with a root LLM that iteratively calls navigation and delegation tools (constrained to a structured JSON action protocol rather than arbitrary Python, for compliance) to spawn subsystem agents over limited document subsets before synthesizing a final answer. - The good: A blinded, paired qualitative expert evaluation (3 domain experts, 75 paired query-answer comparisons) found a statistically significant preference for the RLM pipeline on Utility (Wilcoxon signed-rank p < 0.001, RLM win-rate 54.7% vs. RAG 24.0%), corroborating the automated RAGAS-based metrics. - The missing: The automated Faithfulness metric is not comparable across pipelines (RAG is scored against retrieved chunks, RLM against subsystem summaries), so the one metric where RAG "wins" (0.877 vs. 0.747) cannot be read as a real grounding disadvantage for RLM, and the study never resolves this with a metric computed on a common reference. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners scoping a RAG-vs-agentic-retrieval decision under real cost/governance constraints, especially the TOE-framework-derived design requirements (Section 4.4) and the enterprise implementation principles (Section 7.4); the core empirical finding (RLM more accurate and trusted, ~4.6x slower, ~5.2x more tokens) is a single n=1 company case study and the effect sizes should not be taken as universal.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (45) and Novelty (40) pull the composite down: the RLM architecture is Zhang et al.'s, the contribution is an enterprise-constrained adaptation (JSON action protocol replacing the Python REPL), and the evaluation is architecture-vs-architecture with no ablation, no non-agentic baseline (full-context stuffing over only 600 documents, or BM25), an LLM judge from the same model family as the systems being judged, and n=3 experts on 75 paired ratings. Impact (35) reflects a confidential single-company prototype with no code release and ~5.2x token / ~4.6x latency overhead whose p95 nearly exhausts the 170k budget. Completeness (65) is a real positive: the authors flag the non-comparable Faithfulness metric themselves rather than claiming the win, and are direct about the single-company, single-domain scope. Weighted: 0.15*40 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*35 = 44.
systems Novelty 40 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 65 Resilience 50 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations are increasing AI investment (BCG: corporations expect to double 2026 AI spending) but ~75% report no measurable ROI as of late 2024, a gap attributed to both technical constraints (context-window limits, degraded performance with long or middle-buried context, weak multi-step reasoning) and organizational factors (fragmented data systems, low trust in ungrounded outputs, unclear governance). At the case company (Essity, R&D division), stakeholders across roles reported spending significant time manually searching fragmented, low-interoperability systems (SharePoint, lab systems, project archives) to reconstruct historical project knowledge, with information sometimes lost entirely when project teams disband. The specific gap addressed: existing single-pass RAG cannot reliably synthesize information across multiple documents or perform multi-hop reasoning (e.g., "how have reporting requirements evolved over time" requires retrieving and connecting facts across many documents), and no prior work had evaluated whether recursive/iterative retrieval architectures (RLM) offer a practical advantage over RAG+CoT specifically in a real enterprise document corpus with governance constraints. Key Idea: Replace a single fixed retrieval step with an iterative, tool-mediated delegation loop: a root-level LLM decomposes the query, browses a hierarchical summary catalogue, and delegates bounded sub-tasks to subsystem LLM instances that read only a narrow document slice and return a confidence-flagged answer; the root then synthesizes across subsystem responses rather than committing early to a fixed top-k retrieval. The paper's secondary contribution is methodological: replacing the original RLM's arbitrary Python code-execution loop with a structured JSON action-protocol over a fixed tool set, to satisfy a no-code-execution governance constraint (NFR1) without abandoning the iterative architecture. Methodology: The system has three stages: (1) a preprocessing pipeline that parses PDF/Word/PowerPoint documents, masks PII via regex-pattern rules, chunks text deterministically (800 characters, 100-character overlap), embeds chunks into a SQL-backed vector index, and builds a bottom-up hierarchical JSON summary catalogue (per-document then per-folder LLM summarization); (2) two alternative model pipelines built on the same data: RAG (top-k=5 semantic retrieval, ranked by normalized similarity, fed into a few-shot CoT-instructed prompt) and RLM (root system with 5 tools -- get top-level tree, browse group, broad/regex search, delegate -- iterating over a fixed token/iteration budget with subsystems restricted to their delegated document subset, each returning a confidence label and found/not-found flag); (3) a chat frontend exposing citations and token/latency metrics. Three design decisions stand out: (a) the CoT few-shot exemplars for RAG were constructed via a label-free quality filter -- diversity-sampled Auto-CoT with self-consistency across 5 temperature-varied reasoning chains, clustered via k-means (k=3) on 15 held-out queries, filtered by pairwise-cosine-similarity consistency scores (well-justified, avoids requiring labeled ground truth in a domain where none exists); (b) the RLM's structured-JSON action protocol substitutes for RLM's original Python REPL specifically to satisfy the no-external-code-execution constraint (well-justified given the enterprise governance requirement, though it forfeits the flexibility of arbitrary code); (c) subsystems return a confidence attribute (low/medium/high) and found/not-found flag that propagates into the final answer, used to satisfy provenance/insufficient-evidence requirements (FR3/FR4) -- reasonable, though the confidence label itself is unvalidated (no check that the LLM's stated confidence correlates with actual correctness). Key Results: Automated evaluation (RAGAS-style LLM-as-judge, 80 synthetic Q&A pairs: 20 single-hop specific, 30 multi-hop specific, 30 multi-hop abstract) shows RLM outperforming RAG on Answer Correctness (mean 0.624 vs. 0.506) and Answer Relevancy (0.907 vs. 0.806), with RAG's correctness degrading most on multi-hop queries (RAG multi-hop-abstract: 0.515 vs. single-hop 0.542; RLM stays closer: 0.531 vs. 0.768). Faithfulness favors RAG (0.877 vs. 0.747) but the paper explicitly flags this comparison as non-comparable across architectures because the reference context differs (raw chunks for RAG vs. subsystem-summary answers for RLM). Efficiency: RLM took ~4.6x longer (96.5s vs. 20.9s mean execution) and consumed ~5.2x more tokens (48,994 vs. 9,441 mean), with p95 token usage (157,482) approaching the fixed 170,000-token computational budget. Qualitative expert evaluation (3 domain experts, 40 sample queries, 75 total paired ratings, 5-point Likert on Utility/Alignment/Trust) found RLM preferred on Utility (Wilcoxon signed-rank p < 0.001, RAG win-rate 24.0% vs. RLM 54.7%) and directionally on Trust (p = 0.051, RLM win-rate 52.0% vs. RAG 26.7%), but no significant difference on Alignment (p = 0.127, high 29% tie rate). No baselines beyond the RAG-vs-RLM comparison are used; there is no comparison against a naive full-context LLM or a simpler keyword search baseline. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (an enterprise-constrained implementation of the RLM framework using a structured action protocol instead of code execution) combined with ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (the diversity-sampled Auto-CoT-with-self-consistency method for building few-shot exemplars without labeled data) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (first head-to-head RAG-vs-RLM comparison on a real enterprise unstructured-document corpus, using both automated RAGAS metrics and blinded expert ratings). The RLM architecture itself is not novel (published by Zhang et al., cited as ref [11], reportedly from 2026 per the bibliography, i.e., a very recent unpublished/preprint framework); this thesis's novelty is the enterprise-constrained adaptation and its empirical validation, not the underlying method. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolating which RLM design choices (delegation, subsystem count, confidence flagging) drive the improvement; the comparison is architecture-vs-architecture, not component-vs-component. The automated evaluation uses an LLM-as-judge (the same model used by the pipelines), a known source of self-evaluation bias not discussed or mitigated. The expert evaluation has n=3 raters and n=75 paired comparisons with substantial missing coverage (raters skip queries outside their expertise, explicitly described as unbalanced), and the paper itself flags Alignment and Trust results as "indicative rather than precise" given this small sample. No code, data, or model weights are released (proprietary enterprise deployment, explicitly for confidentiality reasons); all reported examples are masked/paraphrased and none of the actual document content, project identifiers, or the synthetic test set are shared, which is appropriate given the confidentiality constraint but means the evaluation cannot be externally reproduced or audited. The synthetic ground truth used for Answer Correctness is itself LLM-generated from the same corpus, and the paper acknowledges this "introduces a potential layer of bias and noise" and that the judge penalizes omission of specific facts even when the model's answer is otherwise valuable, a Goodhart's-Law-adjacent concern the authors surface honestly. Faithfulness is explicitly flagged by the authors as not cross-architecturally comparable, a genuinely careful and unusual level of honesty about a metric's limitations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built, deployed prototype evaluated via both automated benchmarking and real-user/expert assessment in a live enterprise environment). - Practical: Yes, deployed on a company-approved commercial cloud platform using standard hosted LLM endpoints (Claude Sonnet 4.5) and vector search; no custom hardware required. - Efficient: RLM incurs a substantial overhead (~5x tokens, ~4.6x latency) relative to RAG, with p95 token usage nearly exhausting the fixed 170,000-token budget, meaning cost scales poorly with query complexity at the tail. - Effective: The evidence (RAGAS metrics + blinded expert Utility/Trust ratings) supports the claim that RLM answers real enterprise queries more accurately than RAG, not merely a proxy metric, though the sample size (75 paired expert ratings, 3 raters) is modest for the strength of the "superior choice" conclusion drawn in the Conclusion chapter. - Scalable: Not tested. The corpus was fixed at 600 documents from a single domain (project documentation); the paper explicitly lists corpus-size scaling and cross-domain generalization as future work, and the fixed iteration/token budget suggests the RLM's per-query cost would grow further, not shrink, at larger corpus scale, since delegation to more documents implies more subsystem calls. The work delivers a deployed prototype (web interface, no public code release) rather than a reusable library, plus a bounded methodological contribution (the label-free CoT exemplar-selection procedure) that could be reused by other teams facing the same "no ground truth available" constraint. Strengths: - Genuine methodological care in adapting an academic architecture (RLM's Python REPL) to a hard governance constraint (no arbitrary code execution) via a structured JSON action protocol, explicitly reasoned through rather than glossed over. - Combines an automated LLM-as-judge benchmark with an independent, blinded, paired human-expert evaluation and reports where the two diverge (Faithfulness vs. Trust) rather than cherry-picking the metric that favors the preferred architecture. - Grounds system design requirements in a structured Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework derived from stakeholder interviews and a formal use-case prioritization funnel (41 candidates scored down to 1), giving the design requirements section (FR1-FR4, NFR1-NFR5) clear empirical traceability rather than being asserted a priori. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors are direct about three limitations: (1) findings are from a single-company, single-domain (project documentation) case study and claims of generalization to other organizations, use cases, or data sources are not guaranteed; (2) evaluation and integration support were done on a voluntary basis by staff without dedicated project time, constraining the number of queries/responses assessed and limiting design-iteration depth; (3) the front-end user-experience was not directly tested as part of the evaluation, so factors like the RLM's visible reasoning trajectory or the source-panel layout could have influenced expert Utility/Trust ratings independent of underlying answer quality, a confound the authors flag but cannot rule out. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No comparison against a simple non-agentic baseline such as full-context stuffing (feeding the whole corpus, given it is only 600 documents) or a keyword/BM25 search baseline, so the incremental value of semantic RAG itself (versus RLM) over a trivial baseline is not established. The LLM-as-judge for automated metrics is the same model family used to generate the answers being judged, a self-evaluation bias the paper does not address or control for (e.g., no cross-model judge, no human-vs-LLM-judge agreement check). The RLM's self-reported confidence flag (low/medium/high) is never validated against actual answer correctness, so it is unclear whether the flag meaningfully signals unreliable answers or is itself an unverified LLM claim about its own reliability. The efficiency comparison would benefit from a cost-normalized quality metric (e.g., correctness per dollar or per token) to make the "is the RLM's overhead worth it" trade-off, which the Discussion chapter explicitly poses as the central open question, more directly answerable from the reported numbers. Bottom Line: A practitioner scoping an enterprise RAG-vs-agentic-retrieval decision gains a concrete, honestly-reported (if small-sample) data point favoring iterative/recursive retrieval for multi-hop query distributions at roughly 5x token and latency cost, plus a reusable no-code-execution RLM adaptation pattern for regulated environments; the generalization claims should be discounted given the single-company, single-domain, n=3-expert evaluation, and a reader after a rigorous quantitative benchmark of RLM-style architectures should look for a larger, cross-domain, ablated follow-up rather than treating this thesis's numbers as broadly transferable.
Search Results
#140 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Multi-Model Business Intelligence Exploration for Monitoring and Improving Institutional Reporting

V Schnabl · repositum.tuwien.at · simulation environments for business processes - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a validated TO-BE workflow redesign for TU Wien's ReposiTUm institutional repository, using Condition and Asset Networks (the procan.do methodology) to diagnose root causes of data-quality failures and a 10,000-trial Monte Carlo simulation to quantify the redesign's impact before implementation. - Why it's important: Inaccurate or incomplete publication metadata threatens the university's statutory Intellectual Capital Statement and can trigger conservative funding estimates or budget retentions under Austrian financing law, so validating a process fix before touching a live compliance system has direct financial stakes. - How it does it: It traces undesired outcomes (validation failures) backward through explicit Condition Networks to root causes (a submitter competence gap, a dysfunctional full-record-reversion feedback loop modeled via STAMP), then engineers a unidirectional validation architecture with automated DOI-based metadata acquisition, and stress-tests it with a parameterized Monte Carlo digital twin calibrated on 20,998 real submission records (2023-2025). - The good: The empirical grounding is unusually strong for a process-redesign thesis — rejection rates (11% cumulative), stage-level wait times (up to 187 hours mean at the Library stage), and DOI availability (43%) are extracted directly from the production Oracle/DSpace database rather than assumed. - The missing: All quantitative results are ex-ante (simulated), not ex-post; the task-duration parameters feeding the simulation are triangular distributions from expert estimation, not measured HCI telemetry, so the headline 65% touch-time reduction is only as reliable as those expert guesses. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone doing institutional repository/CRIS modernization or applying Design Science Research with process/asset network modeling to legacy migration; it is a single-institution case study with a small (n=4) expert panel, so treat the specific percentages as illustrative rather than generalizable benchmarks.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (55) and Completeness (55) are the strongest dimensions: the diagnosis is calibrated on 20,998 real submission records from the production Oracle/DSpace database, and rejection rates, stage wait times, and DOI coverage are measured rather than assumed. Novelty (40) and Non-Obviousness (35) pull the composite down, since applying an existing methodology (procan.do Condition/Asset Networks, STAMP, Design Science Research) to one institutional repository is a competent application, not a new formulation. Practical Impact (40), the heaviest weight at 0.30, is limited because every headline number (65% touch-time reduction) is ex-ante simulated with triangular task durations from a four-expert panel, and no system was actually deployed. Resilience (45) suffers from the same gap: the ex-post evidence that would defend the redesign does not exist. Weighted composite: 0.15*40 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.30*40 = 45.
systems Novelty 40 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Academic institutions that receive partial public funding must submit accurate, auditable reports of scientific output to justify budgets and comply with statutory mandates (in Austria, the University Act 2002 and University Financing Ordinance). At TU Wien this depends on ReposiTUm, a legacy DSpace-CRIS 5 repository whose data-acquisition workflow evolved organically into a fragmented, high-friction process: a nine-step submission wizard, three sequential validation tiers (RIS, Library, Faculty), and a "full record reversion" policy that returns the entire record to the submitter on any late-stage error. The people entering metadata (often delegated administrative assistants, not the actual authors) frequently lack the bibliographic, taxonomic, and project-context knowledge the workflow assumes they have, producing altered titles, misclassified publication types, and dummy placeholder entries. Simultaneously, the underlying DSpace-CRIS 9 migration lacks a Product Owner or formal requirements governance, so technical debt and undocumented scope drift compound the operational problem. The paper's stated goal is not a general theory of institutional reporting but an analytically grounded single-case study of how to diagnose and fix this class of problem. Key Idea: The core methodological move is treating business-process diagnosis and system architecture design as a single connected exercise via the procan.do methodology's Condition Networks and Asset Networks: rather than mapping activity flows alone (as BPMN or Process Mining would), the thesis explicitly traces causal chains from an undesired outcome ("validation is insufficient") backward through stakeholder-specific conditions (submitter competence, API reliability, interface usability) to concrete architectural assets, then forward again into testable Behavior-Driven Development scenarios. This lets the authors distinguish structural root causes (a dysfunctional feedback loop, per Leveson's STAMP framework) from surface symptoms (high error rates), and it is the vehicle used to justify why interface tweaks alone cannot fix the problem. Methodology: The thesis follows a three-phase Design Science Research cycle scoped by Runeson's case-study guidelines. Phase 1 (Chapter 4) applies procan.do Steps 1-3: define Scope of Work via an IDEF0 context diagram, identify the Effect of Interest (validation failure), and build Condition Networks (Sunshine/Rainy Day cases) that back-track the failure to root causes — primarily a triple submitter competence gap (bibliographic, taxonomic, project-context) and a STAMP-framed inadequate feedback control loop (full record reversion instead of localized correction). Phase 2 (Chapter 5) applies Steps 4-5: an optimized TO-BE architecture built on three pillars — an Import-First/Identifier-Driven automated metadata acquisition strategy (DOI-based, via Crossref/Scopus APIs), a Unidirectional Validation policy that collapses the three-tier pipeline into a single expert step with in-place correction (replacing full-record rejection), and a structured requirements checkpoint (Definition of Ready enforced by a Product Owner, formalized via Behavior-Driven Development Gherkin scenarios). Four incremental, additive "solution packages" (Procedural Streamlining, Structured Requirements Checkpoint, Asynchronous Expert Operations, Identifier-Driven Maximum Automation) operationalize these pillars for testing. Phase 3 (Chapter 6) applies Step 6: a dual evaluation combining semi-structured qualitative expert interviews (four domain experts across Library, RIS, software engineering, and Faculty) for architectural fitness, and a quantitative 10,000-trial Monte Carlo simulation (Python, fixed seed) built as a "digital twin,
